Another mitigation: exploit our abundant execution data – by year 5 we’ll have lots of circuit logs; if our initial AI approaches falter, we pivot to data-centric approaches (maybe simpler heuristic algorithms using our data). Finally, we budget these projects such that a failure won’t sink the company – core deliverables (orchestrator, trust fabric) are not dependent on AI success (they add value but aren’t required for the platform to function for customers). So, in worst case, Discovery remains an R&D side project (a potential nice-to-have) and doesn’t affect the viability of our core product, which continues to deliver value through traditional means[35][342].
●	Regulatory and Security Risks: Baseline expects we navigate compliance (IP protection, data privacy) proactively and have no major breaches. Risk: Regulatory changes or security incidents – e.g., a new law might restrict how/where we store audit data, or a vulnerability could compromise our ledger or watermark scheme (which would be reputationally damaging). Mitigation: We engage regulators early (we plan participation in standards and maybe regulatory sandboxes) to anticipate legal requirements[91]. For instance, if data localization laws tighten, we ensure our policy engine and deployment can satisfy those by configuration (we already account for region policies). If a sweeping patent law change came (e.g., requiring registration of AI-generated inventions), our provenance system is already well-placed to help, turning a risk into an opportunity. On security, we treat every release as if it could be audited; we did supply chain hardening in V8 and continue scanning and third-party audits. If a breach or flaw does occur (e.g., watermark defeated by an adversary), we have a fail-closed approach – we’d halt that feature (perhaps temporarily disable watermarking or ledger adds) until a fix is out, and be transparent with customers (our append-only logs help show what happened and that we took action)[343][279]. We likely establish a security response process by mid-roadmap, including perhaps a bug bounty to catch issues early[344][345]. Our kill criteria include any sign of “systematic weakness” triggers redesign rather than pushing on a flawed path[346]. For example, if the blockchain ledger approach was deemed insecure or too slow at scale, we’d pivot to an alternate design (we flagged this as a possible kill condition and would implement the simpler solution promptly)[157]. Essentially, we would rather backtrack on a design than erode trust by insisting on it. Insurance (cyber insurance) and contractual protections will also help mitigate financial impact of any incidents by year 8–10.
●	Financial and Funding Risks: We assume staged funding and eventually revenue growth. Risk: Funding shortfall or revenue delay – e.g., a planned round doesn’t materialize due to market downturn, or customers take longer to pay for the product. Mitigation: Keep burn adaptable: our modular hiring plan means we can slow hiring if needed to extend runway (particularly in R&D heavy areas which can scale down gracefully if cash is tight). We also pursue diversified funding: perhaps government grants for quantum (since we do cutting-edge stuff in national interest areas like PQC, error correction) to supplement VC funds. If revenue is lagging, consider services offerings to generate cash (e.g., consulting for quantum strategy using our expertise, or offering training on our platform) in the interim. Also maintain a lean structure – by focusing on evidence-backed development, we reduce waste. We set financial go/kill triggers: e.g., if we planned to raise Series B by end of 2027 but metrics aren’t there, consider extending Series A or seeking strategic investors at possibly lower valuation but with partnership benefits – basically be flexible. We also keep potential exit options open as a risk mitigation: if by year ~5 the standalone path looks risky, we might negotiate acquisition while our IP is still valued, which, though not the original plan, secures the technology’s development and the team’s mission. Our internal governance board keeps an eye on spend vs. milestone achievements; if we miss a milestone that a funding event hinged on, we immediately enact cost-saving and pivot plans (as noted in our governance section with metrics-driven pivot on high-level finances)[347][348].
Each of these risks is tracked with owners and mitigation plans in our risk register[313]. By continuously updating that and tying it to our go/kill decision points, we ensure no surprise derails the program completely – we either execute contingencies to stay on course or consciously pivot the strategy with the core vision (a trusted, autonomous quantum ecosystem) still in sight. In both the baseline and more adverse scenarios, the emphasis is on maintaining trust (from users, partners, investors) by being transparent, evidence-driven, and ready to adjust course long before a risk becomes an existential threat[299][332]. This way, at the 10-year mark, whether as an independent company or part of a larger entity, the mission launched in V1.0.0 will have delivered a lasting impact on the quantum computing industry.

[253] [254] Invarian_Vision.docx
file://file_000000000878722f805a823bab32f16c



1.  	Orientation Snapshot (Very Short)
2.      Three-Plane Ecosystem: Invarian’s A4Q platform spans three interdependent product planes. Discovery (Quantum Asset Generative Foundry, QAGF) focuses on AI-driven generation/optimization of quantum circuits and error-correcting codes. Governance (Quantum IP Provenance Ledger, QIPL) provides a secure trust fabric (policy engine, provenance ledger, watermarking) to enforce IP rights and compliance across multi-cloud quantum workflows. Autonomy (Fault-Tolerant Autonomy Engine, FTAE) delivers adaptive quantum orchestration for real-time control of quantum resources (scheduling jobs, managing fault-tolerance, carbon-aware routing)[1][2]. Each plane corresponds to a major product pillar in the 10-year strategy, ensuring that Discovery/QAGF drives quantum content innovation, Governance/QIPL ensures trust and compliance, and Autonomy/FTAE provides an operational runtime for quantum computing.
3.      Unified Monorepo & Core Services: The A4Q platform is built as a single monorepo with modular components aligned to these planes. Key directories include /services (core microservices like trust-fabric and orchestrator), /platform (common libraries and compiler components such as qir-adapters and the Rust watermark-pass), /ml (machine learning tools e.g. autocal-rl for calibration and qec-decoder for error correction R&D), and supporting modules (SDK, infra, ops). This structure (established by the GA V8 release) maps ownership and KPIs per package – e.g., the compiler team owns the compile latency invariant[3]. The Trust Fabric service (Go) implements the Trust Contract Engine (OPA/Rego policies) and an immutable provenance ledger (backed by Postgres), exposing APIs like /v1/seal (seal job with contract), /v1/append (ledger write), and /v1/verify (watermark check)[4]. The Orchestrator service (Go) handles job scheduling and execution – clients submit jobs via /v1/submit and check /v1/status; the orchestrator routes each job to an available backend based on cost, queue, and live carbon intensity signals[5]. By design, these services run in high-availability mode (active/active across regions) to ensure failover: the GA baseline already demonstrated sub-second zone failovers and <60 s region recovery[6].
4.      Compiler & Watermarking Pipeline: A4Q’s compiler subsystem (Rust) ingests high-level circuits (OpenQASM3, QIR) and lowers them to a portable intermediate representation (the “Portable Intent IR”). It injects invisible watermarks into quantum circuits via a custom watermark-pass (e.g. slight gate angle biases) to trace IP provenance[7]. The compiler attaches metadata like depth overhead (Δdepth) to ensure watermarking stays within strict limits (budget ≤0.5% extra circuit depth)[8]. This pipeline enables hardware-agnostic execution: the IR and watermark tags are recognized across multiple QPU backends, enforcing IP tracking without breaking execution. Platform adapters in platform/qir-adapters provide compatibility with standard toolchains (e.g. QIR, LLVM passes), making sure A4Q’s enhancements (like watermark tags) align with emerging standards.
5.      ML Toolkit & Calibration Services: Under the /ml directory, A4Q includes experimental AI/ML components that feed into Discovery and Autonomy planes. AutoCal-RL (Python/Rust) applies reinforcement learning to qubit calibration – adjusting control parameters to minimize errors – and runs alongside vendor calibration routines to evaluate improvements. A QEC Decoder module ingests error syndromes from quantum error correction experiments to suggest syndrome decoding strategies[9]. In the GA (V8) baseline these were non-critical pilot features (used in lab “digital twin” environments only), but they lay the groundwork for future autonomous calibration and anomaly detection. Over time, these ML services will transition from offline R&D pipelines to integrated components (e.g. automatically tuning qubits in production or flagging error patterns to the scheduler).
6.      Infrastructure, Deployment & Observability: A4Q follows a “real-first” ops ethos – everything is deployed with production in mind. The monorepo’s /infra and /ops directories contain Docker Compose configs for local dev, Helm charts for cluster deployment, and Terraform/IaC scripts for cloud setups[10]. By V8, the platform was fully containerized and Kubernetes-ready, with a built-in observability stack. Each microservice exposes a /metrics endpoint (Prometheus format) registering all DK invariant metrics (latency histograms, error rates, etc.), and OpenTelemetry traces covering end-to-end flows[11]. Standard dashboards (Grafana) and alerting rules are configured to watch these metrics in real time – for example, an alert triggers if ledger append latency or watermark TPR deviates beyond budget[12]. The SDK/CLI (/sdk, TypeScript) provides developer-friendly access: users can run a4qctl submit <circuit> to send a job, attach a trust contract (specifying region, privacy or IP constraints), and then monitor execution via CLI or API[13]. All client-server calls are secured with mutual TLS plus post-quantum cryptography (PQC) wrapping, anticipating future quantum threats[14].
7.      Design Invariants (DK) – Reliability and Performance Gates: From day one, A4Q established 10 immutable SLO invariants (“DK gates”) that the platform must meet or exceed. These include p95 latency targets (e.g. ≤250 ms compile, ≤120 ms ledger append, ≤20 ms PQC encryption overhead), accuracy and availability minima (watermark true-positive ≥99%, ≤0.5% added circuit depth, system uptime ≥99.5% with failover recovery <60 s), and efficiency goals (audit log coverage ≥99%, carbon cost ≤55 gCO₂ per job, end-to-end job success ≥92%)[15]. These figures were codified as constants in the codebase and enforced in CI/staging – any merge that breaches a DK threshold causes a fail-closed build (block promotion)[16]. Notably, the GA release (v1.1/V8) proved all 10/10 DK invariants in real operation (e.g. achieved ~47 ms compile p95, 100% watermark detection in test jobs)[17]. Beyond the baseline, stretch goals (“Ops Gold” targets) were defined to add safety margin – e.g. tightening key latencies by ~10% (compile to 230 ms, ledger to 110 ms) and requiring zero-error canary runs ≥15 min before any production update[18]. This invariant-driven discipline (with evidentiary artifacts like JSON logs and SHA256 hashes for every test run) is the backbone of the execution program. New features must inherit or extend these DK gates – performance, security, and reliability are never compromised for scope[19].
8.      10-Year Vision and Release Eras: The high-level Invarian roadmap (V10) lays out five 2-year eras from late 2025 to 2035[20]. Each era corresponds to a major version milestone (v1.x through v5.x/6.0) and concentrates on advancing each plane: e.g. Era 0–2 (2025–27) focuses on post-GA hardening and pilot deployments, Era 2–4 on scaling to multi-tenant ecosystems, Era 4–6 on fault-tolerant readiness and broad adoption, Era 6–8 on optimization at scale, and Era 8–10 on full autonomy and industry standardization[21]. The V10 plan specifies goals, feature themes, and “Go/Kill” gates for each era, ensuring that progress is evidence-based. In the sections below, we translate this vision into an actionable execution program: first detailing a 24-month engineering checklist (Phase 1), then outlining the 2-year era milestones (Phase 2), followed by stakeholder narratives (Phases 3–5) that tie the technical plan to partner and investor value.
________________________________________
1.      0–24 Month Execution Program (Phase-by-Phase Checklists)
 We divide the first 24 months post-GA into six 4-month phases (Phase 1 = months 0–4, …, Phase 6 = months 20–24). Each phase has specific goals and a set of concrete task blocks. Bolded tasks are mission-critical for achieving a “v1.0.0 production-ready” system. All tasks follow a structured format (Owner, Timebox, Steps, Acceptance tests, etc.) to ensure evidence-driven completion. The emphasis is on eliminating any “simulated” or stubbed components from the GA baseline, hardening the core workflows (/seal, /submit, /status, telemetry, etc.), integrating real dependencies (PQC key management, QPU backends, carbon data feeds), and establishing a repeatable deployment + monitoring pipeline. By the end of Phase 6, the platform should support a credible external pilot deployment with full observability of all DK invariants in a production-like environment.*
Phase 1 (Months 0–4) – Post-GA Hardening & DevOps Foundation
 Phase Goals (0–4mo): Establish a robust development and deployment baseline, enforce real-world configurations (no simulation modes), and address any GA shortcuts. Key goals include: (1) Enforce Real-Mode Security – wire in real PQC key management (KMS/HSM) and disable any dev-only bypasses[22]; (2) DK Metrics & Telemetry – ensure every invariant is measured in runtime (compiler, ledger, etc.) and exported via /metrics[23]; (3) Deployment Pipeline – get a one-click local stack (Docker Compose) and a reproducible cluster deploy (Helm/K8s) working with basic monitoring; (4) Stability Testing – run initial chaos and load tests to validate uptime and failover behavior against specs.
·         Task ID: P1-T1
 Name: Freeze DK Constants & Enforce “Real Mode” Defaults
 Plane: Cross-Cutting (Applies to all services)
 Package(s): services/common/constants.go, global env/config
 Type: PROD-REQUIRED
 Owner: PMO Lead (with SRE support)
 Timebox: 2 weeks (Month 0–1)
 Dependencies: GA codebase (V8) in place; CI pipeline accessible
 Steps:
·         Define all Design Key (DK) invariants as constants in a common module (if not already) – e.g. CompileP95Millis=250, LedgerAppendP95=120, etc.[24][25]. Ensure stretch targets are also captured (e.g. ST_COMPILE_P95_MS=230).
·         Audit config files and environment flags for any simulation toggles (e.g. A4Q_SIM); set real-mode as the default. Remove or hard-lock any “backdoor” dev settings (require A4Q_SIM=0 unless an explicit override for tests)[26].
·         Update startup logs to print out the loaded DK constants (banner listing all invariants) to clearly indicate the budgets in effect[27].
·         Implement a CI static check or linter step that scans for forbidden dev patterns (e.g. no calls to fake backends, no ALLOW_SIM_BACKSTOP unless intended)[28].
·         Commit changes and run full test suite; any test assumptions about relaxed values or sim modes must be updated to match the real-mode defaults.
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Code constants: The services/common/constants.go (or equivalent) contains all 10 DK invariants with correct values[29]. Unit tests confirm these are loaded and used by services.
·         Simulation off: Running the stack with default env yields A4Q_SIM=0 (and sim backdoor disabled). If someone attempts to start in sim mode, the system should log an error or refuse (fail-closed)[30].
·         CI gate: A static analysis job or startup check fails if any placeholder stub or disallowed dev flag is present (e.g. an explicit “SIMULATION” token triggers a CI fail).
●	An artifact (JSON or log) from the first successful run in real-mode is stored (e.g. _artifacts/run_header.json) showing version info and DK budgets (with a matching SHA-256 checksum in repo)[31].
 Notes/Risks: A potential risk is breaking developer workflows that relied on sim mode. Mitigate by providing alternative local testing configurations (perhaps a dev profile that still respects invariants but uses a local simulator QPU behind the scenes without altering core logic). This task is foundational – it sets a zero-trust stance in code: everything is “real” unless explicitly sandboxed with proper gates.
●	Task ID: P1-T2
 Name: Instrumentation of DK Metrics & Observability Baseline
 Plane: Cross-Cutting (All services)
 Package(s): services/common/otel.go, ops/observability, infra/compose
 Type: PROD-REQUIRED
 Owner: SRE/Observability Lead
 Timebox: 4 weeks (Month 1–2)
 Dependencies: P1-T1 (DK constants) complete; basic services running locally
 Steps:
·         Add metric collectors in each service for every DK invariant: e.g. in Trust Fabric, record a4q_ledger_append_ms (histogram) for ledger latency; in Orchestrator, a4q_recovery_s (histogram) for failover time; etc.[32]. Use consistent naming (a4q_*) and attach units/labels as needed.
·         Verify metrics exposure: Boot the stack via Docker Compose (trust-fabric + orchestrator + any dependencies like Postgres). Hit /metrics on each service and confirm all new metrics appear with the expected labels and zero values by default[33].
·         Implement a basic load generator (could be a script or using the CLI) to submit test jobs and drive metrics. For example, submit 100 dummy circuits via orchestrator to accumulate data for compile latency, success rate, etc.
·         Configure Prometheus (docker or local) to scrape the services’ /metrics. Import or create a Grafana dashboard JSON focusing on DK metrics (p95 lines, success %, carbon/job, etc.).
·         Set up alert rules for any metric breaching its threshold over a period (initially, just log/notify). For instance, an alert if a4q_wm_tpr < 0.99 over 5 min, or if a4q_job_gco2e exceeds 55 gCO₂ on average[34][35].
·         Document the metrics endpoint in the README/ops docs (what each metric means and its DK target).
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Hitting GET /metrics on Trust Fabric returns all governance-plane metrics (ledger latency histogram buckets, watermark TPR gauge, audit coverage percentage, etc.)[23][36]. Orchestrator’s /metrics shows uptime, recovery, carbon, success rate metrics.
·         A sample Grafana dashboard (export JSON as artifact) displays real-time values for all 10 DK invariants. SLO lines or annotations are in place (e.g. a red line at 120 ms on the ledger latency chart).
·         Include an artifact like _artifacts/v1_metrics_baseline_pass.json with a snapshot of metrics after a test run, plus a script log showing no missing metrics (could be as simple as counting expected metric names)[33].
●	The presence of alerts is verified by manually triggering one (e.g. artificially delay ledger writes to exceed 120 ms and see an alert fire). This can be captured in a testing log.
 Notes/Risks: Metric collection must be extremely lightweight (especially for compile and ledger paths) – ensure histogram buckets and Prom client overhead don’t themselves violate latency budgets. We may need to aggregate less granularly or sample if performance is affected. This task ensures visibility: we can’t manage or improve what we don’t measure, so by end of Phase 1 we have a quantitative pulse on the entire system[34][35].
●	Task ID: P1-T3
 Name: Deployable Stack – Docker Compose & Helm Charts v1
 Plane: Cross-Cutting (Infra/Ops)
 Package(s): infra/compose, infra/helm, CI/CD pipeline
 Type: PROD-REQUIRED
 Owner: DevOps Engineer
 Timebox: 4 weeks (Month 2–3)
 Dependencies: P1-T2 (observability) ideally done (so that charts include monitoring), Postgres instance available
 Steps:
·         Finalize a Docker Compose setup for local development: Define services for trust-fabric, orchestrator, Postgres DB, and an optional Prometheus + Grafana. Ensure the services in compose use the latest container images or local builds. Test that a developer can bring up the entire stack with docker-compose up and run a sample job through orchestrator to completion (using a simulator or dummy QPU backend).
·         Develop Helm charts (or Kustomize manifests) for deploying A4Q to a Kubernetes cluster. Chart should cover core components (trust-fabric Deployment + Service, orchestrator Deployment + Service, a Postgres StatefulSet, config Maps for env variables including DK constants, a secret for PQC keys or Vault address, etc.). Include subcharts or hooks for monitoring (Prometheus scrape annotations on pods, etc.).
·         Include toggles in Helm values for environment specifics (e.g. enable/disable the ML services as they might not run in prod yet). By default, focus on a minimal but production-representative install (two instances of each service to mimic HA, etc.).
·         Run the Helm chart on a test K8s cluster (e.g. Kind or Minikube locally, or a dev namespace in cloud). Iterate until helm install brings up all pods healthy. Use the CLI to submit a job on the cluster deployment as a smoke test.
·         Set up a basic CI pipeline job that builds docker images from the monorepo, pushes them (to a local registry or test registry), and then lints/validates the Helm chart (using helm template + kubeval, etc.). This ensures the deploy config doesn’t break as code changes.
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Compose up evidence: a short recorded output or log that docker-compose up launched trust-fabric, orchestrator, DB, and both /v1/healthz endpoints report OK. As an artifact, provide a script (scripts/dev_compose_smoketest.sh) that automates this health check (exit 0 if all good).
·         Helm deployment evidence: a screenshot or text capture of kubectl get pods showing all components running in a cluster. Additionally, an artifact like _artifacts/helm_release_test.log capturing a successful install and a test job submission (with expected outcome).
·         CI integration: The CI logs (or a badge) confirming the Docker image build and Helm lint pass on every commit. We can capture one such CI log’s key part as _artifacts/ci_deploypipeline_pass.txt.
●	The Helm chart should be versioned (e.g. a4q-0.1.0.tgz) and attached for internal use – indicating we have a repeatable deployable package.
 Notes/Risks: Running stateful components (Postgres) in CI or local may require tuning (e.g. using an ephemeral volume). Also, the orchestrator will need a placeholder backend (since in Phase 1 we might not yet integrate a real QPU cloud). We can use a dummy execution service that pretends to run the circuit – ensuring orchestrator flows complete. In later phases we’ll replace this with real backend integration.
●	Task ID: P1-T4
 Name: Chaos & Failover Drill (HA Test)
 Plane: Autonomy (Resilience)
 Package(s): services/orchestrator, ops/observability
 Type: PROD-REQUIRED
 Owner: SRE Lead
 Timebox: 2 weeks (Month 3–4)
 Dependencies: P1-T3 (deployable stack) running on a cluster; baseline metrics (P1-T2) in place
 Steps:
·         Define a chaos test plan targeting uptime and recovery invariants. For example: “If an orchestrator instance dies, a standby instance takes over within <1 sec (zone failover); if entire region A goes down, region B’s orchestrator handles jobs within <60 sec.”[6].
·         Spin up the platform in an HA mode: e.g. 2 orchestrator pods across 2 zones (or simulate zones via taints), and a primary/replica Postgres for trust-fabric (to mimic cross-region).
·         Use a load script to continuously submit jobs (could be dummy lightweight jobs) at a steady rate. Monitor the /v1/status of jobs and the metrics (uptime, success%).
·         Induce failures: e.g. kill the active orchestrator pod or cut network to the primary DB to simulate region failure. Observe system behavior: the standby orchestrator should pick up queued jobs, trust-fabric should switch to the replica DB (if configured).
·         Record timings: measure how long the outage lasts until service is restored. Also verify no data loss (jobs should eventually either complete or be marked failed and auditable).
·         Adjust configs if needed (e.g. tweak orchestrator heartbeat or retry intervals to meet the timing). Repeat until results consistently meet Uptime ≥99.5% and Recovery <60 s targets[35].
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Test report documenting at least two scenarios: (1) Orchestrator instance crash -> failover time observed (should be ~1 second to satisfy zone restart spec); (2) Simulated region outage -> failover time (target <60 s)[37][38]. For each, include timeline logs or metric graphs.
·         The metrics logs (Prometheus or custom) show that during chaos events, the a4q_uptime and a4q_recovery_s metrics remain within thresholds (or dip briefly but auto-recover). For instance, an artifact _artifacts/failover_test.json can summarize downtime duration vs. budget.
·         All jobs submitted either completed or were safely rolled back and recorded. Check that the ledger has either a completion entry or an aborted entry for each test job (no job “stuck” unrecorded).
●	Verify the trust-fabric ledger’s audit coverage metric remains ~100%, meaning even during failover, every job had an auditable outcome (no lost transactions)[35].
 Notes/Risks: This early chaos testing in a dev environment helps catch configuration issues. Real-world failover may be slower due to network latency; we should keep some buffer. The task also validates our active/active design under duress, which is a cornerstone of the GA promise. Document any weaknesses (e.g. if Postgres replica lag caused >120 ms append latency) so they can be addressed in future tasks.
●	Task ID: P1-T5
 Name: Initial Security Audit & Secrets Management
 Plane: Governance (Security)
 Package(s): services/trust-fabric, infra/secrets (Vault integration)
 Type: PRE-PROD
 Owner: Security Engineer (Governance)
 Timebox: 3 weeks (Month 3–4)
 Dependencies: Basic system up (Phase 1 deploy); real-mode enforcement (P1-T1) done
 Steps:
·         Conduct a code-level security review of the trust-fabric and orchestrator: look for any hard-coded secrets, disabled crypto (e.g. PQC using dev keys), or missing authentication on APIs. Cross-check against GA compliance requirements.
·         Implement integration with an external KMS/HSM for secret management: e.g. use HashiCorp Vault or AWS KMS for storing the PQC public keys or any signing keys used in watermarking. Replace any in-memory or file-based key storage with secure fetch from Vault at startup (with caching for performance)[22].
·         Ensure that session-scoped keys are used for PQC envelopes: e.g. orchestrator gets a fresh public key per session from the KMS, rather than reusing one long-term key (mitigating certain attack windows)[39].
·         Add configuration for key rotation policies: e.g. the trust-fabric should be able to seamlessly rotate its signing key or re-encrypt ledger entries with new PQC keys, within a maintenance window. (Document how to do this, even if not automated yet.)
·         Run a penetration test pass on the dev deployment: use tools or scripts to attempt common vulnerabilities (SQL injection on the ledger API, unauthorized calls to /v1/seal without auth, etc.) to validate the mTLS + auth layers.
·         Fix any issues found (e.g. tighten HTTP headers, ensure all routes require proper JWT or client cert, etc.).
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         The system no longer stores any plaintext secret or long-lived key in code or in environment variables. All sensitive keys are fetched from Vault/HSM or generated ephemeral per run. Provide a config snippet or log that shows trust-fabric successfully retrieving a key from Vault (or using KMS API) on startup.
·         Security checklist document created (store as _artifacts/security_baseline.md) listing each checked item (authentication, encryption, injection protection, etc.) and status (pass/fail). All high-priority items must be “pass” for Phase 1 completion.
·         If Vault is used, an audit log from Vault shows access events for keys (to prove keys are indeed used and rotated via Vault, not hard-coded).
·         Test results from pen-testing (could be as simple as saved logs from an OAuth test script or nmap/OWASP ZAP scan) showing no critical vulnerabilities.
 Notes/Risks: This task is about shifting from “dev ready” to “prod ready” in security posture. Some fixes might be quick (config tweaks), others might open new work (if, say, we lack a proper auth token exchange between services – though mTLS covers most). The dependency on KMS will resurface in Phase 2 when we do a full HSM integration, but here we achieve a basic level: keys not in code, and stubs from earlier versions (like the “stubbed key rotation in V6” noted in docs) are now replaced with real mechanisms[22].
Phase 2 (Months 4–8) – External Integration & Policy Layer
 Phase Goals (4–8mo): Begin integrating with real external systems and flesh out governance policies. Key objectives: (1) Real QPU Backend Integration – replace any dummy quantum backends with actual cloud QPU APIs (IBM Q, IonQ, etc.), proving the orchestrator can submit and retrieve results from at least one provider[37]; (2) Enterprise Key & Ledger Hardening – deploy the trust-fabric ledger in multi-region mode and trial a blockchain/consortium ledger in parallel (for audit immutability)[40][41]; (3) Advanced Policy Enforcement – implement fine-grained data residency, export control, and retention policies via the Trust Contract engine (e.g. region tags, timed deletion of sensitive logs)[42]; (4) Watermark & Carbon Analytics – build tools to extract and verify watermarks from results, and integrate live carbon-intensity data to start routing jobs for ESG goals[43][44]. By the end of Phase 2, we aim to have a fully “enterprise-ready” stack: secure, compliant, and connected to real hardware (even if at small scale).
·         Task ID: P2-T1
 Name: Integrate First Quantum Hardware Backend (IBM Quantum)
 Plane: Autonomy (Execution Backend)
 Package(s): services/orchestrator, platform/qir-adapters
 Type: PROD-REQUIRED
 Owner: Autonomy Lead (Orchestrator Engineer)
 Timebox: 6 weeks (Month 4–5)
 Dependencies: P1 tasks done (particularly P1-T3 deploy pipeline, P1-T4 basic stability); IBM Quantum API credentials available (or similar provider)
 Steps:
·         Abstract backend interface in orchestrator: define a Go interface for quantum backends (methods like SubmitJob(qir: Payload) -> JobID and PollStatus(JobID) -> Completed/Running/Error). Refactor orchestrator’s existing submission logic to call through this interface (currently it may just simulate or directly call a local simulator).
·         Implement a provider plugin for IBM Quantum: using IBM’s SDK or REST APIs. Likely this means packaging the IBM Q client (Python or REST calls) and invoking it from orchestrator (possibly via a Python sidecar or using their REST endpoints for job submission). Note: use credentials securely (from Vault or env).
·         Add support in the compiler QIR adapter to target the specific backend’s requirements (e.g. ensure QIR output is compatible with IBM’s runtime or translate to OpenQASM if needed). This may involve writing a translator or ensuring the Portable IR can be turned into IBM Q’s circuit format[7][8].
·         Test E2E: Submit a simple circuit (e.g. a Bell state) through the CLI with orchestrator configured to use IBM backend. The job should be accepted, appear in IBM’s queue, run on real hardware (or their simulator), and the result should flow back into our system.
·         Handle asynchrony: likely the IBM API is asynchronous (job ID returned, poll for completion). Ensure orchestrator’s /v1/status correctly reflects this (e.g. shows “running on IBM[Job#]”).
·         Implement basic error handling and timeouts for external jobs (if job hangs or fails, orchestrator marks it failed after X time, per policy).
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         The orchestrator’s backend abstraction is in place and documented (e.g. backend.go interface with at least one implementation). This is evident from code review (or attach a snippet in an artifact).
·         A demo output: run a4qctl submit --backend=ibm example.qir and show via logs or CLI that the job goes to IBM Q (e.g. “Job submitted to IBM Q, ID=XYZ”) and later our system receives a result. Provide the CLI output or orchestrator log as _artifacts/ibm_backend_test.log.
·         Monitor the job’s metrics: confirm that using the real backend doesn’t break invariants like compile p95 or orchestrator throughput (one job on real hardware may take long in wall-clock but that’s expected; ensure it doesn’t, say, crash our orchestrator thread).
·         If IBM’s queue is slow, simulate a scenario to ensure our a4q_job_success% still calculates properly (jobs might be pending long; perhaps exclude pending from success calc or count them carefully).
●	Go/Kill criteria: by end of Phase 2, decide if IBM integration is stable enough to keep on by default. The evidence of at least one successful run is a Go signal. Document any issues (latency too high, etc.).
 Notes/Risks: Working with an external cloud API invites flakiness and new failure modes. We should sandbox this integration (e.g. feature flag “--backend=ibm” vs. default local) until proven. Also, keep an eye on PQC: ensure we still wrap submissions in PQC encryption if required (data traveling to cloud). This is a big step towards making the orchestrator multi-QPU and thus proving out the “no stubs” commitment[43].
●	Task ID: P2-T2
 Name: Enterprise Key Management (Vault Integration Full)
 Plane: Governance (Security/Compliance)
 Package(s): services/trust-fabric, services/common/pqc
 Type: PROD-REQUIRED
 Owner: Governance Lead (Security Engineer)
 Timebox: 4 weeks (Month 4–5)
 Dependencies: P1-T5 (basic KMS usage) completed; Vault server accessible (dev or stage mode)
 Steps:
·         Refine the trust-fabric’s PQC envelope logic to use Vault for all crypto ops. For example, when sealing a job, instead of using a local libsodium call for Kyber KEM, call Vault’s transit API to perform the encryption (so the key never leaves Vault hardware)[39]. If Vault is unavailable, define fallback (possibly fail-closed, or a cached key if allowed by policy).
·         Similarly, manage the ledger signing key via Vault: any digital signature on ledger entries or watermarks should use keys stored in HSM (or at least have Vault manage the private key).
·         Implement a key rotation procedure: e.g. generate a new key version in Vault and have trust-fabric seamlessly start using it for new operations, while still accepting old signatures for validation. This might involve versioned key IDs in ledger entries.
·         Performance test the Vault calls to ensure the overhead stays within the DK budget (PQC overhead ≤20 ms p95)[45]. If direct calls are too slow, consider caching session keys (Vault can issue a batch key for a session to amortize cost). Ensure that even with caching, any breach of the 20 ms target triggers a safe mode (like degrade to using the cached key for more operations or log an incident)[39].
·         Update deployment: the Helm chart from P1-T3 should now include Vault address config, and possibly a sidecar agent if needed. Also, add a secret management runbook (how to add a new key, etc.).
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Latency test results: A report _artifacts/pqc_vault_latency.csv showing the distribution of Vault encryption call latencies. It should confirm p95 ≤ 20 ms (or note if slightly above, and what mitigation is in place)[39].
·         Trust-fabric logs in debug mode showing key operations going through Vault. For instance: “Vault KMS: Encrypted job payload with key=v1 (12 ms)”. Provide a sanitized log excerpt as evidence.
·         A simulated key rotation event: e.g. rotate the watermark signing key. After rotation, submit a job and verify that watermark verification still passes (i.e. trust-fabric recognizes both old and new keys during transition). Document this test (perhaps in the security checklist or a separate rotation test log).
●	All relevant config in source control (no manual secrets): e.g. Vault address & role are in values.yaml (non-secret), and the actual token/credentials are referenced via K8s secrets (not in code). A diff or snippet can show removal of any leftover plaintext keys in code.
 Notes/Risks: Vault integration in dev environment can be finicky (network calls, etc.). We might employ Vault’s dev server for testing but need to replicate production hardness (e.g. TLS to Vault, auth via Kubernetes JWT). This task closes the loop on “addressing stub from V6” where key management was faked[42][46]. Now, by Phase 2, key security is truly production-grade, with evidence to satisfy an audit.
●	Task ID: P2-T3
 Name: Distributed Ledger Deployment (A/B Regions and Blockchain Trial)
 Plane: Governance (Ledger)
 Package(s): services/trust-fabric/ledger, infra/chains
 Type: PRE-PROD (pilot mode for blockchain)
 Owner: DevOps or Backend Engineer (Governance)
 Timebox: 6 weeks (Month 5–6)
 Dependencies: Phase 1 HA test (P1-T4) done; Postgres dual-instance available; consider an open-source blockchain (Hyperledger or similar) for trial
 Steps:
·         Active/Active SQL Ledger: Configure the production Postgres ledger in multi-region: two instances (A and B). Set up logical replication or a consensus layer so that both can accept writes but stay consistent (or at least primary/replica that can failover without data loss). Test by writing entries in region A and reading in region B, and vice versa. Fine-tune settings to keep append latency low (target still 120 ms p95)[42][47].
·         Blockchain Shadow Ledger: Stand up a small blockchain network in parallel. For example, a Hyperledger Fabric or a Quorum network with 2–3 nodes (they could all run locally for test). Implement a plugin in trust-fabric to dual-write ledger entries: every time a job is sealed or completed, in addition to writing to Postgres, also submit a transaction to the blockchain ledger (encapsulating job ID, hash of circuit or watermark, etc.). This chain will serve as an immutable audit trail.
·         Keep the blockchain write async to avoid impacting p95 latency; perhaps write to a local queue and have a background routine commit to chain. Monitor how much overhead this adds.
·         Evaluate performance: Measure the blockchain commit latency and throughput. If it’s too slow (e.g. >250 ms per txn), it can only be pilot. Check if it affects our audit coverage metric.
·         By end of Phase 2, decide on viability: if the chain approach adds negligible overhead and provides real benefits (tamper-proof multi-org audit), plan to integrate fully next era; if it’s problematic, plan to stick with optimized SQL + periodic notarization instead[48].
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         The dual-region SQL ledger is demonstrated: Provide a short report _artifacts/ledger_HA_test.md describing a test where region A is taken offline and region B continues servicing ledger writes (and later syncs back). The report should indicate no data loss and measured failover times.
·         Trust-fabric metrics show ledger_append p95 still within 120 ms even with replication on (maybe slightly improved if reads are balanced)[47]. If overhead increased, note it clearly.
·         The blockchain pilot: a log or excerpt showing trust-fabric successfully writing a transaction to the blockchain for a job (with tx ID, etc.). If using Hyperledger, maybe an explorer UI screenshot showing the entries.
·         Comparison notes: Document the added latency from blockchain writes and any issues (e.g. chain consensus delays). Also note if any pilot partner is interested in this feature – by Phase 2 we might have one design partner who cares about on-chain verification.
●	A clear go/kill criteria for blockchain is recorded (e.g. “if chain overhead >5%, or no pilot demands it by end of year 2, we stick to SQL with periodic hash notarization”)[48]. This will guide Phase 3/era decisions.
 Notes/Risks: Operating a blockchain in parallel is complex; this is why it’s marked PRE-PROD. We are effectively evaluating a tech option. The risk is time – ensure this doesn’t derail core development. Limit scope: e.g. don’t build a full smart contract system now, just a basic append-only log to compare with SQL. Keep regulators/partners looped in: if none see value, we might deprioritize later[49].
●	Task ID: P2-T4
 Name: Trust Contracts – Policy Engine Extensions
 Plane: Governance (Policy/Compliance)
 Package(s): services/trust-fabric/policy (OPA modules), services/trust-fabric/routes.go
 Type: PROD-REQUIRED
 Owner: Governance Lead (Policy/Compliance Engineer)
 Timebox: 5 weeks (Month 6–7)
 Dependencies: Base OPA engine from GA exists; possibly legal input on policies
 Steps:
·         Expand the Trust Contract DSL to cover new policy clauses: e.g. data residency (user can specify country/region and the trust-fabric will enforce job routing accordingly), export control (flag certain algorithms as export-restricted, requiring special logging or blocking cross-border runs), privacy tagging (mark jobs that handle personal data, enforce shorter log retention)[50][51].
·         Implement these in OPA/Rego policies or Go code: e.g., add a check in the /v1/seal handler that if a job’s contract says “region: EU”, then the orchestrator chosen must have region=EU (or else reject). For retention, perhaps trust-fabric spawns a background job that deletes telemetry after X days for jobs with a “private” tag.
·         Write OPA unit tests for each policy (if using Rego, simulate inputs). Also test integration: try sealing a job with a contract that violates policy (e.g. region=EU but the only available backend is US) and confirm it’s rejected with clear error message.
·         Integrate audit logging: ensure every policy decision is logged (for audit and debugging). E.g. “Job 123: denied – violates data residency (EU required)”.
·         Update documentation for users on how to specify these new contract fields. Possibly also update CLI to support flags for them (e.g. --region EU).
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Example trust contract JSON with new fields (like {"region": "EU", "retainDays": 30}) and evidence that it’s enforced: show that if we violate it, job is not accepted. If we adhere, job goes through and logs confirm region constraint was applied.
·         OPA policy file (or code diff) included in an artifact showing the new rules. E.g. _artifacts/policy_module_v2.rego with rules for residency, export, etc.
·         Tests: a summary of test cases and results for each new rule. (Could be in a markdown or test output). For instance: “Test X: Submit US region job to EU-only backend – expected: denied; result: denied (PASS)”.
·         Confirm that these features do not break DK invariants: e.g. adding checks shouldn’t make /v1/seal API exceed, say, tens of milliseconds. Perhaps measure the overhead of policy evaluation (likely negligible, but document that p95 append still ≤120 ms with these checks).
●	Having this in place by Month 8 means we can demonstrate to enterprise pilots that we have multi-jurisdiction compliance controls – a big trust win.
 Notes/Risks: Policy design might need legal review (especially export control). We likely use simple placeholders (“if job tagged ITAR, only run on ITAR-approved backend”) until experts weigh in. Keep the engine flexible for updates. This task strengthens the governance plane’s value prop by moving beyond just watermarking to full workflow governance across jurisdictions[51][52].
●	Task ID: P2-T5
 Name: Watermark Extraction & Verification Toolkit
 Plane: Discovery→Governance (IP Provenance)
 Package(s): platform/watermark-pass, services/trust-fabric, tools/wm-inspector
 Type: PRE-PROD (tooling, not on critical path)
 Owner: Compiler/Platform Engineer
 Timebox: 4 weeks (Month 7–8)
 Dependencies: Basic watermark embedder in compiler exists; need sample circuits with watermark
 Steps:
·         Develop a watermark inspector tool (could be a CLI under /tools). Given a circuit (or execution results), this tool attempts to detect the embedded watermark signature. In GA, watermarks might have been simple (e.g. slight angle biases)[53]; create a more robust detection algorithm if needed (maybe using statistical tests to find the bias).
·         Integrate the tool with trust-fabric’s verification API. Possibly extend /v1/verifyWatermark endpoint: user can submit a final circuit or result and the service returns a confidence that our watermark is present.
·         Ensure TPR (True Positive Rate) remains ≥99%: test the system on a batch of 100+ watermarked circuits vs. 100 non-watermarked, verify detection hits >=99% on the watermarked and near 0% false positives[53]. Adjust the watermark encoding if needed (maybe increase signal slightly) to maintain TPR.
·         Also measure Δdepth overhead on circuits with watermark: ensure it’s still ≤0.5%. If any more complex watermark scheme is tried, keep overhead within budget[53].
·         Package results: perhaps the tool outputs a JSON with “watermark_found”: true/false and some metrics. This can be part of evidence artifacts for IP claims.
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         A report on watermark detection: e.g. _artifacts/watermark_tpr_test.md showing that in 200 test cases, the watermark was detected 99+% when present, and negligible false detects on clean circuits.
·         If improvements were made (like multi-layer watermarking as hinted in roadmap), describe them and confirm they meet invariants (e.g. new watermark bits didn’t push compile time or circuit depth beyond threshold)[35].
·         The trust-fabric’s API documentation updated to show a watermark verification call, and a successful example (with sample input and output).
●	For completeness, run a real end-to-end scenario: compile a circuit with watermark, execute (even on a simulator), then use the toolkit to extract watermark and confirm it matches the ledger record or contract. Provide logs of this scenario as evidence that all pieces connect.
 Notes/Risks: Given watermarking is a core IP protection, having an independent check tool is important for external audits or disputes. The risk is if our watermark is too trivial (some V6 note suggests early versions were simplistic)[54] – if so, a malicious party might remove it. While a more robust scheme may be an R&D effort beyond 24 mo, at least ensure our existing scheme’s detection is rock-solid. Document limitations candidly (maybe a Phase 4 task will be to upgrade the watermark method).
●	Task ID: P2-T6
 Name: Carbon-Aware Scheduling (Phase 1 – Data Feed & Tagging)
 Plane: Autonomy (ESG/FinOps)
 Package(s): services/orchestrator, services/common/carbon.go
 Type: PRE-PROD (feature-flagged)
 Owner: Autonomy Engineer (Scheduler)
 Timebox: 4 weeks (Month 7–8)
 Dependencies: Basic orchestrator running; access to a carbon intensity API (e.g. ElectricityMap or WattTime)
 Steps:
·         Implement a carbon intensity fetcher module: given a region or datacenter, fetch current grid CO₂ intensity (gCO₂/kWh). Use a public API (with caching to avoid rate limits).
·         Add logic in orchestrator’s scheduling: if the feature flag for carbon-aware scheduling is ON, then for each queued job, retrieve the carbon scores of candidate backends and prefer the lower-carbon one (if other factors equal). Or optionally, if intensity is above a threshold, delay the job until it drops (if within allowed slack time)[43][55].
·         Add a field in job status or metrics: e.g. each job when completed gets tagged with carbon_gco2 (estimated grams CO₂). This can be calculated from known factors (duration * hardware power, or via the data feed’s integrated calculation if available). Ensure this is recorded to the ledger or telemetry.
·         Unit test with dummy data: e.g. simulate two regions with different carbon levels and see that jobs route to the greener region when flag enabled.
·         Keep the feature off by default in production config (as it’s experimental). But have it on in a dev scenario to start collecting data on potential savings.
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Show a side-by-side run: one with carbon feature off, one on. In the “on” case, if one region’s carbon is higher, jobs preferentially go to the other region. Provide orchestrator logs or metrics illustrating this decision (e.g. “Job 456: delayed 5s for lower carbon” or “routed to eu-west (100 gCO₂/kWh) over us-east (300 gCO₂/kWh)”).
·         The metric a4q_job_gco2e is now populated for jobs[34][35]. Confirm that in a test scenario, the average gCO₂/job was, say, 10–15% lower with the feature on (this is the stretch goal in roadmap)[56][57]. Provide a small dataset or chart as evidence.
·         Check that carbon scheduling doesn’t break any SLO: no significant increase in wait times beyond acceptable, and orchestrator throughput unaffected in the long run. If some jobs were delayed for too long due to high carbon, ensure they eventually run (maybe enforce a max delay).
·         Decision documented: criteria for success (if by end of era we see ≥10% emission reduction without major downsides, continue; if negligible benefit or large delays, reconsider approach)[58].
 Notes/Risks: This is our first foray into ESG optimization – results may vary widely depending on actual backend distribution and grid data. We should be careful not to violate any SLAs; for now it’s opt-in. The data collected here (how often jobs get delayed and by how much) will inform whether this becomes a core feature or remains experimental. Also, consider user transparency – perhaps log or annotate jobs if they were delayed for carbon reasons, so it’s clear why.
Phase 3 (Months 8–12) – Pilot Deployment & v1.0 “GA” Readiness
 Phase Goals (8–12mo): Reach a level of maturity to support an external pilot (design partner) deployment by the end of the first year. Focus areas: (1) End-to-End Validation – run a full Operational Readiness Review (ORR) style test suite on the platform: all invariants, failure cases, recovery, etc., and produce evidence artifacts (json logs, dashboards) to prove readiness[59][60]; (2) Documentation & Support – prepare admin guides, user docs, and an internal playbook so that a partner’s ops team can trial the system; (3) POC with Real Workload – deploy A4Q in a sandbox at a partner (e.g. a national lab or enterprise R&D) and run a meaningful workflow (not just sample circuits – maybe a small variational algorithm or QEC cycle) to shake out integration issues; (4) Standards & Feedback – engage with external bodies by presenting our work on QIR watermarking and provenance, getting early feedback and buy-in[61][62]. By Phase 3’s end, we target an internal version v1.0.0 (or v1.2 as per roadmap) that we consider “production release candidate.” All critical tasks for a credible production system must be done by now (marked PROD-REQUIRED).
·         Task ID: P3-T1
 Name: Full-stack ORR (Operational Readiness Review) Simulation
 Plane: Cross-Cutting (Quality Gates)
 Package(s): All (integration testing) – scripts in ops/orr_tests
 Type: PROD-REQUIRED
 Owner: QA Lead / PMO
 Timebox: 4 weeks (Month 8–9)
 Dependencies: All Phase 1–2 prod-required tasks complete; stable environment available
 Steps:
·         Define an ORR checklist structured similarly to past execution plans (sections for performance, security, recovery, etc.)[63][64]. Each item should correspond to a DK invariant or major capability. Example items: “Compile p95 ≤250 ms under load – PASS/FAIL”, “Chaos test: orchestrator failover <60 s – PASS/FAIL”, “Simultaneous job load 100 jobs with ≥92% success – PASS/FAIL”, etc.
·         Create or update automated test harnesses for each item: e.g. a script to submit N jobs and measure compile latency distribution; a chaos script to kill pods and measure downtime; a data integrity script to verify all jobs in ledger have corresponding outputs. Leverage earlier tasks’ scripts and artifacts.
·         Execute the full battery of tests on a fresh deployment (could be a one-time large test on a cloud cluster to simulate a production env). This might run overnight or over a few days to gather enough data (especially for reliability metrics like uptime).
·         Collect all outputs in a structured format under _artifacts/ORR_v1/. This includes raw data (CSV/JSON), and a summary markdown or PDF that marks each check PASS or FAIL.
·         Where something fails or is borderline, create issue tickets and either address them immediately (if minor) or document as known limitations with a mitigation plan. For example, if ledger append p95 came out to 130 ms (above 120 ms target), note if it was an outlier or if code needs optimization – then schedule that fix.
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         A comprehensive ORR Report (e.g. A4Q_v1_ORR_Checklist.md) listing ~10–15 critical checks, each with status and evidence reference. All DK invariants must be “PASS” or have acceptable justification if slightly off with a plan to meet them shortly.
·         The report should tie to artifacts: e.g. “Compile Latency: PASS (p95 = 180 ms) – see metrics snapshot file X”[65], “Recovery: PASS (max 18 s) – see chaos test log Y”. Essentially, a reviewer should be able to verify each claim with the linked artifact.
·         We expect by this time that indeed all DK gates are satisfied (our GA baseline did, and we’ve aimed to not regress). If any regression happened due to new features (like Vault or IBM integration), they must be addressed or feature-flagged off for the pilot.
·         Evidence of stretch goals (Ops Gold) is nice-to-have: e.g. maybe our test shows compile p95 is actually ~50 ms (well under 250). Highlight those as “buffer achieved” which gives confidence.
●	The ORR artifacts are stored with checksums (fingerprint the results to prevent tampering, as per our SSOT ethos)[66].
 Notes/Risks: This is a critical milestone – essentially a simulated “Go/No-Go” for external release. Treat it seriously: involve team members in reviewing results, possibly do a dry run earlier (Month 8) to catch issues. If we cannot produce convincing evidence for a category, either improve the system or adjust the plan (with stakeholder sign-off if we claim something will be fixed later).
●	Task ID: P3-T2
 Name: External Pilot Deployment (Design Partner #1)
 Plane: Cross-Cutting (Deployment/Support)
 Package(s): infra/helm, documentation, support scripts
 Type: PROD-REQUIRED
 Owner: Program Manager (with DevOps)
 Timebox: 6 weeks (Month 9–10)
 Dependencies: P3-T1 (we have ORR pass), plus earlier integration tasks (QPU, KMS) stable
 Steps:
·         Identify the pilot partner and environment: assume a friendly organization (e.g. a research lab or a cloud division) that agreed to trial A4Q. Gather their infrastructure details (K8s cluster or VMs? network setup? any security review needed?).
·         Prepare a deployment bundle for them: likely our Helm chart packaged, with an accompanying guide. Remove any dev-only settings (ensuring A4Q_SIM=0, features set appropriately for their use case). Provide guidance on resource sizing (CPU/memory for services, etc.).
·         Work with their ops team to install the platform in their environment. This might involve on-site or remote sessions. Address issues that come up (e.g. networking, firewall for our components, connecting to their quantum hardware if any, etc.).
·         Once up, run a joint shakeout test: e.g. we run some known workloads on their deployment. Possibly rerun parts of ORR in their env to ensure it’s not breaking (though maybe scaled down if they have smaller hardware).
·         Establish monitoring and support channels: set up our Prom/Grafana to federate or they give us guest access, so we can watch DK metrics during the pilot. Also, set up a Slack/Teams or similar for quick support if something fails.
·         Leave them with a small user demo or training: e.g. show their team how to submit a job, how to interpret trust-fabric logs, how to retrieve a watermark report, etc. Essentially, treat this as enabling a future customer.
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Deployment confirmation: an email or checklist from the partner confirming “A4Q version 1.0 deployed on X environment, all pods running, basic health checks passed”. Could incorporate as a signed-off section in our report.
·         If allowed, gather some metrics from their deployment after a week of running. For instance, show a Grafana snapshot where all DK metrics are green in their env, or a snippet of their /metrics output proving invariants hold outside our lab.
·         At least one meaningful workload executed: e.g. the partner ran an algorithm of interest (maybe a small quantum optimization). Document it: “Pilot X ran a 5-qubit QAOA on IonQ via A4Q, and got result Y. A4Q orchestrated submission, ledger recorded it, watermark verified.” This real-world story (with data) is invaluable as evidence of value.
·         Collect feedback from the partner: any pain points? If they encountered bugs or missing features, list them (with plan to fix or improve in next phases).
●	This task is done when the partner can use the system for a basic use case without our constant intervention – i.e., we achieved a measure of usability and stability in a foreign environment.
 Notes/Risks: An external pilot always has uncertainties – their environment might uncover new issues (network latency, unknown hardware behaviors). We should be ready to patch quickly or apply hotfixes. Also, manage expectations: emphasize this is a pilot, not final product. The success criterion isn’t perfection, but that it basically works and demonstrates the promised capabilities (with our team able to support any hiccups). Success here is a huge de-risk for the project – it’s the first validation by an outsider.
●	Task ID: P3-T3
 Name: Standards Contribution & IP Alignment
 Plane: Governance (Standards)
 Package(s): platform/watermark-pass (spec), docs/standards
 Type: PRE-PROD (external collaboration)
 Owner: CTO or Chief Scientist
 Timebox: 3 weeks (Month 10–11, can overlap with pilot support)
 Dependencies: A stable description of our watermark and provenance approach; maybe membership in relevant working group
 Steps:
·         Finalize a technical whitepaper or draft specification describing A4Q’s quantum watermarking schema and the QIR provenance pass. Include how our watermark is embedded and detected, and how the provenance info is logged (so others could implement or verify it).
·         Engage with standards bodies (e.g. the QIR Alliance, IEEE, or ISO quantum groups). Submit the draft as a proposal or at least present it in a meeting[61][62]. Emphasize that our implementation is a reference for these ideas (which elevates A4Q’s credibility).
·         Likewise, any learning on trust fabric policy or carbon accounting – share at least in a community forum if possible (without giving away competitive advantage). The goal is to show we align with emerging best practices, easing adoption concerns.
·         Internally, cross-check our patent/IP strategy: ensure that by contributing to standards we’re not losing IP protection unjustly. Most likely, watermark methods might be patented or in process – coordinate with legal to do this properly (sometimes submitting to standards requires a letter of assurance re: licenses).
·         Capture any feedback or changes needed from the standards groups and incorporate it into our backlog (for future phases).
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         A submitted document (or slide deck) to a standards body is the primary artifact. For instance, A4Q_QIR_Watermark_StandardDraft.pdf and a confirmation email/minutes from the working group acknowledging receipt[61].
·         Evidence that our ideas are taken seriously: e.g. an invitation to co-author a standard, or positive feedback from a standards committee. Even a quote or email snippet can be recorded to show momentum.
·         Our monorepo might gain a standards/ directory containing these proposals (as allowed publicly) or at least our reference implementation tagged as compliant with standard draft X.
●	This doesn’t have a pass/fail like tests, but success is measured by engagement: We want A4Q to be seen as the reference implementation of quantum IP governance best practices. Achieving this by year 1 sets a strong foundation for the ecosystem narrative.
 Notes/Risks: Time of contributors is a risk – doing standards work while pushing product can strain resources. But the upside is huge in credibility and shaping the playing field. We should allocate a slice of key architects’ time here, and perhaps involve an external collaborator or advisor who sits on these committees to help champion our approach.
●	Task ID: P3-T4
 Name: Version 1.0.0 Release Packaging
 Plane: Cross-Cutting (Release Management)
 Package(s): infra/helm, docs/CHANGELOG.md, CI/CD
 Type: PROD-REQUIRED
 Owner: Release Manager / DevOps
 Timebox: 2 weeks (Month 12)
 Dependencies: All critical fixes from pilot feedback applied; ORR passed; documentation updated
 Steps:
·         Freeze a release branch for v1.0.0 (or v1.2.0 as per internal versioning). Bump versions in code, ensure all modules are on consistent version numbers.
·         Generate final artifacts: Docker images (tagged v1.0.0), Helm chart (versioned), CLI binaries (if any standalone), and a combined release notes document.
·         Write release notes highlighting major features and known limitations. Also include instructions for installation (so that someone outside our team could attempt it, though likely still with our support).
·         Verify the build is reproducible: do a clean clone of repo, follow our README, and confirm you can get the same images and pass all tests (to simulate an open-source or external user scenario, if applicable).
·         Tag the repo and archive the release (possibly upload to an artifact repository or container registry).
·         Optional but good: run one more round of critical tests on the exact release artifact (e.g. deploy the Helm chart one more time and run a sanity suite) to ensure nothing slipped in the final packaging.
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Git tags and checksums: The repository has a v1.0.0 tag, and an artifact like A4Q_v1.0.0_SHA256.txt listing checksums of all release images/files (for integrity).
·         The CHANGELOG.md or release notes file clearly delineates what’s delivered. It should map to our execution program: e.g. “Integrated KMS (Vault) for key management”, “IBM Quantum backend support (beta)”, “PQC invariants enforced (compile p95 ~50 ms achieved)” etc. Basically, it’s a marketing-plus-technical summary of 12 months of work.
·         The release package is provided to the pilot partner and possibly other early adopters/investors as needed. Evidence of that can be a distribution email or an internal announcement.
·         At this point, all tasks marked PROD-REQUIRED should be completed or explicitly deferred with rationale. So we can create a final v1.0 completion checklist verifying each such task status (should be all done).
 Notes/Risks: It’s mostly administrative, but important. The risk is rushing – don’t cut corners on the final integration testing. This sets the tone for future releases (we want a reputation that our tagged releases are solid). Also consider if we want to designate this release as “GA” or if we still call it a beta – since the roadmap mentioned v1.1 was GA. Given our context, this might effectively be v1.2 GA. Terminology aside, by Month 12 we have a product we’re confident to put in a partner’s hands as a supported system.
Phase 4 (Months 12–16) – Feature Expansion & Performance Tuning
 Phase Goals (12–16mo): With a stable baseline out, focus returns to innovation and improvement, guided by pilot feedback. Major aims: (1) Introduce Advanced Features in Controlled Mode – e.g. AutoCal-RL integration in live environment behind flags, and Anomaly Detection for job error rates (initially just logging alerts) as a precursor to full autonomy[67][68]; (2) Tighten Performance – revisit any invariants where we only marginally passed and optimize (e.g. can we reduce ledger latency further via indexing or concurrency? Achieve stretch targets consistently?); (3) Multi-Cloud and Multi-Tenant Prep – lay groundwork for supporting multiple cloud providers simultaneously (since by next era we want multi-cloud brokerage) and for organizations sharing the system (improving user isolation, quota management); (4) Environmental Hardening – expand testing to new scenarios (different cloud platforms, on-prem deployment, scale-out tests with more nodes) to ensure the design generalizes. This phase is about going from one successful pilot to many, by improving scalability and adding the first layer of autonomous controls that differentiate A4Q.
·         Task ID: P4-T1
 Name: Magic-State Simulator & Anomaly Detection (Autonomy R&D)
 Plane: Autonomy (FT Prep)
 Package(s): services/orchestrator (sub-module), ml/anomaly-detect
 Type: R&D SPIKE
 Owner: Autonomy Lead (with ML Eng)
 Timebox: 6 weeks (Month 12–14)
 Dependencies: Basic system metrics from pilot (error rates, etc.), maybe some data from hardware or simulator
 Steps:
·         Implement a Magic State Factory simulator within the orchestrator or as a separate service. Since real FTQC hardware might not exist yet, simulate the production/consumption of magic states (resources needed for quantum error correction)[69]. This simulation will generate events when resources are low or when throughput is limited, so we can test scheduling logic for those conditions.
·         Develop an Anomaly Detection module (could leverage something like an online statistical test or a simple ML model) that monitors job error rates, queue wait times, etc. When it sees a spike or trend deviating from norm (e.g. job failure rate > threshold or calibration drift indicated by increasing error), it flags it[70][71]. Initially, just log alerts, do not auto-intervene.
·         Integrate these into orchestrator: e.g. orchestrator subscribes to anomaly events and could mark a backend as “needs attention” (but won’t automatically reroute yet, unless trivial). Also incorporate anomaly info in telemetry (so ops can see it on dashboards).
·         Test by intentionally causing an anomaly: e.g. feed the orchestrator some “bad data” (simulate a QPU with high error rate) and see if our detector catches it. Adjust sensitivity to balance false positives/negatives.
·         This is a precursor to full self-healing: we want the groundwork in place such that in the next phase or era, we can enable automatic responses (pause a backend, trigger recalibration, etc.). For now, ensure detection is reliable.
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         A running magic-state simulation in a dev environment that produces realistic load. Provide a snippet of logs or metrics showing, for example, “Simulated FT Resource: 80% utilized” and orchestrator reacting by maybe queueing fewer jobs or just reporting it.
·         The anomaly detection logs showing detection of an injected issue. For instance, we purposely drop qubit calibration in the simulator, and logs show “Anomaly detected: error_rate spike on Backend-1 (5σ deviation)”.
·         The anomaly alerts should also reflect in metrics (maybe expose a4q_anomaly_alerts_total or similar counter) and we ensure no performance impact in normal conditions.
●	Document a plan in which anomalies we’d auto-handle in future. Perhaps as a simple table: “If detection of X, then future action Y (not implemented yet)”. This sets up tasks in Era 2–4.
 Notes/Risks: Being R&D, it’s okay if this doesn’t directly deliver a prod feature by end of 16 mo. But we want evidence that the system can evolve into the “autopilot” role. We must be careful to keep this isolated (feature-flagged) so it doesn’t interfere with the stable core. On the flip side, this keeps the team sharp on the advanced goals, preventing stagnation after hitting the initial release.
●	Task ID: P4-T2
 Name: AutoCal-RL Deployment in Shadow Mode
 Plane: Discovery→Autonomy (Calibration)
 Package(s): ml/autocal-rl, services/orchestrator (hooks)
 Type: PRE-PROD (pilot feature)
 Owner: ML Engineer (Calibration)
 Timebox: 4 weeks (Month 13–14)
 Dependencies: Basic AutoCal-RL working in lab; anomaly detection (P4-T1) might feed into this
 Steps:
·         Containerize or package the AutoCal-RL service so it can run alongside the orchestrator in a cluster. This service should be able to connect to either a real QPU’s calibration interface or our simulator’s knobs.
·         Integrate orchestrator with AutoCal suggestions: e.g. when anomaly detection or a periodic timer triggers, orchestrator queries AutoCal-RL for recommended calibration actions (like “tune qubit 5 frequency by X”). However, do not auto-apply in production yet; just log what would have been done or maybe apply to simulator.
·         Run the AutoCal-RL in the background during pilot (if the partner has a test device or we use a simulator). Gather data on how often it suggests changes and whether those changes would improve metrics.
·         If the partner is willing, perhaps do an out-of-band test: apply one calibration suggestion manually to see if it indeed improved error rates. Use that as anecdotal evidence.
·         Ensure the presence of AutoCal-RL does not degrade performance. It might need a lot of compute (RL training episodes), so maybe allocate a separate node or GPU for it and ensure it doesn’t starve core services.
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         AutoCal service running: show that we can deploy the autocal-rl container and it stays healthy. For instance, an artifact log where it receives a periodic state from orchestrator (like current error rates) and outputs a suggestion (which we capture and log).
·         Show an example calibration suggestion: e.g. “Qubit 7 T1 is drifting, suggest increasing bias current by 0.05” (whatever form it gives). This demonstrates the ML is doing something concrete.
·         If possible, evidence of improvement: maybe on simulator, we see before calibration vs after calibration error metrics. Or a partner’s qubit improved (that would be golden). If so, record that experiment result.
●	Confirm that when running, the invariants are unaffected (the RL loop might be heavy but should not cause compile latencies to spike etc.). If necessary, throttle or only run during idle periods. Document this consideration.
 Notes/Risks: This brings some of the Discovery-plane R&D into a real environment. The risk is it might not yield any noticeable benefit yet (NISQ devices calibrate well enough manually). That’s okay; even a null result is learning. The key is we integrate the pipeline now, so when devices scale or drifting becomes bigger issue, we’re ready. This also impresses sophisticated partners that we have an eye on automated tuning, which is part of the “Autonomy” promise.
●	Task ID: P4-T3
 Name: Performance Tuning Sprint (Ledger & Compile)
 Plane: Cross-Cutting (Optimization)
 Package(s): services/trust-fabric (ledger), platform/compiler
 Type: PROD-REQUIRED (to ensure headroom)
 Owner: Performance Engineer
 Timebox: 4 weeks (Month 14–15)
 Dependencies: Pilot usage metrics (to identify bottlenecks), ORR results (which show which metrics were closest to limits)
 Steps:
·         Analyze logs/metrics from pilot and ORR: identify any hotspots. For example, if ledger append p95 was 110 ms (just under 120), that’s close – try to lower it. Or if compile p95 was 200+ ms under certain loads, focus there.
·         Ledger optimizations: e.g. add an index to the ledger table to speed up writes, batch writes in trust-fabric (commit every 5 entries in a transaction), or tune Postgres parameters for low-latency mode. Also consider reducing chatty operations – maybe the trust-fabric was flushing fsync on every append; if so, loosen to group commits while still meeting durability. Verify audit coverage remains 99%+.
·         Compiler optimizations: e.g. profile the Rust compiler service to see if there are slow passes. Perhaps caching results for common circuits, or reusing compiled components (if a circuit’s subroutine repeats, avoid re-compiling). Also ensure parallelism: if compile is single-threaded, allow multiple threads for different jobs.
·         Implement chosen optimizations and run microbenchmarks on them. Use representative circuits (small and larger) to measure improvement.
·         Also revisit PQC overhead: now that Vault is integrated, see if any overhead can be shaved. Possibly by maintaining persistent connections to Vault or using faster ciphers if acceptable.
·         At the end, update the metrics expectations: maybe we can now commit to stretch targets as the new normal.
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         A comparison before vs after table of key metrics. For example: Ledger append p95: was 105 ms, now 80 ms (25% improvement); Compile p95: was 180 ms, now 120 ms under same load. Present this in _artifacts/perf_tuning_comparison.csv or similar.
·         If improvements allow, adjust the constant values for stretch goals in code (or at least note that we consistently beat the original DK, which is great).
·         No negative impact on correctness or stability – run regression tests focusing on ledger consistency and compiled circuit equivalence (no bug introduced in watermark, etc.).
●	After tuning, run a subset of ORR tests again to confirm all invariants still pass (they should, ideally with more margin). Provide logs of those test runs to validate.
 Notes/Risks: This is classic optimization: must ensure not to break things in pursuit of speed. But given we have thorough tests, we’ll catch issues. Gains are important not just for bragging rights but to ensure as load increases or features add overhead, we still stay within budgets. Also, any improvements here directly increase our “moat” by having better performance than competitors, and reduce cloud costs (e.g. if we cut CPU time per job).
●	Task ID: P4-T4
 Name: Multi-Cloud Broker Design & PoC
 Plane: Autonomy (Multi-cloud)
 Package(s): New service services/broker (or extend orchestrator)
 Type: PRE-PROD (architecture PoC)
 Owner: Cloud Integration Architect
 Timebox: 5 weeks (Month 15–16)
 Dependencies: P2-T1 (one cloud integrated already), readiness to integrate another (e.g. AWS Braket or IonQ)
 Steps:
·         As we plan for Era 2–4 with multiple cloud providers, create an initial design for a Broker service that sits above orchestrators to route jobs across clouds. Alternatively, decide to extend the orchestrator to handle multiple clouds internally. Document pros/cons.
·         Implement a minimal PoC: e.g. integrate a second provider (if IBM is done, try AWS Braket or IonQ Cloud). Instead of fully coding again in orchestrator, try a decoupled approach: spin up a separate orchestrator instance or plugin for the second provider and have a Broker component decide which orchestrator (IBM vs AWS) gets each incoming job.
·         Criteria for broker decision can be simple initially (round-robin or based on a job tag from the user). The key is proving that we can dynamically choose between heterogeneous backends.
·         Ensure the trust-fabric ledger and policies are agnostic to this – i.e., jobs from any backend still uniformly recorded and governed. Identify if any changes needed (like including a “cloud” field in job records).
·         This is a technical spike, so after building it, evaluate if the approach is viable for production in future. It might be fine to keep one orchestrator that handles all clouds via plugins (if complexity is low). Or a broker might be better if we envision many orchestrators for different zones/vendors.
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         A short design spec or diagram stored in repo (like docs/multicloud_design_v1.md) explaining how multi-cloud routing will work. This should align with the vision that by Year 3 we support dynamic brokerage[72][73].
·         A working demo of a job going to a second provider: e.g. “Submitted job X targeting IonQ via broker, completed successfully.” Provide logs showing the broker receiving the request and delegating to appropriate orchestrator or API.
·         Record any performance penalty or complexity: e.g. the broker adds 10 ms overhead – noted to be acceptable. Or “we had to disable feature Y for provider Z because not supported” – document these gaps for future tasks.
●	This PoC might not be used in production now, so it’s okay if it’s messy. But acceptance is we have high confidence on approach for real implementation in Era 2–4. Possibly file follow-up tasks or user stories for that future work, ensuring continuity.
 Notes/Risks: Multi-cloud is strategically important for not being locked to one vendor and for optimizing cost/queue times. Risk is each provider is different (different APIs, queue behaviors). Overcoming that with a clean abstraction is challenging. This task is the first step to ensure we won’t be caught off-guard when a customer asks, “Can you also run on my preferred quantum cloud?” By having a plan and a PoC, we can say “yes, it’s in roadmap and here’s how.”
●	Task ID: P4-T5
 Name: UX and API Polish (CLI/SDK & Docs)
 Plane: Discovery & Governance (User Experience)
 Package(s): sdk/ts, docs/
 Type: PRE-PROD (usability)
 Owner: Developer Experience Engineer / Technical Writer
 Timebox: 4 weeks (Month 15–16)
 Dependencies: Core features in place so we can document them properly; pilot feedback on usability
 Steps:
·         Incorporate feedback from the pilot users on the CLI and API. Maybe they requested clearer help messages, or shorter commands, or a client library in Python. Address low-hanging fruit: improve CLI help text, add examples for common tasks (like sealing a job with a contract).
·         Ensure the SDK/CLI covers new features: e.g. if we added region tags or multi-backend selection, the CLI should have flags for those (with sensible defaults). Update the CLI reference in docs accordingly.
·         Write a Quickstart guide in the docs: “Deploy A4Q and run your first quantum job in 30 minutes” style. Use Phase 3 experience to make sure it’s accurate.
·         Clean up API responses if needed: e.g. ensure /v1/status returns a clear state machine (maybe unify the format, add any missing fields like carbon used, etc.).
