·         Possibly prepare a simple demo UI (not required, but if time: even a basic web dashboard to show job list and status). This can be helpful for less technical stakeholders. If not now, at least design it for future.
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Updated user documentation in the repo (or website) with clear instructions and examples. Check that someone outside the dev team can follow it to perform key operations. We could measure this by asking a new hire or colleague to attempt the quickstart.
·         The CLI/SDK version bump (v1.0.0 to match backend) published if applicable (like npm package for JS SDK). This ensures external parity.
·         List of improvements done from feedback: e.g. “Added --output json to CLI for scripting, as requested; improved error message when contract violation occurs,” etc.
·         This is more subjective, but ideally pilot users or internal testers now report a smoother experience. Perhaps include a short testimonial or at least no open high-priority UI complaints in our issue tracker.
 Notes/Risks: It’s easy to focus only on back-end and let UX lag. This task is to prevent that. Good usability can be an early differentiator and makes pilots happy. The risk is always time; but given heavy tech work earlier, a breather to polish UX also helps the team prepare to support more users in the next phases.
Phase 5 (Months 16–20) – Scaling Out & Multiple Pilots
 Phase Goals (16–20mo): Leverage our now-polished v1 to onboard more environments and push the envelope in capacity. Key goals: (1) Second & Third Pilot Deployments – scale the playbook to additional partners (e.g. one more cloud vendor partnership and one enterprise research group), validating that the platform generalizes; (2) Multi-Tenancy Basics – implement the first cut of organization-level isolation and admin controls so multiple teams or companies could use one deployment (even if just in logically separated projects); (3) Tighten SLOs – by now, push uptime, availability, and support processes towards enterprise-grade (99.9%+ SLA targets, 24/7 monitoring in place, etc.); (4) Prepare for Fault-Tolerance Era – if any early FTQC prototypes or simulators are available, integrate them in a test capacity, and ensure our design (esp. orchestrator and anomaly modules) aligns with their needs. Essentially, Phase 5 is about demonstrating that A4Q isn’t a one-off demo but a scalable service that can handle more users, more hardware, and stricter reliability demands as we head towards year 2.
·         Task ID: P5-T1
 Name: Multi-Tenant Access Control & Org Isolation
 Plane: Governance (Trust Fabric)
 Package(s): services/trust-fabric/auth, services/trust-fabric/ledger
 Type: PROD-REQUIRED (for broader adoption)
 Owner: Governance Engineer (Access Control)
 Timebox: 5 weeks (Month 16–17)
 Dependencies: Single-tenant pilot stable; possibly need an identity provider or we implement basic auth
 Steps:
·         Implement a notion of “Organization” or “Tenant” in the trust-fabric. This could be as simple as adding a tenant_id field to ledger entries and policies, and scoping API keys or client certs to a tenant.
·         Integrate with an authentication/identity system: possibly issue separate client certificates for each tenant, or use OIDC tokens that include tenant info. At minimum, create a mapping from credentials to tenant IDs in our config.
·         Ensure that all relevant API calls enforce tenant isolation: e.g. one org cannot query another org’s job status or ledger entries. Add checks in endpoints like /v1/status, /v1/telemetry/ingest to filter by tenant (based on auth context).
·         Add a tenant admin role concept: e.g. an org admin can see all their users’ jobs, but not others; our ops can have a super-admin that can see everything (for support). Use OPA policies for this or simple role logic.
·         Modify ledger queries to allow cross-tenant view only if super-admin or via explicit trust agreements (maybe not now, but design for an “exchange” scenario in era 2–4).
·         Test by simulating two tenants in one deployment: try to breach isolation (should fail). Also ensure metrics and audit logs indicate tenant context (so we can show, for instance, each tenant’s usage).
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         The system can be deployed in a multi-tenant mode and has been verified to isolate data. Provide a test log where User A (tenant Alpha) tries to access Job X of tenant Beta and receives an authorization error (while permitted actions still work).
·         Updated API docs to describe how clients authenticate and how to specify tenant context if needed. Possibly provide a script to generate separate client certs for different orgs for our CLI.
·         A database check: ensure that ledger entries now carry tenant info and queries use it (like SELECT * FROM ledger WHERE tenant_id=?). Confirm that an org cannot even indirectly infer others’ data (like IDs or counts).
·         Performance impact should be negligible (just an extra filter in queries), confirmed by basic benchmarks (no regression in append p95 due to multi-tenant logic).
●	Achieving this is a prerequisite to onboard more pilots on a shared platform vs. separate deployments; it also sets stage for a future Quantum IP exchange network where multiple parties share a ledger but still enforce boundaries.
 Notes/Risks: Multi-tenancy touches security critically, so be thorough. The risk is missing an edge case (we’ll do another security review focusing on multi-tenant in Phase 6 perhaps). Also, consider rate-limiting per tenant to prevent one from starving the system – maybe add basic quotas. Not mandatory now, but note it.
●	Task ID: P5-T2
 Name: Pilot Deployment #2 (Cloud Provider Partner)
 Plane: Autonomy/Governance (Integration)
 Package(s): Deployment configs (tailored), possibly services/orchestrator (if new backend needed)
 Type: PRE-PROD (expanding usage)
 Owner: Business Dev Engineer / Cloud Partner Lead
 Timebox: 6 weeks (Month 17–18)
 Dependencies: P4-T4 (multi-cloud PoC) done; P5-T1 (multi-tenant) done so we can isolate if needed
 Steps:
·         Coordinate with a cloud vendor (e.g. if IBM was first, now try AWS Braket or Azure Quantum as an official partner pilot). Their interest: showcasing how A4Q can drive usage of their hardware with added trust/compliance.
·         Deploy A4Q on their infrastructure or in a configuration that connects to their hardware seamlessly. Possibly, they might host A4Q components or we do it in our cloud but with access to their devices. Work out networking and auth details (likely whitelisting our orchestrator to call their endpoints).
·         Focus on integration specifics: ensure our orchestrator can talk to their queue API (this might involve finishing an adapter for their platform, similar to IBM in P2-T1). Leverage multi-cloud architecture: maybe run two orchestrator instances – one tuned for each provider – and have the trust-fabric and rest unified.
·         Once up, run a demonstration workflow pertinent to that provider’s strength (e.g. if IonQ’s system is good at certain algorithm, run that).
·         This pilot also tests our multi-tenant: perhaps we keep this deployment logically separate, or if we manage multiple pilots in one environment, we use the multi-tenant feature to isolate this partner’s runs from the first pilot’s runs. Decide based on practicality and partner comfort.
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Confirmation from the cloud partner that A4Q was successfully connected to their quantum service. E.g. an email or report: “A4Q orchestrator successfully dispatched jobs to [Vendor] QPU and retrieved results.”
·         Show a job executed on new hardware via A4Q: evidence similar to pilot 1, but with Provider B’s context (job IDs, logs from their system, etc.).
·         Evaluate any differences: Did we maintain our invariants? Possibly hardware wait times are longer, but that’s outside our control – ensure we handle it gracefully (jobs in running state longer but not timing out incorrectly).
·         Document any partner requests for features – e.g. maybe they care about cost optimization more (like choose cheapest among their devices). Capture those as potential future items.
●	The result is two reference deployments in two distinct contexts, proving versatility. This also likely yields a second friendly reference for investor discussions or case studies.
 Notes/Risks: Aligning with a cloud provider means ensuring we’re not stepping on their toes. Emphasize complementarity: we bring governance and multi-cloud, not competing with their platform. If they have their own orchestrator, position A4Q as an overlay or neutral party that can aggregate across providers. Politically, success here might mean deeper partnership or even M&A interest – handle carefully.
●	Task ID: P5-T3
 Name: Pilot Deployment #3 (Enterprise R&D Use Case)
 Plane: Discovery/Governance (Use-case validation)
 Package(s): Possibly integration with their dataset or dev environment
 Type: PRE-PROD
 Owner: Solutions Engineer / PM
 Timebox: 6 weeks (Month 18–19)
 Dependencies: v1.0 stable; perhaps QAGF prototype available to leverage
 Steps:
·         Identify an enterprise or lab with a specific problem (e.g. pharmaceutical optimizing a molecule, or bank optimizing a portfolio) who is willing to try A4Q with their use-case. This pilot uses our system not just to run generic circuits but to attempt an AI-generated or optimized solution (tying in a bit of our Discovery plane).
·         Work closely to encode their problem: if Discovery (QAGF) prototype is available, use it to generate some candidate circuits or strategies for them. If not, at least use our platform to manage and audit a known quantum algorithm applied to their data.
·         Deploy as needed (maybe on their cloud, or on ours with secure access to their data). Ensure trust-fabric features like data residency or audit are configured to meet their compliance (enterprises will care about that).
·         Over a few weeks, have them run experiments – A/B testing with and without our generative suggestions if applicable.
·         Gather results and feedback: Did A4Q simplify their workflow? Did our provenance ledger help in tracking IP or reproducing results? Any improvement due to generative approach (if used)?
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         A case study draft (internal) describing what was done: e.g. “Using A4Q Discovery, ACME Bank tried quantum-inspired portfolio optimization. The system generated 50 candidate circuits, 1 of which outperformed their baseline by 5%.” Or if no improvement, state that clearly but note the learning.
·         Feedback from their domain experts about our platform’s strengths/weaknesses. E.g., maybe they loved the audit logging but want a better UI for results – note that.
·         Technical evidence: logs or ledger excerpts showing the runs. Possibly an artifact from trust-fabric indicating multiple parties (their quants and our system) collaborated (like multiple identities signing off on runs, etc.).
●	With three pilots now, we should start seeing patterns of requests or areas to focus next (maybe everyone asks for feature X). Summarize these emerging themes as part of acceptance, feeding the roadmap.
 Notes/Risks: This pilot might be the most challenging since it’s not just tech but solving a real problem. We must be careful not to over-promise on results – the goal is to validate the platform’s utility, not necessarily to beat the state-of-art in algorithms (though that’d be nice). Keep them happy by delivering on integration and ease, even if the quantum advantage is still nascent. A success story here is gold for investor and market narratives.
●	Task ID: P5-T4
 Name: High-Availability & SRE Processes
 Plane: Autonomy/Governance (Operations)
 Package(s): ops/ (runbooks, on-call procedures), monitoring infra
 Type: PROD-REQUIRED (for scaling prod)
 Owner: SRE Lead
 Timebox: 4 weeks (Month 19–20)
 Dependencies: At least one pilot running 24/7 to observe; Phase 4 perf improvements in place
 Steps:
·         Formalize SLOs/SLAs for the service based on pilot data: e.g. commit to 99.9% uptime excluding scheduled maintenance, etc. Set up monitoring to track these (we already have alerts for DK breaches; expand to monthly SLO reports).
·         Create runbooks for common ops tasks: node replacement, scaling up the cluster, performing a backup/restore of ledger, key rotation procedure, etc. These should be detailed enough for an on-call engineer to follow under stress.
·         Establish an on-call rotation within the team (even if small) and define incident response protocols. E.g. if alert X fires, who investigates, what steps to take, how to escalate.
·         Perform a planned disaster recovery drill: simulate a total outage scenario (loss of a region, or a corrupted database backup). Walk through using runbooks to recover (e.g. restore ledger from backup or failover to DR site). Measure how long it takes and ensure it’s under acceptable RTO (recovery time objective). If not, refine process.
·         Enable automated canary tests in staging if possible: for every new build or config change, run a minimal ORR subset and require 15 minutes of error-free operation before promoting to pilots (this echoes our Ops Gold stretch goal)[64][74]. If any regression, auto-rollback (our Helm charts can support quick rollback).
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Written SRE runbook docs exist (could store in ops/runbooks/ directory). List at least e.g. “Restart procedure for orchestrator (zero downtime)”, “Failover trust-fabric to DR DB”, etc. Each should have been test-run and updated with actual outcomes.
·         DR drill results: a report _artifacts/dr_drill_report.txt showing what was tested and timing. For example, “Simulated primary DB loss at 12:00, system restored from replica by 12:05 (5 min downtime)” – which meets 60 s? Actually 5 min is 300 s, above our original DK, so aim to optimize or at least note gap. Maybe that scenario isn’t fully covered by DK (which was 60 s failover for region, not for full DB restore). Regardless, show we have a plan for worst-case.
·         On-call schedule and alert rules are in place – show a screenshot of our PagerDuty/Slack alert config if applicable.
·         This is somewhat process-oriented, so evidence is mostly documentation and logs from drills. The outcome is that by 20 mo, we behave like a mature service provider: proactive monitoring, clear protocols – which is crucial as we prepare to possibly manage more customers or hand off to others to operate.
 Notes/Risks: The risk is always the unknown unknowns in ops. We mitigate by practicing failure now in non-prod. Another risk: team burnout as on-call starts – ensure it’s reasonable, and maybe by now consider hiring a dedicated ops engineer if usage grows. This task greatly increases confidence for any paying customers that we can meet SLAs and handle issues professionally.
Phase 6 (Months 20–24) – Consolidation & Launch Prep
 Phase Goals (20–24mo): Finalize the transition from a pilot-phase project to a product ready for broader release or commercial launch. Emphasis: (1) Incorporate Final Feedback – any open issues or feature requests from pilots are resolved or scheduled; polish any remaining rough edges; (2) External Audit/Certification – if aiming for specific industry compliance (e.g. an external security audit, ISO certification, or academic review for the science), initiate those and pass at least one formal review[75][76]; (3) Marketing Collateral & Launch Narrative – work with business team to craft case studies, testimonials, and performance stats to support a public launch or next funding round; (4) Roadmap Update – use all we’ve learned to revise the long-term plan and ensure the next 2-year era’s goals are properly scoped. By the end of Phase 6 (~24 months), we aim to have version v2.0 defined and perhaps in development, while officially rolling out v1.x to real customers beyond pilots. Essentially, Phase 6 bridges execution to date with the strategic shift to scaling mode.
·         Task ID: P6-T1
 Name: External Security & Compliance Audit
 Plane: Governance (Security)
 Package(s): All (audit scope) – focus on services/trust-fabric, infra, policies
 Type: PROD-REQUIRED (for enterprise launch)
 Owner: Security Lead (with external auditors)
 Timebox: 6 weeks (Month 20–21)
 Dependencies: Multi-tenant & all security features in place; runbooks ready
 Steps:
·         Engage a third-party security firm to conduct a penetration test and architecture review. Provide them scope (likely the entire deployed system in a staging environment with dummy data).
·         Internally, prepare by running our own audit scripts one more time and fixing any minor things. Ensure all dependencies (e.g. container images, libraries) are up to date with no known CVEs.
·         The auditors perform testing over a few weeks. We coordinate to answer questions and provide any needed access (source code snippets, design docs).
·         Receive their report. Triaging: fix any critical or high findings immediately. Mediums as feasible or document why not a risk. Possibly re-test to confirm fixes.
·         In parallel or after, if targeting compliance like ISO 27001 or SOC 2, start gathering required documentation (some of which we have, like runbooks, incident policy from P5-T4). Maybe not get certified in 24 mo (that can take longer), but at least be audit-ready and perhaps complete a readiness assessment.
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         A summary of the security audit results. Ideally: “No critical issues found; X minor suggestions fixed.” If there were serious issues, evidence we fixed them (like patch diffs). Possibly a letter or attestation from the auditor that we passed or improved to their satisfaction.
·         Updated security documentation reflecting any changes or new best practices. E.g. if they recommended better encryption here or there, we did it.
·         If relevant, a compliance readiness report. For example, a gap analysis showing we meet Y out of Z controls for SOC 2, with a plan to address the rest.
●	Achieving this gives us a green light to approach more conservative customers (who will ask “have you been independently vetted?”). It’s also a de-risk for investors concerned about security.
 Notes/Risks: Opening up to external auditors can be humbling – but better now than after a breach. There’s a schedule risk if major issues appear; that could delay broader launch. But given our diligence (fail-closed, etc.), hopefully only small things emerge (or none). This milestone is often needed before a big enterprise contract or before an investor term sheet for scaling, so it’s well worth it.
●	Task ID: P6-T2
 Name: Finalize Era 2–4 Roadmap & Team Handoff
 Plane: Cross-Cutting (Planning)
 Package(s): docs/roadmap_v2.md, backlog tickets
 Type: N/A (Management)
 Owner: CTO/Architect (you) with PMO
 Timebox: 4 weeks (Month 21–22)
 Dependencies: Experience from pilots and performance data; updated partner/investor input
 Steps:
·         Revisit the original 10-year blueprint (V10) in light of actual progress. Identify where we are on track (likely all Era 0–2 done), and where assumptions changed. For example, maybe quantum hardware is slower to progress, so Era 2–4 goals on fault-tolerance might shift out; or maybe new opportunities (like a government project) arose which we slot in.
·         Draft a concrete execution plan for Era 2–4 (Months 24–48). This will be a high-level plan similar to our Phase breakdown but at epic level, aligning with the tasks we list in Section 2 below. Prioritize things like: multi-tenant production rollout, extended autonomy features (magic state manager, etc.), turning QAGF from beta to product, expanding to more customers.
·         Align this with budget and team growth plans: e.g. note if we need to hire additional ML researchers for QAGF or more SREs for scaling. The roadmap should include role growth (the V10 doc likely outlines how team size grows by era)[77]. Update if needed (maybe we grew faster or slower).
·         Prepare to handoff some responsibilities: e.g. if new team leads will own sub-projects, ensure documentation and current status are clear for them to pick up.
·         Review this plan with key stakeholders (founders, lead investors, pilot partners if relevant) to ensure buy-in. Adjust if needed.
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         A Roadmap v2.0 document published in the repo or internally, covering Years 2–4 in detail (objectives, key features per plane, expected releases v2.x to v3.0, and rough timelines)[78].
·         A meeting or sign-off from leadership on this plan (could just note in changelog or minutes).
·         We should see continuity: tasks in our backlog now link forward into this new roadmap (no big gaps). For instance, if multi-cloud broker PoC was done in P4-T4, Era 2–4 plan explicitly has “Productionize multi-cloud broker by Q2 2028” etc. Essentially ensure nothing we learned is lost.
●	Also, check that our long-term invariants approach remains valid: decide if any DK thresholds need update for Era 2 (maybe tighten success% or add new ones like “consortium ledger consistency 99.999%”) and include those in the plan.
 Notes/Risks: Without this step, the team might drift after initial goals. Also investors often want to see an updated roadmap after key milestones are met. The risk is being too optimistic or pessimistic – use real data from 2 years to calibrate expectations for next 2. Communicate that clearly to avoid misalignment.
●	Task ID: P6-T3
 Name: Go-to-Market (GTM) Launch Prep
 Plane: Cross-Cutting (Product/Marketing)
 Package(s): collateral in docs/ (case studies, one-pagers)
 Type: N/A (Business deliverable)
 Owner: Product Manager / Founder
 Timebox: 4 weeks (Month 22–23)
 Dependencies: Pilot successes (from P5) to showcase; possibly investor input on TAM
 Steps:
·         Create case study documents for each pilot (with their permission). These should highlight the problem, how A4Q was used, and the outcome/benefits (even if modest). Ensure to quantify any wins (e.g. saved X hours of manual work, enabled Y% faster experiment cycles, improved compliance posture as evidenced by audit logs, etc.).
·         Compile a performance brief that includes key metrics achieved: e.g. “A4Q Platform by the numbers: 0.047 s compile time, 1.0 watermark TPR, 99.7% uptime in pilot, 3 cloud backends integrated, 2 partner deployments, etc.” This is drawn from our technical evidence but presented in executive-friendly terms.
·         Draft a pitch deck or one-pager for potential customers: focusing on value prop per plane (Discovery = faster quantum solution discovery, Governance = IP protection, Autonomy = reliable quantum operations). Use visuals if possible (architecture diagrams, screenshots of dashboard, etc.).
·         Prepare any needed demo environment or video: e.g. a recorded demo showing submitting a job and tracing it through trust-fabric ledger and watermark verification – to make it tangible for non-technical audience.
·         Align with business model: if by launch we intend to sell this as a SaaS or on-prem product, make sure materials clarify that (and that our deployment process supports it). E.g. if on-prem is needed, emphasize our K8s/Helm ease; if SaaS, highlight multi-tenant and security readiness.
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         A set of polished documents/presentations ready for external use. Possibly attach as PDFs to our internal repo or share with sales team.
·         Feedback from a friendly outsider (advisor or investor) who reviews the materials and gives a thumbs-up that it’s compelling and clear. Adjust if necessary.
·         Clear articulation of TAM (total addressable market) and how these pilots extrapolate to that – probably in narrative form rather than code, but we should document reasoning (like “Pharma R&D, Financial optimization, and Cloud providers are initial verticals – combined TAM $X – and A4Q proved value in each during pilot”).
●	This is more business-y, but it’s the culmination of tech proof into market strategy. We’ll use it directly in Phase 4 narrative for investors and in partner discussions.
 Notes/Risks: The risk is overstating capabilities – be honest but optimistic. Also ensure we don’t publish anything confidential without permission. Aim that by 24 mo, if someone asks “What has Invarian accomplished?”, we have a crisp answer and materials to back it up.
●	Task ID: P6-T4
 Name: Final v1.x LTS Release & Transition to Era 2 Team
 Plane: Cross-Cutting (Release/Ops)
 Package(s): infra/helm, support portal
 Type: PROD-REQUIRED
 Owner: Lead Engineer / Release Manager
 Timebox: 3 weeks (Month 23–24)
 Dependencies: P6-T2 (roadmap) done so team knows new structure; all critical fixes from audits/pilots done
 Steps:
·         Prepare a Long-Term Support (LTS) version of the v1 branch – likely v1.3 or v1.4 – incorporating all final bug fixes and minor improvements from pilot feedback and audit. This will be the stable version we maintain while new development proceeds on v2.0.
·         Document a maintenance plan for v1.x: e.g. security patches will be backported for 12 months, etc., so that early users can rely on it. Ensure this policy is communicated to any customers.
·         Officially close out Phase 1 program: hold a retrospective with the team, celebrate successes, document lessons learned in engineering practices. Archive the execution plan documentation (maybe tag this very doc as v1.0.0 complete).
·         Re-organize the team as needed for Era 2: perhaps split into sub-teams (Platform Core vs. Research vs. Deployment), assign owners for new epic areas. Make sure everyone knows the plan ahead (from P6-T2’s roadmap).
·         If new hires or transfers are planned around this time, integrate them with knowledge transfer sessions from those who built the v1 features. Essentially ensure continuity.
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Release notes for the final v1.x LTS, noting it’s LTS and what that means (we might also give it a codename for marketing consistency).
·         A short maintenance policy doc published, so stakeholders know our support commitment.
·         Internal retrospective notes highlighting what went well (e.g. “DK invariants discipline saved us many times”) and what to improve (maybe “start external testing earlier next time,” etc.). Use this to improve Era 2 execution.
·         Team roles chart updated for next phase – e.g. listing new leads for new initiatives like FTAE or QAGF productization, etc. This could be an internal memo or part of roadmap doc.
·         Celebratory evidence: perhaps an internal demo day or blog post on reaching this milestone (if not external, at least internal to motivate the team).
 Notes/Risks: It’s easy to rush into the next thing; don’t skip the closure. Consolidating gains and knowledge ensures we don’t repeat mistakes. The risk is some team members might roll off or lose context; mitigate by thorough documentation and perhaps keeping key people engaged in advisory capacity if moving off. By doing this formally, we set a professional tone for continuous development and signal to investors that we execute in stages with clear wrap-ups and next steps.
________________________________________
1.      2-Year Eras and Product-Launch Iterations (Years 2–10)
 With the initial two-year foundation laid (v1.x baseline and pilot deployments), the roadmap shifts to era-level planning in ~2-year increments. Each era corresponds to a major evolution of the platform, adding new capabilities and expanding the ecosystem. Below we outline each era’s focus, strategic goals per plane, and key deliverables and exit criteria. (Refer to the V10 10-year plan for detailed context[20].)
Era 1 (Years 0–2, 2025–2027) – Post-GA Stabilization & Pilot Proving
 Summary: In Era 1, the priority is to operationalize the MVP. The Autonomy plane evolves from a basic orchestrator to a reliable multi-backend scheduler (v2.0) handling real cloud QPUs with rudimentary adaptive control[37][55]. The Governance plane hardens the trust fabric into an enterprise-grade “Trust Fabric +” (v1.2→v2.0), integrating external KMS, distributed ledger options, and enforcing strict IP provenance in production settings[22][42]. The Discovery plane begins as a pure R&D track: a prototype Quantum Asset Generative Foundry (v0.1) is developed in-house to generate quantum circuits and QEC codes, but it’s not yet customer-facing[79][80]. Key goals include meeting all DK invariant SLOs in real deployments, achieving at least two successful pilot installations (design partners), and obtaining initial industry validation (e.g. contributing A4Q’s watermarking approach to standards bodies). By the end of Era 1, we aim to exit with a credible v1 platform: one that is secure, measurable, and has demonstrated value in controlled environments – setting the stage for external scale-up.
●	Era 1 Item I1: Production Security & Invariants Lockdown
 Plane(s): Governance (with cross-cutting impact)
 Major Deliverables: Complete integration of PQC key management via HSM/KMS (no in-memory secrets)[22]; enforce “REAL mode only” operations (no simulation backdoors); embed all DK SLOs as runtime monitors and CI gates (compile, append, uptime, etc.) – essentially deliver the platform exactly meeting its spec on performance and reliability[15][16].
 Prerequisites: Executed tasks P1-T1, P1-T2, P1-T5 – DK constants frozen and metrics in place; V8 GA baseline available as starting point.
 DK/SLO Targets: All original DK invariants must be met in pilot env (e.g. compile p95 ≤250 ms, ledger p95 ≤120 ms, watermark TPR ≥99%)[81]. Establish stretch targets as Ops Gold budgets (e.g. compile ≤230 ms) and ensure no regressions against them.
 Exit Criteria: Internal v1.1 GA declaration with evidence: a full ORR run passes (10/10 DK gates met) and “security backstops” engaged (any attempt to violate invariants or use sim mode is blocked in CI/staging)[63][26]. Auditors (internal or external) sign off that the system can be run in a production setting from a security/compliance perspective. Essentially, by end of Year 2, Invarian can say: “Our platform is stable and secure enough for real workloads” – backed by data and checklists.
●	Era 1 Item I2: Enterprise Trust Fabric Deployment
 Plane(s): Governance
 Major Deliverables: Trust Fabric v2.0 with enterprise features: multi-region ledger replication (active-active Postgres across data centers)[42]; a pilot blockchain-based ledger node running in parallel to evaluate tamper-proof consortium mode[41]; external OPA policy packs for data residency and privacy (e.g. automated log retention limits)[50]; integration of external Vault/HSM for all key operations. Also, initial contributions to standards – e.g. submit QIR provenance tag schema and watermark spec to industry group (achieved mid-era)[61].
 Prerequisites: Execution of tasks P2-T2 (Vault integ), P2-T3 (ledger HA & blockchain PoC), P2-T4 (policy extensions). V1 trust fabric from GA was single-region with in-memory keys; those weaknesses must be removed by delivering this item.
 DK/SLO Targets: Governance-plane SLOs remain as DK (append ≤120 ms, audit ≥99%)[47]. Stretch improvements: achieve ledger append p95 <100 ms via optimizations; maintain watermark detection TPR ~1.0 in diverse workloads (250/250 detections in V8, aim similar in pilots)[82][83]. Contractual SLA introduced: e.g. ledger service 99.9% availability and audit query ≤1 s for recent entries (to satisfy enterprise compliance).
 Exit Criteria: At least one pilot customer running the full trust fabric stack in production-like conditions. For example, a design partner’s quantum lab uses A4Q trust fabric to seal and track all jobs for a period of 3 months with no security incidents. Evidence includes their sign-off or a case study stating that cross-boundary watermarking and KMS integration functioned as expected in their environment. Also, an internal decision on the blockchain ledger: if shadow ledger showed <5% overhead and added audit value, go to implement multi-node in next era; if it proved impractical (too slow or no demand), pivot to simpler hardened SQL with periodic notarization[48]. Essentially, by end of Era 1, the trust fabric is battle-tested in the field and either ready to network-extend (if consortium ledger viable) or optimized as standalone service.
●	Era 1 Item I3: Autonomous Orchestration Pilot
 Plane(s): Autonomy
 Major Deliverables: Orchestrator v2.0 with real multi-backend support: integrated at least one major QPU cloud API (IBM, IonQ, etc.) replacing the prior simulator stubs[37]; multi-region failover fully implemented (demonstrated active/active orchestrator deployment in pilot)[84]; initial carbon-aware scheduling feature (jobs can be tagged or delayed based on real-time grid emissions, behind a feature flag)[55]; a prototype AutoCal-RL calibration service running in parallel (not affecting production yet, but collecting data)[85]. Also, orchestrator exposes telemetry for all reliability metrics (so SREs can trust it in production).
 Prerequisites: Execution of tasks P2-T1 (1st backend integ), P2-T6 (carbon scheduling rudiment), P4-T1/P4-T2 (introduced anomaly detection & calibration in shadow). GA orchestrator (v1) was proven reliable for a single region & simulated backend; this item expands it to real hardware and preliminary adaptive behavior.
 DK/SLO Targets: Maintain DK uptime ≥99.5% and recovery <60 s[86] in multi-region pilot deployments. Ensure job success rate ≥92% even as multiple backends are used (some failure modes might be outside our control, but orchestrator should retry or failover to keep success% high)[56]. Carbon scheduler stretch: aim for 10–15% average CO₂ reduction for jobs without exceeding SLA (tracked in pilot as an experiment)[56][58].
 Exit Criteria: Orchestrator is proven in a real-world pilot environment where it schedules across at least two quantum backends and handles load and failures gracefully. For instance, a partner runs jobs on both an IonQ device and an IBM device through one orchestrator, and we observe >5% throughput boost or cost savings due to multi-QPU scheduling (a “Go” signal)[87]. Conversely, any sign of instability (or OPA policy latency >50 ms impacting throughput) is resolved or feature toggled off (e.g. if carbon delays hurt more than help, we pause that feature)[57]. By end of Era 1, the orchestrator is considered production-grade for general use, with advanced features in pilot mode – effectively setting stage to evolve into a true “autonomy engine” next.
●	Era 1 Item I4: QAGF Prototype & Demonstration
 Plane(s): Discovery
 Major Deliverables: Delivery of a Quantum Asset Generative Foundry (QAGF) prototype v0.1 – an internal tool that can generate or optimize small quantum circuits using AI/ML techniques[79][80]. Key features: a pipeline for generative circuit synthesis (likely under a new service or as offline notebooks), integration of a feedback loop where generated circuits can be fed into A4Q’s compiler and maybe run on a simulator/hardware with results fed back for model training[88]; an initial QEC code suggestion module using heuristic or ML (e.g. propose an error-correcting code given a noise profile)[89]. The QAGF is not released as a product yet, but a working prototype is used internally and perhaps in one design partner engagement.
 Prerequisites: Tasks P2-T5 (watermark toolkit) indirectly supports IP tracking needed, P4-T1 (anomaly data) and P4-T2 (AutoCal data) provide ML footholds. Primarily, dedicated R&D effort from the Discovery team from project start to build models and pipeline (no hard external dependency, but needs data from simulations and possibly hardware noise profiles).
 DK/SLO Targets: No formal SLO (R&D), but define experimental KPIs: e.g. at least 30% of auto-generated circuits are non-trivial (not duplicates of training set)[80]; and ideally one AI-designed circuit or error mitigation yields >5% improvement on a test problem vs. known methods[80]. The prototype should not degrade platform SLOs – any integration with production (like executing a generated circuit) must still respect compile ≤250 ms etc., so likely scope is kept separate or batch-mode to avoid impact[90].
 Exit Criteria: A successful demo by end of Year 2: for example, using QAGF, the team generates a small variational circuit for a chemistry problem, runs it through A4Q, and finds it performs comparably to a human-designed ansatz. Or a custom error-correcting code from QAGF that shows a 5–10% improved logical error rate in simulation[91]. This “one clearly valuable asset” would justify continued investment[92]. If instead after 2 years, QAGF hasn’t produced anything novel (all outputs are mediocre or too large to run), that’s a pivot signal: re-scope the approach or focus on narrower optimization tasks[93]. The go-forward decision: Go if at least one promising result (even if in sim) and partner interest; Kill/Pivot if it’s a money sink with no progress – possibly pivot to using external generative tools or focusing QAGF on a specific niche like error mitigation which might be more tractable[94].
Era 2 (Years 2–4, 2027–2029) – Scaling Up & Early Ecosystem Integration
 Summary: In Era 2, A4Q moves from bespoke pilots to a scalable multi-tenant platform and begins knitting into a broader ecosystem of quantum services. Autonomy: Evolve orchestrator towards a predictive, self-healing system (introduce anomaly-driven adjustments, simulate early fault-tolerance control loops)[95][96]. Reliability targets tighten (aim for “zero downtime” operations with 99.99% uptime design) and prepare for upcoming FTQC by implementing resource management constructs (e.g. magic-state factories) even if hardware isn’t ready[97][98]. Governance: Expand the trust fabric to multi-organization mode – enabling cross-organization ledger networks or consortium governance. This means multiple stakeholders can share a ledger or verify each other’s ledger entries, pushing towards an industry quantum IP exchange[99][100]. Emphasis on standards adoption and potentially spinning off the trust fabric as a neutral foundation (if beneficial)[101][102]. Discovery: Grow QAGF from prototype to a beta feature integrated with the platform. By end of Era 2, users (internal or select external) can invoke generative suggestions via the A4Q SDK to get algorithm recommendations or QEC improvements[99][103]. Success in this era is measured by wider adoption: multiple organizations using A4Q (some via a shared network), more cloud providers integrated, and initial real-world use of AI-generated quantum solutions.
●	Era 2 Item I1: Federated Trust Fabric & Multi-Org Ledger
 Plane(s): Governance
 Major Deliverables: Transition A4Q’s governance plane from single-tenant deployments to a federated model. Launch a permissioned ledger network where multiple organizations (cloud providers, research consortia) run ledger nodes that interoperate[99][100]. Develop cross-node trust sync – e.g. periodic consensus or notarization between ledgers to ensure a global view of IP transactions (could build on the blockchain PoC if it was a go). Implement organization onboarding workflows (admin APIs to add a new org, assign them keys/certs, etc.). Strengthen policy engine rules to handle multi-org scenarios – e.g. allow conditional sharing of assets between orgs with mutual trust contracts. Possibly introduce smart contracts or advanced watermarking for inter-org transfers (like if Org A wants to allow Org B to use a circuit for X runs, enforce via contract on ledger). Deliver unified audit tools that can query the federated ledger seamlessly.
 Prerequisites: Era 1’s trust fabric must be solid (Item I2 from Era 1). Decision to pursue consortium ledger (if blockchain path chosen, implement fully; if not, maybe use a distributed database with lighter consensus). Also need stakeholder buy-in: likely have identified 2–3 partner orgs willing to participate in a shared network by now (perhaps one cloud vendor, one national lab, etc.).
 DK/SLO Targets: Governance SLA goes up: target 99.99% ledger integrity (no downtime, no inconsistency)[75] – meaning the network tolerates node outages without losing audit capability. Append latency might loosen slightly per node but aim to keep effective p95 <200 ms across network by caching or local commits. Introduce metrics like consistency lag (how quickly ledgers sync – target under 5 s). Watermark TPR invariant stays ≥99%, depth overhead ≤0.5% globally. Possibly tighten audit coverage to 99.9% (with multiple orgs, ensure near-total capture of events).
 Exit Criteria: Deployment of an industry consortium trust fabric on a small scale: e.g. 3 independent organizations (perhaps Invarian + 2 partners) exchanging quantum IP through a shared ledger. Demonstrate a scenario where a quantum asset flows from Org A to Org B with full provenance maintained via our network. Measure overhead: if consensus overhead or multi-node lag stays within acceptable bounds (say <5% throughput hit vs. single org), continue scaling; if the approach proves too slow or cumbersome, pivot to a simpler centralized model with opt-in verification (maybe maintain one ledger service but allow others to query/verify snapshots)[99][100]. A key external marker: by end of Era 2, A4Q’s governance model should either be on track to become an industry standard (with multiple adopters), or we decide to adapt and support whichever standard is emerging (ensuring we remain compatible rather than isolated)[104][105]. In summary, exit when we have either achieved multi-org operation with real users or made a conscious strategic decision on how to proceed (with evidence from pilots to guide that choice).
●	Era 2 Item I2: Adaptive Autonomy & Self-Healing Cluster
 Plane(s): Autonomy
 Major Deliverables: Elevate the orchestrator into a more autonomous control system. Key features: implement a Magic-State Resource Manager in anticipation of fault-tolerant QPUs – even if hardware isn’t ready, simulate the production and consumption of magic states and incorporate scheduling logic to manage this resource (throttle jobs when magic-state budget is low, etc.)[69][70]. Introduce a real-time Anomaly Detection & Response loop: feed orchestrator (and possibly a dedicated monitoring service) with streams of system metrics (error rates, queue times) and enable automatic mitigations on certain triggers – e.g. auto-pausing a specific backend if error rates spike (and rerouting jobs elsewhere)[71][68]. Potentially split the orchestration layer into two parts: a local FT Controller (running close to hardware for low-latency adjustments) and a global scheduler in the cloud for high-level decisions[106][107] (this depends on hardware progress). Also deepen carbon-aware scheduling: by now, orchestrator should routinely route jobs to low-carbon regions when latency/cost tradeoffs allow, and possibly integrate with data center energy APIs to schedule large jobs at optimal times[108][109].
 Prerequisites: Era 1 orchestrator (Item I3) delivered stable multi-backend ops. Also likely need data from any early FTQC experiments (maybe small logical qubit tests in labs) to calibrate our magic-state simulation. P4-T1 (anomaly detection PoC) and P4-T2 (AutoCal integration) laid groundwork – those get productized here (i.e. anomaly detection moves from shadow to active mode gradually).
 DK/SLO Targets: Uptime goal increases – design for 99.99% (5 min annual downtime) for orchestrator, implying automated failovers with near-zero downtime at cluster level[110][98]. Recovery time target global <30 s (and <5 s for zone-level restart)[110][111]. Introduce Automation SLOs: e.g. Anomaly Response Time <1 s (system reacts to a fault within a second by isolating the issue)[112][113]. Perhaps track Magic State Utilization Efficiency – ensuring our scheduler uses available FT resources ≥X% efficiently (a new metric once FTQC online)[69][71]. Carbon usage goal: >50% of cumulative compute run in low-carbon mode (if capacity exists)[71][114]. All original DK invariants still hold or tighten (maybe job success ≥95% now as systems mature).
 Exit Criteria: A4Q’s orchestrator is capable of running extended autonomous operations with minimal human intervention. For instance, simulate a continuous 24-hour run with varying loads and failure injections, and the system auto-recovers everything (no manual resets) – basically achieving “hands-off” operations for a day, which is a precursor to real FTQC management. If any predictive features caused instability (e.g. false anomaly triggers oscillating actions), tune or disable until resolved, with a decision on what goes fully live vs. stays in test. By end of Era 2, we should have either actual hardware integration or high-fidelity simulation such that when a small FTQC machine comes, we can plug it in and our autonomy features will immediately add value[115]. Also externally, if cloud providers release their own schedulers, we either ensure our differentiation (tightly coupled governance and multi-cloud as our edge) or consider integration (maybe our autonomy engine can feed into their system)[73]. Exit = orchestrator is not just a scheduler, but a rudimentary “autopilot” for quantum operations, proven in at least a test or simulated FTQC scenario, and ready to manage real quantum data centers as they appear.
●	Era 2 Item I3: QAGF Integration & Beta Release
 Plane(s): Discovery (with ties to Autonomy)
 Major Deliverables: Evolve the Quantum Asset Generative Foundry from a research project into a platform-integrated feature (likely labeled beta by end of era). This includes developing a service/API endpoint (e.g. /v1/generateCircuit or an SDK call) that allows users to request an AI-generated circuit or calibration routine via the A4Q platform[100][103]. The QAGF (or “Quantum Discovery Studio, QDS”) will incorporate models trained on expanding data – including results from our pilots and possibly open repositories. Build a Knowledge Base to store AI-discovered circuits, performance metrics, and context (so the system learns what worked)[116][117]. Implement hardware-aware generation: users can specify a target QPU profile (qubit count, topology, error rates) and QAGF will tailor outputs to it[117][118]. Tie in the QEC Decoder module: use feedback from decoding experiments to guide generative models (e.g. reward circuits that are easier to error-correct)[118][119]. Possibly open up an extension interface for external researchers – e.g. allow plugging in custom generative models or contribute data (making QAGF somewhat extensible and community-friendly)[119][120]. By late Era 2, consider releasing QAGF as an “experimental” feature to design partners or power users for feedback.
 Prerequisites: Era 1 QAGF prototype (Item I4) delivered a working model and at least one promising result. Also need lots of data: by now, we’ll have more circuits from our own ops and possibly collaborations (the item suggests seeking academic partnerships, which presumably we did in Era 1 to feed this)[121]. Also the QEC decoder (from GA) matured enough to integrate.
 DK/SLO Targets: QAGF now gets internal SLO-ish metrics: e.g. Recommendation Precision – at least X% of generated suggestions meet a minimum improvement threshold (target something like ≥20% of suggestions provide >5% performance gain on their test problem)[122][123]. Turnaround Time – ensure generation requests complete in reasonable time (target <30 s for small problems, possibly by using pre-trained models or HPC backend)[122][123]. Also track adoption: number of QAGF-generated results per quarter – aiming for an increasing trend, indicating user trust in AI suggestions[124][125]. Importantly, QAGF integration must not impede core SLOs: generating a circuit might be heavy, but we likely run it async or on separate resources. If QAGF starts interacting with runtime (like dynamic optimizations mid-execution, which was envisioned)[126][127], ensure that any such feature is carefully budgeted (maybe only on certain long jobs, and with safe fallback if slow).
 Exit Criteria: By end of Era 2, QAGF is a credible value-add, either as a stand-alone beta product or as a distinctive feature within A4Q. Concretely, we expect at least one or two case studies of QAGF success: e.g. “A4Q Discovery suggested a new error mitigation technique that improved logical qubit stability by 15% on XYZ device,” or “Auto-generated circuit for [customer problem] beat their hand-crafted solution by 10%.” These tangible wins would allow us to label QAGF a beta feature and possibly start charging for it or bundling it into deals as a differentiator. If QAGF underperforms (no clear wins, or users find it not useful), then we decide how to adjust: maybe narrow its scope or integrate an external better performing algorithm if one exists (ensuring our platform can still host it)[128]. Another exit outcome could be to pivot QAGF’s positioning: for instance, if an open-source generative tool surpasses us, we might incorporate it rather than compete, focusing our unique value on tying generation to our trust ledger (so IP from AI is protected/traced – something others might not do)[128][129]. In any case, by the end of Era 2, we should either be moving forward with QAGF as a selling point or have a clear plan to avoid it being a money sink. Success is if at v3.0 (end of era) we can say: “A4Q Discovery Studio is now available in beta – it has demonstrated the ability to accelerate quantum solution design, and several early users have incorporated its suggestions with positive results.”[130][131].
●	Era 2 Item I4: External Ecosystem Connectors
 Plane(s): Cross-Plane (Integration/Standards)
 Major Deliverables: Solidify A4Q’s integration points such that it can plug into other ecosystems and vice versa. This includes building SDKs/APIs in additional languages (e.g. a Python SDK if demand dictates, since many quantum folks use Python – as hinted about adding /sdk/python if needed)[132][133]. Develop bridges to popular quantum software: for example, integration with Qiskit or Cirq such that circuits from those frameworks can be submitted into A4Q seamlessly (using our QIR adapters). Possibly provide a plugin for cloud provider services – e.g. if AWS Braket users want to use A4Q’s trust fabric, we could supply a middleware or cross-account setup. Work on standards compliance: by now, ensure our QIR output and watermarking follow any finalized standards from IEEE/P1838 or QIR Alliance (if the standard diverged, update our implementation accordingly to maintain compatibility). Also, create exportable modules of our platform if needed: e.g. if some users want just the trust ledger without orchestrator, make that possible as a standalone offering (as suggested, maybe offer trust fabric as a separate product)[101][134]. Another connector is GRC/Compliance tool integration – link our audit logs to enterprise governance tools (so CISOs can integrate A4Q logs into their dashboards)[135][136]. The goal is to reduce friction for adoption: A4Q should fit into existing workflows and not require everyone to adopt a wholly new stack all at once.
 Prerequisites: Basic CLI/SDK exists (TypeScript CLI from GA); likely triggered by pilot feedback: e.g. “we need a Python API to embed A4Q in our Jupyter notebooks.” Standardization efforts from Era 1 matured so we know what to align with.
 DK/SLO Targets: No new SLOs directly, but define success in terms of coverage: e.g. by v3.0, have SDK support for 2–3 major languages (TS/JS from start, add Python, maybe Go or Rust if needed for enterprise integration). For standards, target that any reference circuits or data that come out of A4Q can be read by external tools with minimal or no conversion, meaning we truly adhere to accepted formats (like a QIR with provenance tags that an external auditor tool can parse). If we do modular offerings, ensure that decoupling doesn’t break invariants when integrated (e.g. if someone runs trust fabric standalone, they should still enforce DK metrics relevant to it, like audit coverage).
 Exit Criteria: A4Q is considered an interoperable platform by end of Era 2. Evidence: external developers are able to use our SDK in their preferred environment (we might see community contributions or at least pilot partners coding with our Python SDK if we provide one). Our watermarking and ledger schemas perhaps become part of a standards draft or at least plug into others’ solutions – e.g. maybe another startup or an open-source project uses our watermark schema for their own IP tagging, showing acceptance. In concrete terms, exit when at least one non-Invarian product or open source tool successfully integrates with A4Q via our published APIs – could be a cloud provider hooking their hardware into our orchestrator via our Broker API, or a research lab’s custom quantum software calling our trust ledger for notarization. We also expect by end of Era 2 that major quantum cloud players are either partners or not blockers: if a de-facto standard emerges (maybe one provider’s governance solution), we have either aligned with it or ensured we support it inside our platform[104][105]. Ultimately, Era 2 success is A4Q is not operating in a silo: it’s recognized and integrated in the wider quantum tech landscape, making it much stickier and paving the way for wide adoption in Era 3 when hardware scales up.
Era 3 (Years 4–6, 2029–2031) – Fault-Tolerance Readiness & Broad Adoption
 Summary: Era 3 is about preparing for and capitalizing on the advent of practical fault-tolerant quantum computers (FTQC), projected around 2030-2031. The platform must evolve into a full Quantum Autopilot for FTQC operations and become an indispensable middleware for quantum data centers. Autonomy: Deliver a Fault-Tolerant Ready (v4.0) release that splits operations between low-latency on-prem controllers (close to QPU hardware) and global orchestration[106][107]. Implement real-time feedback loops for magic-state distribution, failover mechanisms for logical qubits (if one node fails, shift to another seamlessly)[108][109]. Enhance energy management so that large-scale quantum jobs align with sustainability (target ≥50% renewable energy usage for quantum ops)[108][109]. Governance: Establish A4Q’s trust fabric as the industry standard for quantum governance by this era. Possibly launch a foundation or neutral governance network if not done in Era 2, to ensure broad adoption and regulatory trust[101][102]. Achieve formal compliance certifications (FIPS, ISO, etc.) and external audits green-lit for mission-critical use[137]. The trust fabric should be so reliable and accepted that even governments and cross-border collaborations rely on it (“TCP/IP of Quantum Trust”). Discovery: Push QAGF from beta to a production-grade tool integrated into daily workflows. By v4.0, A4Q’s Discovery plane should regularly deliver improvements in algorithms or calibrations for users, and could even start influencing hardware design (suggesting error correction strategies or co-designing circuits with hardware constraints in mind)[138][126]. Possibly brand it as a separate module or product line if it becomes significant (e.g. “A4Q Discovery Suite”). The overall focus is scaling up usage: multiple FTQC sites running under A4Q orchestration, numerous organizations using the trust network, and the technology being validated by external authorities (auditors, standards bodies). It’s a pivot from proving ourselves to being a cornerstone in the quantum ecosystem.
●	Era 3 Item I1: FTQC Autopilot Deployment
 Plane(s): Autonomy
 Major Deliverables: Full integration and deployment of A4Q’s autonomy features on actual fault-tolerant quantum hardware (or the very best prototypes available). The orchestrator likely bifurcates into two coordinated layers: (1) an on-premises FT Controller that interfaces directly with the FTQC rack, handling microsecond-level tasks (e.g. live error rate monitoring, magic state injection management), and (2) a Global Orchestrator in the cloud that coordinates across multiple such machines/sites for high-level scheduling and failover[106][107]. Develop advanced HA for logical qubits: if a QPU node fails, the system can trigger a failover to a backup QPU, with state handoff if possible (this could involve support from hardware – e.g. teleporting state – but at least orchestrator must orchestrate at job level)[108][109]. Implement maintenance automation: orchestrator should schedule its own calibration windows, software updates, etc., in coordination with usage patterns (like an “autonomic data center”)[139][140]. Achieve deep carbon-optimization: e.g. pause non-urgent workloads if the grid goes dirty and resume when green energy is abundant, possibly integrate with battery/energy storage at the facility to smooth consumption[141][139]. Essentially deliver the “Quantum Data Center OS” capability by end of Era 3.
 Prerequisites: Era 2’s autonomy enhancements (anomaly response, magic state sim) must have been validated to now apply to real hardware. We need collaboration with hardware providers – likely by 2029, some partners have early FTQC with ~100-1000 logical qubits[142]. If hardware is delayed, plan B is demonstrating on high-fidelity simulators. Strong dependency on new vendor APIs (they might expose hooks for scheduling logical qubits or retrieving deeper telemetry – we must integrate those). Also, our own systems must be extremely robust by now (99.99% avail achieved in Era 2).
 DK/SLO Targets: Aim for effectively zero unplanned downtime – design for 99.99%+ uptime cluster-wide[110][98]. Recovery should be near-instant for most failures (sub-second for local, a few seconds for regional). Introduce throughput metrics: e.g. guarantee the orchestrator can sustain at least X logical operations per second with Y% utilization of the expensive hardware (ensuring we aren’t the bottleneck)[112][143]. For failover, define an RTO for logical qubit transfer or job migration (maybe target <1 s for an interrupted job to resume on backup). Carbon efficiency metrics pivot to measure “flops per CO₂” – ensure each generation of improvements yields better efficiency (a selling point to environmentally conscious users)[144][145]. Maintain all existing invariants in scaled environment, maybe even tighten (e.g. compile p95 might need to remain low even as circuits become bigger – possibly by parallelizing compilation).
 Exit Criteria: A4Q is successfully managing a long-running fault-tolerant quantum workload that would be impractical to handle manually. For example, orchestrating a multi-day computation on a small FTQC cluster, during which the system automatically handled error corrections, qubit failovers, calibrations, and energy optimization, without human intervention, and completed the task within SLA. Also, external trust: by end of Era 3 we likely have customers relying on us for production – e.g. a quantum cloud provider might be running their operations through our platform or an enterprise with an on-prem quantum data center uses A4Q as its operations console. The orchestrator’s value should be clearly demonstrated by metrics: e.g. “Using A4Q Autonomy, uptime of quantum service improved from 95% to 99.9%, and utilization increased by 20%” – these are big deals in expensive quantum hardware context[146][147]. If any aspect underperforms (say our global scheduler can’t keep up with scale), that’s identified and rectified or we partner with someone (maybe co-develop with a cloud provider’s scheduler). Essentially, exit Era 3 when A4Q can credibly claim to be “proven in a fault-tolerant quantum environment” – even if those are early and small, we’ve done it, positioning us as the go-to solution as FTQC scales further.
●	Era 3 Item I2: Industry-Standard Quantum Governance
 Plane(s): Governance
 Major Deliverables: Achieve broad adoption and formalization of A4Q’s governance model. This likely involves launching the trust fabric as an industry consortium or standard service. We might have established a Quantum Governance Alliance or similar, where major players (hardware vendors, cloud providers, national labs) either run nodes or at least subscribe to the framework[101][102]. Possibly transfer stewardship to a neutral foundation to bolster trust (like how Linux or Internet standards are managed)[148][102] – this could mean open-sourcing parts of the ledger code or at least establishing open governance of the protocol. Implement any interoperability protocols needed: if some competitors have their own watermark or ledger, ensure cross-verification is possible (we might create adapters to prove transactions from one system in another)[149][150]. Add retrospective audit AI: use AI/ML to scan years of ledger data to find anomalies or compliance issues automatically (pattern detection on audit logs)[149][151]. Also by this era, update the cryptography if needed (post-quantum crypto standards might have changed or require bigger keys – our system should have evolved accordingly to remain secure against quantum attacks even as actual quantum power grows)[151][152]. Essentially, treat the trust fabric as stable infrastructure: less about adding new features, more about scaling, performance tuning (if volume of transactions is huge by now, consider ledger sharding or hierarchical ledgers for efficiency)[153][154] and meeting any new legal requirements (maybe new privacy laws – ensure our policy engine can encode those). Possibly turn the trust fabric into a revenue source itself (if it’s offered standalone to those who want just governance without full stack).
 Prerequisites: Era 2’s multi-org and early network must have proven value or been adjusted. Also likely require legal and policy work: by now, governments or regulatory bodies might be looking at how quantum IP is managed, so engaging them (maybe we got government pilots). Standard bodies may have drafts – we need to align or lead them.
 DK/SLO Targets: At this stage, performance is secondary to trust: ensure no ledger inconsistencies or security breaches – target 100% integrity (perhaps 99.999% formally)[75][155]. Availability very high: network should survive regional outages without losing audit data (no single point of failure)[75][156]. Compliance metrics: e.g. 100% of data deletion requests or retention policies are automatically enforced (demonstrating we meet evolving privacy rules)[157][158]. Still maintain decent performance at scale (if millions of transactions, keep latency manageable < a few hundred ms by scaling out)[159][160]. Possibly have formal verification on critical parts (e.g. formally verify smart contracts for ledger logic to assure no vulnerabilities). Achieve external certifications: by end of Era 3, we aim to be FIPS-140, ISO 27001 or similar certified, and maybe passed a governmental security review for usage in sensitive contexts[137][161].
 Exit Criteria: The A4Q governance plane is widely recognized as the “legal/trust layer” for quantum operations. We know we’ve succeeded when, for example, a major quantum provider or large enterprise mandates usage of our trust fabric or standard for their quantum workflows. Alternatively, if a different standard took hold, success means we have integrated it such that our product still thrives (maybe our software supports that standard seamlessly, so customers use our implementation to comply with it)[104][105]. Also by exit, we expect that regulators or auditors can interface with our system easily: e.g. a government can audit an organization’s quantum IP usage via our provided dashboards or exports, and they trust the results. If we consider monetization or moat: by Era 3, if not earlier, our governance network and data (anonymized insights from it) could form a moat – e.g. historical data of quantum usage patterns that only we have. Exit Era 3 when quantum governance becomes “boring” (in a good way) – akin to TLS for internet: it’s just there, trusted, and any serious player uses it[162][163]. That is the hallmark of success: it’s ubiquitous and stable to the point it’s no longer a differentiator but required infrastructure[164][165] (which secures our position in the ecosystem long-term).
●	Era 3 Item I3: Discovery Plane General Availability
 Plane(s): Discovery
 Major Deliverables: Transform the Discovery plane into a fully supported product offering. By v4.0, A4Q’s generative and AI-driven capabilities should be integrated such that they materially improve user outcomes regularly. This includes deploying QAGF as a persistent cloud service (scalable model-serving infrastructure, possibly GPU-accelerated)[138][166] to handle concurrent generation requests. Implement model versioning and governance: allow users to choose or trust certain model versions (like how one might use a specific compiler version)[167][168], and log model version used in the ledger for each suggestion (ensuring reproducibility and IP clarity). Possibly incorporate dynamic use of QAGF in runtime: for instance, during a long running job, if conditions change (error rates spike), orchestrator can query QAGF on-the-fly for a better error mitigation or circuit tweak[126][127] – merging Discovery with Autonomy in advanced scenarios. Expand the scope of Discovery plane to hardware co-design: use QAGF techniques to not only suggest algorithms but maybe propose hardware tuning (e.g. “Given our data, a slightly different qubit connectivity or a new calibration routine could improve results”) and feed those suggestions to hardware teams or loop them into calibration processes[169][170]. In essence, the plane that started as “AI in the loop” now becomes “AI drives significant improvements in either algorithms or system performance.” Also ensure IP of AI outputs is handled: integrate with trust fabric so that any model-generated asset is ledgered and properly attributed (to mitigate IP concerns of machine-generated inventions).
 Prerequisites: Era 2’s QAGF beta (Item I3 of Era 2) showed viability and user interest. Also, more data by now: we have years of execution logs, which can be used to train better models (our ledger is a goldmine of algorithm performance data – harness it within privacy bounds)[171][172]. Possibly needed HPC upgrades (maybe we invest in more GPUs or TPUs to support heavy AI tasks).
 DK/SLO Targets: Discovery will have SLOs akin to a product: e.g. Recommendation Quality SLO – maybe define that X% of suggestions have value above threshold (though that’s tricky to guarantee). More straightforward: Service reliability – ensure the QAGF service has high uptime (it’s now prod, so 99%+ maybe, though not critical path) and Response time – at least for smaller requests, <30 s or some interactive threshold[122][123]. Track usage metrics: aim for a steady increase in fraction of users using QAGF features. For example, by end of Era 3, target that >50% of active projects on A4Q have used at least one AI-generated asset in their workflow, showing it’s becoming mainstream. Maintain that QAGF suggestions never violate core invariants – e.g. if it outputs a complex circuit, our compile time might spike, but ensure pipeline can handle or the suggestion is flagged as impractical. Possibly by now we impose internal limits (like QAGF won’t suggest a circuit that would obviously break our latency budgets unless explicitly asked).
 Exit Criteria: The Discovery plane is either a “crown jewel” or a well-integrated assistive feature of A4Q by this time[173][174]. We exit Era 3 when we have multiple evidence points of QAGF delivering tangible innovation: e.g. several clients credit A4Q Discovery for new patentable algorithms or significant speed-ups in their quantum solutions. If QAGF succeeded marvelously, perhaps it becomes a key driver of platform adoption (people join A4Q network because they want access to the best quantum AI assistant). If it’s moderately useful, it’s at least an integrated tool that adds value occasionally and differentiates us from low-level orchestration-only solutions. There’s a scenario where QAGF flops or remains niche – by this era we should know: if uptake is low and external tools did better, maybe we have pivoted to incorporate those external tools (ensuring our platform still has discovery capabilities, just not all developed in-house). A kill scenario would have likely happened earlier if it was a total miss; by Era 3 if it’s still not working out, we probably scale it down to minimal maintenance and focus elsewhere. But assuming partial success: exit Era 3 with QAGF either fully productized or at least clearly proven in one domain (maybe it’s extremely good at error correction proposals – so we focus it there and market that heavily). In summary, end of Era 3, Discovery plane should move from R&D to ROI: it produces returns in terms of better algorithms or higher system performance, justifying the decade-long investment.
●	Era 3 Item I4: Global Scaling & Platform Packaging
 Plane(s): Cross-Cutting (Business/Deployment)
 Major Deliverables: As adoption grows, adjust the platform’s deployment and packaging for scale. This might involve creating a “Quantum Data Center Kit” edition of A4Q (mentioned in strategy)[175][176] – essentially a bundled offering that a large organization can deploy as the management layer for their entire quantum infrastructure, possibly including hardware racks, etc. We may consider splitting the monorepo or modularizing for different use cases: e.g. a lightweight version for those who only need governance with others’ orchestration, or a full stack for new quantum data centers (some hints in plan about possibly splitting core vs experimental, plugin architecture)[177][178]. Ensure our platform can accommodate different deployment models: some clients might want fully managed cloud service (we host A4Q as SaaS for them), others might need on-prem for sensitive jobs. By Era 3, we likely support both via configuration and containerization improvements. Also refine multi-cloud/hybrid cloud capabilities to an extreme: if a user wants to burst across all available QPUs globally, our platform should handle that smoothly (maybe integrate with scheduling systems or brokers beyond our control). In terms of business, possibly prepare an LTS release (v4.x LTS) as quantum enters mission-critical phase – organizations will want stable long-term support versions. Another deliverable: extensive third-party certifications: e.g. if quantum is used in healthcare or finance by now, we might need compliance with HIPAA or financial regs – ensure the platform can meet those (through security and logging features, which we partly did). This era might also involve evolving our licensing or open-source strategy: e.g. open-sourcing parts to become standard (especially if making a foundation for trust fabric, etc.), while keeping key intellectual property proprietary (maybe the AI models or certain optimization engines).
 Prerequisites: Growth in user base and hardware availability. We’d have feedback from various deployments about how they want to consume our tech (cloud vs. on-prem, etc.). Era 2/3 tasks on multi-tenancy and multi-cloud matured our architecture to be flexible. Also probably need organizational scaling: bigger dev team to handle multiple product flavors.
 DK/SLO Targets: At this stage SLOs might be formalized into SLA tiers for different customers (e.g. standard vs. premium support). But from tech view: ensure that even at global scale, we meet core SLOs. Possibly adapt invariants for scale – e.g. we might accept slightly higher latencies for enormously large circuits or networks, but we’d document those. Perhaps emphasize data durability now: guarantee no data loss ever, courtesy of backups and distributed systems (quantum computations may produce huge data; durability is key for trust)[179][180]. Also ensure zero-downtime upgrades: by now, as we do new releases, we can deploy updates without taking down the network (rolling upgrades of nodes, etc.)[181][182] – an SLO could be “governance network updates with 0 downtime or inconsistency”[181][182].
 Exit Criteria: A4Q is “productized” to the level that it can be scaled out with relatively lower friction. We’ll know this when, for example, a new quantum data center can be set up using our kit/documentation with minimal direct engineering from our side – in other words, our solution becomes replicable and deployable by integrators or customers (the way classical data center software is). Another sign is if we have partner companies (maybe large cloud vendors or IT firms) who include A4Q as part of their offerings or reference architectures. Possibly by Era 3 end, M&A or commercialization moves happen (if not earlier): e.g. maybe an offer from a big cloud provider to buy in or license our tech exclusively – how we handle that is strategic, but our goal was to remain independent as a neutral layer. If independent, we should have revenue streams by now (selling licenses or support for the kit, etc.). In any case, exit Era 3 when A4Q is no longer a bespoke solution built by a core team for each deployment, but a true platform that others can adopt, integrate, and even build on (e.g. third-party developers creating add-ons, tools, maybe a small ecosystem around our APIs). Technically, we likely consolidate repository or break it into maintainable modules by now if needed (monorepo might still work, but if too large, may have split core vs optional as plan suggested)[177][178]. The end of Era 3 positions us for the final stretch where quantum computing becomes mainstream critical infrastructure, with A4Q ideally entrenched as the default operating layer for it.
Era 4 (Years 6–8, 2031–2033) – Optimization and Expansion
 Summary: By Era 4, quantum computing is assumed to be delivering real value and possibly part of critical systems. A4Q’s focus shifts to optimization over new features – ensuring the platform scales ultra-reliably and efficiently as usage grows, and integrating with classical infrastructure as needed (hybrid workflows, HPC synergy). Autonomy: Fine-tune scheduling algorithms using data from thousands of runs – maybe introduce reinforcement learning or advanced optimization techniques to squeeze maximum throughput and minimize error accumulation[183][184]. Implement global scheduling tricks like routing jobs based on time-of-day or regional cooling costs (send jobs to where it’s night for better cryo stability, etc.)[185][139]. Ensure by now 4-5 nines reliability (99.99–99.999% uptime) across the system and basically no manual calibrations missed (system auto-schedules all maintenance with no lapses)[146][186]. Governance: Enter a phase of ubiquity and maintenance. Rather than major new capabilities, ensure the trust fabric remains “invisible and ultra-reliable” – it’s just there, like plumbing, requiring minimal changes[187][188]. Performance tune ledger for high volume (millions of transactions), possibly implement hierarchical or sharded ledgers if needed[189][153]. Keep up with any new compliance regime but mostly hold steady and harden. Discovery: If Discovery proved successful, by now it’s a key part of the innovation cycle. Possibly integrate it deeper such that the platform not only runs quantum jobs but actively improves them over time (like AIOps but for quantum – continuously analyzing results and proposing adjustments). Or if Discovery plateaued, it might be an optional module that some use but it’s not core for all – in which case we focus on supporting those who do use it and keep improving models with new data. Era 4 is also about exploring adjacent markets: maybe adapt our orchestration to hybrid quantum-classical workflows (embedding into HPC schedulers), or offering our autonomy tech for classical HPC too (if quantum growth stalls or merges with classical HPC, our tech might find broader use). The guiding principle: by Era 4, A4Q should be a mature, low-risk backbone – we want to remove any fragility and ensure it can run indefinitely as quantum scales.
●	Era 4 Item I1: Maximal Efficiency Orchestration
 Plane(s): Autonomy
 Major Deliverables: Focus on optimizing performance and throughput using advanced techniques. Incorporate machine learning optimization into scheduling: e.g. train models on historical job data to predict the best scheduling decisions (learning which job order minimizes total error or idle time)[190][184]. Possibly deploy an RL agent that continuously tweaks scheduling policies (subject to invariants) to improve metrics like utilization or queue wait times, creating a self-optimizing loop. Implement truly global scheduling: if quantum computing resources are distributed worldwide, design algorithms to route tasks considering global criteria (latency, cost, error rates, even geopolitical constraints) – e.g. automatically send tasks to where energy is cheapest at the moment or where capacity is free, almost like an international exchange for quantum jobs[141][139]. Also integrate networked quantum computers if they exist (handle entangled operations across sites). Add maintenance automation enhancements: the system might now proactively suggest hardware improvements scheduling, e.g. “QPU X needs a 1-hour calibration every 24h and auto-schedules it at lowest load period.” Provide near real-time feedback to hardware: maybe orchestrator can adjust pulse sequences on the fly via API if model predictions say it’ll reduce error – bridging software and hardware control more tightly (requires hardware support).
 Prerequisites: Fault-tolerant ops from Era 3 stable. We have abundant run data to train ML (we’ll have logs from years of operations, perfect for training scheduling heuristics). Possibly HPC integration: some tasks here akin to HPC scheduling problems – we might leverage or integrate with HPC schedulers for hybrid tasks by now.
 DK/SLO Targets: Aim for five-nines (99.999%) uptime for core orchestration services in this era – effectively continuous operation (some quantum services might become like utilities). Perhaps set targets like “no missed calibration events” (100% adherence to calibration schedule, meaning we never drop the ball on maintenance). Throughput improvement: demonstrate that with our ML-optimized scheduling, a given quantum workload can be executed with, say, 20-30% higher success or 30% less idle time than with static scheduling (some metric to show we’re getting better output per hardware input)[147][191]. Keep latency budgets largely same or slightly adjusted for scale (compilation might be distributed by now if circuits are huge, but ensure overall pipeline still meets user needs). Also, maintain improvements in carbon: maybe target quantum operations at 70%+ renewable-powered by orchestrating around grid conditions (taking advantage of any energy storage integration by scheduling heavy jobs when renewable is high).
