 Exit Criteria: The orchestrator operates at a level where resource usage is maximized and overhead minimized such that any further improvements yield diminishing returns – essentially we consider it “fully optimized.” For example, if we can claim that the orchestrator consistently keeps all expensive QPU resources >90% utilized without sacrificing job quality, that’s a huge win (given idle time is lost $$). Also by this point, our system should handle any scale thrown at it: e.g. if there are 100 QPUs globally, orchestrator can juggle them; if there are 1000 concurrent jobs, the scheduling still works smoothly. Another measure: user feedback might shift – earlier, success was not crashing; now success is users trust our scheduler so much they let it run autonomously and focus on other things, because they know it’s near optimal. We exit Era 4 when we have basically achieved a stable, highly efficient quantum operations platform that perhaps could be handed off to a broader operations team or even packaged for others to run – i.e. no heroics needed from original devs to keep it going. This sets stage for final era which is more about scaling out usage rather than tech development.
●	Era 4 Item I2: Sustainment & Compliance LTS (Governance)
 Plane(s): Governance
 Major Deliverables: Enter “maintenance and ubiquity” phase for governance plane[187][188]. Deliver a v5.0 LTS of the trust fabric focusing on stability, performance tuning, and ease of integration. Possibly implement ledger scaling enhancements like sharding or hierarchical ledgers if the volume is enormous (e.g. local ledgers per site that roll-up to global for performance)[189][154]. Ensure integration with any new tech (for example, if confidential computing enclaves are used to run parts of ledger for extra security, incorporate that)[192][193]. Expand to support any new legal frameworks: e.g. if there’s a new global quantum IP treaty or updated patent laws for quantum algorithms, our contracts should adapt (this likely means updating contract templates or adding policy rules to handle new categories). Create read-only replicas or analytics portals for third parties: e.g. regulators or auditors might get a special node or API to read ledger data without compromising security[194][195], ensure that’s available and easy to use (could just be an export tool with cryptographic proofs to verify integrity without giving full ledger access). Achieve any remaining compliance certifications that have emerged (maybe by now some quantum-specific standards exist – we’d be first to comply). Work on making the trust fabric so reliable it’s boring: e.g. refine update processes so even major upgrades happen with no downtime (blue-green deployments of ledger nodes, etc.)[181][182]. Essentially treat it like critical infrastructure akin to core internet protocols – slow, careful changes with lots of testing, and mostly keeping it running and scaling.
 Prerequisites: Governance widely adopted from prior era. Possibly a foundation or governance board now exists – we coordinate changes through that (like how Internet RFCs evolve – our ledger might have formal improvement proposals). So a pre-req is that our governance tech is accepted enough that we can’t just change it arbitrarily; backward compatibility matters.
 DK/SLO Targets: Emphasize data durability and continuity – e.g. guarantee no data loss or perhaps even claim something like “ledger durability 12 nines” (like cloud storage claims) meaning practically impossible to lose data due to our replication and backup strategies[179][180]. Also emphasize upgrade safety – no downtime or inconsistency on updates (we might set a goal of being able to do rolling upgrades with zero ledger write interruptions). Possibly commit to external transparency: e.g. a public status or audit feed that shows the health of the trust network in real-time. Otherwise, maintain previous SLOs, maybe boasting that even at 100× the volume, append latency and query times remain low due to our scaling improvements.
 Exit Criteria: The governance plane runs in a steady-state at internet scale. That means: dozens or more organizations, millions of transactions, and it’s just working with minimal fuss. We might decide by end of Era 4 that the trust fabric is essentially “done” feature-wise and moves into long-term sustainment mode (like how TCP/IP hasn’t fundamentally changed in decades). Possibly the core team reduces involvement except for oversight, with maybe a dedicated ops team or foundation maintaining it. From a business perspective, this could be when we shift our focus to higher layers (discovery, new applications) as governance yields diminishing returns aside from keeping it compliant and up. Exit Era 4 when any significant governance concerns for quantum computing are addressed or a clear plan: e.g. if new IP issues arise, our framework can handle them; if not, it quietly logs everything and everyone trusts it. Also by then, if any competitor solutions exist, either they have merged with ours or become irrelevant. Essentially, A4Q governance either becomes the de facto public utility (our ideal outcome) or we have successfully integrated to coexist such that our customers stay within our ecosystem regardless.
●	Era 4 Item I3: Unified Hybrid Quantum-Classical Workflows
 Plane(s): Cross (Discovery & Autonomy)
 Major Deliverables: By 2031-33, quantum computing likely coexists with classical HPC for complex workflows. A4Q should fully support hybrid workloads: e.g. an end-to-end pipeline where classical compute (maybe an AI or an optimization routine) interweaves with quantum subroutines. Deliver integrations or modules so that A4Q orchestrator can manage not just quantum jobs but also coordinate classical tasks around them. Possibly integrate with established orchestration frameworks like Kubernetes, Slurm or cloud serverless platforms, bridging quantum jobs into those systems. For example, implement a plugin where A4Q can be invoked as a resource scheduler inside a larger HPC job or vice versa. The deliverable could be something like an A4Q-HPC Bridge container that sites can deploy to allow HPC clusters to offload parts of tasks to A4Q and wait for results, with trust fabric ensuring provenance through the whole pipeline. Also incorporate classical data in governance: ensure our ledger can link classical dataset references and quantum results, providing a holistic audit (so one can trace final result back through classical and quantum steps). On the Discovery side, allow classical compute suggestions too – e.g. our AI might suggest classical pre/post processing improvements along with quantum circuits (embedding Discovery in hybrid sense). Essentially break the silos: A4Q becomes part of the general compute fabric, not an isolated quantum island.
 Prerequisites: A4Q matured in its own domain first. By now, likely user demand or partnerships with HPC centers drives this (maybe some Era 3 pilots with national labs combining HPC + quantum). Possibly need to implement chunking or streaming if quantum tasks become subprocesses of larger computations (like feed data iteratively).
 DK/SLO Targets: New metric could be end-to-end workflow latency or success – ensure adding quantum via A4Q doesn’t become a bottleneck in larger pipelines. Possibly commit that our overhead for orchestration in hybrid scenarios is minimal (like if HPC job calls a quantum job through us, we add only say 5% overhead vs. direct usage – and that overhead buys them governance and integration). Continue to ensure security across domains: e.g. if passing data between HPC and quantum, no leaks or trust breaks. Track how often our decisions incorporate classical context (maybe an internal metric: e.g. our scheduler defers a quantum job because it knows a classical pre-computation isn’t done – measure such synchronization correctness).
 Exit Criteria: At least one high-profile demonstration of a complex hybrid workflow (maybe solving a grand challenge) executed smoothly with A4Q coordinating the quantum parts. For instance, a climate modeling simulation (classical HPC) that calls a quantum subroutine for a specific chemistry calc, runs via A4Q, returns and continues – all orchestrated seamlessly. Achieving that and having the involved parties trust and adopt this approach is success. It means our platform has transcended pure quantum and became a part of the broader computing ecosystem. Another exit marker: if major cloud providers incorporate A4Q as the default way their users access quantum through their cloud pipelines (for compliance or ease), then we’ve basically become embedded in the infrastructure. By Era 4’s end, the line between quantum and classical computing management should blur – with A4Q (or its protocols) enabling that fusion. This sets up the final era where quantum computing is mainstream and integrated with everything, and our role is to keep it running and push any last frontier (like autonomous science).
Era 5 (Years 8–10, 2033–2035) – Full Autonomy and Industry Standardization
 Summary: The final era envisions A4Q’s platform reaching a capstone maturity where it is the default quantum operating environment, and potentially shifting focus from growth to sustaining a long-term presence or exit strategy. Autonomy: Attain full “closed-loop” autonomy – quantum systems run with almost no human intervention beyond setting objectives. The platform might achieve a “12 nines” reliability in some components and be capable of handling multi-day, highly complex computations automatically. At this point, we might even allow the system to handle decisions like scheduling experiments for optimization or calibration without being asked (self-driving operations). Governance: By Year 10, A4Q’s governance plane should be an industry staple akin to TLS for the internet[196]. Possibly we see regulatory endorsement (maybe governments require quantum compute to use provenance solutions like A4Q for critical tasks). The plane moves into an oversight role – ensuring all new developments (like if new cryptographic threats or new legal requirements emerge) are promptly addressed, but otherwise minimal changes. Discovery: If successful, the Discovery plane becomes a major innovation driver: perhaps routinely discovering better algorithms than human experts, and possibly integrated into research cycles (labs using it to co-design algorithms and hardware). If not, it may be a niche tool or have pivoted to a collaborative model (maybe A4Q Discovery turned into an open platform where many contribute models/ideas). Business/Strategy: Year 10 likely marks a transition: either preparing for large-scale commercialization beyond early adopters (if quantum is by then widely adopted, A4Q could become a large enterprise or acquired by a bigger player to scale further), or, if the tech stabilized, consolidating everything into a robust “Quantum Data Center Kit” product for the long haul. The final state should be that A4Q’s decade of work resulted in a moat – either in data, standards, or network effects – that secures its role in the quantum ecosystem for the foreseeable future.
●	Era 5 Item I1: End-to-End Autonomous Quantum Operations
 Plane(s): Autonomy (with Discovery assist)
 Major Deliverables: Demonstrate truly fully autonomous operation of a quantum computing facility or service for an extended period (say, months) with zero unscheduled downtime and zero manual tuning. The orchestrator (now merged with FTAE logic completely) should be able to start from a fresh day, calibrate all qubits, run user workloads, handle any errors, optimize scheduling, and maintain itself (like applying any software patches in a rolling fashion) without human intervention. We might call this the “lights-out” quantum data center. It likely requires a finishing touch of coupling with Discovery: e.g. the system might automatically try new error correction strategies at runtime if it detects performance degrading, essentially learning and adapting on the fly (closed-loop learning)[126][127]. Also incorporate digital twin concepts: maintain a simulated model of the quantum system that the autonomy engine uses to test out adjustments virtually before applying (minimizing risk). Achieve near real-time anomaly response (sub-second) and predictive maintenance (system fixes issues before they cause downtime). At this stage, human role shifts to just specifying high-level goals/policies and reviewing audit logs; the system runs quantum ops at peak efficiency on its own.
 Prerequisites: Era 4’s optimizations complete and stable. Possibly require advanced AI (maybe integration of next-gen AI to help manage complexity). All components (orchestrator, calibrator, anomaly detector, QAGF) working seamlessly together.
 DK/SLO Targets: The ultimate stretch goals – e.g. Uptime 99.999%+ (a downtime of minutes per year or less, essentially as reliable as best cloud infrastructure)[146][147]. Perhaps a target of X days of continuous autonomous operation metric (e.g. “system ran 100 days straight handling diverse jobs with no human help”). Efficiency at maximum, etc. At this point, any further improvement might be beyond diminishing returns. Possibly our metrics transition to more business-level (like cost optimization, user satisfaction) since technical SLOs are all green.
 Exit Criteria: Achieve a milestone like “Year 10 demo: A4Q manages a complex quantum computing workload for 30 days fully autonomously, with all SLOs met and improved upon without human touch.” This would be akin to proving the concept of a self-driving quantum computer environment. Another criterion: key stakeholders (labs, companies) trust the system enough that they operate in autopilot mode routinely – we have references saying “We let A4Q run our quantum computing cluster and it has performed flawlessly, better than our team could.” Essentially, exit when autonomy is no longer a risk but an expectation – new quantum facilities would not consider manual operation feasible or cost-effective, they’d go straight to an A4Q-like solution. This cements our original vision of AI-managed quantum systems.
●	Era 5 Item I2: Quantum Governance as Public Infrastructure
 Plane(s): Governance
 Major Deliverables: By the end of Year 10, formalize the status of the trust fabric as the industry standard (if not already fully achieved in Era 4). This could involve transferring full governance of it to an international standards body or foundation (if that adds credibility and longevity). Ensure any remaining intellectual property issues are resolved – e.g. if our watermarking or ledger have patents, either widely license them or move them under a foundation so that the standard can live on and not be encumbered (since by now our moat is more in integration and ecosystem than the IP itself). Possibly see government regulation incorporate our framework: e.g. a law might say “Quantum cloud providers must maintain an audit trail meeting criteria X” which align with what our trust fabric does – effectively, our design becomes required compliance (that’s a huge moat if so). Technical deliverables might be final security hardening for the very long term: e.g. if by Year 10 we worry about quantum computers attacking our PQC, maybe we update crypto again or incorporate quantum-proof hardware enclaves as mentioned[192][193]. But largely, focus on making sure the governance plane can run with minimal involvement indefinitely (maybe by now our company’s direct involvement is minimal, if an open community drives it). Possibly an LTS release v6.0 that is meant to be stable for many years with only minor updates.
 Prerequisites: Achievements in Era 3/4, plus likely collaboration with policy makers and standards communities. Also ensure business alignment: if our revenue depended on governance services, we might shift to support/maintenance model as it commoditizes.
 DK/SLO Targets: Maintain highest integrity and availability as earlier. Possibly define an official SLA or legal standard around it. E.g., commit to an annual external audit of the trust network to certify it (like how financial systems get audited) – by Year 10, perhaps the trust fabric has gone through real audits by external regulators and passed, which might become an ongoing requirement (so incorporate that as part of operations).
 Exit Criteria: The quantum IP provenance and governance problem is “solved” to the satisfaction of industry and government, with A4Q’s solution at the core of that. Evidence: e.g. a majority of quantum transactions globally are recorded through a mechanism derived from or compatible with A4Q’s ledger; or an industry consortium (with all big players) continuously operates our trust network. Also, that this system has survived the test of time – perhaps by Year 10 there was an attempt at a security breach or a major legal case that used our audit trail, and the system held up, proving its worth and durability. Exit when we can confidently claim that the trust fabric will continue as the backbone of quantum computing trust beyond our direct involvement – in other words, a legacy that persists (which is good for the world, and for our moat if we remain a key maintainer or service provider around it).
●	Era 5 Item I3: A4Q Ecosystem & Moat Consolidation
 Plane(s): Cross-Cutting (Business/Ecosystem)
 Major Deliverables: Solidify any remaining competitive advantages and lock-in. If not already, by Year 10 gather and leverage the data moat: a decade of operation yields unique datasets (performance stats, failure modes, algorithm improvements) – we can use that to continuously improve and also to train AI models that perhaps no competitor can match (like our Discovery plane models become best-in-class due to data). Possibly publish selective insights (anonymized) to position us as thought leaders (e.g. annual “Quantum Computing Report” based on aggregate usage data). Expand the partner ecosystem: if others built tools on top of our APIs, ensure that’s thriving – maybe app stores or certified plugins by third parties (some might provide specialized scheduling policies or domain-specific circuit libraries that integrate with A4Q). In terms of product, possibly unify everything into a single cohesive platform offering known as something like “Quantum Data Center OS 1.0” (or as per docs, maybe call it Quantum Data Center Kit around v6.0)[197][198] – which might even mark a strategic shift like making parts open source to drive adoption while selling enterprise features/support, etc. Essentially, make it so that building a quantum data center or service without A4Q is reinventing the wheel foolishly – i.e. our platform becomes the obvious foundation (like Linux for servers). From a business standpoint, prepare for either IPO or acquisition or self-sustaining profitability – whichever path chosen, ensure the technical state supports it (e.g. if IPO, highlight how we own standards and network effects; if acquisition, our tech is cleanly integrable; if independent profit, ensure operational efficiency).
 Prerequisites: All technical goals largely met, broad usage achieved. This item is about strategy and polish rather than big new tech, leveraging what’s built.
 DK/SLO Targets: Not many new technical SLOs – mostly maintain excellence. Possibly incorporate user-level SLOs: by now we could commit to customers certain outcomes, e.g. “job success rate ≥ X” or “turnaround time ≤ Y for certain workloads” due to our confidence. But these are more SLA/business terms built on our proven DKs.
 Exit Criteria: At Year 10, Invarian (or A4Q platform) stands as a clear leader in quantum software infrastructure, with either a strong independent business or merged into a larger one but recognized as the core of that segment. We consider the 10-year execution program complete: all major technical risks mitigated, the product-market fit achieved (customers rely on it), and a moat established (through data, standards, and network effect of partners using the system). Essentially, we end Era 5 when there’s no longer a question of “will this approach succeed?” – it has succeeded, and the remaining work is incremental improvement and scaling with the growth of the quantum industry. At this point, if the question was de-risking and capturing TAM, we have either captured it or positioned to, and could even think of adjacent expansions (maybe applying our trust or orchestration to other domains). The final snapshot would be that A4Q’s legacy – reliable autonomous quantum operations with provable trust – is firmly in place, fulfilling the vision that started a decade prior.
●	Partner-Facing Narrative (Ecosystem & Integration Story)
 For cloud providers, QPU vendors, research labs, and enterprise platform partners, the A4Q roadmap translates into a collaborative growth story. In the near term (Years 0–2), our platform enables immediate wins for partners by overlaying trust and efficiency on existing quantum offerings. What this enables for you in Years 0–2:
●	Seamless Integration with Your Stack Today: We provide drop-in adapters (QIR/OpenQASM interfaces, gRPC APIs) so you can connect your quantum hardware or cloud service to A4Q’s orchestrator with minimal friction[84]. In practice, this means you can offer your users advanced job scheduling, cross-platform IR compatibility, and watermark-based IP protection on top of your existing SDKs[7]. We’ve done the heavy lifting on containerization and Helm deploys, so A4Q can run alongside your environment (or in your cluster) – effectively an add-on quantum OS layer that elevates your hardware with governance and autonomy features out-of-the-box. For example, within months we can integrate an IonQ or IBM QPU into our scheduler, enabling multi-provider job orchestration that you can showcase to customers as “cloud-agnostic quantum processing” with trust guarantees.
·         Enhanced Offerings for Your Customers: By partnering early, you can differentiate your quantum service with A4Q’s capabilities. Discovery Plane benefit: if you’re a QPU vendor, our Quantum Asset Generative Foundry can start generating error-correcting code tweaks or novel circuits tuned to your hardware – giving you and your users a pipeline of better-performing algorithms specific to your device[117]. Governance benefit: if you’re a cloud provider or lab, using A4Q’s trust fabric means you can assure enterprise customers that their IP and compliance needs are met – every circuit submission is sealed, signed, and audited across jurisdictions[22][51]. This is something you can co-market: e.g. “Quantum jobs on [YourCloud] are protected by Invarian’s provenance ledger – providing IP traceability and post-quantum security from day one.” It reduces barriers for conservative clients (finance, government) to start using your quantum offerings.
·         Pilot Collaborations and Co-Development: In years 0–2, we prefer working closely with a handful of design partners. For you, this means bespoke attention and influence. We will set up joint pilot deployments (say in your lab or a sandbox environment) where our engineers and your team co-tune the system to your needs. You get early access to features (like the carbon-aware scheduler, calibration automation) that can help showcase new angles of your platform (e.g. running more eco-friendly quantum computations, or achieving higher uptime through automated failovers)[55][38]. We invite partners to steer our roadmap too – if you need a certain policy in the trust engine or support for a unique hardware event stream, we’ll prioritize it. Essentially, in years 0–2 you’re not just adopting a product, you’re helping shape an ecosystem tool to fit your business.
·         Minimal Disruption, Maximum Proof: Importantly, our approach in the first 2 years is overlay, not overhaul. We don’t ask you to abandon your existing software – A4Q wraps around it. For example, your users can keep using your portal or SDK; A4Q’s CLI/SDK can work behind the scenes or in parallel, funneling jobs through our orchestrator which then calls your APIs[199][14]. This means early partnership is low-risk: we demonstrate value quickly in a contained manner (perhaps routing a subset of jobs through A4Q for a side-by-side comparison). You get data on improvements and can decide on deeper integration over time.
Looking **mid-term (Years 2# 0. Orientation Snapshot (Very Short)
●	Three-Plane Product Pillars: Discovery, Governance, and Autonomy are the three interdependent planes of the A4Q platform. Each corresponds to a product pillar: Discovery (QAGF/QDS) – an AI-driven Quantum Asset Generative Foundry for circuits and error-correcting codes; Governance (QIPL/QTF) – a secure Quantum IP Provenance Ledger (trust fabric) for watermarking, compliance, and multi-cloud IP enforcement; Autonomy (FTAE/AQO) – a Fault-Tolerant Autonomy Engine providing adaptive orchestration of quantum workloads (scheduling, error management, resource optimization).
●	Unified Monorepo & Core Services: The platform is built as a single monorepo with modular components aligned to these planes[3]. Key directories include /services (e.g. trust-fabric, orchestrator), /platform (e.g. compiler qir-adapters, watermark-pass), /ml (e.g. autocal-rl calibrator, qec-decoder), plus /infra (Helm charts, Docker Compose), /ops (observability and ops tools), and /sdk (TypeScript CLI and client SDK)[3][13]. This structure was established by the V8 GA release as the baseline blueprint.
●	Trust Fabric Service (Governance Plane): A Go-based microservice implementing the “trust contract engine” and provenance ledger[4]. It exposes APIs like /v1/seal (seal jobs with a watermark and policy), /v1/append (append to immutable ledger), /v1/verify (verify watermark signatures), and supports deterministic replay of job records. It enforces region-specific and privacy policies (via OPA/Rego), uses post-quantum crypto (PQC) for encryption/signing, and embeds cross-boundary watermarks into circuits at submission time for IP tracing. By GA, its ledger uses Postgres in active/active HA mode across regions.
●	Orchestrator Service (Autonomy Plane): A Go-based policy-aware scheduler that manages the job lifecycle – receiving /v1/submit requests, queueing and routing jobs to available quantum backends based on cost, queue length, and carbon intensity of data centers (for ESG optimization). It provides /v1/status for job state, handles dynamic resource allocation, and tracks uptime and failover. In the GA (V8) architecture, the orchestrator runs in an active/active multi-region setup, achieving fast failovers (zone restart <1 s, regional failover <60 s) to meet high availability requirements[6].
●	Compiler & Watermarking Pipeline: A Rust-based compiler (in /platform) with QIR and OpenQASM3 front-ends translates high-level circuits into a portable IR. A custom watermark-pass injects invisible watermarks (e.g. slight gate angle biases) into circuits for IP provenance. The compiler also computes Δdepth (circuit depth overhead) to ensure watermarking stays within a 0.5% depth budget[8]. By design, the watermark has a true-positive detection rate ≥99% with negligible performance impact. This pipeline ensures multi-hardware compatibility while tagging each quantum job with traceable metadata.
●	ML/Control Toolkit (Discovery/Autonomy): Under /ml, the platform includes experimental machine-learning tools such as AutoCal-RL (an automated calibration loop using reinforcement learning for tuning qubits) and a QEC Decoder for error syndrome decoding[9]. These Python/Rust components run in simulation or lab environments (digital twins) to optimize performance and reliability, but are not in the critical production path as of GA. They lay the groundwork for the Discovery plane’s AI-driven optimization and for future autonomous control features in the Autonomy plane.
●	Infrastructure, Deployment & Observability: The /infra directory contains deployment config (Docker Compose for local dev, Helm charts for K8s) and IaC scripts, while /ops/observability provides telemetry and monitoring setup[10]. By V8, the entire stack runs in containers with Kubernetes support, and each service exports structured metrics covering all key SLO invariants (latency histograms, success ratios, etc.)[11]. A Prometheus/Grafana monitoring stack and alerting dashboards are in place to track SLO compliance in real time (e.g. alert if ledger append latency or watermark TPR deviates from the budget)[12]. This ensures first-class observability for reliability and performance.
●	SDK and CLI: A TypeScript CLI (a4qctl) and SDK in /sdk give users and partners access to platform functions[13]. Through CLI commands (e.g. a4qctl submit <circuit>), users can submit quantum jobs, attach trust contracts (specifying IP and compliance requirements), and query job results/status. The CLI communicates with platform services over gRPC/HTTP with mutual TLS and PQC-protected headers[14], aligning with post-quantum security standards. This developer interface enables integration of A4Q into external workflows and testbeds from day one.
●	Design Key Invariants (DK SLOs): The architecture is governed by ten numeric DK invariants – essentially non-negotiable SLO targets encoded as constants in the code and CI pipeline[15]. These include p95 latencies (e.g. compile ≤ 250 ms, ledger append ≤ 120 ms, PQC crypto overhead ≤ 20 ms), accuracy and reliability metrics (e.g. watermark TPR ≥ 0.99 with ≤0.5% depth overhead, audit log coverage ≥ 99%, job success ≥ 92%), and operational targets (e.g. system uptime ≥ 99.5%, recovery < 60 s, carbon cost ≤ 55 gCO₂/job)[45]. All 10/10 DK invariants were met or exceeded by the V8 GA baseline (for example, actual compile p95 ~47 ms and watermark detection TPR ~1.0 were demonstrated)[17]. These invariants are enforced via fail-closed CI gates and must pass in staging environments before any release is promoted[16]. The strict SLO discipline (with even tighter “Ops Gold” stretch goals for extra safety margins) provides a foundation of trust and performance upon which the 10-year roadmap is built.
●	10-Year Integration Plan (Eras): Building on the GA baseline (labeled ~v1.1), the Invarian V10 roadmap defines five eras in 2-year increments from late 2025 to 2035. Each era (0–2, 2–4, 4–6, 6–8, 8–10 years) corresponds to a major release train with specific focus per plane. For example, Era 0–2 (2025–27) is Post-GA stabilization leading to v2.0 with pilot deployments; Era 2–4 (2027–29) emphasizes scaling up and early ecosystem integration; Era 4–6 (2029–31) targets fault-tolerance readiness and broad adoption; Era 6–8 (2031–33) focuses on optimization and expansion of the mature platform; and Era 8–10 (2033–35) achieves full autonomy and industry standardization as the capstone[200]. Each era has well-defined goals, new capabilities, tightened SLOs, and “go/kill” decision checkpoints to ensure evidence-based progress. This phased roadmap will guide the execution program outlined next.

0. Orientation Snapshot (Very Short)
·         Three-Plane Product Pillars: Discovery, Governance, and Autonomy are the three core planes of the Invarian/A4Q platform. Each plane corresponds to a product pillar and target application: Discovery (QAGF/QDS) provides an AI-driven Quantum Asset Generative Foundry for designing quantum circuits and error-correcting codes; Governance (QIPL/QTF) provides a secure Quantum IP Provenance Ledger (the “trust fabric”) for watermarking, compliance, and cross-organization IP management; Autonomy (FTAE/AQO) acts as a Fault-Tolerant Autonomy Engine for orchestrating quantum workloads – handling scheduling, error management, carbon-aware optimizations, and failover for future fault-tolerant quantum computers.
·         Unified Monorepo & Core Modules: The entire platform is developed in a single monorepo, with directories aligned to the planes. Key components include services/ (microservices like trust-fabric for the provenance ledger and policy engine, and orchestrator for job scheduling/execution), platform/ (compiler integration such as qir-adapters and the watermark-pass for embedding watermarks in quantum circuits), ml/ (machine learning tools like autocal-rl for automated calibration and qec-decoder for error-correction decoding), sdk/ (e.g. a TypeScript CLI/SDK for developers to submit jobs and interact with the system), infra/ (Helm charts, Docker Compose configs for deployment), ops/ (observability, metrics dashboards, and ops scripts), and standards/ (design docs and compliance specs). This modular layout (established in the V8 GA baseline) is the single source of truth for both code and design, enabling transparent tracking of changes.
·         Design Key Invariants (DK SLOs): Ten numerical invariants define non-negotiable SLO targets across performance, accuracy, and operations. For example, p95 compile latency ≤ 250 ms, ledger append latency ≤ 120 ms, PQC crypto overhead ≤ 20 ms, watermark detection True Positive Rate ≥ 99% (with ≤0.5% circuit depth overhead), audit log coverage ≥ 99%, job success rate ≥ 92%, system uptime ≥ 99.5%, recovery time < 60 s, carbon cost per job ≤ 55 gCO₂[1]. The V8 GA baseline (internal version ~v1.1) met or exceeded 10/10 of these invariants – e.g. achieving ~47 ms compile p95 and ~100% watermark detection in tests[2]. These invariants are hard-coded in the system and enforced via fail-closed CI gates; every code change or release must demonstrate (in staging) that these SLOs are upheld, providing a foundation of performance and reliability for all future work[3].
·         V8 GA Baseline and “Ops Gold” Discipline: The current baseline (V8, considered GA) is a production-ready prototype that proved out core functionality and DK SLOs. “Ops Gold” stretch targets (even tighter than the DK invariants, e.g. aiming for ~10% lower latency on key metrics) have been defined to add safety margins[4]. The team uses an evidence-based ops discipline: every test run produces artifacts (logs, JSON metrics snapshots with SHA-256 hashes) and any SLO regression blocks promotion[5]. This rigorous approach ensures that new features or integrations never compromise baseline security, performance, or reliability – any expansion must inherit the invariant checks.
·         10-Year Roadmap & Release Eras: The Invarian V10 plan lays out a decade-long roadmap divided into five eras of ~2 years each[6]. Each era corresponds to a major version milestone and focuses on advancing each plane in turn. For example, Era 1 (0–2 yrs, 2025–2027): Post-GA stabilization and pilot deployments leading to a v2.0 release; Era 2 (2–4 yrs, 2027–2029): scaling up to multi-organization use and early ecosystem integration (e.g. multi-tenant governance and initial QAGF beta features); Era 3 (4–6 yrs, 2029–2031): achieving fault-tolerance readiness and broader adoption (e.g. first FTQC integrations, governance standards adoption, Discovery plane GA); Era 4 (6–8 yrs, 2031–2033): optimization at scale and ecosystem expansion (maximizing performance, hybrid quantum-classical workflows, platform as an industry staple); Era 5 (8–10 yrs, 2033–2035): full autonomy and industry standardization (completely self-driving quantum operations and trust fabric as public infrastructure)[6][7]. Each era has explicit goals, tightened SLO targets, and Go/Kill decision checkpoints to ensure we only proceed when evidence shows milestones are met.
·         Execution Program & Evidence-Driven Checkpoints: The roadmap execution is managed via explicit phased plans and checklists. The next 24 months (Era 1) are broken into six 4-month phases, each with concrete tasks and deliverables (see Section 1). Each task follows a structured template (Owner, Timebox, Steps, Acceptance criteria, etc.), often reusing the format of prior Ops Readiness Checklists (“Section/Purpose/Precondition/Procedure/Verify/Acceptance”) to ensure every change is verifiable. Bolded tasks in the 0–24 month plan denote mission-critical items required for the initial production launch. By the end of Phase 6 (24 months), the goal is to have a credible v1.0.0 production release that can be deployed in external environments with all DK invariants observable in real time and all previously “simulated” components replaced with real integrations. Subsequent eras (Section 2) then build on this base, with each era concluding in a major release and an evidence-based review before scaling up. In parallel, all design decisions and evidence artifacts are recorded in an append-only manner (monorepo design docs and audit logs) to maintain an immutable trail of progress. This execution philosophy – plan in measured phases, enforce invariants, produce evidence at each step – will guide the program through the entire 10-year journey.
1. 0–24 Month Execution Program (Phase-by-Phase Checklists)
Below we break down the first two years post-GA into six 4-month phases. Each phase has specific goals and a set of actionable task blocks following the structured format (Task ID, Name, Plane, Packages, etc.). Bold task names indicate work that is critical for achieving a v1.0 production-ready system by the end of Phase 6. (All tasks are important, but non-bold tasks can be considered supporting or exploratory). Each task produces concrete evidence – from passing test logs to deployed features – tying directly into the DK invariants. By the end of Phase 6 (month 24), we expect to have all core features hardened (no stubs), the full deployment pipeline in place, and a pilot deployment running in a real (partner) environment with full observability of the DK metrics.
Phase 1 (Months 0–4) – Post-GA Hardening & DevOps Foundation
Phase Goals (0–4 mo): Establish a robust development/deployment baseline and eliminate any remaining shortcuts from the GA prototype. Key objectives include: (1) Real-Mode Security – integrate real Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) key management (KMS/HSM) and disable any “simulation mode” or insecure dev bypasses in the codebase; (2) DK Metrics & Telemetry – instrument the system to measure all DK invariants in runtime and expose them via unified /metrics endpoints for monitoring; (3) Deployment Pipeline – implement one-click deployment for both local (Docker Compose) and cluster (Helm/Kubernetes) environments, with basic observability baked in; (4) Stability Testing – conduct initial chaos tests and load tests (especially focusing on uptime and recovery invariants) to validate that the system meets reliability specs under failure scenarios. This phase is about “grounding” the prototype: ensuring the system runs in a realistic environment with no dummy components.
·         Task ID: P1-T1 – Name: Freeze DK Constants & Enforce “Real Mode” Defaults
·         Plane: Cross-Cutting (applies to all services)
·         Package(s): services/common/constants.go, global config/env
·         Type: PROD-REQUIRED
·         Owner: PMO Lead (with SRE support)
·         Timebox: 2 weeks (Month 0–1)
·         Dependencies: GA codebase (V8) available; CI pipeline operational for baseline tests
·         Steps:
a.      Define all Design Key invariant values as constants in a common module (if not already), e.g. CompileP95Millis = 250, LedgerAppendP95 = 120, etc., and include “Ops Gold” stretch values (e.g. STRETCH_CompileP95 = 230 ms).
b.     Audit configuration files and environment flags for any simulation or dev-only toggles (e.g. an A4Q_SIM mode or hard-coded test keys). Remove or default-disable any “backdoor” settings. The system should run in full real mode by default (require an explicit, internal-only override if simulation is ever needed for testing).
c.      Update service startup routines to log the loaded DK constant values on launch (a banner listing all invariants and their budgets) to clearly indicate the system’s operating SLO assumptions in any environment.
d.     Implement a CI check (or static code scan) that fails if prohibited dev patterns are present – e.g. any reference to fake keys, dummy backends, or SIMULATION flags in the production code. This ensures no code can accidentally ship with a simulation fallback.
e.      Run the full test suite with the new “real-mode only” configuration. Update tests that assumed relaxed settings or used internal simulation hooks. All tests should now pass with real-mode defaults (or be adjusted to use allowed test interfaces).
·         Acceptance / Evidence:
o   The services/common/constants.go (or equivalent) contains all 10 DK invariant definitions with the correct target values, and these are referenced throughout the code instead of any hardcoded numbers. Unit tests confirm each service picks up the intended constants.
o   Running the platform with default settings shows that any attempt to enable a simulation mode or use test credentials is either ignored or explicitly blocked. For example, if a developer tries to start the orchestrator with A4Q_SIM=1, the service should refuse to start or log an error and proceed with real mode.
o   The CI pipeline includes a static analysis step that greps for forbidden dev code (e.g. "//SIMULATION-ONLY" markers or usage of stub classes) and fails the build if any are found. A sample CI log (saved as an artifact) shows the static check passing once all such code is removed.
o   An artifact (e.g. _artifacts/run_header.json) from a test deployment captures the startup banner with all DK constants listed and a SHA-256 hash of this configuration, demonstrating immutability of SLO settings. This artifact is stored for audit, proving that the system is running with the intended invariant values.
●	Notes/Risks: This foundational task may break developer workflows that relied on simulation; to mitigate, provide an alternative approach for local testing (e.g. a “dev profile” that uses a local quantum simulator behind the scenes, but still goes through the real code paths). The outcome is a codebase locked to real-world assumptions – a necessary zero-trust posture to ensure all further testing and development reflect production realities.
●	Task ID: P1-T2 – Name: Instrumentation of DK Metrics & Observability Baseline
·         Plane: Cross-Cutting (All services)
·         Package(s): services/common/otel.go, ops/observability, infra/compose
·         Type: PROD-REQUIRED
·         Owner: SRE / Observability Lead
·         Timebox: 4 weeks (Month 1–2)
·         Dependencies: P1-T1 completed (invariants constants in place); basic services running in local dev environment
·         Steps:
a.      Implement metric collectors in each core service to track every DK invariant in real-time. For example: in the Trust Fabric service, record a histogram metric a4q_ledger_append_ms for ledger append latency; in the Orchestrator, track a4q_recovery_seconds for failover recovery time; include gauges or counters for watermark detection rate, job success count/%, etc. Use consistent naming (prefix all metrics with a4q_ and include units in metric descriptions).
b.     Expose a Prometheus-compatible /metrics HTTP endpoint on each service (if not already) and verify that all the new metrics are being reported. Start the full stack via Docker Compose (trust-fabric + orchestrator + dependencies like Postgres) and confirm that hitting /metrics on each service returns metrics including the new DK invariants (they may be zero when idle).
c.      Develop a basic load generator script (or extend the CLI) to simulate activity and produce non-zero metrics. For example, submit 100 dummy jobs via the orchestrator (pointing it to a trivial QPU simulator) to accumulate data for compile latency, queue wait times, success rates, etc. Ensure watermarking is invoked on these jobs so that watermark TPR metrics get exercised.
d.     Set up a local Prometheus instance (as part of infra/compose or using a container) to scrape the services’ /metrics endpoints. Then create a Grafana dashboard (JSON definition) focusing on the DK metrics: show p95 latency lines for compile and ledger, success percentage, watermark TPR, carbon per job, etc., with threshold markers at the SLO targets.
e.      Implement basic alerting rules for critical metrics breaching their thresholds (can be just logging an alert for now). For example, configure an alert if a4q_wm_tpr drops below 0.99 or if a4q_job_carbon_gco2 exceeds 55 gCO₂ for any job. This sets the stage for 24/7 monitoring in later phases.
f.        Document the new observability features in the repo’s Ops README: list each metric name, its meaning, and the target value (DK SLO). Provide instructions on how to run Prometheus/Grafana with the Compose setup to visualize the metrics.
·         Acceptance / Evidence:
o   Using GET /metrics on both the Trust Fabric and Orchestrator in a test environment returns a list of metrics that includes entries for all 10 DK invariants. For example, the Trust Fabric’s output shows a4q_ledger_append_ms_bucket and ..._count metrics, a a4q_watermark_tpr gauge, etc., and the Orchestrator’s output shows a4q_uptime (uptime percentage or downtime counter), a4q_recovery_seconds, a4q_job_success_ratio, and a4q_job_carbon_gco2.
o   A sample Grafana dashboard (exported as dashboard_DK_metrics.json and attached as an artifact) displays real-time graphs for each invariant. SLO thresholds are annotated (e.g. a red line at 120 ms on the ledger latency chart). A screenshot or JSON export is saved to the repo to serve as a baseline “operations dashboard.”
o   An _artifacts/metrics_baseline.log (or similar) is captured, showing the system under the load test: it should include entries like “Submitted 100 jobs, compile_p95=XX ms, ledger_append_p95=YY ms, watermark_tpr=1.0, job_success=100%” etc., demonstrating that metrics move and can be collected. All values should be within expected ranges for a lightly loaded test (e.g. compile p95 might be 50–100 ms in this scenario, well under the 250 ms budget).
o   An example alert is triggered in a controlled way (e.g. temporarily introduce a 200 ms delay in ledger writes to cause ledger_append_ms to breach 120 ms). The system should log an alert or event. Evidence of this (alert log or notification in the console) is captured, proving that the monitoring is active.
●	Notes/Risks: Care must be taken that adding metrics does not itself degrade performance (especially in tight loops like the compiler or ledger write). Use asynchronous or sampling approaches if needed. This task is crucial: “you can’t manage what you don’t measure.” By the end of Phase 1, we gain full visibility into system health, which will be essential for confidently ramping up in later phases. Potential risk is that metric instrumentation reveals inconsistencies or flakiness in the prototype – which is precisely the point; we will address any such findings in subsequent tasks.
●	Task ID: P1-T3 – Name: Deployable Stack – Docker Compose & Helm Charts v1
·         Plane: Cross-Cutting (Infra/Ops)
·         Package(s): infra/compose, infra/helm, CI/CD pipeline
·         Type: PROD-REQUIRED
·         Owner: DevOps Engineer
·         Timebox: 4 weeks (Month 2–3)
·         Dependencies: P1-T2 (metrics/observability) ideally finished so that deployment includes monitoring; availability of a Postgres service for the ledger
·         Steps:
a.      Finalize a Docker Compose configuration for local deployment. Define services for the Trust Fabric, Orchestrator, a Postgres database (for the ledger), and auxiliary components like Prometheus and Grafana (if not heavy, include them in the dev stack). Test that a developer can bring up the entire system with docker-compose up and all services reach a healthy state. Use the CLI or API to run a sample job through the orchestrator (pointing to a dummy QPU backend) to verify the end-to-end flow in the compose environment.
b.     Develop Helm charts (or Kustomize manifests) for deploying A4Q to a Kubernetes cluster. This includes charts for the core services: a Deployment for trust-fabric (with configMap or Secret for settings like DK constants, PQC keys, etc.), a Deployment for orchestrator, a StatefulSet for Postgres (with persistent volume), and Services for each API. Incorporate the monitoring setup: e.g. include Prometheus annotations on pods or a subchart for metrics. Parameterize the charts via values (e.g. the number of replicas for HA, enabling/disabling optional components like ML services that might not run in all envs).
c.      Provide sane defaults in the Helm values such that deploying the chart in a dev cluster (e.g. Minikube or a test cloud cluster) will result in a “mini-prod” environment: e.g. two instances of each service for basic HA, with readiness/liveness probes configured.
d.     Test the Helm chart on a local K8s (KinD or Minikube) or a cloud dev cluster. Iterate until helm install a4q brings up all pods successfully. After deployment, use the A4Q CLI or API to submit a test job and confirm it goes through (similar to the compose test).
e.      Integrate this deployment process into CI: set up a pipeline step that on each merge can package the Helm chart (e.g. a4q-0.1.0.tgz), possibly spin up a lightweight K3s-based cluster in CI or use a kind cluster to do a helm install for smoke testing. At minimum, validate that the templates render correctly (helm template + kubeval) and maybe run helm install --dry-run to catch errors. Also push the Docker images built from the monorepo to a registry (even a local one for testing) so that the Helm chart can pull them.
·         Acceptance / Evidence:
o   Local Compose Up: Provide a short log or script output showing that docker-compose up launched the core services. For example, _artifacts/compose_smoketest.log could show each container starting and a final message like “Submitted test job X... Job X completed on dummy backend.” The /v1/healthz endpoints of orchestrator and trust-fabric return OK in this setup.
o   Helm Deployment: A captured output of kubectl get pods (or similar) after installing the Helm chart, demonstrating that all expected components (orchestrator, trust-fabric, postgres, etc.) are running in a cluster. Additionally, an artifact (like _artifacts/helm_install_output.txt) from a test job submission on the cluster deployment confirms the system works outside of Docker desktop.
o   CI Pipeline: The CI/CD logs include steps for building images and packaging the chart. We should have a screenshot or log snippet of a successful CI run where images are built and a Helm lint/test passes. Possibly publish an artifact or badge in the repo indicating “Deploy infrastructure validated ✅”. For example, _artifacts/ci_deploypipeline_pass.txt summarizing that the deployment config is valid.
o   The Helm chart (and compose file) are versioned and stored (e.g. the chart is checked into repo or in an internal registry) so we have a repeatable deployable package for any environment going forward.
●	Notes/Risks: Running stateful components (like Postgres) in CI or local might require tuning (ephemeral volumes, etc.). Also, in Phase 1 we may not yet have a real QPU integration – the orchestrator might still use a dummy execution service. That’s acceptable here; the goal is to ensure the plumbing is in place. We use a placeholder backend (e.g. a local simulator container) so that orchestrator flows complete. Later phases will replace this with real hardware integration. Achieving this task means any engineer (or automated pipeline) can bring up the A4Q stack in a consistent way – a huge step for developer velocity and eventual customer trials.
●	Task ID: P1-T4 – Name: Chaos & Failover Drill (HA Test)
·         Plane: Autonomy (Resilience)
·         Package(s): services/orchestrator, ops/observability
·         Type: PROD-REQUIRED
·         Owner: SRE Lead
·         Timebox: 2 weeks (Month 3–4)
·         Dependencies: P1-T3 (deployable stack) running on a cluster; baseline metrics from P1-T2 in place (to measure results)
·         Steps:
a.      Define a chaos test plan to specifically validate the uptime and recovery DK invariants. For example: Scenario A: if an orchestrator instance crashes, does a standby instance take over in <1 second (within a single zone)? Scenario B: if an entire region or availability zone goes down (simulated), does the system recover (fail over to a secondary region) in <60 seconds? Outline 2–3 such scenarios.
b.     Configure the environment in an HA setup: e.g. deploy 2 orchestrator instances on separate zones (or simulate zones with taints/affinity in k8s) and set up the Trust Fabric with a primary and standby database (or a replicated Postgres). This mimics a multi-AZ deployment even in a test cluster.
c.      Write a load script to continuously submit jobs (they can be simple dummy jobs) to the orchestrator at a steady rate (e.g. 1 job/sec or as appropriate) and monitor both the /v1/status of submitted jobs and the metrics (especially a4q_uptime and a4q_recovery_seconds). Let it run for a baseline period under normal conditions.
d.     Induce failures according to the test plan: e.g. manually kill the primary orchestrator pod, or pause the primary database to simulate a DB outage. Observe the system’s behavior: the surviving orchestrator instance should take over queued jobs; the Trust Fabric should either fail over to the replica or queue transactions until restored, etc.
e.      Measure the outage and recovery times using the metrics and logs. For instance, measure how long job processing was stalled, and when it resumed. Ensure no data is lost – all jobs should either complete eventually or be marked failed in the ledger with full audit trail.
f.        Tweak configuration if needed (e.g. shorten orchestrator heartbeat intervals or increase DB timeout settings) and repeat the chaos tests until the results consistently meet the targets (≥99.5% uptime over the test window, recovery within 60 s for region failure).
·         Acceptance / Evidence:
o   A test report (e.g. _artifacts/chaos_test_report.json) documenting at least two scenarios with timestamps: (1) Orchestrator instance crash failover – how many seconds until the backup instance picked up new jobs (expect ~1 s or less downtime for intra-zone failover); (2) Simulated region outage – how long until the secondary site took over (target <60 s). For each scenario, include log extracts or metric graphs that illustrate the failover timeline.
o   Metrics collected during the chaos test indicate that uptime remained within acceptable bounds. For example, the a4q_uptime metric may dip during an induced failure but should not drop below the 99.5% threshold when averaged, and the a4q_recovery_seconds metric for the region outage scenario should report a value <60. A screenshot of the Grafana panel or a JSON export highlighting these metrics during the test can serve as evidence.
o   All jobs that were submitted during the chaos experiments have an outcome recorded in the ledger. This can be verified by querying the Trust Fabric after tests: every job ID either has a completion entry or a failure entry with an appropriate error. No job was lost silently. Additionally, the Trust Fabric’s audit log coverage metric remains ~100% even through the failure (meaning the system continued to log events or caught up once recovered).
o   A brief post-mortem document is produced, noting any weaknesses uncovered (e.g. “During DB failover, ledger append latency spiked to 200 ms for 5 seconds, exceeding 120 ms SLO – will address in Phase 2”). This shows we’ve captured lessons for improvement.
·         Notes/Risks: Introducing chaos in a test environment early is vital to build confidence. There is a risk that failures could corrupt the dev environment (e.g. split-brain if both DBs thought they were primary). Mitigate by using safe configurations (maybe an etcd or Patroni for Postgres if needed). These tests validate our HA design assumptions under stress – a cornerstone for claiming “enterprise-ready.” Any issue found here should result in a ticket for Phase 2 (like adding a queue buffer or improving leader election). By completing this, we have empirical evidence that the system can self-heal and meet its reliability SLOs in a controlled setting.
Phase 2 (Months 4–8) – External Integration & Policy Layer
Phase Goals (4–8 mo): With the foundation in place, begin integrating real external systems and fleshing out governance policies for enterprise use. Key goals: (1) Real Quantum Hardware Integration – replace any remaining dummy/simulator backends with an actual cloud QPU provider API (prove that our orchestrator can submit and retrieve results from at least one real quantum hardware platform); (2) Enterprise Key Management & Ledger Hardening – deploy the Trust Fabric in a multi-region configuration and introduce robust key management (PQC keys via an external Vault or HSM) so that no secret keys reside only in memory or code; also, evaluate a blockchain-based ledger node as a parallel tamper-evident audit trail (consortium readiness); (3) Advanced Policy Enforcement – implement fine-grained governance policies via the trust fabric’s contract engine (e.g. enforce data residency constraints, export control rules, and automatic data deletion/retention policies as required by enterprise compliance); (4) Watermark & Carbon Analytics – build tooling to extract and verify watermarks from quantum results (to ensure IP tracing works end-to-end) and integrate live carbon-intensity data feeds to start scheduling jobs with environmental awareness (laying groundwork for “carbon budget per job” scheduling). By the end of Phase 2, the platform should be enterprise-ready: connected to real hardware, secured with proper key management, enforcing real-world policies, and offering compliance features needed for enterprise pilot deployments.
·         Task ID: P2-T1 – Name: Integrate First Quantum Hardware Backend (IBM Quantum)
·         Plane: Autonomy (Execution Backend)
·         Package(s): services/orchestrator, platform/qir-adapters
·         Type: PROD-REQUIRED
·         Owner: Autonomy Lead (Orchestrator Engineer)
·         Timebox: 6 weeks (Month 4–5)
·         Dependencies: All Phase 1 tasks done (stable core services and deployment); credentials/access for at least one quantum cloud (e.g. IBM Quantum or similar) obtained
·         Steps:
a.      Abstract the notion of a quantum execution backend in the orchestrator. Define a clear interface in Go (or appropriate language) for backend operations (e.g. SubmitJob(qirPayload) -> jobID and PollJobStatus(jobID) -> status/result). Refactor the orchestrator’s current submission logic (which may still be simulator-specific) to call through this interface.
b.     Implement a provider plugin for IBM Quantum. Use IBM’s API/SDK (possibly through their Python client or REST endpoints) to submit QIR jobs to the IBM Quantum cloud. This may involve writing a small shim in the orchestrator (or a sidecar service in Python) to handle authentication and job submission, then poll for results. Securely manage credentials (e.g. load IBM Quantum API keys from Vault or env variables rather than hardcoding).
c.      Update the QIR adapter or compilation pipeline to ensure compatibility with the target backend. For example, if IBM requires OpenQASM or specific QIR dialect, implement the necessary translation. This could mean using our platform/qir-adapters to transform or validate QIR for the IBM backend (ensuring the quantum circuit we generate is executable by IBM’s systems).
d.     Perform an end-to-end test: use the CLI to submit a simple quantum circuit (e.g. a Bell state or small circuit) with the orchestrator configured to use the IBM backend. Verify that: a) the orchestrator accepts the job and returns a job ID; b) the job is successfully created on IBM’s system (e.g. visible in IBM’s queue); c) after some wait, the orchestrator fetches the result and marks the job complete; d) the result is stored in our platform (and possibly can be retrieved via our /v1/status or similar endpoint).
e.      Handle asynchronous behavior: likely the external API is asynchronous (job submission returns immediately with an external job id, and we must poll). Ensure our orchestrator’s status tracking handles this (e.g. status: running (external: IBM#12345)). Implement timeouts or error handling: if the external job fails or hangs beyond a policy threshold, mark it failed and record info in the ledger.
f.        Document this integration: update the orchestrator README or docs to explain how to configure the IBM backend (what credentials are needed, any region specifics). Make note of any limitations (e.g. job size limits, queue delays) so they’re understood in pilots.
·         Acceptance / Evidence:
o   The orchestrator codebase now contains a backend abstraction (e.g. an interface BackendProvider and an implementation for IBM). A code snippet or generated documentation can be provided as evidence (e.g. an artifact backend_interface.txt showing the interface definition and the IBM implementation’s class signature).
o   A test execution log (_artifacts/ibmq_integration_test.log) demonstrating a submitted job via A4Q that runs on IBM Quantum. It should show: CLI/API request -> orchestrator logs (“Job submitted to IBM backend, external ID=XYZ”) -> some polling -> orchestrator logs (“Job XYZ completed, result received”) -> trust-fabric logging the completion and sealing result.
o   The DK invariants are not broken by this integration: for instance, compile p95 might include the compile+submit time now – we ensure it’s still within budget for a simple job (if external queue time is long, that’s considered execution time, not compile). More importantly, the orchestrator continues to function (doesn’t crash or deadlock with async jobs). The a4q_job_success metric should count the IBM-run job as successful, and other metrics (latencies) should remain in expected ranges (acknowledging external execution time is outside our control). A brief note confirms that one slow hardware job doesn’t affect the orchestrator’s responsiveness for others (due to async handling).
o   We have at least one real quantum job result captured in our ledger. For example, a snippet from the ledger or job record showing a real result bitstring or output from IBM. This can be used as a demo to stakeholders (“our platform ran a circuit on real hardware and captured the result/provenance”).
●	Notes/Risks: This is a major integration point – initial issues may include dealing with external API limits, network latency, or different error modes (e.g. queue timeouts). We should treat this as proving the architecture: if something in our orchestrator design doesn’t fit well (e.g. maybe we need an event loop for external callbacks), address it early. Also, ensure security: no sensitive IBM credentials should ever appear in logs or be hardcoded. By accomplishing this task, we demonstrate that A4Q is not just a closed sim – it can talk to real quantum machines, a key milestone for any credible quantum platform.
●	Task ID: P2-T2 – Name: Enterprise Key Management (Vault Integration Full)
·         Plane: Governance (Security/Compliance)
·         Package(s): services/trust-fabric, services/common/pqc (crypto modules)
·         Type: PROD-REQUIRED
·         Owner: Security Engineer (Governance)
·         Timebox: 4 weeks (Month 5)
·         Dependencies: P1-T1 (real-mode and DK constants) ensured no dev keys; a corporate Vault or HSM service accessible for testing; orchestrator and trust-fabric running in cluster (from Phase 1)
·         Steps:
a.      Deploy or connect to a HashiCorp Vault (or cloud HSM/KMS) for managing cryptographic keys used by the Trust Fabric. Establish a path or policy for our application (e.g. a dedicated a4q/ namespace in Vault).
b.     Update the Trust Fabric service to use Vault for all key operations: key generation, storage, and retrieval. For instance, when the service needs an encryption key or signing key (for sealing or ledger entries), it should call Vault’s API rather than generating or storing keys locally. Configure it such that keys are persisted in Vault’s secure storage, and only references or key IDs are held in memory.
c.      Implement periodic key rotation policies if applicable: e.g. set a schedule or manual trigger to rotate the ledger signing key or watermarking keys, retrieving new ones from Vault. Ensure that rotating keys doesn’t break verification (likely by versioning or caching old keys as needed for verifying historical entries).
d.     Remove any leftover hard-coded keys or test certificates from the codebase. For testing, we might use a development Vault with unseal keys etc., but the integration should be as close to production practice as possible (maybe use Vault’s dev server for local, but still through the API).
e.      Harden the key usage in Trust Fabric: ensure that if Vault is unreachable, the system fails safe (e.g. refuse to append new ledger entries rather than falling back to insecure mode). Add robust error handling and logging for key operations.
f.        Test the end-to-end flow: perform a “seal” operation via the Trust Fabric (e.g. seal a job with a watermark) and verify in logs that the key came from Vault (Vault audit log can confirm access). Also test failure modes: e.g. revoke the Vault token and attempt an operation – trust-fabric should log an error and ideally bubble up a clear failure (without crashing).
·         Acceptance / Evidence:
o   The Trust Fabric configuration now includes external key management settings (e.g. Vault address, role, etc.), and documentation (in README or deployment notes) explains how to configure Vault for A4Q. An artifact like vault_policy.hcl could be included, showing the Vault policy that A4Q uses (e.g. capabilities to read/write its key paths only).
o   A test execution (documented in _artifacts/vault_integration_test.txt) shows that after deploying the updated Trust Fabric, keys are indeed not stored locally. For example: the log might show “Retrieved key ledger_signing_key_v1 from Vault” during startup. The Vault’s audit log or API query confirms that keys were created/accessed at the expected times.
o   All cryptographic operations (sealing a job, signing a watermark, encrypting a log entry) still work as before from an external perspective – meaning our API endpoints produce the same outcomes – but now with the assurance that the secret material is centrally managed. As evidence, demonstrate that a ledger append can occur on one instance of trust-fabric, then that instance is destroyed and a new instance (with access to Vault) can continue verifying or appending using the same keys (proving keys persisted outside the container).
o   Security review sign-off: have a security-oriented team member do a mini-review that all secrets (certs, keys) previously in config files or memory-only are now in Vault/HSM. This could be captured as a checklist “No plaintext secrets in repo ✅; Vault used for X, Y, Z ✅”. By Phase 2 end, we should be able to say no customer secret or critical key is stored insecurely in the platform.
●	Notes/Risks: Introducing an external dependency (Vault) means we need to handle its availability – this foreshadows needing an HA Vault or fallback mode. However, that’s acceptable because losing key management essentially means the platform is not operational (which is true in any secure system). This integration removes a major “MVP shortcut” from earlier versions (where keys might have been in-memory or static). Achieving this is a prerequisite for any enterprise trust in the system’s security.
●	Task ID: P2-T3 – Name: Distributed Ledger Deployment (A/B Regions and Blockchain Trial)
·         Plane: Governance (Ledger)
·         Package(s): services/trust-fabric/ledger, infra/chains (if blockchain components)
·         Type: PROD-REQUIRED
·         Owner: Governance Lead (Ledger Engineer)
·         Timebox: 6 weeks (Month 5–6)
·         Dependencies: P1-T4 (basic HA test done); at least two cluster environments or the ability to simulate multi-region; Vault integration (P2-T2) in place for secure keys
·         Steps:
a.      Set up the Trust Fabric ledger in an active-active multi-region configuration. This may involve running two instances of the Trust Fabric service each with its own database (Postgres) in different regions, and implementing a replication or consensus mechanism. Likely, use Postgres native replication or a logical replication slot such that ledger entries in Region A are streamed to Region B and vice versa. Ensure consistency (no divergent ledger).
b.     Develop or integrate a consortium ledger component (blockchain trial): e.g. run a small Hyperledger Fabric or Quorum node that mirrors the ledger entries onto a permissioned blockchain. Alternatively, implement a simple append-only log chain with cryptographic linking of blocks (if using a full blockchain is overkill, a simplified approach might be to chain ledger transactions with hashes). The goal is to evaluate tamper-proof, distributed ledger tech for cross-org trust. Run this in parallel to the normal DB: the trust-fabric service on append could also push a hash or entry to the blockchain node.
c.      Implement ledger integrity monitoring: add a background process or admin API that compares the ledgers between Region A and B (and the blockchain if applicable) to detect any divergence. If using a blockchain, verify that our internal DB’s state is anchored (e.g. the latest block hash matches the hash of our last entries).
d.     Extend policy logic to consider multi-region context: e.g. if region-specific data residency policy is in place, ensure that replication obeys it (maybe some data should not replicate if policy forbids cross-border transfer – this might be a design decision whether not to replicate certain entries at all or to anonymize them). For now, focus on technical replication, but keep hooks for policy control.
e.      Test the distributed setup: simulate Region A and Region B by deploying two trust-fabric instances (in two namespaces or two cloud regions). Perform a series of transactions (seals, appends) in Region A only, ensure they appear in Region B’s ledger view after replication, and vice versa. Then simulate a network partition: cut off replication, generate some transactions in each region, then restore connectivity – decide how conflicts are resolved (perhaps design such that each org writes only to its ledger segment or use timestamps to merge). Because it’s active-active, conflict resolution strategy (or a single source of truth leader election) is needed – evaluate which approach is feasible now (maybe designate one as primary for writes in pilot).
f.        Evaluate the blockchain node performance and utility: while running the above tests, record the latency overhead of also writing to the blockchain (does it slow down ledger append significantly?). If overhead is minor (e.g. <5% increase in append p95), this is promising. If it’s large, consider making blockchain async or optional. Document the results and determine if the consortium ledger approach is viable to pursue beyond a trial.
·         Acceptance / Evidence:
o   Two ledger instances running in different zones maintain a unified view of transactions. Evidence: a query or API call to each instance after tests shows the same set of ledger entries (perhaps with region tags but otherwise identical). Provide a dump or hash (e.g. ledger head hash) from each to show consistency.
o   Performance data: show that ledger append p95 remains within ~120 ms across the distributed setup. If, say, cross-region replication adds latency, we might see 150 ms p95 when both sides are committing. If so, note how we mitigated (maybe local commit then async replicate to keep local latency low). Ideally, an artifact like _artifacts/multi_region_latency.csv demonstrates append times with and without multi-region enabled.
o   If blockchain integration was enabled, show an example blockchain ledger entry corresponding to a trust-fabric transaction (e.g. a block containing a hash of a quantum job record). If using Hyperledger or similar, show that our entries are recorded in that system. Include a snippet of the chain state or a transaction receipt as evidence.
o   Document any policy decisions: for example, if we had to choose one region as primary writer (active-passive instead of true active-active) due to conflict issues, note that and the plan to improve. Exit criteria isn’t necessarily a perfect multi-master system, but we need at least one robust multi-site deployment strategy by now (even if it’s primary/replica with failover).
o   The outcome should be that we can deploy the trust fabric in a resilient manner across data centers – a prerequisite for enterprise trust. A success criteria: If Region A goes offline, Region B can continue processing new transactions within X seconds, and no data is lost. Provide evidence of a simulated region outage test: e.g. kill Region A, then use Region B’s API to verify it has all entries up to the point of failure and can still accept new ones.
●	Notes/Risks: True distributed ledgers are complex. For now, we might accept some manual steps or imperfect consistency (design partners likely tolerate a short outage in a pilot). The blockchain trial is experimental – it may be discarded if it proves too slow or unnecessary, but doing it now informs our path. The main risk is time: this task is heavy. We should prioritize core multi-region DB replication first (that’s directly needed for HA), and treat blockchain as a parallel PoC. We must also be mindful of regulatory concerns (if simulating cross-border, ensure we think how to prevent forbidden replication – though implementing that fully may be out of scope in Phase 2, awareness is key).
●	Task ID: P2-T4 – Name: Trust Contracts – Policy Engine Extensions
·         Plane: Governance (Policy/Compliance)
·         Package(s): services/trust-fabric/policy (OPA/Rego modules), services/trust-fabric/api
·         Type: PROD-REQUIRED
·         Owner: Governance Engineer (Policy Lead)
·         Timebox: 4 weeks (Month 6)
·         Dependencies: Base trust-fabric service running; P1-T1 (real mode) done; possibly need input from partner compliance requirements
·         Steps:
a.      Define a set of governance policies that must be enforced at the trust-fabric level for enterprise use. Examples: Data Residency – quantum job data and results cannot leave certain regions; Export Control – certain algorithms (identified by tags) cannot be run on certain backends or must have special approval; Retention – audit logs should auto-delete or archive after X days for privacy. Collect requirements from potential partners or legal guidelines to prioritize these rules.
b.     Implement these policies using the Trust Fabric’s “trust contract” engine. Likely, this involves writing Rego policies (if using Open Policy Agent) or similar rule definitions in services/trust-fabric/policy. For data residency, for instance, the policy might check a job’s declared region tag against the orchestrator’s target and either permit or deny scheduling if it violates region rules. For export control, maintain a list of restricted identifiers and enforce conditions on /v1/submit.
c.      Extend the trust-fabric APIs to allow per-organization policy configuration. Perhaps introduce an admin API to upload or toggle policies for a given tenant (though in this phase we might hardcode for the pilot’s needs). At minimum, structure the policy engine such that rules can be enabled/disabled via config (so we can quickly adapt to partner feedback).
d.     Ensure that when a policy is violated, it fails closed. E.g., if a job submission contravenes a residency rule, the orchestrator should get a clear denial and the event is logged (with a reason) in the audit log. Define standard error codes/messages for these scenarios so that it’s user-understandable.
e.      Write unit tests or scenario tests for each policy: e.g., simulate a submission with a “EU-only” tag being sent to a US-based QPU – the trust fabric should reject it. Simulate a request to delete logs older than N days – a procedure or cron in the trust-fabric should remove or anonymize those entries (and verify it doesn’t break the audit chain).
f.        Document the available policy controls and how to configure them for a deployment. This will be critical for the pilot admin guide. Include examples of policy definitions in the documentation.
·         Acceptance / Evidence:
o   The trust-fabric service now includes a policy module with concrete rules. Provide a few example policy definitions (maybe attach the Rego file snippet as evidence). There should be rules covering at least: location-based restrictions, time-based retention or access, and possibly user role checks.
o   Test results showing the policies in action: for instance, _artifacts/policy_tests.out could list scenarios: “Test: Submit job with region=EU to backend=US -> Result: DENIED (policy data_residency_violation)” and “Test: Query audit log after 30 days -> older entries removed per retention policy.” All such tests should pass.
o   During a dry-run or actual pilot simulation, try an action that violates a policy and confirm that the system logs the attempt and prevents it. For example, attempt to fetch a job result by a user who doesn’t own it (if multi-tenant context) – trust-fabric should refuse. Evidence could be an audit log entry like “Policy deny: user X attempted unauthorized access to job Y.”
o   Compliance officer review: ideally, have someone (internal or partner) review these policies against their requirements. The acceptance is that they agree the mechanism meets the need (perhaps not formally signed off, but at least informally). This can be captured as meeting minutes or an email (not for the output here, but as part of our process).
o   The outcome by Phase 2 is that we can tell an enterprise stakeholder: “We have a policy enforcement engine in place. Here are the rules it currently supports… (with ability to expand).” Achieving that is a go for moving into a real pilot in Phase 3.
●	Notes/Risks: Overloading with too many policies could slow down performance (each job might trigger multiple checks). We should keep the initial set lean and focused on what our first pilot demands. Also, need to be careful that policies don’t conflict; if two policies both apply, ensure deterministic order or combine them. There’s a risk of making the system too rigid – we address this by allowing policies to be tuned via config. Phase 2 is an ideal time because we can still adjust before external users are on the system.
●	Task ID: P2-T5 – Name: Watermark Extraction & Verification Toolkit
·         Plane: Discovery → Governance (IP Provenance)
·         Package(s): platform/watermark-pass, services/trust-fabric, tools/wm-inspector
·         Type: PROD-REQUIRED
·         Owner: Compiler/SDK Engineer
·         Timebox: 3 weeks (Month 7)
·         Dependencies: Basic watermarking mechanism exists from GA (V8); need sample quantum results to test extraction (can use simulator or real hardware results from P2-T1)
·         Steps:
a.      Develop a watermark inspector tool that can take a quantum circuit or an execution result and detect the presence of our watermark. This could be a standalone script or part of the SDK/CLI.
