Essentially, if our compiler’s watermark-pass embeds a signature in circuit angles or qubit allocations, this tool should reverse that: given a final measured outcome or an executed circuit, it should confirm “yes, this originated from A4Q” or extract the encoded job ID.
b.     Extend the trust-fabric verify API (/v1/verify) to use the above capability. E.g., /v1/verify could accept a results payload (or circuit) and output a verification report: which watermark bits were found, matching which ledger record (if any). This ties the Discovery side (circuit IP) to the Governance side (ledger provenance) for end-to-end proof.
c.      If not already done, refine the watermark embedding scheme for reliability: ensure that it doesn’t degrade the circuit’s performance (the GA tests showed ~1.0 TPR, but now test on more circuits). Possibly generate a variety of circuits (random, known algorithms) and simulate them with noise to make sure watermark detection stays ≥99% TPR. Adjust the scheme if needed (e.g. use error-correcting code on the watermark bits).
d.     Provide a command in the CLI or a script to run a watermark end-to-end test: take a circuit, watermark it (via our compiler pass), execute it (maybe via local simulator or IBM from P2-T1), then use the verification tool on the result to confirm the watermark. Automate this so we can demonstrate IP provenance working to stakeholders easily.
e.      Package the watermark toolkit for external use if appropriate: perhaps include it in the SDK as a library so partners could verify watermarks on their own (e.g. a partner could take a circuit result and independently verify it came through A4Q by using our library, without needing full access to ledger). Even if not fully external now, design the interface for that future use.
·         Acceptance / Evidence:
o   A documented watermark verification procedure exists. For example, the output of a test run might be: “Input circuit X sealed with watermark ID 0xABC123. After execution, verification tool found watermark ID 0xABC123 in result – match ✅.” This could be captured in _artifacts/watermark_test.txt. It should include metrics like “watermark bits detected: 10/10, confidence: 99.5%.”
o   The /v1/verify API can be called with either (a) a job ID (to cross-check that job’s result in the ledger vs. the watermark we expect) or (b) a raw result plus claimed job ID, and it will return a boolean or report. Provide an example API call in documentation or as a screenshot of a CLI invocation: e.g. a4qctl verify --job-id 123 --result-file results.json returns “Verified: Watermark matches ledger entry 123 (submitted by Alice on 2025-11-01).”
o   Internal tests show watermark detection true positive rate remains high (≥99%). If possible, include a small table of test circuits vs detection success in a design note or test output. There should be no case where a watermark collision or mismatch occurs in the normal flow; if there is, document how we’ll mitigate (like increasing watermark strength or using alternative embedding).
o   This functionality is tied into our evidence collection: for instance, as part of ORR in Phase 3, we’ll likely use this to show that an external result can be traced. Having it done by Phase 2 means in the pilot (Phase 3) we can actually demonstrate IP provenance working not just in theory but with real data.
●	Notes/Risks: Watermarking is a differentiator for our platform; we need to ensure it’s robust. A risk is that certain quantum hardware or optimizers could strip or alter our watermark (e.g. hardware compilers recompile the circuit and remove subtle marks). We should coordinate with hardware providers if possible to ensure our method survives (maybe they treat our watermarked gates as protected). For now, testing on available simulators/hardware gives us some confidence. The toolkit being ready also positions us to engage standardization (we can present this method in Phase 3 standards efforts).
●	Task ID: P2-T6 – Name: Carbon-Aware Scheduling (Phase 1 – Data Feed & Tagging)
·         Plane: Autonomy (ESG/FinOps)
·         Package(s): services/orchestrator, services/common/carbon.go (new module)
·         Type: PROD-REQUIRED
·         Owner: Autonomy Engineer (Scheduler/Optimization)
·         Timebox: 3 weeks (Month 8)
·         Dependencies: Basic job submission flow working (Phase 1); access to a carbon intensity API (e.g. electricityMap or similar) or static data for regions
·         Steps:
a.      Integrate a carbon intensity data source. For simplicity, start with a public API or dataset that given a region and time returns an approximate CO₂ emissions rate (gCO₂/kWh). If an API is used, code a small client in services/common/carbon.go to fetch current carbon intensity for the regions our QPUs or datacenters are in. If API not feasible, use a static schedule (e.g. average by time of day).
b.     Extend the orchestrator to tag incoming jobs with an estimated carbon cost if executed immediately versus delayed. Essentially, when a job comes in, look up current carbon rate for the target region; perhaps also look ahead (if the API gives forecast data) to see if it’s expected to drop soon.
c.      Policy for scheduling: Implement a simple carbon-aware scheduling policy: e.g. if current carbon intensity is above a certain threshold and the job is not urgent, the orchestrator could queue it a bit longer to wait for a cleaner window. Initially, just compute the metric and log it or expose it. We might not auto-delay jobs yet without user consent. Instead, mark in the job record “executed at carbon intensity X gCO₂/kWh”.
d.     Provide an API or field in job status for carbon info. E.g., /v1/status for a job includes carbon_estimate: 12 gCO₂. If we did delay, also show delayed_by: 5 minutes for lower carbon. This ensures transparency.
e.      Test the feature in a controlled way: Simulate different carbon conditions by using a fake API endpoint or adjusting returned values. Submit jobs with a flag indicating they are “carbon-flexible” vs “urgent”. Verify that flexible jobs get delayed if carbon is high (perhaps implement a simple threshold like >100 gCO₂/kWh triggers a max 10 min delay). Ensure urgent jobs ignore the policy and run immediately.
f.        Document how carbon-aware scheduling works and how an admin can configure it (e.g. set the threshold or turn it off). For now, likely keep it always on but with minimal impact (just logging) unless explicitly configured to delay.
·         Acceptance / Evidence:
o   The orchestrator logs now include carbon intensity info for each job. For example, _artifacts/carbon_schedule_test.log might contain entries: “Job 456 scheduled at 14:30, region=us-east, carbon_rate=450 gCO₂/kWh -> decision: run (above threshold but job marked urgent)” and “Job 457 scheduled at 14:32, carbon_rate=450 -> decision: delayed 5 min (flexible job)”. These logs demonstrate the logic is executing.
o   The job ledger or status output includes a carbon estimate. We can show a snippet of a job record from the trust-fabric that has a field for carbon usage, e.g. carbon_gco2: 50 (if it ran at a cleaner time) or similar.
o   A controlled test where we force a high carbon scenario and have a flexible job results in that job being executed later than it would have been, and a reduction in carbon intensity is observed (if using dummy data, we can simulate a drop). Even if just for concept, record this outcome.
o   Importantly, no negative impact on SLOs: ensure that this scheduling doesn’t break the job success or latency invariants beyond what is allowed. If we delay by 5 minutes, that’s okay if the user allowed it, but our system should still be responsive. We might have to note that this could conflict with the “compile p95 ≤ 250 ms” invariant if we interpret that strictly (but compile latency likely measured from start of processing, not including an intentional delay). So acceptance is that all automated delays are accounted as intentional and don’t signal system slowness.
o   This sets us up to claim in partner conversations that we have basic environmental optimization built-in. As evidence, we can show a simple Grafana panel of carbon intensity vs time with job executions marked, or a report stating “X% carbon savings by deferring Y jobs by Z minutes”. That could be a stretch, but even a hypothetical example is useful.
·         Notes/Risks: We need to be careful not to compromise fairness or SLAs by holding jobs. Also, carbon data might be unreliable or not granular for specific quantum data centers. Phase 2 is about building the scaffolding. Real impact might be small now, but by starting, we demonstrate commitment to ESG goals. The risk is complexity in the scheduler – we keep it minimal (maybe off by default or only logs) until we and partners are comfortable enabling it actively.
Phase 3 (Months 8–12) – Pilot Deployment & v1.0 “GA” Readiness
Phase Goals (8–12 mo): Achieve the maturity required to support an external pilot deployment (with a design partner) by the end of the first year, and declare an internal “v1.0.0” release candidate. Focus areas: (1) End-to-End Validation – conduct a full Operational Readiness Review (ORR) style test suite across all planes: simulate real workloads, failure cases, and ensure every DK invariant and fail-safe is verified with evidence (essentially a dry run of production operations); (2) Documentation & Support Prep – produce admin guides, user documentation, runbooks, and set up basic support processes (on-call rotation, incident response procedures) so that an external team can run or co-manage the platform; (3) Pilot Execution with Real Workload – deploy the A4Q platform in a partner’s environment (or a dedicated testbed that mimics one) and run a meaningful quantum workload end-to-end (not just trivial examples, but e.g. a small variational algorithm or a QEC benchmarking routine) to shake out integration issues and gather feedback; (4) Standards & Ecosystem Engagement – start engaging external bodies (QIR Alliance, IEEE/ISO groups, etc.) by presenting our watermarking and governance approaches, getting early feedback and buy-in from the community. By the end of Phase 3, we expect to have an internal v1.0.0 GA candidate that we are confident in supporting in a real environment, with all critical production-ready tasks complete.
·         Task ID: P3-T1 – Name: Full-Stack ORR (Operational Readiness Review) Simulation
·         Plane: Cross-Cutting (Quality Gates)
·         Package(s): All (integration testing) – e.g. tests and scripts under ops/orr_tests/
·         Type: PROD-REQUIRED
·         Owner: QA Lead / PMO (with Tech Leads of each plane)
·         Timebox: 4 weeks (Month 8–9)
·         Dependencies: All Phase 1–2 PROD-REQUIRED tasks completed; a stable integrated environment (could be our staging cluster) available for test; metrics & logging in place (from Phase 1–2)
·         Steps:
a.      Define the ORR test plan covering every DK invariant and major failure mode. This includes: performance tests (submit a mix of jobs to measure compile and ledger p95 latencies under load), security tests (attempt unauthorized actions to test policy enforcement), reliability tests (deliberately crash components to test HA beyond what was done in Phase 1), and operational procedures (backup/restore of ledger, rotation of keys, etc.). Essentially a comprehensive checklist that a real ops team would run before go-live.
b.     Automate as much of the test plan as possible using scripts in ops/orr_tests. For performance, maybe create a harness that submits 1000 jobs with various sizes to measure p95 latencies and success rates. For reliability, use chaos scripts to kill pods, drop network, etc., measuring uptime. For security/compliance, include tests for each policy (e.g. try to violate residency or run a disallowed circuit).
c.      Execute the ORR tests in our staging environment (which should mirror a production setup as closely as possible). Collect all relevant data: metrics, logs, any outputs from test scripts. This will generate evidence artifacts for each category.
d.     Triage any failures or SLO misses immediately. If, for example, the test shows ledger p95 is 130 ms (above 120 ms) under a certain load, decide if we need to optimize now or can defer. Only minor or non-critical issues can be deferred past this phase – anything affecting our core promises must be fixed or at least understood with a mitigation.
e.      Iterate tests if fixes are made. The goal is to get a “pass” on the ORR checklist: meaning all DK invariants are within acceptable range in a production-like scenario, and all critical failure recoveries are within spec (e.g. uptime, recovery, data integrity all good).
f.        Prepare an ORR report that summarizes the readiness: list each invariant and test result, list each tested failure scenario and outcome, and any open items. This report will be an artifact we can show internal stakeholders (and later to partners or auditors) to demonstrate rigor.
·         Acceptance / Evidence:
o   A complete ORR checklist document (stored in repo, e.g. docs/ORR_v1_report.md) enumerating tests and outcomes. Each DK invariant should have a row: e.g. “Compile latency p95 target ≤250 ms: Pass (achieved 210 ms in test of 1000 jobs)”. Each major failure scenario should similarly have a result: e.g. “Simulated DB failover – recovery in 45 s, no data loss: Pass.” Any Fail or Near Miss should be clearly marked with a mitigation plan.
o   Raw artifacts from the ORR run are archived (e.g. _artifacts/orr_full_run_metrics.json for metrics data, _artifacts/orr_logs.zip containing logs during tests). We should highlight a couple of key graphs: perhaps a load vs latency graph, or uptime graph during chaos, to show evidence of meeting SLOs.
o   All critical issues found during ORR are resolved or accepted with sign-off. For instance, if job success came in at 90% vs 92% target due to some transient errors, we either fixed the cause or documented why in pilot it’s acceptable and how we’ll improve. The acceptance is that there are no unknown critical bugs by end of ORR.
o   Team sign-off: a brief statement from tech leads or QA lead saying “We consider build X ready for pilot use” can be included in the report. While not a code artifact, this ceremonial sign-off is part of readiness.
o   Essentially, evidence that by Month 9 we have internally pressure-tested the system end-to-end and are confident in its performance and reliability data. This will directly feed into the decision to proceed with the external pilot (the next task).
●	Notes/Risks: This task could surface uncomfortable truths – which is exactly its purpose. We schedule it in Phase 3 so we still have a little time to fix issues before an external pilot sees them. The risk is if something big shows up (e.g. orchestrator can’t handle sustained load) – then we might have to adjust pilot scope or quickly optimize. That’s why involvement of all leads is needed to rapidly respond. Success here is a go/no-go gate internally: we don’t proceed to Phase 3 pilot until ORR is essentially green.
●	Task ID: P3-T2 – Name: External Pilot Deployment (Design Partner #1)
·         Plane: Cross-Cutting (Deployment/Support)
·         Package(s): infra/helm, docs/ (deployment guides), support scripts (as needed)
·         Type: PROD-REQUIRED
·         Owner: Program Manager (with DevOps and SRE assisting)
·         Timebox: 6 weeks (Month 9–10)
·         Dependencies: P3-T1 (ORR) completed with acceptable results; Phase 2 integrations (QPU, Vault, etc.) stable; a willing design partner identified and environment provisioned
·         Steps:
a.      Identify pilot partner and environment details. Suppose we have a design partner (e.g. a national lab or a cloud provider’s internal team) ready to trial A4Q. Gather their requirements: what cloud or on-prem environment will we deploy to? What access or accounts do we need? Do they require security review before software installation? Align on a scope for the pilot (which features they will use, what success looks like for them).
b.     Prepare a deployment bundle for the partner. This likely includes our Helm chart (from P1-T3) packaged at version 1.0.0, along with a deployment guide (step-by-step doc tailored to their environment). Remove or minimize any dev-only configurations: ensure A4Q_SIM=0 and all real integrations are turned on. Pre-set any config known (e.g. their Vault address, their hardware credentials if they have their own QPU access). Provide guidance on hardware sizing (CPU/RAM for each component, etc. based on our tests).
c.      Work closely with the partner’s ops team to install the platform in their environment. This could involve on-site support or screen-sharing sessions. Execute the Helm install (or equivalent) and troubleshoot issues (network policies, firewall, permissions, etc.). Expect some environment-specific adjustments (for instance, if they use a specific cloud storage instead of PVCs, adapt our config accordingly).
d.     Once deployed, run a shakeout test in the partner environment. This means executing a set of test jobs (similar to ORR but probably smaller scale) on their setup to ensure everything works: submit jobs, check they run on the actual hardware (or their simulator), verify metrics are coming through, see that the ledger is recording, etc. Any issues (like missing permissions to call their QPU) should be resolved here.
e.      Establish monitoring and support channels for the pilot duration. For example, set up our Prom/Grafana to either federate metrics from their cluster or have them share a dashboard with us. Also set up a Slack/MS Teams channel or similar with their team for quick communication. Define incident response: if something breaks, how do they contact us (pager? email?). Possibly plan for a weekly sync meeting to discuss progress and issues.
f.        Provide a mini-training or onboarding to the partner’s users. Demonstrate how to use the A4Q CLI/SDK to submit jobs, how to interpret the trust-fabric logs (for watermark provenance), how to view the carbon metrics, etc. Essentially, treat this like enabling a customer: make sure at least a couple of their people know how to drive the platform (with our support in the background).
·         Acceptance / Evidence:
o   A confirmation (email or signed checklist) from the partner that “A4Q version 1.0 is deployed in our environment and operational.” This could be a simple document where they tick off: all pods running, can submit a test job, can see metrics, etc. Capturing this as an artifact shows that the deployment was completed successfully.
o   Metrics or logs from the partner environment after, say, one week of operation. Ideally, a Grafana snapshot showing all green DK metrics in their environment under some real usage. If direct data sharing is sensitive, then at least a report: e.g. “In first week, partner ran 50 jobs, all within SLOs – compile p95 180 ms, ledger p95 90 ms, watermark TPR 100%, uptime 100%.” Having this real-world data proves our system works outside our lab.
o   At least one meaningful workload executed by the partner on the system. For example: “Partner X executed a 5-qubit QAOA optimization problem on IonQ via A4Q. The platform orchestrated the job, recorded it in the ledger with ID #123, and watermark verification later showed the result was provenance-traceable.” Document the story of this workload (what was run, what was achieved). This serves as a case study and validates real value.
o   Feedback from the partner captured. We should have a list of any issues or feature requests they noted during deployment and initial use. Acceptance includes that none of these issues are show-stoppers (if one was, we address it immediately or have a workaround). The presence of feedback is expected; we’ll channel it into our roadmap. But the partner should express overall satisfaction that things are working as promised. Possibly include a positive quote like “We were able to deploy A4Q and run our first quantum jobs with minimal hassle…” (if available).
o   This task is complete when the system is not just running in the partner environment, but we have transitioned into a support mode – meaning day-to-day, the partner can use it (with our oversight). That is a huge milestone: it’s effectively our platform’s first external “customer.”
●	Notes/Risks: Every environment is unique – expect deployment hiccups (networking, RBAC, etc.). We built flexibility in Phase 1–2 (compose and helm) to ease this, but reality will still throw curveballs. Key is to document everything: any manual steps taken here should be fed back into our automation later. Also manage scope: the pilot doesn’t necessarily use every feature (maybe Discovery plane is off if not needed). That’s fine; focus on core stability. Another risk is security review on their side – provide materials (our ORR report, etc.) if needed to assure their infosec team. Success here not only validates tech but also our ability to execute collaboratively with a partner.
●	Task ID: P3-T3 – Name: Standards Contribution & IP Alignment
·         Plane: Governance (Standards & Ecosystem)
·         Package(s): platform/watermark-pass (spec documentation), docs/standards/
·         Type: PRE-PROD (External Collaboration)
·         Owner: CTO or Chief Scientist
·         Timebox: 3 weeks (Month 10–11, overlapping with pilot support)
·         Dependencies: A stable description of our watermarking and provenance approach (from Phase 2 tasks); ideally involvement in or knowledge of relevant working groups (QIR Alliance, etc.)
·         Steps:
a.      Prepare a technical whitepaper or draft specification detailing A4Q’s quantum circuit watermarking and IP provenance scheme. Include the rationale, the method of embedding metadata in QIR/quantum circuits, how it’s extracted, and how it ties to the ledger. Ensure this document is clear enough for an external audience (it might form the basis of a standards proposal).
b.     Identify relevant standards bodies or industry groups (for example, the QIR Alliance for anything QIR-related, IEEE Quantum, or national QC standards initiatives). Submit the draft or present it in a meeting. For instance, request to be on the agenda of the next QIR Alliance call to discuss “Quantum Circuit Watermarking for IP Protection.” Emphasize we have an implementation and data (which boosts credibility).
c.      Similarly, compile any insights from our trust fabric design that might influence emerging standards in quantum cloud governance or multi-tenant quantum computing. Without giving away sensitive competitive info, prepare at least talking points or a blog post for the community about how we approach quantum job logging, carbon accounting, etc. This positions us as thought leaders.
d.     Internally, coordinate with legal/IP counsel regarding what we contribute. For example, if we have patents filed for watermarking, ensure we’re clear on licensing if we propose it as a standard. Possibly get a legal letter ready if needed (common in standards contribution to declare you’re willing to license necessary IP under fair terms).
e.      Incorporate any feedback from these engagements back into our backlog. If the QIR community, for example, suggests using an existing metadata field for provenance instead of our approach, note that for Era 2 implementation. If standards folks highlight a compliance requirement we missed, log it. Essentially treat this as an early peer review of our approach.
f.        As part of this narrative, highlight our involvement publicly if possible (without overcommitting). For instance, a blog or tweet: “Invarian is collaborating with the QIR Alliance on standardizing quantum circuit watermarking – ensuring IP provenance for quantum algorithms.” This starts building external validation and interest.
·         Acceptance / Evidence:
o   A published artifact like “A4Q Quantum Watermarking Specification Draft v1.0” in our docs/standards directory or on an external site. The content should detail our watermark schema (e.g. how we encode jobID in angles or circuits) sufficiently for others to implement or critique.
o   Proof of engagement: meeting minutes, an email from a working group, or a conference workshop slide that shows we presented our approach. For example, a snippet of an IEEE workshop agenda with our talk listed, or an email from the QIR Alliance, “Thank you for the draft, we will review...”. Even better, any positive commentary: e.g. a working group member saying “This is interesting, could be very useful for quantum IP management.”
o   Internal IP check: a short memo that our patent strategy is aligned with what we’re sharing. (We don’t include this publicly, but ensure it’s done.) Acceptance for us is that we’ve not inadvertently given away crown jewels without protection.
o   By doing this in Phase 3, we expect not a full standard adoption (that can take years), but evidence of serious consideration by the community. We want A4Q to be seen as the de facto reference implementation of quantum IP governance. A measure of success: maybe being invited to co-author a proposal or join a committee officially. If that happens (say, we join a standards working group on quantum metadata), that’s great evidence in our investor narrative later.
o   The output of this task also ensures our internal alignment: it forces us to solidify our approach in writing and compare with state-of-art, which is valuable before we scale further.
●	Notes/Risks: Time is a big factor – contributing to standards can be slow and resource-consuming. But the upside is high in credibility and shaping the field. We allocate limited time here (3 weeks, likely spread out) and possibly involve an external advisor who already sits on a committee to champion our ideas. The risk is exposing our ideas to competitors; we mitigate by ensuring we’ve secured IP (patents filed) by now for core innovations. Overall, this effort is about long-term ecosystem play, which investors and partners will appreciate even if it doesn’t yield immediate product features.
●	Task ID: P3-T4 – Name: Version 1.0.0 Release Packaging
·         Plane: Cross-Cutting (Release Management)
·         Package(s): infra/helm (charts), docs/CHANGELOG.md, CI/CD release pipelines
·         Type: PROD-REQUIRED
·         Owner: Release Manager / DevOps
·         Timebox: 2 weeks (Month 12)
·         Dependencies: All critical fixes from pilot feedback applied; P3-T1 ORR passed; documentation updated (user guides from earlier tasks)
·         Steps:
a.      Create a release branch and freeze code for v1.0.0 (which might correspond to our internal V1.2 since V1.1 was GA prototype). Increment version numbers in all modules according to semantic versioning. Ensure consistency (e.g. SDK marked v1.0.0, services have v1.0.0 tags).
b.     Generate final build artifacts: build Docker images for each service with the v1.0.0 tag and push to the designated registry. Package the Helm chart as a4q-1.0.0.tgz. Build any CLI binaries (if the CLI is distributed separately, e.g. a a4qctl binary for multiple OS) and prepare them for download.
c.      Compile release notes (CHANGELOG). Highlight major features delivered since GA (V8): e.g. “Added support for IBM Quantum backend, Vault integration for keys, multi-region ledger, etc.” List any known limitations or tech preview features (maybe Discovery plane is still experimental, etc.). This ensures transparency to early adopters.
d.     Include installation instructions geared toward an external audience. Our internal deployment guide from P3-T2 can be simplified for general use. For example, “Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster, Vault, etc., then run helm install….” Even if we don’t expect strangers to install without us, writing this is a good exercise to see if anything is unclear.
e.      Perform a clean-slate test of the release artifacts: have an engineer (not using dev environment) follow the instructions from scratch (new clone of repo, use the published images or build from release branch) to bring up the system. This simulates an open-source user or a new team setting it up. All tests should pass in this fresh setup.
f.        Tag the repository in git as v1.0.0. Archive the release: e.g. create a GitHub release (if applicable) or store the artifact bundle in an internal server if it’s private. Essentially, mark this version in a way that it cannot be altered (for audit and rollback reference).
·         Acceptance / Evidence:
o   Release Artifacts: The existence of a4q-platform-1.0.0.tgz (Helm chart package), Docker images labeled :1.0.0 for each service, and possibly a a4qctl-v1.0.0-linux.zip and similar for CLI. These are all accessible to the team/partner (and to external users if we open source or distribute to them).
o   The CHANGELOG.md (or release notes doc) in the repo updated with an entry for v1.0.0, dated appropriately, listing features and known issues. For example: “v1.0.0 – Highlights: Integration with real QPU (IBM), multi-tenant ledger support (beta), full metric/telemetry endpoints, etc. Known limitations: QAGF is experimental, system tested up to N jobs/hour, etc.” This shows we are communicating the maturity level clearly.
o   A screenshot or log from the clean install test (artifact _artifacts/v1.0.0_clean_install.log) showing steps like cloning the repo, deploying the Helm chart, and successfully running a sample job. This proves that someone other than the dev who built it can get it running using only the packaged materials and documentation.
o   The git tag v1.0.0 in our repository (we can list the commit hash in the report). Also, evidence that the CI/CD pipeline ran for this tag and perhaps automatically published the artifacts. If we have CI badges or a release pipeline, note that it passed for v1.0.0.
o   Perhaps an internal sign-off email or meeting notes where the team acknowledges the release is cut. (In a real org, this might be an internal announcement like “v1.0.0 is released!” summarizing what’s next.)
o   With this task complete, we formally have a production-ready v1.0 in hand. The next step beyond 24 months would be turning this into broader availability (which aligns with Era 2 starting). The acceptance of P3-T4 essentially marks the end of our 0–24 month execution program with success.
·         Notes/Risks: It’s mostly administrative but very important not to rush this. Skipping proper packaging can lead to headaches (inconsistent versions deployed, etc.). We should treat this as if we were releasing to an open source community or external customers, even if at first it’s just for design partners. That mindset ensures quality. One risk is any last-minute bug from pilot feedback that slipped – if something is critical, we might decide to include the fix and cut 1.0.1 quickly. That’s okay; our process should accommodate patch releases. Overall, this step sets the tone: our v1.0 should be solid and well-documented, establishing credibility for all future releases.
2. 2-Year Eras and Product-Launch Iterations (Years 2–10)
With the initial 0–2 year foundation laid (Era 1 culminating in v1.0/v2.0), the roadmap proceeds in four subsequent era-level iterations of approximately 2 years each. Each era corresponds to a major evolution of the platform’s capabilities, tightens key SLOs, and targets specific strategic milestones (often ending with a major version release and a checkpoint review). Below, we outline Eras 2 through 5, including their high-level goals and a checklist of major items to accomplish in each era. (We include Era 1 for completeness, summarizing what was achieved in the first 2 years, although the detailed tasks were covered in Section 1.)
Era 1 (Years 0–2, 2025–2027) – Post-GA Foundation & Pilot Proving
Strategic Goals: In Era 1, the priority is to harden the GA prototype into a secure, measurable product and prove its value in a controlled setting. Goals include: bringing Discovery plane from concept to a working prototype (internal QAGF v0.1, not yet external); establishing Governance plane reliability (all DK invariants met in real deployments, PQC security integrated, multi-region ledger basics); and developing Autonomy plane fundamentals (orchestrator managing real QPU backend, basic failover and carbon-awareness). Success by end of Era 1 means having at least two successful pilot installations (design partners running real jobs) and initial industry validation (e.g. contributed our watermark spec to a standards discussion).
Era 1 Key Items:
·         Era 1 Item I1: Production Security & Invariants Lockdown
·         Plane(s): Governance (with cross-cutting impact)
·         Major Deliverables: Fully integrate PQC key management (all encryption/signing keys out of code and into KMS/HSM), enforce “real mode only” (no simulation backdoors in production builds), and embed all DK invariant checks as runtime monitors and CI gates. Essentially deliver the system exactly meeting its spec for performance and reliability from day one. By end of Era 1, every service runs with real cryptography and every release is blocked if it deviates from p95 latency, TPR, uptime, etc. targets[8][9].
·         Prerequisites: Completion of Phase 1 tasks (P1-T1, P1-T2, etc.) establishing constants and metrics; GA (V8) code as baseline.
·         DK/SLO Targets: Maintain all original DK invariant targets in actual pilot environment (compile p95 ≤250 ms, ledger append ≤120 ms, etc.). Introduce stretch Ops Gold budgets (e.g. compile ≤230 ms) and ensure no regressions against them[9].
●	Exit Criteria: An internal v1.1 GA declaration backed by evidence: a full ORR run passes with 10/10 DK gates met, and all “security backstops” (like sim-mode off, fail-closed on errors) are verified[10]. Internal or external auditors sign off that the platform can run in a production setting securely. By end of Year 2, we can confidently say “The system is stable and secure enough for real workloads,” backed by data and checklists. This green-lights moving on to broader deployments.
●	Era 1 Item I2: Enterprise Trust Fabric Deployment
·         Plane(s): Governance
·         Major Deliverables: Trust Fabric 2.0 with enterprise-grade enhancements: multi-region ledger replication (active-active across data centers for resilience), a pilot implementation of a blockchain/consortium ledger running in parallel for tamper-evident audit[11], external policy packs (e.g. OPA modules for region privacy and data retention) integrated into the trust engine[12], and Vault/HSM-backed key operations for all cryptographic actions. Also, initial contributions of our provenance spec to an industry forum by mid-era (signaling external validation)[13].
·         Prerequisites: Execution of key Phase 2 governance tasks (P2-T2 Vault integration, P2-T3 multi-region ledger, P2-T4 policy engine). The GA trust fabric (single-region, in-memory keys) must be upgraded to meet these deliverables.
·         DK/SLO Targets: Governance-plane reliability and integrity are tightened: ledger availability target 99.9% (tolerate region failover), while append latency must not degrade significantly with multi-region (target p95 still ~120 ms or better through caching). Audit coverage stays ≥99% or improves (aim 99.9% with redundant logging). Watermark detection TPR remains ~100% even as system scales[14].
●	Exit Criteria: Deployment of the trust fabric in a pilot enterprise setting with zero single points of failure. For example, run our ledger across two regions for a month with no data inconsistency, and demonstrate the consortium ledger concept by having a partner run a verifying node. Another exit checkpoint: our watermarking and IP schema is submitted to a standards body (indicating our governance model is on path to wider adoption). Essentially, by Era 1’s end, any major governance weakness (single-region risk, key insecurity) is removed, and the trust fabric is production-ready for multi-org use.
●	Era 1 Item I3: Autonomous Orchestration Pilot
·         Plane(s): Autonomy
·         Major Deliverables: Orchestrator 2.0 capable of interfacing with real quantum hardware and handling basic autonomous operations. This includes integration with at least one cloud QPU provider API (demonstrated with IBM in Phase 2) and basic multi-backend support (the architecture to add more providers is in place). It also covers initial failover logic for orchestrator instances (proved via chaos tests) and the introduction of carbon-aware scheduling in prototype form. In short, the orchestrator moves from a single-instance demo to a cluster-aware, plugin-extensible service that can run real jobs on real devices.
·         Prerequisites: Phase 2 Autonomy tasks (P2-T1 IBM backend, P2-T6 carbon feed) and Phase 1 tasks ensuring orchestrator metrics and HA.
·         DK/SLO Targets: Continue meeting p95 compile and submission latencies even with the external call overhead (which might not count fully against our SLO, but we aim to hide latency via async operation). Uptime for orchestrator service 99.5%+ (one failover within 60 s allowed, as per invariant). Job success rate may still be ~92% target, recognizing real hardware adds noise – but we aim to maintain success% by catching failures and retrying where sensible. Carbon per job is tracked but not yet optimized (just measured).
●	Exit Criteria: By end of Era 1, we have run at least one meaningful quantum workload through the orchestrator on real hardware in a pilot environment. The orchestrator has shown it can juggle multiple jobs and recover from a crash without losing work (evidence from chaos tests in Phase 1). We likely label a version v2.0 at this point (end of 2027) to signify readiness for more users. The platform is no longer tied to a simulator or single cloud – it’s demonstrated in principle as a multi-cloud quantum scheduler with basic autonomy. This sets the stage for scaling up in Era 2.
●	Era 1 Item I4: QAGF Prototype & Demonstration
·         Plane(s): Discovery
·         Major Deliverables: An initial Quantum Asset Generative Foundry (QAGF) prototype (v0.1) developed in-house. This is largely an R&D achievement: building an AI-driven tool that can suggest quantum circuits or error-correcting codes, integrated partially with our platform. By end of Era 1, this QAGF is not customer-facing but we demonstrate it on internal benchmarks – e.g. generating a small circuit that, when run through A4Q, improves some outcome or a QEC decoder that reduces error on sample data[15]. The prototype likely runs offline or in a sandbox environment due to maturity, but the core algorithms (maybe a reinforcement learning model for circuit generation) are in place.
·         Prerequisites: Machine learning expertise allocation; possibly data collected from the pilot runs to train models. QAGF doesn’t depend on earlier phases except having the infrastructure to plug into (SDK, etc.).
·         DK/SLO Targets: N/A for DK invariants (since this is not yet in production use). However, success metrics are defined for discovery R&D: e.g. “QAGF suggested circuit yields X% improvement in some metric vs baseline” or “auto-generated error correction scheme achieves Y logical error rate on standard noise model.” Essentially scientific benchmarks to show the AI discovery concept works at least in some cases.
·         Exit Criteria: A successful internal demo day or technical report showing QAGF results. For instance, show that the QAGF can take a given problem and output a quantum circuit that we then watermark, execute, and evaluate. If results are promising, we might release a beta feature flag in the platform (e.g. a hidden CLI command like a4qctl suggest-circuit). The key is to have laid the groundwork so that in Era 2 we can integrate QAGF for real users. Another sign of exit: one or two publications or patent filings on the generative algorithms (establishing thought leadership and IP). Era 1 treats Discovery plane as a parallel track that now can merge into the main product by next era.
Era 2 (Years 2–4, 2027–2029) – Scaling Up & Early Ecosystem Integration
Strategic Goals: In Era 2, the platform evolves from single-pilot to multi-tenant, multi-partner usage. The focus is on scaling and opening up: enabling multiple organizations to use a shared network (federated governance), expanding orchestrator to handle many cloud backends, and introducing Discovery features to users in beta form. We target Version 3.0 by the end of this era, which should support a broader ecosystem. Key goals per plane: Governance: externalize the trust fabric into a multi-organization ledger network (potentially spin it off as a neutral backbone for quantum IP exchange); Autonomy: introduce adaptive, self-healing features in production (anomaly detection loops actively managing errors, early fault-tolerance controls for small QPU prototypes); Discovery: integrate QAGF into the platform as a beta service (users can request algorithm suggestions or code optimizations via the A4Q SDK). We also aim to tighten SLOs as usage grows (e.g. higher uptime and audit standards) and get first revenue through design partner subscriptions or PoCs. By Era 2’s end, A4Q should transition from an internal pilot to something that can be offered to select external customers (beyond the original design partners).
Era 2 Key Items:
·         Era 2 Item I1: Federated Trust Fabric & Multi-Org Ledger
·         Plane(s): Governance
·         Major Deliverables: Transition the governance plane from serving a single organization (our team + one partner) to a federated model. Launch a permissioned ledger network where multiple independent organizations (e.g. a couple of cloud providers, a national lab, a university) each run a trust-fabric node that interoperates[16]. Implement cross-node trust synchronization – perhaps via periodic consensus or cross-signing of each other’s blocks to ensure a unified global view of IP transactions[17]. Enhance onboarding workflows: admin APIs to add a new organization, provision keys/certificates for them, and define inter-org policies (who trusts whom, what assets can be shared). Possibly introduce smart contracts or advanced watermarking for inter-org asset transfer (e.g. if Org A allows Org B to use a circuit for N executions, enforce that via the ledger contract)[18]. Provide unified audit tools that can query across the federated ledger seamlessly. Essentially, the trust fabric becomes networked, handling multi-tenant usage akin to a consortium blockchain for quantum IP.
·         Prerequisites: Era 1 trust fabric solid (particularly Era 1 I2 outcomes). A decision on tech approach: if we validated a blockchain in Era 1, either fully implement it now or choose a proven distributed DB for multi-org. Also, by now we likely have 2–3 interested partner orgs to form this network.
·         DK/SLO Targets: Governance availability gets stricter: target 99.99% ledger integrity uptime – meaning even if some nodes go down, the network as a whole keeps auditing (tolerate f failures). Ledger append latency might increase due to cross-node consensus; aim to keep effective p95 < 200 ms across the network by using caching or local commits with async sync[19]. Introduce new metrics like consistency lag (time for all nodes to sync a transaction) with a target (e.g. <5 s for global consistency). Watermark TPR invariant stays ≥99%, depth overhead ≤0.5% across any cross-org workflow. Possibly tighten audit coverage to 99.9% (with multiple orgs, we ensure even more complete capture of events)[20].
●	Exit Criteria: Deployment of an industry consortium ledger on a small scale. For example, by 2029 we have 3 independent organizations (Invarian + two partners) exchanging quantum IP via a shared network[21]. A demonstration scenario: Org A designs a quantum asset with QAGF, watermark-seals it, Org B executes it on their QPU via orchestrator, Org C verifies the result – all mediated by our federated trust fabric. All transactions appear on each org’s ledger copy with cryptographic consistency. Moreover, if one ledger node is taken offline, the others continue and backfill the data on reconnection (no loss of audit). Achieving this proves our governance model scales beyond a single company – a big step toward being an industry standard. It would also mark the trust fabric’s potential independence (perhaps by era’s end, we consider making it a foundation or consortium-managed project to encourage adoption)[22].
●	Era 2 Item I2: Adaptive Autonomy & Self-Healing Cluster
·         Plane(s): Autonomy
·         Major Deliverables: Elevate the orchestrator (and overall runtime) into a more autonomous control system. Key new features: implement a Magic-State Resource Manager in anticipation of small fault-tolerant QPU prototypes coming online[23][24]. Even if true FTQC isn’t here yet, simulate the “magic state factory” bottleneck and incorporate scheduling logic that can throttle or queue jobs based on scarce FT resources. This prepares the orchestrator for handling early logical qubits and their expensive ancilla state generation. Also, introduce a real-time Anomaly Detection & Response loop: feed the orchestrator (or a dedicated monitoring service) with streams of system metrics (error rates, job latency anomalies, QPU status) and have it automatically take mitigating actions (like pausing job submission to a backend that’s showing errors, or re-calibrating via an autocal routine)[24]. In Era 1 we logged anomalies; now in Era 2 we start to act on them, albeit in a cautious way (maybe start with alerting human operators or rerouting jobs, and gradually move to fully automated responses as confidence grows). Essentially, we aim to make the platform self-healing for common issues by end of Era 2.
·         Prerequisites: The groundwork from Phase 4 (P4-T1 anomaly detection, P4-T2 AutoCal integration in shadow) and any small-scale FTQC experiments (if available) to calibrate our magic-state model. Ideally, by year ~3 or 4, there might be prototype error-corrected qubits or at least high-fidelity simulators we can test with.
·         DK/SLO Targets: Tighten operational invariants: recovery time target might go down (e.g. aim <30 s for failover, anticipating more critical uptime needs). Uptime target might effectively be 99.9%+. Introduce new autonomy metrics like “anomaly resolution time” (how fast the system detects and addresses an issue). Aim for at least some incidents to be resolved without human intervention. The presence of the Magic-State manager means orchestrator throughput might be measured in logical operations – ensure scheduling overhead doesn’t reduce effective QPU utilization by more than a few percent. Carbon efficiency could also be improved (maybe by adaptive scheduling off peak carbon hours automatically – extending what we began in Phase 2).
●	Exit Criteria: By end of Era 2, run the platform in a “lights-off” mode for an extended period (e.g. a week or a month) on a test cluster or partner environment – where no human intervenes, and the system handles routine hiccups (like transient QPU errors or overload) on its own. Document examples: e.g. “Anomaly module detected a spike in error rate on QPU-X and automatically halted new submissions to it, preventing wasteful jobs for 1 hour until the error cleared.” Also, if possible, demonstrate our scheduler handling a simulated FTQC scenario: e.g. simulate a quantum computer with magic state limitations and show our orchestrator scheduling around that (throttling job flow to not exceed magic state production). If actual small FT hardware exists by 2029, aim to test on it – e.g. integrate with IBM or Google’s first logical qubit systems in a lab to show we can manage qubit allocations. A successful exit means we have confidence the Autonomy plane can handle the coming complexity of early fault-tolerant machines in Era 3. We should see in metrics that our self-healing features improved reliability (perhaps job success rate rises to e.g. 96-98% due to auto-retries and calibrations, vs 92% baseline).
●	Era 2 Item I3: QAGF Integration & Beta Release
·         Plane(s): Discovery (with ties to Autonomy)
·         Major Deliverables: Evolve the Quantum Asset Generative Foundry from an internal prototype to a platform-integrated beta feature. Concretely, by end of Era 2 users (internal power users or select external beta testers) can invoke generative suggestions through the A4Q interface. For example, a user can call an API or CLI command to “Suggest an optimized circuit for problem X” or “Generate a candidate error-correcting code for my qubit noise profile,” and QAGF will return something usable[25]. This requires integrating the QAGF pipeline with our orchestrator (to test/evaluate generated circuits) and trust fabric (to watermark and record provenance of AI-generated IP). Also deliver a knowledge base or library where these generated assets can be stored, searched, and reused (this might tie into the trust fabric or a separate model repository). The QAGF should leverage data from our now-multiple deployments: e.g. use circuits and results accumulated in the ledger as training data to improve suggestions. Major deliverable is a QAGF Beta by era’s end – likely marked as “experimental” but available in the platform for feedback.
·         Prerequisites: Era 1 QAGF prototype results, plus more data gathered from pilots (we should have lots of job metrics and perhaps circuits to learn from). Possibly need dedicated ML infrastructure to train models (could reuse our cloud accounts or require collaboration with a partner lab for compute).
·         DK/SLO Targets: The Discovery plane introduces more qualitative metrics – e.g. usefulness of suggestions – which are hard to encode as invariants. But we will impose some: any QAGF-generated circuit that we propose to user should meet baseline performance (for instance, if it’s meant to solve a problem, it should run within certain depth/gate count). Also, ensure QAGF operations don’t overwhelm the system: maybe limit generation jobs to background threads, etc. A possible invariant: QAGF suggestion latency – e.g. respond with a suggestion within 5 minutes for a moderate problem (so that it’s interactive enough). If integrated into orchestrator runs (like auto-tuning circuits), ensure it doesn’t add more than 10% overhead to compile time on average.
●	Exit Criteria: By 2029, at least a couple of real use cases where QAGF adds value have been demonstrated with external users. For example, a partner used the A4Q QAGF to improve a quantum circuit and achieved better results (say, higher fidelity or fewer qubits). Or an auto-generated error correction scheme was tested on a noisy simulator and outperformed a baseline code. These case studies should be documented. Additionally, the QAGF feature is accessible via the A4Q SDK in a controlled beta (perhaps only enabled for certain users or with an “experimental” flag). Feedback from these early users is collected to guide improvements. Essentially, exit when Discovery moves from pure R&D into the product – even if not fully mature, it’s no longer just internal. Another marker: perhaps a publication or major demo of QAGF at a conference, establishing Invarian as a leader in AI-designed quantum solutions. By integrating QAGF, A4Q starts to differentiate strongly: not just managing quantum jobs, but also helping create them.
●	Era 2 Item I4: External Ecosystem Connectors
·         Plane(s): Cross-Plane (Integration & Standards)
·         Major Deliverables: Solidify A4Q’s integration points such that it can plug into the broader ecosystem easily[26]. This includes building connectors or adapters for popular quantum software frameworks and cloud platforms: e.g. support ingesting circuits from Qiskit or Cirq directly (via QIR adapters) if not already; provide a Terraform or CloudFormation module for deploying A4Q in enterprise clouds; integrate with identity providers (OAuth/OIDC) so that enterprise users can SSO into A4Q’s services, etc. Essentially, make A4Q interoperable. On standards, push further: by era’s end, perhaps our watermark format or ledger schema is on track to become an industry standard (if Era 1 had initial discussions, Era 2 might see formal adoption or at least multiple external implementations starting to appear). Deliver a published spec or open API documentation for A4Q’s key interfaces (/submit, /status, etc.) so third parties can write clients or integrate without our direct involvement. Possibly release an open-source reference of the trust fabric client or parts of the SDK to encourage ecosystem pickup.
·         Prerequisites: Most core features stable so we can focus on polish and integration. Also likely needs feedback from initial external users about what integration they want (e.g. “Can we plug this into our existing CI pipeline?”).
·         DK/SLO Targets: Not directly about SLO tightening, but one implicit target: maintain security and performance even with external integrations. For example, if we integrate with external ID systems, ensure auth remains <50 ms overhead and doesn’t compromise security. Also, by broadening access, we might need to tighten capacity metrics: e.g. system should handle say 5 organizations, 100 users, 1000 jobs/day by era’s end – that could be an informal SLO for scale.
·         Exit Criteria: A4Q can be deployed or used by new organizations with much less friction than in Era 1. Concrete measure: at least one or two new deployments happen largely through documented connectors rather than our devs hand-holding. For instance, an enterprise takes our Terraform module and spins up A4Q in their environment with minimal help, or a cloud provider we partnered with wraps A4Q into their marketplace. Also, external tools start to consider A4Q: e.g. a quantum algorithm researcher can use A4Q’s SDK alongside Qiskit in a notebook seamlessly to leverage watermarking or scheduling. Another exit sign: our trust fabric or watermark spec might be referenced by other companies or included in a standards draft, meaning we successfully seeded our approach externally. Overall, Era 2 should end with A4Q not being a silo, but rather a connected participant in the quantum software ecosystem – making it easier to onboard the larger wave of users expected in Era 3.
Era 3 (Years 4–6, 2029–2031) – Fault-Tolerance Readiness & Broad Adoption
Strategic Goals: Era 3 centers on positioning A4Q as the go-to platform for the dawn of fault-tolerant quantum computing (FTQC) and scaling adoption beyond early partners. By this time (~2030), it’s anticipated that the first fault-tolerant quantum machines may be operational or imminent. A4Q must be ready to manage those at scale (the Autonomy plane must effectively become the “autopilot” for quantum data centers)[27]. Simultaneously, the Governance plane should reach industry standard status – ideally the A4Q trust fabric becomes the default for quantum IP management across the industry (either through open standard or a widely adopted platform)[28]. The Discovery plane should graduate to GA, meaning any user can leverage AI-driven quantum algorithm assistance as a normal feature, not just in beta. On the business side, Era 3 aims for broad adoption: converting design partners into paying customers, onboarding many new users (possibly through cloud marketplace or SaaS offerings), and establishing A4Q as infrastructure in multiple quantum computing centers. By end of Era 3, we target a Version 4.0 release timed with the availability of FTQC hardware, labelled perhaps “Fault-Tolerance Ready,” and an accompanying checkpoint (a major ORR or even external audit) to decide on full production scale-out.
Era 3 Key Items:
·         Era 3 Item I1: FTQC Autopilot Deployment
·         Plane(s): Autonomy
·         Major Deliverables: Full integration and deployment of A4Q’s autonomy features on actual fault-tolerant quantum hardware (or the very best quantum prototypes available)[27]. This means the FTAE (Fault-Tolerant Autonomy Engine) subsystem is now actively controlling a quantum system with minimal human intervention. Specifically: the Magic-State Demand Shaper and resource management modules (developed in Era 2) will be running in production, coordinating the complex dance of logical qubit operations and ancilla resource generation[29][30]. The orchestrator might split into two layers at this point: (1) a real-time low-level scheduler that interfaces closely with a quantum control system (possibly co-located with hardware, handling microsecond decisions), and (2) a higher-level global scheduler that feeds jobs and handles failover across multiple quantum nodes[29]. We deliver tight integration with whatever control-plane APIs the hardware provides. Also, advanced failover at the logical qubit level: if one quantum node fails or a logical qubit is lost, our system can reroute or invoke error recovery protocols automatically[31][32]. In short, A4Q acts as the autopilot for a quantum data center – monitoring all aspects and taking actions to maximize uptime and performance of quantum computations.
·         Prerequisites: Access to or partnership with whoever has early FTQC hardware (e.g. national labs or big quantum vendors). All Era 2 autonomy features in place and tested in simulation. Possibly joint development with hardware teams to ensure our software can hook into their control systems (this might involve custom adapters or deploying part of A4Q on-prem at data centers).
·         DK/SLO Targets: These will reach stringent “enterprise data center” levels: System uptime target 99.99% (planned downtime only), job success rate target very high (≥99% now that error correction is in play, except for genuinely unsolvable issues), and new invariants might be considered, like throughput (jobs per hour) to ensure we can handle commercial loads. Recovery time for a node failure might tighten further (<10 s or even seamless if clustering multiple QPUs). Magic-state production and consumption should be optimized – e.g. if a magic state factory is at capacity, our scheduler ensures it’s never over- or under-utilized by more than X%. Carbon per job might drop dramatically if we can schedule heavy quantum tasks at optimal times (plus by now many quantum data centers might be run on renewable energy).
●	Exit Criteria: A demonstrative operation of A4Q on a real small-scale fault-tolerant quantum computer (if available). For example, by 2031 a national lab has a prototype with 100 logical qubits – we deploy A4Q to manage a long-running quantum computation on it, say a 48-hour algorithm experiment, and it runs to completion with no manual resets, automatically handling errors, calibrations, etc. This would be akin to an autonomous vehicle driving cross-country: a stress test proving the autonomy loop works. Alternatively (or additionally), if no full FTQC exists, we simulate one with high-fidelity and show A4Q managing a virtual quantum data center for weeks continuously. This gives confidence to customers that when they get real FT hardware, A4Q is ready. Another exit marker: we likely call this release v4.0 and position it as “deployable in any quantum data center”. It should coincide with the time FTQC hardware becomes operational, capturing the market need exactly when it arises[33][34]. A formal ORR (perhaps with external auditor or partner oversight) at year 6 will decide if FTAE is production-grade – passing that ORR (and a successful demo as described) triggers a “Go” to promote A4Q as ready for FTQC era[35].
●	Era 3 Item I2: Industry-Standard Quantum Governance
·         Plane(s): Governance
·         Major Deliverables: Achieve broad adoption and formalization of A4Q’s governance model across the industry[28]. By this point, the trust fabric should either be the backbone of an industry-wide quantum IP network or its protocols have been standardized such that even other platforms interoperate with it. Deliverables include launching the trust fabric (or its governance code) as a neutral foundation or consortium project (if deemed beneficial, we might spin it out under Linux Foundation or similar) so that multiple vendors and institutions contribute and use it[36]. Additionally, obtaining certifications or regulatory endorsements: e.g. have our ledger or processes compliant with standards like ISO (for security) or recognized by patent offices or export control agencies for tracking quantum algorithms (stretch goal: maybe in 2030, patent offices accept “quantum algorithm ledger records” as proof of prior art or usage). On a technical side, deliver scale and hardening: trust fabric v5.0 that can handle hundreds of organizations, thousands of transactions per second, with robust privacy layers (some transactions might be confidential yet still verifiable via zero-knowledge proofs, etc.). Essentially, our governance platform becomes the de facto legal system for quantum IP globally.
·         Prerequisites: Era 2’s federated network running with a handful of orgs, plus increasing interest from others. We likely need to invest in documentation, external developer support, and simplifying onboarding (maybe containerized nodes, one-line install for new participants). Standardization efforts from Era 1–2 should be nearing completion (e.g. an IEEE standard draft for quantum circuit provenance might be ready to ratify). Possibly also require external audit/certification: we might go through SOC2/ISO27001 compliance by now to satisfy enterprise and government clients fully (some of this was started in Phase 6 of Era 1, now completed).
·         DK/SLO Targets: Governance at scale: ledger consistency and availability at “four nines” or better globally (99.99% or 99.999% uptime – essentially always on). Tighten ledger latency maybe by using more efficient consensus or tiered ledgers; even with hundreds of nodes, aim for sub-second ledger finality for local ops and few seconds globally. Security invariants become formal: e.g. zero critical vulnerabilities per external audit, 100% of code covered by automated security tests, etc. Also possibly energy efficiency: ensure running this distributed trust network is not too costly (maybe track carbon or compute overhead per transaction and aim to minimize).
●	Exit Criteria: By end of Era 3, Invarian’s governance tech is ubiquitously used or acknowledged. Concretely: at least, say, 50 organizations (quantum hardware makers, cloud providers, research consortia, big enterprises) are part of the trust fabric network or using our standards within their own networks. We might count that the ledger is tracking the IP for, e.g., 10,000 quantum programs across the industry. We should also see external contributions: maybe other vendors are hosting trust fabric nodes or have built services atop our ledger API – indicating an ecosystem. If a formal standard was pursued, maybe it’s approved by now (so our format/protocol is an IEEE or W3C standard). Another measure: government or regulatory buy-in – e.g. a government lab uses our trust fabric to comply with some technology control regulation, showing it’s recognized. Essentially, the network effect is in full swing: new quantum startups join the trust fabric rather than build proprietary solutions. Achieving this entrenchment is vital for moat. In terms of deliverables, by year 6 we release v4.x or v5.0 of the trust fabric addressing any remaining enterprise requirements (like LTS stability, integration with legal processes, etc.)[37]. We want that version to be something that can run stably for years as the industry standard.
●	Era 3 Item I3: Discovery Plane General Availability
·         Plane(s): Discovery
·         Major Deliverables: Transform the Discovery plane into a fully supported, GA product offering[38]. That means the QAGF (and related ML tools like autocalibration and QEC decoders) are robust, documented, and available to all platform users, not just early testers. Key deliverables: an integrated “Quantum App Store” or model repository where vetted algorithms and templates (some AI-generated) can be published and shared securely. Possibly a marketplace where third-party developers can contribute new quantum algorithms which are then watermarked and tracked via our governance (closing the loop on IP monetization for creators). Also deliver refined QAGF models that incorporate years of data – by now, the QAGF could have been trained on a wealth of circuits/outcomes from our network, so it should be much smarter than the initial version. Offer user-friendly interfaces for discovery: e.g. a GUI “Quantum Design Studio” (QDS) where users can visually tweak circuits with AI suggestions assisting (the mention of QDS in orientation suggests a front-end for QAGF). In summary, by the end of Era 3 the Discovery plane isn’t just a novelty but a major reason people use A4Q: to accelerate their R&D with AI. We might brand it as “A4Q Discovery Suite – your quantum AI co-pilot”.
·         Prerequisites: Era 2’s QAGF beta feedback incorporated, and probably more ML R&D (maybe we had to explore different model architectures, get more training data). Also, a need for intuitive UX design – might involve hiring or partnering for a better user interface since previously we were backend-heavy.
·         DK/SLO Targets: Some new ones likely: e.g. Suggestion Quality Metric – maybe track user adoption rate of suggested circuits (if 50% of suggestions get used, that’s good). Hard to quantify precisely, but we can measure things like how often a user runs an AI-suggested circuit vs their original, or improvement metrics. For the platform SLOs: ensure that adding discovery features doesn’t degrade performance – any heavy computations (training models, etc.) are done asynchronously on separate infrastructure so as not to delay job processing. Possibly commit to a responsiveness target: “QAGF returns a useful suggestion in <2 minutes for standard use cases” or similar, to keep users in flow.
●	Exit Criteria: Declare the Discovery plane GA (perhaps version 1.0 of the QAGF product or integration) by around 2031. This would mean all major known issues resolved, documentation in place, and we might even have pricing for it (maybe it’s a premium feature or leads to usage-based rev). External validation: have at least e.g. 5 different organizations use the Discovery tools to achieve something noteworthy – such as designing a new algorithm that wins a competition or gets published. If one of our design partner enterprises uses QAGF to invent a patented algorithm, that’s a huge validation (and ideally managed through our platform, reinforcing the IP loop). Another aspect: integration with common workflows – e.g. A4Q’s discovery features plug into Jupyter notebooks, IDEs, etc., by now, so quantum researchers adopt it widely. We might track that X number of quantum developers are using our SDK regularly for discovery tasks. Basically, success is that the quantum community sees A4Q not just as ops plumbing but also as a creativity tool. At the end of Era 3, our platform covers the full spectrum: from idea (Discovery) -> to execution (Autonomy) -> with trust and compliance (Governance) seamlessly. That positions us extremely strongly for the next stage.
●	Era 3 Item I4: Global Scaling & Platform Packaging
·         Plane(s): Cross-Cutting (Business/Deployment)
·         Major Deliverables: As adoption broadens, adjust the platform’s deployment and business model for scale[39]. This includes creating a “Quantum Data Center Kit” – a version of A4Q packaged for easy deployment in large quantum data centers or cloud regions. It might be a combination of software and hardware guidelines so that a new FTQC center can spin up A4Q as their management layer quickly (think of it like OpenStack for quantum centers). This kit could involve reference architectures, automated installers, and tuning for high-throughput scenarios. Also, likely deliver a managed cloud service version of A4Q by this time: Invarian could offer A4Q as a service (SaaS) for those who don’t want to self-host, especially for smaller users who just want to connect to others’ QPUs (we may run a multi-tenant cloud ourselves with A4Q for SMEs to sign up). Another deliverable is cost optimization & billing features – by now we need to incorporate billing support (for our SaaS or for customers to bill their internal divisions) including usage reports, etc., turning the platform into a commercial product. On the technology front, scaling also means testing with many parallel jobs and many concurrent users: delivering improvements like a more distributed orchestrator (multiple orchestrator nodes balancing loads for many QPUs), and maybe sharding the trust fabric if needed for throughput. Essentially, ensure the platform can handle a 10x or 100x increase in workload from the pilot stage.
·         Prerequisites: Widespread interest or user base to justify SaaS offering; capital perhaps to invest in running such service (by era 3 we might have raised Series B/C to fuel growth). Technical work from prior eras on multi-cloud, multi-tenant sets the stage. We also may incorporate feedback from partners who want easier deployment (some might have said “we want to use this, but it’s hard to set up”; by now we solve that at scale).
·         DK/SLO Targets: Extremely robust operations: commit to e.g. 99.999% uptime for critical components for paying enterprise customers (this might involve redundancy across continents). Throughput targets: e.g. system can handle 100k jobs per month, or X TB of telemetry data, etc. Also, cost efficiency becomes a tracked metric – e.g. overhead of running A4Q (compute and storage) is at most Y% of the quantum compute costs, ensuring the solution is cost-effective. Possibly introduce an invariant around latency at scale: ensure that even with 100 concurrent jobs, compile p95 or ledger p95 remain within 1.5x of targets, etc.
·         Exit Criteria: By end of Era 3, A4Q is available in forms that any serious quantum player can access: either they download/deploy the kit or simply sign up for the managed service. A key marker: at least a couple of major quantum hardware vendors or cloud providers have integrated A4Q into their offerings (e.g. maybe one cloud offers “Powered by Invarian A4Q” as an option for their QC service, or a hardware vendor ships an A4Q appliance with their quantum computer). We should have multiple production deployments globally – for instance, 3+ quantum data centers on different continents running A4Q to manage operations. From a business perspective, we likely have a steady revenue stream by now from licensing or SaaS. The platform’s reputation is such that newcomers to quantum choose to use A4Q rather than build in-house solutions. In other words, we become the default backend for quantum computing ops similarly to how e.g. Kubernetes became default for container orchestration. Achieving this by 2031 (as FTQC emerges) means we ride that wave rather than chase it.
Era 4 (Years 6–8, 2031–2033) – Optimization, Expansion & Maturity
Strategic Goals: By Era 4, the platform and industry are entering a more mature phase. Fault-tolerant quantum computers are (hopefully) operational at some scale; our goal now is to optimize and refine the platform for efficiency, cost-effectiveness, and ubiquity. This era likely sees increased competition or alternatives, so we solidify our moat by being the most performant and integrated solution. Focus areas: Autonomy: maximize performance (throughput, scheduling efficiency) and perhaps incorporate advanced machine learning to continually improve operations (AIOps for quantum); Governance: focus on sustainment and compliance at scale – ensuring our system meets all regulatory requirements and remains the trusted ledger as volume grows, potentially obtaining formal legal status for certain functions (like time-stamping IP for patent priority, etc.); Cross-plane: integrating quantum-classical workflows seamlessly – by now, quantum computing will often be part of hybrid HPC workflows, so A4Q must coordinate with classical compute orchestrators (Kubernetes, Slurm, etc.) to manage end-to-end jobs. We also consider global expansion: supporting more languages, regional requirements (data sovereignty issues solved thoroughly), and pushing standards to formal adoption if not done. This era might not introduce radically new features, but rather industrialize the platform: it should run like reliable infrastructure in the background of the quantum economy. By end of Era 4, A4Q might be version 5.x or approaching 6.0, and quantum computing itself will be closer to mainstream, so our product should be “boring” in the best way – stable, trusted, and pervasive.
Era 4 Key Items:
·         Era 4 Item I1: Maximal Efficiency Orchestration
·         Plane(s): Autonomy
·         Major Deliverables: Focus on optimizing performance and throughput of quantum job execution at scale[40]. This involves incorporating advanced techniques like machine learning-driven scheduling: analyze historical run data to predict the best scheduling decisions (which QPU yields best results for a given circuit type at a given time? which jobs can be run back-to-back to reduce calibration overhead? etc.) and incorporate those into the orchestrator’s logic. Possibly develop a “quantum job compiler” that packages multiple logical tasks into one execution if it saves time (similar to how classical systems batch tasks). Also, optimize resource usage: ensure that expensive operations like magic state distillation are shared across jobs where possible, reducing waste. Another deliverable could be latency reduction modes: if some users need ultra-low-latency (for interactive quantum computing?), we tailor scheduling to prioritize that (maybe holding a QPU in ready state). On the hardware integration side, implement deeper integration with QPU control software to cut out overhead – for example, bypassing some cloud layers to talk more directly to QPU for speed. Essentially, by the end of this item, the overhead our platform introduces is minimal, and it may actually improve overall throughput of a quantum data center via intelligent scheduling. Efficiency might also consider energy – scheduling to minimize idle time or to run when energy is cheapest (tying into carbon but also cost savings). The deliverable is a “Quantum Optimizer” module as part of FTAE that continuously tunes operations for maximum output.
·         Prerequisites: Lots of run data (which by year 8 we will have), a stable baseline to improve upon, and possibly new research in ML for scheduling. Need close collaboration with QPU ops teams to understand where bottlenecks are (maybe network latency, cryo cool-down times, etc.) so we can optimize around them.
·         DK/SLO Targets: Stretch or redefine performance invariants: maybe target doubling the effective throughput of QPUs under management (compared to naive scheduling). Invariants could shift to emphasize efficiency metrics like QPU utilization rate – aim for e.g. ≥90% utilization of available quantum compute capacity (if a QPU is idle and jobs are waiting, that’s inefficiency we aim to eliminate). Maintain or improve existing latency SLOs even as load increases. Possibly commit to cost metrics: e.g. overhead CPU cost of orchestrator per 1000 jobs is <X, meaning we scale efficiently on classical side as well.
●	Exit Criteria: Data demonstrating significant improvement due to our optimization features. For instance, after deploying the ML scheduler, a quantum center reports they got 15% more jobs done per day with no hardware changes, or average queue wait times dropped by 50% during peak hours. We should have case studies from at least two large-scale deployments quantifying these gains. Another criterion: AIOps working – the system can automatically detect suboptimal patterns (like a certain QPU always slower for a type of job) and adjust without manual input. If we can show that year 6 vs year 8, under similar workload, A4Q now handles, say, 10x jobs with same hardware, that’s a huge success. Also perhaps by this era, we integrate into cloud providers’ scheduling: maybe our orchestrator plugs into a global scheduler that also manages classical tasks (ensuring, for example, classical post-processing servers spin up just in time to catch quantum results, etc.). Essentially, we exit Era 4 with A4Q being extremely optimized, automated, and symbiotic with the surrounding infrastructure, leaving minimal room for a competitor to claim they can do it better.
●	Era 4 Item I2: Sustainment & Compliance LTS
·         Plane(s): Governance
·         Major Deliverables: Enter a “maintenance and ubiquity” phase for the governance plane[37]. The trust fabric by now is widely used, so our focus is on long-term support (LTS), rock-solid stability, and compliance with any and all regulations in our operating domains. Deliver a v5.0 LTS of the trust fabric: this could be a version we pledge to support for many years with bug fixes, as many enterprise and government users will rely on it deeply. Key features include full integration with global compliance regimes: e.g. the ledger can automatically generate compliance reports (for ISO, for export control, for GDPR if personal data is involved in logs, etc.). Possibly achieve formal certifications – e.g. get the platform certified by external auditors for certain safety or security standards (if not already done earlier, by now it’s a must-have for lagging adopters). Also implement features for data governance: maybe integrating secure multi-party computation or zero-knowledge proofs so that organizations can participate in the network without exposing sensitive info, thereby meeting privacy laws while still sharing a ledger. On sustainment, ensure backward compatibility and smooth upgrades as the network grows – we might freeze some APIs as standards and maintain them for compatibility while introducing new ones carefully. Another deliverable: create detailed governance playbooks for industries – e.g. a template of trust fabric policies for healthcare vs finance (to accelerate adoption in those verticals, covering their specific compliance needs). Essentially, treat the trust fabric not as a new product now but as critical infrastructure that must be absolutely dependable and adaptable to legal requirements.
·         Prerequisites: Achieved broad adoption (Era 3), identified all major compliance requirements by working with large customers, matured dev processes to handle LTS branching, etc. Possibly needed to build a dedicated support team for trust fabric by now.
·         DK/SLO Targets: Focus on qualitative SLOs like reliability and auditability: target 0 unplanned downtime across years (five nines uptime goal – effectively continuous availability with rolling upgrades). Security invariants: maybe commit that any critical vulnerability will be patched within 48 hours and all nodes updated (requires coordination in the network – a challenge we manage via good design, e.g. hot-swappable consensus nodes). Compliance metrics: e.g. 100% of data retention policies are enforced correctly (no data kept beyond allowed time), etc. Essentially, no misses on governance obligations.
