●	Testing governance exports: you request an audit bundle for a time period; we supply a tenant‑scoped package with contracts, ledger entries, watermark proofs, and replay scripts; your internal or external auditors validate that it meets their needs.
●	Onboarding additional internal groups at your org (e.g., another lab team, another business unit) using our multi‑tenant features, mapping access control to your identity system.
●	If appropriate, running a small Q‑Market‑style pilot: e.g., sharing circuits or models between teams or orgs under explicit contracts defined through our system.
At this point, Invarian is part of your operational and governance stack, not just a toy. You still control:
●	Which workloads go through Invarian vs direct.
●	Which parts of our stack run on your infra vs in our hosted environment.
●	Which policies and DK targets you adopt as hard vs soft.
We bring: evidence, automation, and a clear roadmap for how your quantum/autonomous systems can grow safely over the next eras.
________________________________________
3.3 Assurance & Co‑Ownership
The same DK gates and evidence machinery we use internally are designed to protect you.
●	DK gates as shared guardrails:
 When we say “ledger append p95 ≤ 120 ms” or “job success ≥ 92%”, we’re not asking you to accept those on faith. We instrument your workloads and share the dashboards. If we under‑perform, you see it. If we tighten a gate, it’s in config and in CI, not hidden. You can even choose to adopt those same numbers internally as part of your own SLOs.
●	Evidence bundles as mutual protection:
 Our ORR and governance export bundles are useful for both sides:
○	They show that we’ve done due diligence (chaos tests, replay drills, watermark audits).
○	They give you artifacts to show your stakeholders or regulators when something goes wrong (or right).
 In a dispute scenario (e.g., “did this circuit run here?”, “who had access?”), the trust fabric becomes a neutral witness you can rely on.
●	PQC, watermarking, and contracts as shared obligations:
 We wrap all communications with post‑quantum TLS and store keys in KMS/HSM structures. That reduces your exposure to future cryptographic breaks—important if you’re a cloud provider or lab with long‑term secrets. Watermarks and trust contracts ensure that if we or someone else mishandles IP, it’s detectable. These are not our obligations alone; they encode joint responsibilities which you co‑specify and we operationalize.
We are deliberately conservative about hardware timelines. We do not require fault‑tolerant hardware to be useful:
●	Today, we can already reduce wasted runs, provide provenance, and standardize interfaces on noisy NISQ devices.
●	If FTQC takes longer, the need for governance and reproducibility only grows—as more experiments are run and more money is spent on incremental progress.
●	If FTQC arrives faster, our Autonomy engine is positioned to manage those expensive resources efficiently on day one—because we’ve been simulating and co‑designing for that world for years.
Our thesis is: regardless of hardware timing, assurance and governance are non‑optional once quantum/autonomous systems matter. We give you a way to get ahead of that curve, without betting the farm on a particular FTQC date.
________________________________________
3.4 Close: Asks and Next Steps
A Phase 0–2 design partner looks like:
●	You have (or will have) quantum or autonomy‑flavored workloads that you care about beyond toy demos.
●	You either run hardware/services, or you rely on them for important experiments or R&D.
●	You see value in provenance, replay, and safety rails, even before FTQC arrives.
What we ask you to commit:
●	Access: a dev/sandbox environment with appropriate credentials (test QPU account, staging cluster, or simulation endpoints) so we can integrate our platform.
●	Time: a small cross‑functional group (1–3 engineers + 1 stakeholder) for regular working sessions (biweekly cadence is typical) over the first 6–12 months.
●	Feedback: candid, technically‑grounded feedback on where our DK targets, products (QAGF/FTAE) and contracts align with your reality—and where they don’t. We will tune based on that.
What you get:
●	Early access to a governed, multi‑backend quantum operations fabric without having to build it yourself.
●	Influence over how trust contracts, provenance, and markets evolve; we expect design partners to shape the governance model and, eventually, the standard.
●	Visibility and co‑authorship opportunities: case studies, whitepapers, joint presentations demonstrating how your hardware or use‑cases are made “boringly reliable” under Invarian.
●	A head start on operational and governance readiness that others will scramble for later, once regulators and high‑stakes applications catch up.
The very next step is simple: agree on a pilot scope and timeline (e.g., “Bell‑state + one real use‑case over 6 months”) and define the initial DK‑style metrics we’ll track together. Everything else—tooling, instrumentation, contracts—flows from that shared baseline.
________________________________________
4. INVESTOR‑FACING NARRATIVE (SEED → SERIES A/B)
4.1 Why Invarian Exists Now
Invarian wasn’t born out of a pitch deck; it evolved from the A4Q archive—years of hands‑on work building compilers, watermarks, ledgers, and ops patterns for quantum systems. The team spent a long time in “pre‑company mode” answering hard questions:
●	How do you watermark quantum circuits invisibly but reliably, with negligible overhead?
●	How do you enforce SLOs (latency, reliability) when underlying hardware is inherently noisy?
●	How do you design a ledger and policy engine that can stand up to regulatory scrutiny in a future where quantum jobs matter for finance, defense, or infrastructure?
This archive became L0. On top of that, we built the A4Q platform (Quantum_Kuberjelly and trust fabric)—L1. We then designed a 10‑year integration and release plan (V10) that treats quantum not as an isolated technology but as part of a broader ecosystem of AI, autonomy, and governance.
The inflection point now is clear:
●	AI agency: increasingly autonomous AI systems are making decisions and orchestrating compute; they need verifiable control planes and guardrails, especially when tying into non‑deterministic hardware (like quantum) or safety‑sensitive domains.
●	Post‑quantum risk: even before large‑scale FTQC, there is pressure to adopt PQC for sensitive systems. Our trust fabric was designed from day one with PQC in mind, making us an early answer to “how do I secure quantum pipelines before quantum can break my crypto?”.
●	Emerging QPU ecosystems: multiple cloud providers and hardware vendors are offering quantum access, but they lack a common assurance layer—no standard way to prove what ran where, under what constraints, with what reliability.
Invarian exists to fill that gap. We are not “yet another quantum SDK” or a speculative hardware play. We are building the assurance and operations layer for quantum and autonomous systems: the combination of invariants, ledgers, and controllers that make this technology usable at institutional scale.
The opportunity is akin to early cloud orchestration and security tooling—but for quantum:
●	If quantum works, someone must provide the Kubernetes + HashiCorp + PCI compliance equivalent for this new stack. That’s us.
●	If quantum works slower than expected, organizations will still spend heavily on experiments and pilots; they will still need visibility, governance, and security. We monetize that experimentation layer.
Our edge is that we have already done the hard thinking and built much of the L1 platform; we’re not proposing to start now.
________________________________________
4.2 What Is Already Real (V8 / V9)
Today, we have a V8 GA baseline (with V9 on the way) that is unusually mature for a project at this stage:
●	Design Key (DK) invariants baked into code and CI.
 We have ~10 numeric SLOs (compile p95, ledger p95, PQC overhead, watermark TPR, Δdepth overhead, uptime, recovery, audit coverage, carbon/job, success rate) encoded in a shared module and enforced in pipelines. Every build is run against synthetic workloads, and the build fails if any gate is violated. This is not typical research code; it’s production‑style quality gating applied to a nascent area.
●	Ops‑Gold discipline.
 V8/V9 incorporate observability, chaos experiments, and replay tools. A lab ORR‑Lite already exists: we can spin up the stack, run load tests, induce orchestrator/db failures, and show metrics that match our claims. We have runbook‑level artifacts for bringup and teardown.
●	Trust fabric and orchestrator.
 These are running as real services:
○	Trust fabric: API endpoints to seal jobs with contracts, write to a ledger, expose audit logs, and verify watermarks.
○	Orchestrator: job submission, status, and backend abstraction (simulators now, real QPUs in early integrations).
 Both export metrics for DK invariants and are built with multi‑tenant support in mind.
●	Compiler + watermarking.
 We have a QIR‑based compiler pipeline with a working watermark pass demonstrating:
○	Watermark TPR ~100% on lab tests.
○	Δdepth overhead negligible (<0.5%).
○	Compile times well under budget (~tens of ms p95 on benchmarks).
●	Evidence bundles.
 We are in a position to produce small “data room” bundles even at seed stage: metrics exports, replay scripts, watermark test reports, and chaos test logs. That’s not common. It significantly de‑risks claims about performance, reliability, and replay.
In short, we are past the prototype threshold: we have a real stack with SLO‑backed behavior, not a loose collection of scripts. Seed investors aren’t funding a concept; they’re funding the hardening and commercialization of a working baseline.
________________________________________
4.3 The 0–24 Month Plan and Value Inflection
The 0–24 month execution program has three phases, each with its own value inflection and fundraising relevance.
Phase 0 (0–3 months): Lab sanity & repeatability.
●	Goals:
○	Freeze DK gates into CI and configs.
○	Provide one‑command dev stack and lab K8s deploy.
○	Demonstrate canonical E2E scenario (Bell‑state) with watermark and replay.
●	KPIs:
○	All DK gates measured and enforced in CI; ORR‑Lite executed.
○	Developer productivity (e.g., new dev can run full stack in <60 minutes).
●	Funding relevance:
○	This is mostly pre‑seed/early seed execution, providing technical de‑risking evidence to support a Seed or Seed+ close (if not already closed).
Phase 1 (3–12 months): Ship real, small Discovery/Autonomy products.
●	Goals:
○	Integrate with at least one real QPU/backend.
○	Release QAGF v0.1 and FTAE v0/v1 features; first design partner sandbox up.
○	Expose DK dashboards in partner env; begin ORR‑style reviews.
●	KPIs:
○	1–2 design partners with deployed sandbox envs.
○	X jobs executed via Invarian on real QPUs (target: hundreds to low thousands).
○	Stable DK metrics in at least one partner environment (e.g., job success ≥ 92%, ledger p95 ≤ 120 ms).
●	Funding relevance:
○	This is where a Seed → Series A story lives:
■	Real external usage,
■	Reference integrations with a recognizable hardware/cloud vendor,
■	First evidence that QAGF/FTAE provide value (e.g., improvement vs baseline in sample workload).
○	We’d expect a Series A raise toward the end of Phase 1, backed by partner logos, integration demos, and SLO evidence.
Phase 2 (12–24 months): Harden, integrate, and set up Governance/Markets arc.
●	Goals:
○	Full ORR‑style gating on v1.x LTS.
○	Multi‑tenant support, multi‑partner deployments (2–3 design partners).
○	Governance export v1, external security audit, and first paying customers.
●	KPIs:
○	design partners: 3+; 1–2 paying customers under SLA.
○	DK SLO adherence across environments (e.g., ≥95% of time all invariants in green).
○	External security audit passed (no open critical/high).
○	QAGF v1 and FTAE v1 used in at least one production or pre‑production workload.
●	Funding relevance:
○	This sets up a strong Series B:
■	We’d have LTS‑grade platform,
■	multi‑tenant + multi‑env deployments,
■	paying customers,
■	an emerging governance/network angle (multi‑org pilots),
■	and traction data (jobs, workloads, markets).
○	Series B capital would be used to scale go‑to‑market, expand product surface (Q‑ARFN/Q‑Markets), and push into FT‑readiness (B2/B3 bands).
Investors are not asked to fund “wild quantum moonshots”; they’re funding a measured, invariants‑based expansion from lab platform → pilot product → multi‑tenant governance system, with clearly defined intermediate metrics.
________________________________________
4.4 Moats, Standards, and Ecosystems
Our defensibility is multifaceted:
1.	DK culture and evidence‑driven ops.
 Many teams can replicate code; few will replicate a culture that treats SLOs as first‑class citizens from the beginning. DK invariants mean:
○	Every change is evaluated against numeric gates.
○	We accumulate a long‑term history of performance and reliability data.
○	We can offer guarantees (SLAs, audit‑ready exports) early in the market.
That culture is an intangible but very real moat: rewriting our code is easier than cloning our operational discipline.
2.	Trust fabric as infrastructure.
 The ledger, contracts, and watermarking system are designed to become an ecosystem layer, not a point product:
○	They can be implemented as a standalone service (e.g., governance as a service).
○	They can be standardized and adopted by others (we are already aligning with QIR and other emerging standards).
○	They can be used as a “neutral ground” between competing providers.
Once a critical mass of quantum users and providers rely on our trust fabric (or its standard form), switching away becomes hard—not because of API friction but because of trust and audit trail continuity. That’s stronger than typical vendor lock‑in.
3.	Data network effects.
 As we orchestrate more workloads, we collect:
○	Error profiles per backend,
○	Performance outcomes for circuits,
○	Anonymized usage patterns.
This data fuels QAGF and FTAE: better suggestions, better anomaly detection, better scheduling. Competitors without that visibility will be flying blind. Even if they copy our algorithms, they won’t match our trained heuristics and risk models.
4.	Standards and alliances.
 Our V10 plan includes contributing to and eventually co‑governing standards for:
○	Quantum circuit provenance (watermarks & ledgers).
○	Governance contracts (usage constraints, cross‑org sharing).
○	Operational metrics for quantum reliability.
If successful, this gives us standards‑based lock‑in: we help define the rules and can ship reference implementations. Partners and regulators become allies in our moat: they will resist fragmentation that weakens the assurance guarantees we provide.
5.	Institutional positioning.
 We’re explicitly not trying to replace hardware or big cloud providers. We are the neutral infra that makes them more acceptable to regulators and enterprises. That aligns us with their interests, not against them. Over time, those partners and regulators become a source of lock‑in: they will push for “Invarian‑style” assurance, making it hard for other approaches to gain foothold without matching our rigor.
________________________________________
4.5 Risk, Timing, and Quantum Realism
We’re realistic about quantum hardware timelines:
●	Risk: FTQC may arrive later than optimistic timelines. NISQ devices may plateau in practical value for many workloads. Some investors worry that this would sink a quantum‑centric company.
●	Mitigation: Invarian’s value is less about raw quantum advantage and more about managing uncertainty and obligations around these systems:
○	Even if NISQ remains dominant for a while, labs, enterprises, and governments will still run large experiments with real budgets attached. Those experiments need reproducibility, governance, and security. We can already monetize that.
○	Our PQC and governance machinery is useful even for classical/AI systems; we can extend the trust fabric to cover hybrid flows (classical + quantum + AI decisions). That gives us an adjacently valuable market (autonomous system auditing) if quantum stalls.
●	Upside: If FTQC arrives in the 6–8 year window:
○	Quantum data centers will require exactly what we are building: multi‑tenant orchestration, SLO‑backed operations, and governance.
○	The cost of errors will skyrocket (running a large FT computation can be expensive); our ability to orchestrate efficiently and provide evidence will be worth a premium.
○	We will already have relationships with hardware/cloud providers and regulators, positioning us as the obvious choice for FTQC stacks.
We are effectively building an option on the growth of quantum, with positive expected value even if quantum grows slower than hoped, and strongly convex upside if FTQC appears on the timeline we model. Capital is used to build a platform that:
●	Works now for NISQ‑level and AI/automation assurance.
●	Scales later to FTQC without needing a ground‑up redesign.
________________________________________
4.6 Close: Capital Use & Milestone Mapping
Over the next 24 months, capital will be allocated roughly along these lines (order of magnitude proportions, not precise budgets):
●	Platform & Ops (≈35–40%)
○	Strengthen the core platform and DK gating (Phase 0/1).
○	Harden multi‑tenant, KMS/PQC, ORR, and LTS packaging (Phase 2).
○	Hire 1–2 additional devops/SREs and 1–2 platform engineers to keep infra ahead of product.
●	Products (Discovery & Autonomy) (≈25–30%)
○	Mature QAGF from v0.1 → v1 (and toward v2 in B1).
○	Build FTAE v1 (and early v2 features) for AA, anomaly detection, and scheduling.
○	Hire ML + runtime engineers to support these tracks.
●	Governance & Security (≈15–20%)
○	Evolve contracts and trust fabric, implement governance export, run external audits.
○	Participate in standards groups and design governance network experiments.
○	Hire 1–2 security/governance engineers.
●	Ecosystem & GTM (≈15–20%)
○	Support design partner engagements (technical account management, integration support).
○	Build documentation, SDKs, DevEx tooling.
○	Begin building a small GTM org (PM, technical marketing) nearer to Series A/B.
Mapping to milestones:
●	Seed → Phase 0/early Phase 1:
○	Deliver DK‑gated lab platform, real QPU integration PoC, and a first design partner sandbox.
○	Use this to close or complete Seed, possibly with extension if needed.
●	Phase 1 → Series A:
○	Ship QAGF v0.1 and FTAE v0 into a design partner environment.
○	Show real usage metrics (jobs, DK adherence) and 1–2 partner logos.
○	Raise Series A to scale product + governance and add 2–3 more partners.
●	Phase 2 → Series B:
○	ORR‑Full gating, v1.x LTS, multi‑tenant, 2–3 design partners plus first paying customers.
○	Governance export v1 and external security audit complete.
○	Prepare Series B around the “governed quantum ops platform” narrative with early revenue and strong technical evidence.
Beyond Series B, the 2‑year bands (B1–B4) define where to invest:
●	B1 (Years 3–4): multi‑cloud broker, QAGF v2, multi‑tenant SaaS.
●	B2 (Years 5–6): FT‑readiness, governance network v1, standards maturation.
●	B3 (Years 7–8): FT fleets and Q‑Markets.
●	B4 (Years 9–10): full autonomy and consolidation.
At each step, the investment thesis remains consistent: we’re turning a deeply thought‑through archive and a working platform into the durable assurance layer that quantum and autonomous ecosystems will rely on—using a plan that is both technically grounded and operationally executable.
 0. Orientation Snapshot (Very Short)
●	Three Product Planes & Pillars: The platform is structured into three interlocking planes – Discovery (QAGF/QDS) for quantum asset generation & algorithm discovery, Governance (QIPL/QTF) for quantum IP provenance, policy, and compliance, and Autonomy (FTAE/AQO) for fault-tolerant operations and orchestration[1]. Each plane aligns to a major product pillar: Discovery drives quantum content innovation, Governance provides a trust fabric (“legal system” for quantum workflows), and Autonomy delivers a “quantum data center OS” for reliable, optimized quantum computing[1][2].
●	Core Services & Monorepo Modules: Invarian’s A4Q platform is a unified monorepo with modular components mapped to these planes[3]. Key directories include /services (Go microservices like trust-fabric – the policy engine and provenance ledger with APIs like /v1/seal, /v1/append, etc.; and orchestrator – job scheduler handling /v1/submit, /v1/status flows)[4]. /platform houses common runtime components (Rust-based compiler passes: qir-adapters for portable IR integration, and watermark-pass for invisible IP watermarking[5]). /ml contains AI/ML modules (e.g. autocal-rl for automated calibration via RL, qec-decoder for error-correction decoding) – initially experimental in GA but foundation for Autonomy features[6]. Supporting modules include /sdk (TypeScript CLI & client libraries for developers), /infra (compose and helm charts for deployment), /ops (observability stack – metrics endpoints, dashboards, alerts), and /standards (schemas and IR extensions for watermarking and provenance)[7][8].
●	Design Key Invariants (DK SLOs): The architecture is governed by ~10 immutable SLO invariants encoding performance, accuracy, and reliability targets[9]. Examples include compile latency p95 ≤ 250 ms, ledger append p95 ≤ 120 ms, PQC encryption overhead ≤ 20 ms, watermark detection true-positive ≥ 99% with ≤0.5% depth overhead, audit log coverage ≥ 99%, job success rate ≥ 92%, system uptime ≥ 99.5% with failover recovery < 60 s, and carbon cost ≤ 55 gCO₂/job[9][10]. All 10/10 DK invariants were met or exceeded by the GA baseline (v1.1/V8) – e.g. actual compile p95 ~47 ms and watermark TPR ~100% were demonstrated[11]. These invariants are hard-coded and enforced via fail-closed CI gates: any build that violates a DK threshold is blocked[12]. This evidence-driven SLO discipline (with stretch “Ops Gold” goals for safety margin) underpins trust in the platform’s performance[13][14].
●	GA Baseline (V8) and Next Versions: The current GA baseline (V8, internal ~v1.1) established the core architecture and met all DK gates[11]. The 10-Year Integration Plan (V10) defines five major eras (in ~2-year increments) from 2025 to 2035[15]. Each era corresponds to a version milestone (v2.0 through v6.0) and focuses on advancing each plane. For example, Era 0–2 (2025–27) targets post-GA stabilization and pilot deployments leading to v2.0; Era 2–4 (2027–29) emphasizes scaling to multi-tenant, multi-cloud ecosystems; Era 4–6 (2029–31) focuses on fault-tolerance readiness and broad adoption; Era 6–8 (2031–33) stresses optimization at scale and ecosystem expansion; and Era 8–10 (2033–35) achieves full autonomy and industry standardization as the capstone (v6.0)[15][16]. Each era has specific goals, new capabilities, tightened SLO budgets, and Go/Kill checkpoints to ensure evidence-based progress[17]. The plan is deliberately phased to reduce risk – proving out invariants and value in Era 1, then expanding scope confidently in subsequent eras.
●	GA Services and APIs: By V8 GA, all critical services run in containers with defined APIs: e.g. Trust Fabric exposes /v1/seal (attach a quantum job to an IP trust contract and watermark), /v1/verify (verify watermark or provenance), and ledger access endpoints; Orchestrator provides /v1/submit (submit quantum jobs), /v1/status (job status/results), and both services export /metrics for Prometheus[4][18]. The SDK/CLI (a4qctl) allows users to submit circuits, specify contracts (region, privacy, etc.), and monitor execution in a user-friendly way[18]. All client-server calls are secured with mutual TLS plus post-quantum cryptography (PQC) for future-proof security[19]. These interfaces and security measures are key integration points for partners and users from day one.
●	Reliability & Observability Built-in: The GA system already runs in HA (active-active across zones/regions) – demonstrating sub-second failover and <60 s regional recovery in tests[20]. Each microservice implements rich telemetry: a /metrics endpoint exposing runtime metrics for each DK invariant (latencies, error rates, uptime, carbon per job, etc.)[21][22]. Standard Grafana dashboards and alerts monitor these in real time (e.g. alert if ledger append p95 > 120 ms or watermark TPR drops below 0.99)[22][23]. This “measure everything” approach means that from the outset, we have a quantitative pulse on system health and can enforce SLOs continuously.
●	Monorepo Ownership & Evidence Mapping: Each package/module in the monorepo has a clear owner and required evidence artifacts, tracked via unique A4Q IDs. For instance, the compiler (platform/qir-adapters) is owned by the Compiler Lead and must produce evidence of compile latency p95 ≤ [target] ms and Δdepth ≤ 0.5% (e.g. a compiler perf report artifact)[24][25]. The watermark pass is owned by Governance Lead with invariant watermark TPR ≥ 0.99 with ≤0.5% overhead, evidenced by watermark test suites[26]. The trust-fabric (Governance Lead) must show ledger append p95 ≤ 120 ms and audit log coverage ≥ 99%, with ORR binder logs as proof[27]. The orchestrator (Runtime Lead) is accountable for job success ≥ [92%] and carbon cost ≤ [55g], evidenced by carbon-aware routing trials and hybrid workload demos[28]. This cross-walk of module→owner→KPI→artifact (detailed in the A4Q Archive) ensures every feature has measurable goals and proof of completion, fostering an evidence-driven culture.
●	Major GA Components: To summarize the key tech pieces in V8 GA (which form the baseline for all future work): (1) A portable IR compiler with QIR/LLVM integration and watermark injection – ensuring hardware-agnostic execution and IP tagging within a strict overhead budget[5]. (2) A Trust Fabric ledger (Go service + Postgres) that immutably records all quantum job transactions and enforces policy via OPA (contracts for data residency, usage rights, etc.)[4]. (3) An Orchestrator (Go) that abstracts quantum backends (initially using simulated backends) and schedules jobs based on real-time factors (cost, queue length, and carbon intensity)[29][30]. (4) Machine learning pilots: AutoCal-RL and QEC decoder running offline to calibrate qubits and improve error correction – not yet in prod loop, but providing insights and ready to be productized in Autonomy plane[31]. (5) A DevOps stack with one-click Docker Compose for local use and Helm charts for Kubernetes, plus integrated Prometheus/Grafana for monitoring[7]. Together, these pieces delivered a credible v1.1 platform that hits its performance targets and is ready to be hardened for external use.
●	10-Year Vision Highlights: By Year 10 (2035, ~v6.0), Invarian’s platform aims to become the de facto quantum operations layer across the industry[32]. In the Autonomy plane this means a fully autonomous quantum control system managing heterogeneous quantum resources with minimal human intervention (auto-scheduling, dynamic error re-routing, predictive maintenance)[33][34]. In Governance, it means a ubiquitous trust fabric – potentially standardized via an alliance or foundation – that ensures IP rights, compliance, and security for all major quantum workflows (like the TCP/IP of quantum governance)[35][36]. In Discovery, it means an AI-powered “Quantum Design Studio” that routinely contributes new quantum circuits or error correction strategies, accelerating innovation. The roadmap’s later eras (6–10 years) anticipate industry consolidation: by then either Invarian’s tech is embedded in larger ecosystems (cloud providers, national programs) or operates as neutral infrastructure, with clear plans by Year 8 whether to IPO, partner, or be acquired depending on traction[16]. The execution program that follows translates this high-level vision into concrete steps, checkpoints, and narratives to ensure we get there with evidence-backed confidence.
________________________________________
1. 0–24 Month Execution Program (Phase-by-Phase Checklists)
We divide the first 24 months post-GA into six 4-month phases (Phase 1 = months 0–4, ..., Phase 6 = months 20–24). Each phase has specific goals and a set of concrete task blocks. Bolded tasks are mission-critical for achieving a credible “v1.0.0 production-ready” system. All tasks follow a structured format (Owner, Timebox, Steps, Acceptance tests, etc.) to enforce evidence-driven completion. The emphasis is on eliminating any remaining “simulated” or stubbed components from the GA baseline, hardening core workflows (seal/submit/status, telemetry, etc.), integrating real-world dependencies (PQC key management, real QPU backends, live carbon data feeds), and establishing a repeatable deployment + monitoring pipeline. By the end of Phase 6, the platform should support a credible external pilot deployment with full observability on all DK invariants in a production-like environment[37][38].
Phase 1 (Months 0–4) – Post-GA Hardening & DevOps Foundation
Phase Goals (0–4mo): Establish a robust development and deployment baseline, enforce real-world configurations (eliminate dev-mode shortcuts), and shore up any GA weaknesses. Key objectives: (1) “Real Mode” Security – wire in real post-quantum cryptography (PQC) key management (e.g. HSM/KMS) and disable any dev/test-only backdoors[39]; (2) DK Metrics & Telemetry – instrument every service to measure all DK invariants at runtime (compiler latency, ledger latency, etc.) and export them via /metrics for monitoring[40]; (3) Deployment Pipeline – achieve one-click local bringup (Docker Compose) and a reproducible cluster deploy (Helm on K8s) with basic monitoring included[22]; (4) Stability Testing – conduct initial chaos and load tests to validate uptime and failover behavior meets specs (e.g. 99.5% uptime, <60 s recovery)[41].
·         Task ID: P1-T1 – Freeze DK Constants & Enforce “Real Mode” Defaults
 Plane: Cross-Cutting (applies to all services)
 Package(s): services/common/constants.go, global env/config
 Type: PROD-REQUIRED (foundation for all invariants)
 Owner: PMO Lead (with SRE support)
 Timebox: 2 weeks (Month 0–1)
 Dependencies: GA codebase (V8) available; CI pipeline running
 Steps:
·         Define all Design Key (DK) invariants as constants in a common module (if not done already) – e.g. CompileP95Millis=250, LedgerAppendP95=120, etc.[42]. Also capture “Ops Gold” stretch targets (e.g. STRETCH_CompileP95=230ms) for internal reference.
·         Audit config files and environment flags for any simulation or dev toggles (e.g. an A4Q_SIM flag). Set real-mode as the default for all deployments. Remove or hard-disable any “backdoor” dev settings – require A4Q_SIM=0 unless explicitly overridden in a test profile[43]. Essentially, make the production configuration the default behavior.
·         Update service startup logs to prominently print out the loaded DK constant values on boot (a banner listing all invariants and budgets)[44]. This provides quick visibility into which SLO budgets are in effect for any run.
·         Implement a CI static check (or linter rule) that scans the code for forbidden dev patterns or placeholder stubs (e.g. any call to a fake backend, or ALLOW_SIMULATION flags). If such patterns are present in non-test code, fail the build[45][46].
·         Commit these changes and run the full test suite. Update any tests that assumed relaxed values or simulation modes to now align with real-mode defaults. (For example, a unit test that expected a higher latency threshold might need adjustment to the real 250 ms compile budget.)
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         DK constants defined: The services/common/constants.go (or equivalent config) contains all 10 DK invariants with the correct target values[47]. A code review or diff can confirm each constant. Unit tests validate that services load these constants (e.g. a test that the orchestrator uses MAX_LEDGER_APPEND_MS=120).
·         Simulation off by default: When running the stack with default settings, the system operates in real-mode (e.g. A4Q_SIM defaults to 0)[48]. If someone attempts to start a service in sim/dev mode without explicit override, it should either log a warning or refuse to start (fail closed). We capture a log from a startup attempt showing that simulation mode is not allowed without a flag[49].
·         CI gating: The CI pipeline includes a static analysis step that flags any usage of disallowed dev code (e.g. any occurrence of "SIMULATION_MODE" or stubbed implementation)[50]. A screenshot or log of a deliberately introduced fake call causing CI to fail can serve as evidence that the check works.
●	Artifact: “Real Mode Run” – After implementing, run the full system in real mode and store an artifact (e.g. _artifacts/real_mode_boot.json) capturing the startup banner with DK values and a SHA-256 checksum of the config[51]. This proves that the platform is running with intended invariants.
 Notes/Risks: This task sets a zero-trust stance in code – everything is “production-real” unless explicitly sandboxed. A risk is breaking developer workflows that relied on simulation modes. We mitigate this by providing alternate local testing methods (e.g. use a local quantum simulator behind the orchestrator interface rather than a global sim toggle, see P1-T3 Notes[52]). This task is foundational: it locks in our performance and security budgets from day one and prevents regression or sneakily bypassing critical checks.
●	Task ID: P1-T2 – Instrumentation of DK Metrics & Observability Baseline
 Plane: Cross-Cutting (all services)
 Package(s): services/common/otel.go, ops/observability, infra/compose
 Type: PROD-REQUIRED
 Owner: SRE / Observability Lead
 Timebox: 4 weeks (Month 1–2)
 Dependencies: P1-T1 (DK constants in place); basic services running locally
 Steps:
·         Add metric collection hooks in each service for every DK invariant. For example, in Trust Fabric, record a histogram metric a4q_ledger_append_ms for ledger write latency; in Orchestrator, record a4q_recovery_time_s for failover recovery time; in the compiler pass, track a4q_compile_latency_ms[53]. Use a consistent naming scheme (prefix a4q_ and human-readable metric names/units).
·         Verify metrics exposure: Launch the stack via Docker Compose (TF + Orchestrator + dependencies like Postgres) and hit each service’s /metrics endpoint. Confirm the new metrics appear with appropriate labels and default values (e.g. a gauge for watermark TPR initially at 1.0 or 0, histograms present with 0 counts)[54][55].
·         Implement a basic load generator (script or extension of our CLI) to generate some activity and thus data for these metrics. For example, submit 100 dummy jobs via the orchestrator (possibly using a built-in simulator backend) to accumulate data for compile latency, job success%, etc. Ensure metrics like a4q_job_success_fraction or a4q_wm_tpr update accordingly.
·         Set up a local Prometheus instance (as part of docker-compose or separate) to scrape these metrics endpoints. Import or create a Grafana dashboard (JSON) focusing on our DK metrics: p95 latency charts, success rates, carbon per job, etc.[56][57]. Annotate the dashboard with red lines or thresholds at the DK targets (e.g. red line at 120 ms on ledger latency chart) for visual clarity.
·         Establish basic alert rules for any metric breaching its threshold for a sustained period (for now, just log a warning or print to console). E.g., alert if a4q_wm_tpr < 0.99 over 5 min, or if a4q_job_gco2_per (carbon per job) exceeds 55 g for more than N jobs[23]. This will evolve later into a full alerting system.
·         Document this observability baseline: update README or ops/ docs explaining each metric and its corresponding invariant (e.g. “a4q_ledger_append_ms: Ledger append latency, target ≤120 ms p95”). This helps new developers and external auditors understand how we enforce performance.
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Metrics endpoints live: Hitting GET /metrics on Trust Fabric returns all governance-plane metrics (e.g. latency histograms for ledger and contract seal, a gauge or counter for audit log coverage)[58]. Hitting /metrics on Orchestrator shows uptime and recovery metrics, carbon accounting, job success rate, etc.[58]. We will attach a sample of each /metrics output confirming presence of ~10 key metrics (covering all DK invariants).
·         Dashboard & alerts: An exported Grafana dashboard (JSON) is saved as an artifact (e.g. _artifacts/v1_dk_dashboard.json) showing real-time values for all DK invariants[59]. It should include SLO threshold indicators. Additionally, a screenshot of an example alert (e.g. artificially induce a condition where ledger latency >120 ms and capture the alert triggering) is provided[60].
·         Metrics smoke test artifact: Provide a log or JSON from the load generation test (e.g. _artifacts/metrics_smoketest.log) showing that after submitting dummy jobs, metrics like compile_p95 and job_success% were computed and are within expected ranges[61]. For instance, “Submitted 100 jobs, compile p95 = 180 ms (PASS), job_success = 100% (PASS)”.
●	Confirm that metric overhead is negligible (we didn’t break any latency by adding instrumentation). We might include a before/after performance snippet or note that no service exceeded its p95 due to metrics collection (if needed, adjust histogram granularity or sampling)[62].
 Notes/Risks: We must ensure instrumentation itself doesn’t violate budgets – e.g. collecting a histogram on every compile could add overhead. Mitigation: use coarse buckets or sample at lower rate if needed, as long as it still captures p95 accurately[62]. This task is crucial because “you can’t improve what you don’t measure” – by end of Phase 1 we have a quantitative view of system performance. This lays the groundwork for all evidence-driven improvements in later phases.
●	Task ID: P1-T3 – Deployable Stack – Docker Compose & Helm Charts v1
 Plane: Cross-Cutting (Infra/Ops)
 Package(s): infra/compose, infra/helm, CI/CD pipeline
 Type: PROD-REQUIRED
 Owner: DevOps Engineer
 Timebox: 4 weeks (Month 2–3)
 Dependencies: P1-T2 (metrics/obs ready so charts include monitoring); Postgres DB instance available for ledger
 Steps:
·         Finalize a Docker Compose configuration for local development and testing. Define services for Trust Fabric, Orchestrator, a Postgres database, and optional Prometheus+Grafana. Ensure the compose file uses either local built images or pulls the latest container images from CI. Test that a developer can run docker-compose up and bring up the entire stack, then submit a sample job via CLI and see it complete end-to-end (likely using a dummy QPU backend for now)[63].
·         Develop initial Helm charts (or Kustomize manifests) to deploy A4Q onto a Kubernetes cluster. The chart should include Deployments for core services (trust-fabric, orchestrator), a StatefulSet or Deployment for Postgres, ConfigMaps for environment (embedding those DK constants or referencing them), and possibly sub-charts for monitoring components (Prometheus scrapings, Grafana dashboards)[64]. Provide toggles in values.yaml for optional components (e.g. the ML services which might not run in prod yet, or enabling the carbon feature flag). By default, configure a minimal HA deployment: e.g. 2 replicas of each service to mimic active-active setup[65].
·         Test Helm on a cluster: Use a local K8s (KinD or Minikube) or a dev cloud namespace to install the chart. Iterate until helm install a4q ./charts/a4q brings up all pods healthy. Use a4qctl to submit a test job to the cluster deployment as a smoke test[66].
·         Integrate into CI: set up a pipeline step that on each commit builds Docker images for services, pushes them to a test registry (if available), then lints and validates the Helm chart (using helm template and kubeval to catch errors)[67]. Optionally, run a short deployment in CI (using KinD in a CI job) to ensure the chart works headlessly. The goal is that our deployment config is always in sync with code changes.
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Local Compose up: Provide a short log or recorded output showing docker-compose up launching the stack. Both Trust Fabric and Orchestrator containers reach a healthy state (e.g. /v1/healthz returning OK). As evidence, include a script (e.g. scripts/dev_compose_smoketest.sh) or its output verifying the health endpoints and perhaps running a trivial submit-check workflow[68]. This proves any developer (or external user) can easily spin up A4Q locally.
·         Helm deployment: Screenshot or text capture of kubectl get pods after deploying the Helm chart, showing all components running (e.g. 2x orchestrator, 2x trust-fabric, 1x postgres, etc.)[69]. Also, capture a snippet of a successful job submission on the K8s deployment (e.g. log output or CLI result). Save a full install log as _artifacts/helm_install_test.log documenting the process[69].
·         CI integration: Show that the CI pipeline is building images and validating the chart on each push. We can attach an example CI log segment or badge indicating “Deploy Pipeline Passed”[70]. Additionally, list the artifact versions (e.g. Docker image tags like v1.0.0-alpha and a packaged Helm chart version a4q-0.1.0.tgz)[71]. The existence of these versioned artifacts signals we have a repeatable deployment bundle.
●	Confirm that a new environment can be stood up in <30 minutes using these configs – effectively a basic installation guide is validated. This will be critical for pilot partners in Phase 3.
 Notes/Risks: Running stateful components (like Postgres) in Docker/CI requires some care (ephemeral volumes, etc.)[52]. We also still rely on a dummy quantum backend in this phase (since real QPU integration comes in Phase 2); the orchestrator’s backend abstraction might use a local simulator to “complete” jobs so the pipeline can be tested end-to-end[72]. We note this is a placeholder to be replaced by real backends soon. This task ensures deployment readiness: by end of Phase 1 we have a known-good way to deploy A4Q both locally and on a cluster, which is essential for any external trials.
●	Task ID: P1-T4 – Chaos & Failover Drill (HA Test)
 Plane: Autonomy (Resilience)
 Package(s): services/orchestrator, ops/observability (test scripts)
 Type: PROD-REQUIRED
 Owner: SRE Lead
 Timebox: 2 weeks (Month 3–4)
 Dependencies: P1-T3 (deployable stack on a cluster); metrics from P1-T2 in place for uptime/recovery
 Steps:
·         Define a chaos test plan targeting uptime and recovery invariants. Scenarios should include: (a) one orchestrator instance dies unexpectedly – ensure another takes over queued jobs within <1 s (zone failover); (b) the primary DB/ledger goes down or becomes unreachable – ensure a replica or secondary picks up within <60 s (regional failover)[73]. Document these as specific test cases with expected outcomes (e.g. “Kill Orchestrator A, Orchestrator B should resume scheduling in under 1 s”).
·         Configure the deployment in an HA setup for testing. For example, run 2 orchestrator pods on separate “zones” (could simulate zones via node affinity or taints in K8s) and set up Postgres with a primary-replica configuration (or use a simple active/standby if multi-primary isn’t feasible yet)[74]. Ensure the orchestrator instances use a shared queue or DB so they can seamlessly pick up each other’s work.
·         Write a load script to continuously submit jobs during the chaos test (to simulate a steady workload). This could be a loop of a4qctl submit commands or a small program hitting the API. Monitor job statuses and the custom metrics (like uptime, success rate) as the tests run[75].
·         Induce failures as per the plan: e.g., kill the primary orchestrator pod (or simulate a crash) and observe if the secondary orchestrator continues processing new jobs and/or resumes any in-flight tasks[76]. Then simulate a database outage or network partition to the primary DB and see if the system fails over to the replica (we may need to promote the replica manually in this test if not using an auto-failover DB cluster).
·         Record timings for each failure scenario. Use logs or metrics timestamps to measure how long the service was unavailable and how quickly the system recovered (e.g. time from orchestrator A death to orchestrator B picking up the first new job). Also verify no data loss: all submitted jobs should either complete or be marked failed in the ledger – none should vanish silently.
·         If needed, tweak configuration (e.g. orchestrator heartbeat intervals, DB reconnect timeouts) to meet the timing targets. Rerun until the results consistently show uptime ≥ 99.5% and recovery ≤ 60 s under these failure conditions[77].
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Chaos Test Report: A document (e.g. _artifacts/failover_test_report.md) summarizing at least two key scenarios: (1) Orchestrator instance crash -> observed failover time (should be ~1 s)[78]; (2) Simulated region/DB outage -> observed recovery time (should be <60 s)[78]. Include timeline logs or metric graphs illustrating the failover events. For example, a Grafana screenshot showing a spike in orchestrator downtime that lasts only 500 ms during a pod kill would be compelling evidence.
·         Metrics evidence: Prometheus logs or a JSON extract demonstrating that during the chaos test, the custom metrics captured the events but remained within thresholds. E.g., show that the a4q_uptime metric perhaps dipped momentarily but stayed ≥99.5%, and a4q_recovery_time_s recorded values under 60 for the failovers[79]. An artifact _artifacts/failover_metrics.json could list relevant metric values pre- and post-failure.
·         Ledger audit: Verify that the trust-fabric ledger has a record for every job submitted during testing – none got “lost” even across crashes. We should check that jobs were either completed or marked failed with an error recorded, and that audit coverage remained ~100% (the metric for audit coverage should show no gap)[80]. If a job was mid-execution on a crashed orchestrator, confirm the system either retried it or logged it appropriately in the ledger.
●	It is acceptable if we find issues as long as we document them and have a mitigation. For example, if the Postgres replica lag caused one ledger append to exceed 120 ms, note it and plan to address in Phase 2 (where we do deeper multi-region ledger work)[81]. The test is “passed” when we have demonstrated the ability to recover from common failures within spec or have a clear plan to achieve it.
 Notes/Risks: This early chaos testing in a controlled env will reveal configuration issues now rather than in production. Real-world failover in cloud might be slower due to network latencies, so we build some buffer into our targets. Document any weaknesses observed (e.g. if manual DB failover took 90 s, not 60 s) so they feed into Phase 2 tasks (like automating DB failover)[81]. By doing this in Phase 1, we validate our HA architecture as promised by GA and set a reliability baseline for external stakeholders.
●	Task ID: P1-T5 – Initial Security Audit & Secrets Management
 Plane: Governance (Security)
 Package(s): services/trust-fabric, infra/secrets (Vault integration)
 Type: PRE-PROD (security hardening in dev)
 Owner: Security Engineer (Governance)
 Timebox: 3 weeks (Month 3–4)
 Dependencies: Basic system up (from P1-T3 deploy); “real mode” enforcement (P1-T1) done so no dev shortcuts
 Steps:
·         Conduct a code-level security review of the Trust Fabric and Orchestrator services. Look for any hard-coded secrets, sample credentials, or crypto that is not truly PQC. Also verify all APIs enforce auth (e.g. no accidentally exposed unauthenticated endpoints). Cross-check against GA compliance requirements – ensure no known OWASP or common vulns.
·         Integrate an external KMS/HSM for secret management. For instance, set up HashiCorp Vault (in dev mode for now) or use a cloud KMS. Replace any in-memory or file-based key usage with secure retrieval from Vault at startup[82][83]. Concretely: for PQC keypairs (used to wrap job payloads or sign watermarks), store them in Vault and have services request them (or use Vault’s encryption API directly). Ensure keys are never logged or stored in plaintext.
·         Implement session-scoped keys for PQC operations if not already: e.g. orchestrator might request a fresh ephemeral key from the KMS for each job or session, rather than reusing one long-term key, to reduce exposure window[84]. Document the key rotation approach (even if we don’t fully automate rotation now, define how we would rotate keys and how frequently).
·         Add configuration and scripts to support key rotation in a controlled manner. For example, ensure the Trust Fabric can reload or switch keys without downtime (maybe on startup read latest key version, and allow a maintenance restart for rotation). Note in docs if manual steps are required for now.
·         Run a lightweight penetration test on the dev deployment. This could include using a tool like OWASP ZAP or simply crafting some malicious requests: e.g. test SQL injection on ledger APIs, ensure that /v1/seal cannot be called without proper auth token or mTLS client cert, check that our TLS ciphers are strong, etc. The goal is to catch any glaring security holes before an external pilot.
·         Fix any issues found: e.g. if we find missing input sanitization, add it. If any HTTP headers are missing (CSP, etc.), include them. Ensure that both internal service-to-service calls and external client calls require proper authentication (client-side certificate or JWT).
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         No plaintext secrets: Verify that no long-lived secret or private key remains hard-coded or stored in config files. As evidence, show excerpts of config where secrets have been replaced with Vault references. E.g., an orchestrator log on startup indicating it retrieved keys from Vault (without revealing them)[85][86]. We might also include a Vault audit log snippet showing that keys were accessed (to prove the integration is being used)[87].
·         Security checklist: Create an artifact _artifacts/security_baseline.md that itemizes each security check (auth on all endpoints, SQL injection tests, TLS settings, etc.) and their status (pass/fail)[88]. For Phase 1 completion, all high-priority items should be “PASS.” If any are “FAIL” or “Risk accepted,” explicitly call them out with a plan (so we can track in future tasks).
·         Pen-test results: Provide results from any security scanning tool or scripts. E.g., if using ZAP, attach the report highlighting no critical vulnerabilities found (or if found, they were addressed). If manual tests, a short log of attempts and outcomes (e.g. “Tried SQL injection on /append – input sanitized, no data leak”).
·         Confirm that all external interfaces require auth: e.g. demonstrate that a curl to /v1/submit without a client cert or token is rejected with 401/403. Provide a sample log or CLI output showing the rejection to prove that mTLS or auth is in effect.
·         Summarize any improvements made (e.g. “Integrated Vault for PQC keys – keys are now never stored in plaintext, and rotated via Vault versioning”[83]). This summary becomes part of our evidence to partners that we moved beyond any dev shortcuts from earlier versions[89].
 Notes/Risks: This task transitions the project from “dev-ready” to “prod-ready” posture in security. Some findings might require larger fixes (e.g. if we realize we need a proper OAuth service for user tokens – that might be a new feature). We log those for later phases if they can’t be done immediately. The dependency on Vault will be expanded in Phase 2 (Task P2-T2 will fully productionize KMS usage), but by finishing P1-T5 we at least ensure no secrets are lying around and we’ve closed obvious gaps[89]. Essentially, after Phase 1, we have high confidence that the system is running with real configs (no dummy keys), instrumented for full visibility, deployable in real environments, and resilient to basic failures – ready for Phase 2: external integration.
Phase 2 (Months 4–8) – External Integration & Policy Layer
Phase Goals (4–8mo): Begin connecting the system with real external components and flesh out advanced governance capabilities. Key objectives: (1) Real Quantum Hardware Integration – replace any dummy/simulated quantum backends with actual cloud QPU APIs (e.g. IBM Quantum, IonQ), proving the orchestrator can submit jobs to at least one real quantum provider and retrieve results[90]; (2) Enterprise Key & Ledger Hardening – deploy the Trust Fabric ledger in multi-region mode and trial a blockchain/consortium ledger in parallel to evaluate tamper-proof audit options[91]; (3) Advanced Policy Enforcement – extend the Trust Contract policy engine to support fine-grained governance rules (data residency, export control, auto data deletion) to meet enterprise and regulatory requirements[92]; (4) Watermark & Carbon Analytics – build tools to extract/verify circuit watermarks and integrate live carbon-intensity data into scheduling, enabling our first ESG-aware features[93]. By end of Phase 2, the stack should be “enterprise-ready”: connected to real hardware, secured with enterprise-grade key management, enforcing compliance policies, and offering unique IP/ESG features – even if at small scale.
·         Task ID: P2-T1 – Integrate First Quantum Hardware Backend (IBM Quantum)
 Plane: Autonomy (Execution Backend)
 Package(s): services/orchestrator, platform/qir-adapters
 Type: PROD-REQUIRED
 Owner: Autonomy Lead (Orchestrator Engineer)
 Timebox: 6 weeks (Month 4–5)
 Dependencies: All Phase 1 core tasks done (especially P1-T3 deploy pipeline and P1-T4 stability, to support testing on a cluster); External: access to IBM Quantum (or similar QPU vendor) API credentials
 Steps:
·         Refactor the orchestrator to introduce a backend abstraction interface. Define a Go interface or plugin mechanism for quantum execution backends with methods like SubmitJob(qirPayload) -> JobID and PollJobStatus(JobID) -> (Status/Result)[94]. The current orchestrator likely directly invokes a simulator; replace that with calls through this interface. Keep a default “local simulator” implementation for testing, but ensure it conforms to the same interface.
·         Implement a provider plugin for IBM Quantum. Options: use IBM’s Python SDK via a sidecar or binding, or call their REST APIs. Since orchestrator is Go, one approach is to create a lightweight Go wrapper that calls out to a Python script or cloud function that interfaces with IBM Q’s API. Alternatively, use IBM’s REST endpoints directly from Go (if available). Manage credentials securely (likely via Vault from P1-T5: store API keys there and have orchestrator fetch them)[95].
·         Update the compiler’s QIR adapters if needed to ensure compatibility with the target backend. For example, IBM’s quantum service might expect OpenQASM or a certain QIR flavor – so either adjust our QIR output or add a translation step from our Portable IR to IBM’s format[96]. If IBM requires specific gate set or qubit ordering, incorporate that. (This is a chance to validate our portable IR approach in a real scenario.)
·         End-to-end test: Using the CLI or API, submit a simple circuit (e.g. a Bell state or basic algorithm) with orchestrator configured to use the IBM backend. The orchestrator should accept the job, send it to IBM’s cloud, and eventually mark it complete with results (or failed, if something goes wrong)[97]. Monitor logs to ensure: job submitted -> IBM job ID received -> polling -> result fetched -> our system records completion. This may take some wall-clock time if IBM’s queue is slow, but that’s expected.
·         Handle asynchronous behavior: likely the IBM API is asynchronous (returns a job ID immediately, then you poll or get a callback when done). Ensure orchestrator’s /v1/status endpoint can report “running (on IBM backend, job XYZ)” and eventually “completed” or “failed”[98]. Possibly implement a background goroutine or scheduler to poll external job statuses and update our internal state.
·         Implement basic error handling and timeouts: define what to do if the external job stalls or IBM’s service is unavailable. E.g., if a job is in “Running” state for > X minutes with no update, mark it as failed or timeout (and record in ledger). Also decide on failure modes: if IBM returns an error (like invalid circuit), propagate that error to our job status and ledger.
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Backend interface code: Provide a code snippet or documentation of the new backend interface (e.g. a BackendProvider Go interface in orchestrator code) and note that at least two implementations exist (LocalSim and IBMQuantum). This can be attached as an artifact or code reference[99].
·         Demo run log: An artifact _artifacts/ibm_backend_test.log capturing a real submission using the IBM backend. It should show the CLI command (or API call) made, the orchestrator logging “Submitted to IBM, job ID = XYZ”, some waiting/polling, and then “Job completed, result = ...”[100]. If IBM’s results are accessible, include a snippet of the actual result data returned to prove we got something back.
·         Metrics and invariants check: Confirm that using a real backend doesn’t break our invariants. For example, compile latency should still be within p95 since we’re only changing execution stage (though overall job wall time will be longer due to queue and run time, which is fine). Ensure orchestrator handled it without crashing or memory leaks. We might not measure IBM’s execution time against our SLOs (since that’s external), but we ensure e.g. orchestrator throughput or memory usage stayed stable.
·         If IBM’s API was slow or queued, ensure our job success metric still makes sense – possibly exclude external queue time from “success%” calculation, or document how pending jobs are treated. If any adjustments were needed (like increasing some timeouts), note them.
●	Feature flagging (if needed): If this integration is not yet ready for all users, show that it can be enabled/disabled via config. But ideally, it’s just always available and we can default to a “demo” provider if not configured.
 Notes/Risks: This is a big milestone – first real quantum hardware in the loop. The main risk is dependency on an external service (IBM) that we don’t control; network issues or queue delays could complicate tests. Mitigation: plan tests at off-peak hours or use a simulator mode of IBM if available. We also must keep security in mind: ensure we are using PQC encryption if sending sensitive data to IBM’s cloud (wrap job payloads as we planned)[101]. We will likely treat this as a beta integration – good enough for a pilot demonstration. This task eliminates the last “fake” compute component and fulfills the promise of being hardware-agnostic by proving we can swap in a real QPU with minimal changes[102].
●	Task ID: P2-T2 – Enterprise Key Management (Vault Integration Full)
 Plane: Governance (Security/Compliance)
 Package(s): services/trust-fabric, services/common/pqc
 Type: PROD-REQUIRED
 Owner: Governance Lead (Security Engineer)
 Timebox: 4 weeks (Month 4–5)
 Dependencies: P1-T5 (basic KMS usage done, Vault accessible)
 Steps:
·         Upgrade the Trust Fabric’s cryptography to use Vault/HSM for all operations. In Phase 1 we might have fetched keys from Vault; now use Vault’s transit encryption API for performing cryptographic operations. For example, when sealing a job contract or encrypting a quantum payload, instead of using local libsodium for the Kyber KEM, call Vault to perform the encryption with a key stored in HSM (so the private key never leaves Vault)[101]. This means orchestrator or trust-fabric sends plaintext data to Vault and gets back ciphertext (or vice versa for decryption) under a named key. Ensure proper caching or performance considerations (Vault calls add latency).
·         Manage ledger signing keys similarly: if the trust-fabric digitally signs ledger entries or watermarks, move those keys into Vault. Use Vault’s signing API or at least fetch the key at startup from HSM memory. The goal is that no cryptographic operation of significance uses in-process keys – everything is either in HSM or derived ephemerally.
·         Implement a key rotation procedure and test it. For instance, generate a new key version in Vault for the watermark signing key. Update the trust-fabric to start using the new key for new entries, but still accept/verify old entries with the previous key (so we likely need to keep previous public keys around for verification)[103][104]. This might involve versioning keys (Vault supports key versioning). Document how a rotation is initiated and what downtime, if any, is required (ideally none, perhaps a brief maintenance mode).
·         Performance test the Vault integration: Vault calls will introduce overhead (network + HSM processing). We must ensure it stays within the DK budget for PQC overhead (≤20 ms p95)[105]. If direct calls are too slow per operation, implement caching of session keys or use batch encryption for bulk ops. For example, use Vault to generate a one-time session key and use that for multiple operations to amortize cost, as long as security isn’t compromised[106]. Possibly adjust the PQC overhead invariant if needed or split it into network vs compute overhead.
·         Update deployment configs to include Vault address, credentials, and any required agents (e.g. Vault Agent with Auto-Auth if running in cluster). Ensure our Helm chart now has options to configure Vault (URL, token or Kubernetes auth). Add instructions or a script for setting up Vault in dev mode for testing.
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Latency report: Provide a report (e.g. _artifacts/pqc_vault_latency.csv) or summary of Vault encryption call latencies. It should demonstrate that p95 overhead is ≤20 ms[107][108]. If our tests show, say, 90th percentile = 12 ms and 95th = 22 ms, we need to note that and either optimize or justify it (maybe the DK target is slightly exceeded but we plan to optimize or accept if negligible). The key is to show measurement and any mitigation (like caching) done[106].
·         Secure logs: Trust-fabric logs (with debug on) demonstrating that cryptographic operations are going through Vault. E.g. a log line: “Vault KMS: Encrypted job payload with key tenant_key_v1 in 15 ms”[108]. This proves the integration is active. We will include a sanitized log excerpt as evidence.
·         Rotation test evidence: Document a key rotation event. For instance, rotate the ledger signing key: use Vault to increment the key version, then submit a new job. Show that new ledger entries are signed with the new key (perhaps by a log or by retrieving the entry and checking the signature metadata), while verifying an old entry still works. Include output of a script or log that performed this test[104]. This indicates we can rotate keys without breaking verification of historical data.
·         Config diffs: Show that all plaintext secret references in config are gone. For example, previously we might have had a static PQC key in an env var; now it’s replaced with something like VAULT_TOKEN and key references. A config snippet or values.yaml diff can illustrate the change (no hard-coded keys, only Vault roles)[109].
●	Achieving this will satisfy any enterprise security officer that key management is solid – keys are in an HSM, rotated, and we have evidence of encryption overhead being within acceptable limits[110].
 Notes/Risks: Vault integration can be finicky (especially in dev environments). We’ll use Vault’s dev server for tests but simulate production hardness by using TLS and proper auth methods (Kubernetes auth, etc.)[111]. This task essentially closes a known gap from earlier versions (in V6/V7, key management was stubbed; now it’s real)[110]. There’s a risk that if Vault is down, our system might not function – we have to decide fail-closed (probably yes, fail-closed for security). We document this dependency and perhaps plan a redundant KMS or caching in Phase 3. By Phase 2’s end, our crypto is truly production-grade, which will be a big checkmark in any operations readiness review.
●	Task ID: P2-T3 – Distributed Ledger Deployment (A/B Regions + Blockchain Trial)
 Plane: Governance (Ledger)
 Package(s): services/trust-fabric/ledger, infra/chains
 Type: PRE-PROD (technology evaluation)
 Owner: Backend Engineer (Governance)
 Timebox: 6 weeks (Month 5–6)
 Dependencies: Phase 1 HA test (P1-T4) completed; availability of a second database instance or a blockchain framework (Hyperledger, etc.) for trial
 Steps:
·         Deploy the Trust Fabric’s SQL ledger in a multi-region active/active configuration. E.g., set up two Postgres instances (Region A and Region B). Implement logical replication or use a multi-primary approach so both can accept writes without data loss. Likely we’ll designate one as primary for writes and replicate to the other for this phase (true multi-master might be too complex now). Fine-tune settings to minimize replication lag and to keep write latency low – aim to still meet ~120 ms p95 for append with replication on[112]. Test by writing ledger entries in Region A and reading from Region B, and vice versa, to ensure data consistency. Simulate a failover (Region A down) and confirm Region B can take over writes.
·         Introduce a blockchain-based shadow ledger (pilot) running in parallel with the SQL ledger. For example, set up a small Hyperledger Fabric network or a Quorum (enterprise Ethereum) network with 2–3 nodes (they can be on our existing cluster for test)[113]. Instrument the trust-fabric service to dual-write each transaction: when a job is sealed or completed, it writes to the Postgres ledger as usual and sends a corresponding transaction to the blockchain network[113]. The blockchain transaction could contain a hash of the circuit, job ID, timestamp, and any watermark info – basically the essential audit data for that event. We don’t replace our SQL ledger, we augment it for tamper-evidence and multi-party consensus.
·         Keep the blockchain write asynchronous to avoid slowing down the main path. For instance, have the trust-fabric enqueue the event and have a background goroutine commit to blockchain, or use a fire-and-forget approach if the blockchain client library allows it[114]. Capture any errors (e.g. if blockchain commit fails) but don’t block the main ledger write. We’ll monitor how often (if at all) this lags or fails.
·         Evaluate performance overhead: measure the blockchain commit times and throughput. If each ledger entry triggers a blockchain txn that takes e.g. 200 ms, that’s significant overhead if done synchronously. Our approach is async, but we still need to measure system impact (e.g. memory usage if queue builds up). If overhead > X%, note that the blockchain ledger in current form is not viable as primary. Possibly throttle or sample the blockchain writes (maybe only critical events go to chain).
·         Decide on viability: by end of this task, conclude whether to pursue the blockchain approach in production. Criteria might include: does it add significant trust/value (multiple parties verifying ledger)? What’s the overhead (latency + complexity)? Are any pilot partners interested in a consortium ledger (if yes, that’s a plus)? If chain overhead is minor (<5% latency hit) and partner interest is high, we lean “Go”. If overhead is high or no one cares yet, lean “No-Go” and plan to rely on our SQL ledger with other integrity measures (like periodic hash notarizations). Document this decision clearly[115][116].
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Multi-region ledger demo: Provide a short report (_artifacts/ledger_HA_test.md) describing the active/active ledger test. Include evidence such as: take Region A offline and show that Region B continued to log jobs (with maybe a slight pause during failover)[117]. Then when Region A comes back, show the ledgers sync or that new entries from B propagate back. If using logical replication, describe how we promoted the replica. The key evidence: no data loss and acceptable failover times for ledger writes. If any append latencies increased with replication, report p95 with replication vs without[118]. Ideally, ledger append p95 remains ~120 ms or better with replication on[118].
·         Blockchain pilot results: Show a log excerpt or blockchain explorer screenshot where a trust-fabric event was successfully written to the blockchain[119]. For example: “Txn 0xabc... in block 123 contains Job #45 sealed with hash X”. Provide a mapping of a specific job or ledger entry to its blockchain transaction to prove dual-logging works.
·         Performance note: Document the measured overhead of blockchain writes. E.g. “Each blockchain commit took ~250 ms on average, causing trust-fabric’s async queue to peak at 5 pending txns under load, but main ledger writes stayed under 120 ms p95.” Or “Blockchain writes occasionally lagged 5 s behind, which is too slow for real-time needs, but acceptable for audit if async.” This will back the Go/Kill decision.
·         Go/Kill criteria: Explicitly state what we conclude. For instance: “Overhead was negligible (~2% latency impact) and it provided tamper-proof audit, so in Era 2 we will move forward with a consortium ledger (Go).” Or “The blockchain approach introduced too much complexity/latency for too little gain at this stage (Kill). Instead, we will implement periodic Merkle-tree notarization of the SQL ledger as an alternative for tamper-evidence.”[116]. Ensure this is logged as a decision record.
●	The outcome of this task is basically a yes/no on adopting blockchain in the next phase. Either way, we’ll have evidence to justify the decision: if yes, we have baseline performance and a plan to productionize it; if no, we have data showing it’s not worth it and an alternate plan.
 Notes/Risks: Running a blockchain network is non-trivial and can distract from core dev. We timebox this to avoid rabbit holes[120]. The purpose is to de-risk a potential requirement (some enterprise/government clients might insist on blockchain audit). By doing a pilot, we either incorporate it early or confidently rule it out for now. We will keep partners/regulators in loop on this experiment – their input will weigh heavily on Go/Kill decision[121].
●	Task ID: P2-T4 – Trust Contracts – Policy Engine Extensions
 Plane: Governance (Policy/Compliance)
 Package(s): services/trust-fabric/policy (OPA/Rego modules), services/trust-fabric/routes.go
 Type: PROD-REQUIRED
 Owner: Governance Lead (Policy/Compliance Engineer)
 Timebox: 5 weeks (Month 6–7)
 Dependencies: Base OPA policy engine from GA (we have simple contract checks); possibly legal input on new policy rules
 Steps:
·         Expand the Trust Contract DSL (domain-specific language) and associated OPA policies to support new clauses needed for enterprise governance. Examples: Data Residency – allow a contract to specify regions where the job can or cannot run (e.g. “region: EU” means orchestrator must only use EU-based QPUs); Export Control – flag certain algorithms or payloads as export-restricted, requiring special handling or outright blocking if the execution would cross borders; Privacy/Retention – add a field for data sensitivity that triggers automatic log redaction or shorter retention (e.g. personal data -> keep telemetry only 7 days)[122][123]. Define the syntax for these in the contract JSON or policy (e.g. "region": "EU" or "retainDays": 30).
·         Implement policy enforcement for each new clause. Likely this means writing new Rego rules or Go checks. For region: integrate with orchestrator such that when a job is sealed, trust-fabric checks the contract’s region field against the available backend’s region – if none match, the seal or submit is rejected with an error (ensuring no job runs in disallowed region)[124][125]. For retention: perhaps trust-fabric schedules a deletion of that job’s telemetry after the specified days (could mark it and a background job clears it later). For export control: maintain a list of “sensitive algorithms” (maybe by hash or label) that cannot run in certain jurisdictions – if a contract says exportControlled=true, then orchestrator must verify the chosen QPU region is approved or else abort.
·         Write unit tests for policy logic. For each rule, create test scenarios: e.g. job contract requests EU region but only US backend available -> expect rejection; job with privacy tag -> submit job, then simulate passage of retention period and ensure logs are gone or anonymized. Use OPA unit testing for Rego modules if possible, and integration tests where we attempt actual submissions under various conditions[126][127].
·         Ensure audit logging for policy decisions. Augment the trust-fabric to log whenever a policy rule blocks or modifies a job: e.g. “Job 123 denied: violates data residency (EU only)”[128]. This provides traceability so admins know why something was blocked. Include these logs in our observability (maybe a counter for policy_denials).
·         Update documentation & SDK: Document the new contract fields for users (in API docs or examples). If our CLI can easily support them (like add --region EU flag to a4qctl), implement that convenience. The easier it is for pilot users to use these features, the better.
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Example contract & outcome: Provide one or two concrete examples of the new policies in action. For instance, a contract {"region": "EU", "retainDays": 30} used with a job when only a US backend is available – show that the system rejected the job with a clear error message[129][130]. Then adjust the environment (e.g. simulate an EU backend or change contract) and show the job is accepted and runs, and logs show region constraints being applied. Attach the relevant log or output confirming the enforcement.
·         Policy module artifact: Save the new OPA policy file (e.g. _artifacts/policy_v2.rego) or an equivalent, showing rules for residency, export, retention, etc.[131]. Also include results of running opa test or our integration tests – perhaps as a summary like “5 new policy tests added: all PASS”.
·         Performance check: Confirm that adding these checks doesn’t materially impact the /v1/seal or job submission latency. If needed, note the overhead of policy evaluation (likely negligible, a few ms at most). We can even measure the seal API with and without a heavy policy to show it’s still within, say, tens of milliseconds[132]. The ledger append still should meet 120 ms p95 including these checks.
●	We should highlight that by Month 8 we can demonstrate to a pilot customer that we have multi-jurisdictional compliance controls in place, which is a huge trust win[133]. So evidence might also be a slide or doc snippet prepared for stakeholders summarizing “New Governance Features: e.g. region locking, automated log purging, etc.” though that’s more narrative.
 Notes/Risks: Some policies (like export control) may require expert input or could be sensitive. We implement a straightforward version (“if flagged, only allow on certain backend tags”) and note that it’s adjustable pending legal review[134]. The risk is scope creep – trying to do too much (smart contracts, etc.). We keep it practical: implement what we foresee needed by design partners (likely region and retention) and placeholders for others. This task significantly strengthens our governance value prop beyond watermarking, showing we can enforce complex real-world rules in quantum workflows[135].
●	Task ID: P2-T5 – Watermark Extraction & Verification Toolkit
 Plane: Discovery → Governance (IP Provenance)
 Package(s): platform/watermark-pass, services/trust-fabric, tools/wm-inspector
 Type: PRE-PROD (tooling, not in core path yet)
 Owner: Compiler/Platform Engineer
 Timebox: 4 weeks (Month 7–8)
 Dependencies: Basic watermark embedder exists (from GA compiler); need sample watermarked circuits and results for testing
 Steps:
·         Develop a Watermark Inspector CLI/Tool that can analyze a quantum circuit or execution result to detect our embedded watermark. This could be a Python or Rust tool under /tools/wm-inspector. It might run statistical tests on a given circuit’s gate parameters to find the subtle watermark patterns (e.g. slight angle biases or extra phase gates that encode our signature)[136]. Essentially, we want an independent way to verify that a given circuit carries our provenance mark.
·         Integrate this with the Trust Fabric’s verification API. Possibly extend an endpoint (like /v1/verifyWatermark) where a user can submit a final circuit or even the results of an execution, and the service will run the inspector and report back the likelihood that our watermark is present[137]. This might involve sending the circuit through the new tool and returning a boolean or confidence score.
·         Ensure the watermark true positive rate (TPR) remains extremely high (≥99%). Test the detection on a reasonably large set: e.g. take 100 circuits that we watermarked (through our compiler) and 100 that are not watermarked, and see how often the tool correctly identifies the presence or absence. We expect ≈100% detection on watermarked circuits and ~0% false positives on clean ones[138][139]. If the detection is weaker, consider improving the watermark scheme (maybe make the watermark signal slightly stronger or multi-dimensional).
·         Measure watermark overhead (Δdepth) on circuits to ensure the embed-extract process still meets the ≤0.5% depth overhead budget[140]. Use some benchmark circuits: compare circuit depth with and without watermark, ensure the difference is negligible (<0.5%). Also ensure extraction works even after circuits might have been executed (some noise might affect it, though watermark is in the circuit structure, not just results).
·         Package results and possibly generate evidence artifacts for auditors. E.g., have the tool output a JSON report with fields like "watermark_detected": true, "confidence": 0.999, "extracted_id": "A4Q-GOV-004". Link that with the ledger records so one could prove “this output corresponds to that input via watermark X”. Document usage so external parties (like a partner’s legal team) could run verification on their own if needed.
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Watermark detection report: An artifact _artifacts/watermark_tpr_test.md summarizing the detection tests. For example: “Out of 200 watermarked circuits, 198 were correctly flagged (99% TPR), and 0 false positives in 100 unwatermarked circuits”[138][141]. This demonstrates we meet the 0.99 TPR invariant. If any false negatives or positives occurred, analyze why and adjust.
