·         If we made improvements to the watermark scheme to boost detection (the prompt hints early versions were simplistic[142]), describe them and show that the new scheme still meets the overhead invariant (depth overhead still ≤0.5%, compile time not significantly increased)[143][144]. Possibly attach a before/after comparison of circuit depth or a visual of a tiny circuit with and without watermark to illustrate minimal difference.
·         API example: Show usage of the verification capability. E.g., call the /v1/verifyWatermark endpoint (or run the CLI on a circuit file) and show output: if it’s watermarked, the response might be “Watermark present, ID match, confidence 0.999”. Provide a sample input (maybe a small QASM file or bitstring from results) and the tool’s output as evidence[141][145].
·         End-to-end scenario: Demonstrate a full IP provenance cycle: compile a test circuit with watermark, run it (even on a simulator), then use the new toolkit to extract the watermark and confirm it matches the trust-fabric record. Provide logs or a short write-up of this scenario (e.g., “Compiled circuit X with watermark tag Y, orchestrator executed it, watermark inspector confirms tag Y in output, cross-checked with ledger entry.”)[146]. This will be compelling to show partners how we trace IP.
●	While not required, if this uncovered any limitations (e.g. a malicious user could theoretically strip the watermark by certain transforms), document those as known limitations and perhaps plan a Phase 4 R&D task to enhance watermark robustness[147]. The key acceptance is that we now have an independent verification mechanism, not just injection, which is critical for any dispute or audit about IP provenance.
 Notes/Risks: Watermarking is our core IP protection, so this tool adds credibility. The risk is if our watermark is too weak (maybe easily removed or obscured by noise). We note that in current scope, it’s robust enough for pilot (99% TPR) but not foolproof against a determined adversary[147]. That’s acceptable for now; we’ll likely revisit in later eras to evolve the scheme (maybe quantum-secure watermarks). For now, we ensure transparency and evidence: anyone can run our inspector to confirm provenance, which is a big trust booster for early customers.
●	Task ID: P2-T6 – Carbon-Aware Scheduling (Phase 1 – Data Feed & Tagging)
 Plane: Autonomy (ESG/FinOps)
 Package(s): services/orchestrator, services/common/carbon.go (new module)
 Type: PRE-PROD (feature-flagged experimental)
 Owner: Autonomy Engineer (Scheduler)
 Timebox: 4 weeks (Month 7–8)
 Dependencies: Orchestrator running stable with multiple backends; access to carbon intensity API (e.g. ElectricityMap or WattTime)
 Steps:
·         Implement a Carbon Intensity Data Fetcher component. This could be a simple module or microservice that pulls current electricity grid CO₂ intensity (gCO₂/kWh) for given regions. Many APIs (ElectricityMap, etc.) provide this data per region. Write code to fetch and cache this periodically (say, every 5 minutes) to avoid rate limits[148]. For testing, we can use a public API with a test key.
·         Extend the Orchestrator’s scheduling logic to utilize this data when deciding where to run a job. Specifically, if the feature flag carbonAware=true: when multiple backends (regions) are available, compare their current carbon intensity and prefer the lower-carbon option, all else being equal[149]. Also, if a job is not urgent (perhaps determined by a contract flag or if queue times allow), consider delaying it slightly if the current carbon intensity is high across the board, waiting for it to drop below a threshold[150]. This introduces a new decision factor: carbon score in addition to cost/latency.
·         Add a field to job metadata (and eventually ledger) to record the carbon estimate for each job run. For example, when a job completes, compute an approximate gCO₂ emitted (perhaps (runtime seconds) * (hardware power kW) * (carbon intensity gCO₂/kWh) – we might need rough numbers or ask providers for that). Store this as job.carbon_gco2 and expose via metrics (a4q_job_gco2e)[151]. This will allow tracking average emissions per job.
·         Write unit tests for scheduling decisions with dummy data: simulate two regions with carbon values (e.g. 300 vs 100 gCO₂/kWh) and ensure that if carbon-aware flag is on, a job goes to the greener region, whereas if off, it might go to default or round-robin. Simulate a high carbon scenario where a job is allowed to be delayed by, say, 60 seconds – ensure orchestrator delays assignment and logs it appropriately (“Job 456 delayed 30s for lower carbon”)[149][152].
·         Keep the feature disabled by default in production config since it’s experimental. But have a toggle (env var or config) to enable it for trials or specific users. Possibly run it enabled in our staging environment to start collecting data on potential carbon savings.
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Feature functionality demo: Show a side-by-side scenario: Run the orchestrator with carbon-aware scheduling off and submit a set of jobs – observe region choices. Then turn it on (with disparate carbon data for two regions) and submit similar jobs – observe that jobs route to the region with lower carbon intensity preferentially[152]. Provide logs illustrating this decision process (e.g., “Region EU carbon=100, US=300 – routing job 789 to EU for lower carbon footprint” or “Delaying job 790 by 5s due to high carbon intensity 400 gCO₂ in all regions”)[153][154].
·         Metric tracking: Confirm that the metric a4q_job_gco2e is now populated for each completed job[155]. Provide a snippet of the /metrics output or Grafana showing jobs with their carbon estimates. If possible, demonstrate that in a test where carbon-aware scheduling is on, the average gCO₂/job was lower than in a scenario where it was off (this is the goal – e.g. perhaps a ~10% reduction if the scheduler successfully moved some jobs to greener times/regions)[155][156]. This can be a small table or chart as evidence.
·         No SLO regressions: Verify that enabling this feature doesn’t violate other SLOs. For instance, if a job was delayed for carbon reasons, ensure we did not exceed any promised latency SLAs without permission. Likely in Phase 2 we have no strict SLAs to external users, but we check that our job success rate didn’t drop (jobs aren’t erroneously dropped due to waiting)[157]. Document that orchestrator throughput in steady state is unaffected. If any risk of backlog exists when delaying jobs, mention how we cap the delay.
·         Also produce a decision document capturing criteria for continuing or expanding this feature. E.g., “If by end of Era 1 we observe ≥10% average CO₂ reduction without major downsides, we’ll integrate carbon-aware scheduling as a default in Era 2; if savings are negligible or it causes job delays beyond acceptable, we might keep it opt-in or drop it.”[158]. Essentially, log what metric of success we’ll evaluate (the roadmap stretch goal mentions ~10–15% emissions reduction[159]).
 Notes/Risks: This is our first ESG-oriented feature – results may vary depending on workload and region. We must be careful not to sacrifice core reliability for carbon gains (hence feature-flagging and baby steps)[160]. There’s also a transparency aspect: we should consider notifying users if their job was delayed for carbon reasons (which we plan to log)[161]. The data collected here (how often and how much delay vs savings) will inform whether this becomes a standard offering or remains an optional “green mode”. By implementing now, we position ourselves as forward-thinking (which partners/investors will appreciate), but we do so safely, ensuring no surprise slowdowns without opt-in.
Phase 3 (Months 8–12) – Pilot Deployment & v1.0 “GA” Readiness
Phase Goals (8–12mo): Reach a level of maturity sufficient to support an external pilot deployment (design partner) by the end of Year 1, and finalize an internal “v1.0.0” release candidate. Focus areas: (1) End-to-End Validation – conduct a full Operational Readiness Review (ORR) or “Ops Gold” test suite on the platform: verify all invariants, failure cases, and recovery in a staging environment, producing evidence artifacts for each (metrics logs, audit proofs, etc.)[162]; (2) Documentation & Support Prep – create admin guides, user documentation, and internal runbooks so that an external partner’s team could operate the system (even if with our help initially); (3) Proof-of-Concept (PoC) with Real Workload – deploy A4Q in a partner’s sandbox environment and run a meaningful quantum workload (not just trivial circuits – e.g. a small variational algorithm or QEC cycle), to test integration with real use-cases and gather feedback[163]; (4) Standards & Ecosystem Feedback – engage with external bodies (QIR Alliance, standards organizations) by presenting our approaches (watermarking, provenance, etc.) and incorporate any early feedback, signaling openness and ensuring alignment with emerging standards[164]. By end of Phase 3, we aim to declare a “v1.0.0 GA” internally – all critical tasks for a credible production system done – and have a design partner actually using the platform in a trial. Essentially we prove in a real environment that our platform is production-ready and adds value, backed by evidence.
·         Task ID: P3-T1 – Full-Stack ORR (Operational Readiness Review) Simulation
 Plane: Cross-Cutting (Quality Gates)
 Package(s): All (integration testing harness in ops/orr_tests)
 Type: PROD-REQUIRED
 Owner: QA Lead / PMO
 Timebox: 4 weeks (Month 8–9)
 Dependencies: All Phase 1–2 prod-required tasks complete (the system is feature-complete for v1.0); a stable staging environment available (could be our cluster from P1-T3)
 Steps:
·         Define an ORR checklist structured similarly to prior execution plans (leveraging the DK invariants and risk areas as sections)[162][165]. Include categories such as Performance (latencies, throughput), Security (auth, vault, encryption), Reliability (uptime, failover), Maintainability (backup/restore tested), etc. Each item in the checklist should correspond to a specific DK invariant or capability. For example: “Compile Latency – p95 ≤ 250 ms under nominal load – PASS/FAIL”, “Failover – orchestrator failover within 60 s – PASS/FAIL”, “Audit Completeness – 100% jobs logged & verifiable – PASS/FAIL”[165]. Also include procedural checks like backup restore test, documentation review, etc.
·         Develop or update automated test harnesses for each check where feasible. For performance invariants, use our metrics and load generation tools: e.g. a script to submit N jobs in parallel and measure compile times distribution to verify compile p95 < 250 ms[166]. For reliability, reuse the chaos scripts from P1-T4 to simulate failures and measure recovery times. For security, include at least a run of our security tests (from P1-T5) and maybe a Vault failover test. For new features, test specific cases (e.g. carbon scheduling – run with feature on and off to ensure it doesn’t break things).
·         Execute the full suite in a staging environment that mirrors a production setup (multiple nodes, real KMS, real QPU connection if possible). This might be a prolonged test (over a few days) to gather enough data for things like uptime. We might schedule continuous runs, monitor metrics, and intentionally trigger events (like rotate a key mid-test). Essentially, treat it as a dress rehearsal of running production.
·         Collect all outputs: metrics logs, service logs, test results. Organize them under an artifacts directory (e.g. _artifacts/ORR_v1/). Summarize each test’s outcome and evidence reference. Mark each ORR checklist item as Pass/Fail. If something fails or is borderline, immediately create an issue/ticket to fix or decide if it’s a known limitation we accept for now (with plan). For example, if we find ledger p95 is 130 ms (slightly above 120), note whether that’s due to an anomaly or needs optimization (which could become a P4 task)[167].
·         Have key team members (engineering leads, maybe an external advisor) review the ORR results as if they were a go/no-go gating decision. Do a “dry run” meeting where we walk through each item and ensure we’re comfortable with the evidence. This process may trigger last-minute fixes or hardening tasks (include minor fixes if they can be done quickly; major issues might have to be deferred but explicitly acknowledged).
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Comprehensive ORR Report: An artifact (Markdown or PDF) titled e.g. A4Q_v1_ORR_Report.md listing ~10–15 critical categories, each with specific criteria and a status[168]. For each, provide the result and reference evidence. For example: “Compile Latency: PASS (p95 = 180 ms under 50 concurrent jobs – see metrics_compile.png)[169]; Uptime under chaos: PASS (99.6% over 24h, see failover_test.log)[170]; Vault Integration: PASS (all keys via HSM, see vault_proof.txt); Watermark Detection: PASS (99.5% TPR, see watermark_tests.md)”, etc. All DK invariants must be either Pass or have a consciously approved exception with mitigation[168].
·         Evidence artifacts linked: The report should tie each claim to a file or output. We will provide a zipped folder of artifacts or at least list their paths. For instance, a line in report might say “Ledger Append Latency: PASS – 95th percentile 85 ms (budget 120 ms)” referencing where that data comes from, or point to a Grafana snapshot JSON showing the metric[170]. We expect hundreds of MB of logs/metrics; we’ll store them with checksums to prevent tampering (so results are auditable)[171].
·         The ORR should highlight any stretch goals achieved. E.g. if compile p95 turned out ~50 ms (way under 250), note that as a safety margin achieved[172]. If uptime was effectively 100% in tests, mention that. These buffers give confidence and will feed into marketing narratives.
●	The outcome: we have either all checks pass, or we have documented any shortfalls with a plan to address them very soon (preferably before pilot or by Phase 4). If any critical item fails with no immediate fix, that’s effectively a no-go for GA – but given our disciplined approach, we anticipate passing.
 Notes/Risks: This is essentially our go/no-go internal milestone for GA. It needs to be rigorous. We might do an initial run in Month 8 to catch issues, then a final run in Month 9 after fixes. We involve multiple team members for objectivity. The risk is uncovering a nasty issue late (like a memory leak under long load) – if so, better now than at a partner! We either fix it or clearly document it as a known limitation with mitigation (and communicate that to the pilot partner if needed). By treating this ORR seriously, we ensure we don’t walk into a pilot unprepared. All evidence from here will also bolster investor discussions, as it shows we meet our SLO promises with data.
●	Task ID: P3-T2 – External Pilot Deployment (Design Partner #1)
 Plane: Cross-Cutting (Deployment/Support)
 Package(s): infra/helm (config tailoring), docs/ (pilot guide), support scripts
 Type: PROD-REQUIRED
 Owner: Program Manager (with DevOps & Eng support)
 Timebox: 6 weeks (Month 9–10)
 Dependencies: P3-T1 ORR must be essentially passed (we trust the system); earlier integration tasks (P2) stable (QPU integration, Vault, etc. all working in staging)
 Steps:
·         Identify and prepare for the pilot partner. Assume we have one early design partner (e.g. a national lab or a corporate R&D department) who agreed to try A4Q. Work closely to gather their environment details: do they have a Kubernetes cluster we can use? Any special network or security requirements (VPNs, firewalls)? Obtain necessary access or plan a deployment approach (onsite vs remote). Ensure NDAs etc. are in place as needed.
·         Create a tailored deployment bundle for the partner. This likely involves our Helm chart packaged (from P1-T3) and a configuration specific to them. Disable any dev-only or experimental features unless needed (e.g. ensure A4Q_SIM=0, carbon scheduling off unless they want it, etc.)[173]. Provide recommended hardware specs for the cluster (CPU/RAM for the services, perhaps ask for a couple of nodes). If the partner has their own quantum hardware or cloud accounts, incorporate those credentials (Vault can be set up in their env to store keys). Essentially, produce a pilot deployment plan document.
·         Deploy A4Q in the partner’s environment. This may involve traveling on-site or lots of screen-shares. Use the Helm chart to install on their cluster (or set up VMs if they don’t use K8s). This step will likely uncover environment-specific issues (different storage class, proxy settings, etc.), which we’ll troubleshoot in real-time. Work through them to get all pods running. Ensure our monitoring is also deployed or accessible – we might set up port-forwarding for Grafana or have them set up logging to their system.
·         Once up, run a joint shakeout test in their environment. This could be rerunning a subset of ORR tests (scaled down if their hardware is limited) – e.g. submit 10 test jobs, kill one pod to test failover, check metrics. The goal is to verify the system works end-to-end in their environment as it did in ours[174]. Fix any config issues that surface (e.g. maybe time sync issues if their clusters lack NTP – just hypothetical).
·         Establish ongoing monitoring and support channels. For the duration of the pilot, we should be monitoring the system. Possibly set up our Prom/Grafana to scrape their metrics (if allowed) or get read access to their monitoring. Also set up a direct communication channel (Slack/Teams) between our team and theirs for quick issue resolution[175]. Possibly schedule regular check-ins.
·         Onboard their team: Do a mini-training for their users and admins. Show how to submit jobs via CLI or API, how to attach trust contracts, how to interpret logs or metrics, how to use the watermark verification toolkit, etc. Essentially ensure at least a couple of their staff know how to operate the platform at a basic level[176]. This not only empowers them but also is a learning experience for us (what documentation is lacking, etc.).
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Deployment confirmation: A sign-off from the partner that installation was successful. This could be an email or a section in the pilot plan doc stating “Deployment completed on [date] – all core services running on [Partner] infrastructure, basic health checks passed.”[177]. We can also provide a sanitized kubectl get pods output or similar from their cluster as evidence (if permitted).
·         Functional check at partner site: Evidence that at least one non-trivial workload ran on their deployment. For example, “Partner X executed a 5-qubit QAOA algorithm through A4Q orchestrator connecting to IonQ, and got results Y.” Provide details: job ID, which backend, etc., confirming that our platform facilitated a real use-case[178]. Also mention if watermarking or trust fabric was used: e.g. they sealed the job with an IP contract and later verified the watermark, demonstrating the feature in action.
·         Metrics snapshot: Perhaps a Grafana screenshot from their environment after a week of running, showing all green (all DK metrics within limits)[179]. This would be powerful evidence that even outside our lab, the invariants hold. If direct snapshot not possible, at least get some stats: e.g. “In first week, partner ran 50 jobs, compile p95 was 190 ms, ledger p95 80 ms, job success 100% – all within targets.”
·         Feedback log: Gather initial feedback from the partner’s team – maybe as a short Q&A or a feedback form. Include any quotes or summary: e.g. “Partner found the CLI easy to use, but needed clarification on X,” or “They identified a need for better error message when backend is at capacity – logged as issue #123.” This shows we are collecting feedback systematically[180].
●	The pilot is considered a success (for this phase) if the partner can use the system for at least one real scenario with only minor support from us – i.e., we’re not constantly firefighting, and they aren’t blocked by major bugs[181]. Evidence of this could be a statement like “After initial setup, Partner ran independent tests on Day 2 with no critical issues” or similar.
 Notes/Risks: An external pilot is unpredictable. We mitigate by having our engineers on standby to fix issues quickly (even deploying hot patches if needed). We must set expectations with the partner that this is a pilot/beta – some hiccups are expected and we’ll handle them. The success criteria is not zero bugs, but that we resolve issues quickly and demonstrate the promised capabilities in their environment[182]. This success will be huge: it de-risks the tech (outsider validation) and provides a reference. By end of Phase 3, we want to say “Design Partner #1 has been running on our platform and is happy enough to continue.” That validation is gold for both product confidence and investor storytelling.
●	Task ID: P3-T3 – Standards Contribution & IP Alignment
 Plane: Governance (Standards)
 Package(s): platform/watermark-pass (spec documentation), docs/standards
 Type: PRE-PROD (external collaboration)
 Owner: CTO or Chief Scientist
 Timebox: 3 weeks (Month 10–11, overlapping with pilot support)
 Dependencies: A stable, documented description of our watermarking/provenance approach; possibly membership or access to relevant standards working group meetings
 Steps:
·         Prepare a technical whitepaper or draft specification detailing our quantum circuit watermarking and provenance tagging scheme. This should include: how we embed the watermark (e.g. slight angle perturbations or extra gates, and how it encodes an identifier), how it is detected, the measured overhead (depth <0.5%), and how the ledger ties to these marks[183]. Essentially, translate our implementation into a form that could become a standard or at least inform one.
·         Identify the appropriate standards body or forum (e.g. the QIR Alliance, IEEE P7130 quantum standards, or even W3C if relevant) and contribute our ideas. This could mean formally submitting the whitepaper to a working group or presenting it in a meeting[184]. Emphasize that our approach can be a reference implementation for quantum IP tracking, which might encourage adoption.
·         In parallel, any insights from our governance plane (like our trust fabric data model, or carbon accounting approach) that might influence emerging standards – share those in community forums. For example, if there’s a multi-cloud quantum API group, mention how we handle identity and policy. The goal is not to give away our competitive advantage, but to ensure compatibility and show thought leadership[185].
·         Coordinate with our IP/legal team to ensure we don’t accidentally void patents. If we have patents filed on watermarking, we might approach standards with a letter of assurance (committing to reasonable licensing, etc.). This step ensures we contribute in a way that both influences standards and protects our IP appropriately[186].
·         After presenting or submitting, gather any feedback from the standards community. Perhaps other members raise questions or suggestions (e.g. “what about watermarking for analog quantum data?”). Document these and see if any immediate changes are prudent for us (maybe minor adjustments to align terminology or data formats with what the QIR community expects).
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Submitted Draft: An artifact such as A4Q_QIR_Watermark_Draft.pdf which is the document we sent to the standards group[187]. Also evidence of submission: e.g. an email to the committee or meeting minutes showing we presented. If possible, include a snippet of minutes or an email reply acknowledging our contribution[187].
·         Community Engagement: If we received any official feedback or invitations, note them. For instance, “IEEE Quantum Workshop invited us to co-author an IP tracking standard” or “QIR Alliance asked us to demo our provenance system at next meeting” – any such note or email can be cited as evidence that our ideas are being taken seriously[188][189].
·         Standards Directory Update: Our repo’s standards/ directory might now contain the proposals or reference implementations we contributed (with appropriate license). We can show that in our documentation we’ve marked which parts of our system align with which draft standards. E.g., “We updated our QIR emission to tag custom metadata in line with draft QIR provenance spec v0.2” if that happened.
·         While it’s hard to “pass/fail” this task, success is measured by external recognition: at minimum, our approach is on the radar of standard-setters, and at best, we’re helping shape those standards[189]. We note evidence like being asked to join an official working group or being cited by others.
●	This outcome helps cement our credibility: we aren’t just building a proprietary system in isolation; we’re actively contributing to the quantum ecosystem’s rules and frameworks, which partners and customers will appreciate (and investors too).
 Notes/Risks: Dedicating time to standards could slow feature development, but we limit it to a few weeks and treat it as parallel to pilot (the CTO can handle it while engineers focus on fixes)[190]. The risk of sharing is IP leakage – we mitigate via careful disclosure (we share enough to influence, not our “secret sauce” beyond what’s patent-protected or obvious)[191]. The benefit is high: if our method (watermarking ledger) becomes the standard, we gain first-mover advantage and trust. By end of Phase 3, we ideally have the reputation of being thought leaders in quantum IP governance.
●	Task ID: P3-T4 – Version 1.0.0 Release Packaging
 Plane: Cross-Cutting (Release Management)
 Package(s): infra/helm, docs/CHANGELOG.md, CI/CD (release pipeline)
 Type: PROD-REQUIRED
 Owner: Release Manager / DevOps Lead
 Timebox: 2 weeks (Month 12)
 Dependencies: All critical pilot feedback addressed or scheduled; ORR passed (or any deviations known); documentation updated (from P3-T2, P3-T3 tasks)
 Steps:
·         Create a release branch for v1.0.0 (or v1.2.0 if internal version numbers differ). Bump version numbers across the project to this official release. Freeze new feature merges; only allow bugfixes that were identified in pilot/ORR to be cherry-picked. This ensures a stable baseline.
·         Generate final build artifacts: build Docker images for each service with tag v1.0.0; package the Helm chart (e.g. a4q-1.0.0.tgz) with updated values and defaults (perhaps enabling all core features by default now that they’re stable). Also, if applicable, compile CLI binaries or SDK packages (maybe publish our TypeScript SDK to npm as a4q-sdk@1.0.0)[192].
·         Write release notes / CHANGELOG clearly summarizing what is delivered in v1.0.0. This should map to the major tasks done: e.g. “Integrated HSM-based key management (PQC keys now in Vault), Added IBM Q backend support (beta), Implemented carbon-aware scheduling (experimental, off by default), Achieved compile p95 ~50 ms (20% of budget), etc.” Basically, a bullet list of features/improvements, plus any known limitations (e.g. “consortium ledger feature is pilot-only” or “multi-tenant support is basic; isolation proven in tests but only one tenant used in pilot”). This becomes both a technical summary and a bit of marketing[193][194].
·         Verify the reproducibility of the release. Do a clean clone of the repo on a fresh environment, follow the installation docs exactly, and ensure you can get the system running and passing smoke tests. This is like an external user trying to install it – we want to catch any missing steps or environment assumptions. If anything is unclear (like needing to create a certain Kubernetes secret manually), update the docs accordingly[195].
·         Tag the repository (v1.0.0) and archive all relevant release artifacts in our internal storage (or an artifact repo). Also ensure CI is set to preserve these artifacts (CI might deploy images to a registry, etc.).
·         Optionally, run one last round of sanity tests on the exact release artifacts – basically deploy the v1.0.0 Helm chart on our staging one more time and run a quick test suite, just to double-confirm nothing like a last-minute config change broke things[196]. It’s a belt-and-suspenders check.
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Git Tag and Checksums: The repo shows a v1.0.0 tag at the appropriate commit. We produce an artifact (like A4Q_v1.0.0_checksums.txt) listing the SHA256 of each Docker image, the Helm chart package, etc., to guarantee integrity of the release bits[197]. This artifact is stored and maybe signed by us.
·         Release Notes Document: CHANGELOG.md or Release_Notes_v1.0.0.md is updated with a clear list of features delivered. It should align with the execution plan: e.g. points for PQC KMS, multi-region ledger, pilot success, etc., each of which ties back to tasks we completed[193][198]. This will be shared with stakeholders (maybe even publicly if we were open source).
·         Installation Guide Verified: Provide evidence that someone other than the core devs can follow our deployment docs to stand up the system. Perhaps have a team member not involved in day-to-day dev attempt installation with the released artifacts and confirm success. Document any issues found and their resolution. The goal is to ensure the system is not just working in our environment, but reproducible by others (with our support).
·         Final Test Run: A brief report of sanity tests on the frozen release (maybe _artifacts/v1.0.0_sanity.log). It might say e.g. “Deployed v1.0.0 to staging cluster, ran 20 sample jobs across 2 backends, all passed. Metrics all green.” This just confirms the packaging didn’t introduce any glitch.
·         At this point, all tasks marked PROD-REQUIRED in Phases 1–3 should be complete or explicitly deferred. We can include a v1.0 completion checklist mapping each critical task to its status (should be DONE). If any were deferred (none ideally, but say if one minor thing was left), justify it.
·         Sign-off announcement: Possibly an internal announcement or email: “Version 1.0.0 of Invarian A4Q is officially released on [date]!” listing the highlights. This is more a ceremonial evidence, but a snapshot of that communication shows we have confidence to label it GA.
 Notes/Risks: This is largely administrative but essential for polish. The risk is rushing and missing something – we combat that by dedicating time solely to packaging and docs. Another consideration: what do we call this release externally? We might call it “v1.0 Pilot Release” or similar if we consider the first design partner deployment as GA. However, as per roadmap, GA might have been v1.1; the naming is less important than the fact that we completed all must-have features[199]. By Month 12, we have a product we feel confident handing to users with proper docs and support – this sets the stage for broader use in the next phase.
Phase 4 (Months 12–16) – Feature Expansion & Performance Tuning
Phase Goals (12–16mo): With a stable v1.0 baseline out and a pilot running, pivot back to innovation and improvement, guided by pilot feedback and next-stage goals. Major aims: (1) Introduce Advanced Features in Controlled Mode – begin integrating more Autonomy plane capabilities like automated calibration (AutoCal-RL) and anomaly detection into the live system, but behind feature flags (“shadow mode”) to gather data without risking stability[200]; (2) Tighten Performance – identify any SLO areas where we barely met budgets (or regressed due to new features) and optimize aggressively (e.g. reduce ledger latency further, optimize compile time, ensure we consistently hit stretch goals)[201]; (3) Multi-Cloud & Multi-Tenant Prep – lay groundwork for broader scale: extend orchestrator to handle multiple cloud providers seamlessly (multi-cloud broker design) and enhance the trust-fabric for multi-tenant use (org-level isolation, quotas) in anticipation of onboarding more clients; (4) Environmental Hardening – validate the platform on different infrastructures (different cloud vendors, on-prem installs, larger scale clusters) to ensure our design generalizes beyond the initial pilot environment[202]. In Phase 4, we go from one successful pilot to a platform ready for many, by polishing scalability and adding the first layer of “autonomous controls” that will differentiate A4Q in the long run.
·         Task ID: P4-T1 – “Magic State” Simulator & Anomaly Detection (Autonomy R&D)
 Plane: Autonomy (FTQC Prep)
 Package(s): services/orchestrator (or new sub-module), ml/anomaly-detect
 Type: R&D SPIKE
 Owner: Autonomy Lead (with ML Engineer)
 Timebox: 6 weeks (Month 12–14)
 Dependencies: Pilot environment metrics (we have baseline error rates, etc.), possibly some data from hardware or high-fidelity sim to simulate FT behavior
 Steps:
·         Implement a simplified Magic State Factory simulator within our orchestration environment. “Magic states” are a key resource for fault-tolerant quantum computing (FTQC). Since actual FT hardware might not be available yet, create a module that simulates their production/consumption: e.g. a pool that generates “magic state tokens” at a certain rate, representing an FTQC’s distillation process[203]. Have it integrate with scheduler logic such that if few tokens are available, the orchestrator knows resources are constrained (like simulating limited FT throughput). This is to test how we’d manage scarce FT resources in future.
·         Develop an Anomaly Detection module that monitors streams of metrics (error rates, queue wait times, calibration drift, etc.) and flags abnormal patterns. This could be a simple statistical detection (e.g. if job failure rate > threshold for last N jobs, flag it) or a lightweight ML model (like a running z-score or a small NN). Initially, it just logs anomalies – e.g. “Anomaly: QPU #3 error rate spiked to 5% (norm 1%)”[204]. Do not yet tie it into automated actions, just detection.
·         Integrate the anomaly detection with orchestrator’s view: have orchestrator or a sidecar subscribe to anomaly events. If an anomaly is detected (say, one backend’s error rate jumps), orchestrator can mark that backend as “needs attention” (maybe it stops routing new jobs there temporarily) but still require human confirmation to take action, since we are in early mode[204]. Essentially, implement hooks for response but keep them manual or off by default.
·         Test these in a controlled scenario: Simulate an anomaly by feeding fake data (e.g. tell the anomaly detector that error rate on backend A is now 10x higher). Ensure it logs the anomaly and that orchestrator, if coded to, would mark backend A in a “degraded” state (but not fully remove it unless we had that logic). For magic state sim, simulate a period of heavy usage and see if the orchestrator’s scheduling can reflect it (maybe by limiting new FTQC-type jobs if magic state tokens are low).
·         Keep these features feature-flagged or in “shadow mode” meaning they run and log but do not yet alter core operations automatically. We aim to gather data and trust in them.
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Magic State simulation logs: Provide log output from orchestrator during a simulation scenario: e.g. “MagicStateSim: Produced 50 tokens, consumed 45, remaining 5” and maybe “Scheduler: delaying new FT job as magic state resources low” if we implement a throttle. This demonstrates the simulation is active. We might also plot a small graph of “available vs used magic states over time in simulation run” to show how the system would behave if these were real[203][204].
·         Anomaly detection demonstration: An artifact (maybe _artifacts/anomaly_detection_demo.log) showing that when we introduced an anomaly (like increased error rates on a simulated backend), the system logged it: “Anomaly detected: QPU_X error rate 7% (normal ~1%) – flagged at 12:00:05”[204][205]. If the orchestrator took any action (even just marking it), show that in the log as well: “Backend QPU_X marked as degraded due to anomaly” (for example). Also include how the anomaly clears (if it drops back to normal, do we log “Anomaly resolved” or similar).
·         Integration in telemetry: Perhaps update our metrics to track anomalies – e.g. a counter of anomalies detected. If so, show that metric increments as expected when we simulate anomalies.
·         Since this is R&D, success isn’t measured by immediate impact but by evidence we can detect issues reliably with minimal false positives or overhead. For acceptance, we should note preliminary results like “No false anomalies in 24h of normal pilot data; detected a planted issue within 1 minute.” If we did any offline analysis, mention it.
●	The outcome should indicate we have laid the groundwork for self-healing but are keeping it in test mode. We likely plan in Era 2 to start automating responses.
 Notes/Risks: Being R&D, it’s okay if by end of Phase 4 this isn’t a production feature yet. The risk is overloading production with too-early ML – we avoid that by shadow mode[206]. Also, we need actual data: the pilot’s runs and any hardware data we can get will train/tune these detectors. We ensure capturing as much pilot telemetry as possible to refine anomaly thresholds. This task keeps the team engaged with advanced goals (so we don’t stagnate after initial release)[207], and positions us to claim partial “autonomy” functionality, which is good narrative even if not fully closed-loop yet.
●	Task ID: P4-T2 – AutoCal-RL Deployment in Shadow Mode
 Plane: Discovery → Autonomy (Calibration)
 Package(s): ml/autocal-rl, services/orchestrator (hooks)
 Type: PRE-PROD (pilot feature)
 Owner: ML Engineer (Calibration)
 Timebox: 4 weeks (Month 13–14)
 Dependencies: AutoCal-RL working in lab (from GA it existed as R&D); anomaly detection (P4-T1) maybe provides triggers to use this
 Steps:
·         Containerize or otherwise package the AutoCal-RL service so it can run in our deployed environment. This likely means creating a Docker image for ml/autocal-rl and adding it to our Helm deployment (maybe as an optional component). Ensure it can connect to either real hardware calibration interfaces or our simulated ones. In pilot, maybe they have a test QPU or we use a simulator’s calibration API.
·         Integrate with orchestrator: define when and how orchestrator should consult the AutoCal-RL service. Perhaps we tie it to anomaly detection: e.g. if anomaly detector flags increasing error rates or drift, orchestrator calls AutoCal-RL for a calibration suggestion[208]. Alternatively, schedule periodic calibrations: orchestrator can periodically send current system metrics to AutoCal and get recommended calibration adjustments (but not apply them automatically yet).
·         Do not auto-apply calibration changes in this phase. Instead, log the recommendations. For example, AutoCal might output “Suggest tuning Qubit 5 frequency by +2 kHz.” Or “Reschedule calibration on QPU2 now.” Capture these outputs and perhaps have orchestrator log, “AutoCal suggestion: [action]. Not applied (shadow mode).”[209][210]. If possible, maybe apply it to a simulator to gauge effect, but not on real hardware without approval.
·         If the partner is willing and we have a safe window, maybe try one manual application of an AutoCal suggestion on a real device or high-fidelity sim to see if it improves things. For example, if AutoCal says to adjust something, coordinate with the QPU vendor or use an offline experiment to test that adjustment’s effect. This could yield anecdotal evidence of improvement (though we won’t likely do this frequently yet).
·         Ensure that running AutoCal doesn’t interfere with main operations: it could be compute-heavy (RL training). Possibly dedicate a separate node or resources to it (maybe it even runs on a GPU if needed). Confirm it doesn’t starve the CPU or memory that orchestrator/trust-fabric need[211].
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         AutoCal service running: Show that the AutoCal-RL container is deployed and remains healthy. Possibly an entry in kubectl get pods like autocal-rl-... Running. And logs from it receiving data or sending suggestions. Provide a snippet: e.g. “AutoCal: Received error rate trend [..], recommended calibration: qubit5 +0.05 bias”[210][212]. This indicates it’s functioning integratedly.
·         Example suggestion output: Document one or two specific suggestions it produced and what they mean. For instance: “On 2026-01-10, AutoCal recommended increasing Qubit7’s drive amplitude by 3% after noticing T1 time dropped by 20%. (Not applied automatically.)”[212]. If we did manually apply any suggestion in test, report the outcome (e.g. error rate improved from X to Y). If not, at least show a hypothetical – maybe on simulator we can show before/after error metrics.
·         Performance/Isolation: Evidence that having AutoCal running doesn’t degrade platform performance. For example, after deploying it, compile and orchestrator latencies remained the same. Or we set limits (like it uses at most 1 CPU) and observed no impact. Show resource usage from monitoring if relevant. This ensures we can run it continuously in background without harm[211][213].
·         Adoption by users: If a partner user or internal user looked at suggestions, note any feedback (like “the suggestions looked reasonable vs they were off”). This might be anecdotal, but could be useful to tweak the model.
●	The main acceptance is that by end of Phase 4, we have AutoCal integrated and providing live insights (even if we don’t trust it to auto-tune just yet)[207]. That sets us up to later demonstrate closed-loop calibration improvements.
 Notes/Risks: This brings an ML component into a production env which is non-trivial. We mitigate risk by not letting it directly control anything yet[207]. The risk is also that it may not produce meaningful suggestions (NISQ devices might already be well-calibrated by vendors). We accept that early output might be neutral or even ineffective; even a null result is learning[207]. The key is we now have the pipeline integrated: data flows from orchestrator to AutoCal and recommendations flow back. We can iterate on the model as we gather more data. It also signals to sophisticated partners that we’re working on autonomous tuning, which is part of our long-term Autonomy promise.
●	Task ID: P4-T3 – Performance Tuning Sprint (Ledger & Compile)
 Plane: Cross-Cutting (Optimization)
 Package(s): services/trust-fabric (ledger code & DB tuning), platform/compiler
 Type: PROD-REQUIRED (ensure headroom for scale)
 Owner: Performance Engineer
 Timebox: 4 weeks (Month 14–15)
 Dependencies: Pilot usage data (to know current hotspots), ORR results (to see which metrics were close to limits)
 Steps:
·         Analyze metrics and logs from pilot and ORR to identify performance bottlenecks or close calls. Examples: if ledger append p95 was observed at, say, 110 ms (just under 120 ms target) under moderate load, that’s a candidate for optimization. If compile times spiked to 220 ms for larger circuits, that’s another. Focus on areas where we have little headroom or expect increased load soon[214].
·         Optimize Trust Fabric ledger path: Potential strategies: add an index or optimize queries to speed up writes (maybe our ledger write currently does an extra read we can eliminate). Consider using batch commits – e.g. accumulate a few ledger entries and commit in one transaction to reduce fsync overhead[215]. Tune Postgres parameters for low-latency (perhaps increasing memory for WAL buffers, tweaking fsync settings if safe)[216]. Evaluate if any steps can be parallelized or offloaded (maybe move some non-critical parts of append – like writing to an audit log – to async). We must ensure audit coverage stays 100% though.
·         Optimize Compiler: Profile the Rust compiler to see which passes or operations consume most time. If our watermark-pass or QIR adapter is slow, see if it can be optimized (e.g. optimize our algorithm, or if we were not multi-threaded, allow parallel compilation of independent parts of a circuit)[217]. Possibly implement caching for common library circuits or reuse compiled sub-circuits across jobs if applicable (like a memoization). Ensure any caching doesn’t break watermark uniqueness, etc. Another approach: if compiles are single-threaded, allow multiple compiler instances in parallel (depending on CPU cores) to handle concurrent job submissions.
·         PQC overhead revisit: Now that Vault integration is in, measure if PQC crypto calls added overhead. If so, see if persistent connections or optimized clients help. Possibly move to a faster PQC algorithm if one is standardized and acceptable (our invariant is 20 ms, but if we’re near that, maybe try a smaller parameter set or session keys)[218].
·         Implement the chosen optimizations and test them with microbenchmarks. For ledger: simulate 1000 rapid writes and measure p95 before vs after. For compile: compile a set of circuits of varying sizes and measure improvement.
·         Run a subset of ORR tests (especially those relevant to performance invariants) with the optimized build to verify that indeed we have more headroom now.
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Before vs After metrics: A comparison table or chart for key metrics such as ledger append p95 and compile p95, showing their values before optimization (e.g. from pilot/ORR data) and after our improvements[219]. For example: “Ledger p95: was 105 ms, now 80 ms (≈25% faster); Compile p95 for 5-qubit circuits: was 180 ms, now 120 ms (33% faster)”[219]. Include data for a few scenarios if possible (light load vs heavier load).
·         If we achieved enough headroom to tighten any DK thresholds, mention it. E.g., if we consistently get ledger p95 ~80 ms now, we might internally set a new stretch goal or at least know we can handle more load.
·         Confirm no negative side effects: run regression tests to ensure we didn’t break correctness. For instance, after optimizing ledger, verify that audit log still records everything correctly (maybe run the test where we append and then read ledger entries to ensure none lost). After compiler changes, verify output circuits are identical or logically equivalent (watermark still effective). Provide a brief summary: “All regression tests pass; watermark TPR unchanged at 99% but compile time improved.”[220]
·         Possibly adjust our config defaults to reflect improvements (e.g. if we had any artificial delays or conservative limits, we can relax them now). Document if any DK constants could be updated. If not public constants, at least we know we meet them with margin now, which we can use as a selling point.
●	This sprint’s success means by end of Phase 4, we are confidently operating well within our SLO budgets rather than just barely hitting them. That’s important as we go to multiple customers (safety margin for variability)[221].
 Notes/Risks: Need to ensure optimization efforts don’t accidentally compromise reliability or readability. But given test coverage, we’ll catch issues. Gains here directly affect our moat – better performance than competitors and ability to scale cheaply[221]. Also it preps us for more workload in Phase 5. There’s low risk doing this now when we have real data and a bit of development breathing room after v1.0.
●	Task ID: P4-T4 – Multi-Cloud Broker Design & PoC
 Plane: Autonomy (Multi-Cloud)
 Package(s): Possibly new service services/broker (or extend orchestrator for now)
 Type: PRE-PROD (architecture PoC)
 Owner: Cloud Integration Architect
 Timebox: 5 weeks (Month 15–16)
 Dependencies: P2-T1 (we integrated one cloud in Phase 2); readiness to integrate a second backend (e.g. have API access to AWS Braket or another QPU)
 Steps:
·         Create a design proposal for how to handle multiple cloud backends and potentially multiple orchestrators. Two approaches: (A) one orchestrator service can handle multiple backend plugins internally (our current approach extended), or (B) introduce a higher-level Broker service that routes jobs to different orchestrator instances, each specialized per provider/region[222]. Evaluate pros/cons (broker might add overhead but decouples concerns; single orchestrator is simpler but might get complex with many providers).
·         Implement a minimal proof-of-concept for multi-cloud routing. For example, integrate a second provider (say AWS Braket) in addition to IBM which we did. Instead of fully coding Braket in orchestrator, try a decoupled approach: spin up a second orchestrator instance configured for Braket, and implement a simple Broker component that receives incoming job requests and decides whether to send to Orchestrator_A (IBM) or Orchestrator_B (AWS)[223]. This broker could be just a simple HTTP proxy or a new microservice with a routing table. Criteria could be based on a job parameter (e.g. backend: AWS in the contract) or simple load balancing.
·         Ensure the Trust Fabric and policy still work in this multi-orchestrator scenario. That likely means trust-fabric remains a single authority (or each orchestrator writes to the same ledger). For now, we can keep one ledger that all orchestrators use (via the trust-fabric service as central). If needed, include an identifier of which cloud a job ran on in the ledger entries (like add a “cloud: AWS” field). Modify schemas as needed to differentiate[224].
·         Test the PoC: Submit one job targeted to IBM, one targeted to AWS (maybe by using different backend flags). Verify that the broker sent them to the correct orchestrator and they completed. We might use a dummy or simulator for AWS if we can’t use real hardware in time, but at least implement the API calls to prove we can. Monitor overhead: measure if the broker introduced any significant latency (should be minimal, just routing).
·         Document the approach and results. If the PoC reveals complexity, we might adjust strategy (e.g. decide to keep single orchestrator with plugin architecture but that might become unwieldy at scale vs having multiple orchestrators). This is an architectural decision that will drive how we scale in Era 2 when multi-cloud support becomes a main focus.
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Design spec or diagram: Provide a brief design doc in docs/multicloud_design_v1.md that outlines how jobs flow in a multi-cloud scenario[225]. E.g., we might include a diagram: Client -> Broker -> Orchestrator A (IBM) or Orchestrator B (AWS) -> respective QPU. The doc should capture decisions (like if we lean towards a broker service architecture) and rationale[225].
·         PoC demonstration logs: Show that we successfully ran a job on a second provider via our system. For example, an artifact _artifacts/multi_cloud_demo.log showing a submission with parameter “target: IonQ” and the broker log “Routing job 123 to IonQ orchestrator”, then that orchestrator’s log showing the job executed, and trust-fabric having an entry recorded for it[226]. Even if IonQ or AWS calls were stubbed or simulated, the flow should mimic real calls to confirm our abstraction works.
·         Performance note: Document if the broker added any measurable overhead (maybe it’s negligible like <10 ms)[227]. If any limitations found (e.g. orchestrators currently share state we need to separate), note them and possible solutions (like use a distributed queue or etcd).
·         Follow-up tasks: The acceptance can include a list of follow-up work for productionizing multi-cloud in Era 2, such as “Implement unified queue across orchestrators” or “Add policy to prevent cross-cloud data leak”, etc., showing we have a concrete plan beyond PoC[228].
●	Essentially, success is proving conceptually that we can scale beyond one cloud. By end of Phase 4, we want to confidently say to a prospective customer: “We’ve already prototyped integration with multiple quantum cloud providers – our architecture supports adding new backends as needed.”[229]. The evidence is the working demo and design blueprint.
 Notes/Risks: This is forward-looking: multi-cloud will be crucial to avoid vendor lock-in and optimize costs/queue times. The risk is designing too much prematurely; that’s why we do a modest PoC now to guide Era 2 development, not full implementation[230]. Also, each provider has its quirks (different API shapes, job models) – this PoC will flush out any abstraction issues early. For now, it’s fine if it’s a bit hacky (not production-ready), as long as it informs the robust solution we’ll build in Era 2.
●	Task ID: P4-T5 – UX and API Polish (CLI/SDK & Docs)
 Plane: Discovery & Governance (User Experience)
 Package(s): sdk/ts (CLI/SDK), docs/
 Type: PRE-PROD (usability improvements)
 Owner: Developer Experience Engineer / Technical Writer
 Timebox: 4 weeks (Month 15–16)
 Dependencies: Core features complete (so docs can be finalized); pilot user feedback on CLI/usage; multi-cloud design decisions (in case API needs extension)
 Steps:
·         Gather feedback from pilot users and internal testers on the CLI/SDK usability. Identify pain points: maybe commands were too verbose, error messages unclear, or they wanted a Python library instead of just CLI. For each quick win, plan a fix. E.g., add clearer --help descriptions, shorter aliases for common commands, or more output format options (JSON output for scripts)[231][232].
·         Implement improvements in the CLI/SDK: e.g. if multiple users ask for a Python SDK, perhaps generate or wrap our API in a simple Python client library. Or at least document how to use our REST API in Python. If CLI error messages were confusing, rewrite them to be actionable (e.g. “Failed to seal job: region EU not available – check policy or available backends.”). Ensure any new features (region flags, multi-backend choices, etc.) are fully supported by CLI arguments or SDK methods[231].
·         Write a comprehensive Quickstart Guide and possibly a tutorial. E.g. a guide “Deploy A4Q and run your first quantum job in 30 minutes”. This would include steps from installation to running a sample job and verifying it in the ledger[233]. Use the experience from Phase 3 pilot onboarding to make sure it’s accurate and easy.
·         Review and refine all API responses and data formats. Ensure consistency and completeness. For example, make sure /v1/status returns all relevant info (maybe add job carbon footprint field now), unify date formats, etc.[234]. If there were fields pilot users wanted (like a job cost estimate), see if we can include them now.
·         Optionally, create a simple web dashboard UI for demonstration purposes. If time permits, a minimal web app that lists jobs, their status, and allows simple submissions could be helpful for less technical stakeholders. If not building it now, at least design it and maybe schedule it for Era 2 or as a separate tool. For now, focus on CLI and docs improvements.
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Updated Documentation: The docs/ directory (or a published docs site) contains an up-to-date Quickstart, user guide, API reference, and admin guide. Provide a brief listing of new docs or major sections added (e.g. “Quickstart.md”, “Multi-tenant Admin Guide.md”). Possibly have a new person run through the quickstart to verify it truly works and is easy – include their feedback like “New hire installed and submitted a job in 25 minutes following guide, only minor question on step 4 which we clarified.”
·         CLI improvements list: Show a changelog of CLI changes based on feedback: e.g. “Added --output json to all CLI commands for easier scripting[232]; Improved a4qctl status to display carbon and region info; Shortened a4qctl orchestrator list-backends to a4qctl backends,” etc. Perhaps include a before/after example of usage to highlight how it’s more user-friendly now.
·         Issue closure: Reference specific GitHub issues or feedback items from pilot that have been addressed (e.g. “Pilot request: easier way to query job by name – added feature X to CLI version 1.0.1”). This shows we listened and improved.
·         Pilot user confirmation: If possible, get a brief note from a pilot user after the improvements, like “The new CLI flags and documentation helped our team use A4Q more effectively.” Or at least note that there are no longer open high-priority usability complaints[235].
·         No regressions: Ensure none of these front-end changes break the invariants or core logic (they shouldn’t, as they’re mostly superficial). All automated tests still pass, etc. The acceptance is qualitative here: the platform feels more polished and easy to use, which is crucial as we prepare to scale out to more users in Phase 5[236].
 Notes/Risks: It’s easy to neglect UX when focused on back-end, so this task corrects that. The risk was pilot users getting frustrated at small things – by addressing them, we increase user satisfaction and reduce support burden[237]. We must keep updating docs as things evolve (maybe assign someone ongoing). Good UX will be a differentiator in early market where many competitors might have clunky research-oriented interfaces. Phase 4’s polish sets us up to handle more pilots/customers smoothly in Phase 5.
Phase 5 (Months 16–20) – Scaling Out & Multiple Pilots
Phase Goals (16–20mo): Now that v1.0 is stable and one pilot is running, focus on scaling deployment and proving the platform can handle multiple environments and larger demands. Key objectives: (1) Second & Third Pilot Deployments – repeat and streamline the deployment process for additional design partners (e.g. one with a cloud vendor, one with an enterprise R&D use-case), validating that our playbook is repeatable and the platform generalizes beyond one scenario[238]; (2) Multi-Tenancy Basics – implement organization-level isolation and admin controls so that multiple teams or even multiple client orgs could share one installation safely (or to run A4Q as a SaaS with tenant isolation)[239]; (3) Tighten SLOs & Ops Processes – push reliability toward enterprise-grade (target 99.9% uptime, set up 24/7 monitoring and on-call rotation, etc.), and formalize operational processes (runbooks, incident response drills, backup/restore, etc.) to ensure we can manage a growing production footprint[240][241]; (4) Prepare for Fault-Tolerance Era – begin integrating any early FTQC prototypes or simulators and align our design with their needs (for example, ensure orchestrator and anomaly modules can handle the scale/complexity of logical qubits), basically forward-compatible architecture for Era 3. Phase 5 demonstrates that A4Q isn’t a one-off demo but a scalable service ready for more users, more hardware, and stricter reliability expectations as we approach year 2.
·         Task ID: P5-T1 – Multi-Tenant Access Control & Org Isolation
 Plane: Governance (Trust Fabric / Security)
 Package(s): services/trust-fabric/auth, services/trust-fabric/ledger
 Type: PROD-REQUIRED (for broader adoption)
 Owner: Governance Engineer (Access Control)
 Timebox: 5 weeks (Month 16–17)
 Dependencies: Single-tenant pilot is stable; possibly need an identity provider or we implement basic internal auth
 Steps:
·         Introduce the concept of organizations/tenants in the trust-fabric. This might involve adding a tenant_id or org_id field to major data structures (ledger entries, contracts, etc.)[242]. Decide on how to identify tenants – e.g. by X.509 client cert field, or by API key association. Possibly extend our JWT or mTLS setup to include an Org claim.
·         Implement authentication & identity mapping for multiple tenants. If we have an external identity provider (e.g. partner uses Okta/OIDC), integrate that to get user info including org. If not, at least implement separate API keys or client certs per tenant and map those to a tenant ID internally[243]. For a simple start, perhaps maintain a config mapping cert fingerprints to tenant names.
·         Ensure all relevant Trust Fabric API calls are scope-aware. E.g., a /v1/ledger query or /v1/status should only return records for the tenant associated with the auth token/cert. Add checks in each handler to filter by tenant (likely adding WHERE tenant_id = X in queries, or doing in-memory filter if needed)[244]. Similarly, submission endpoints should tag records with the tenant of the submitter.
·         Add an organization admin role concept. Possibly extend our policy engine or add simple RBAC: e.g. if a user has role OrgAdmin, they can see all jobs of their org; regular users can see only their jobs. Also define a “super-admin” (our ops team) that can see across orgs (for support)[245]. Use OPA or even simple conditionals to enforce this.
·         Modify ledger and telemetry queries accordingly. E.g., if an org admin asks for metrics, ensure they only get their slice or aggregated by tenant safely.
·         Write tests simulating two tenants (say “Alpha” and “Beta”) using one deployment. Attempt cross-tenant access (Alpha user tries to fetch Beta’s job ID) and ensure it’s forbidden. Also test an admin from our side can see both (if we allow that).
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Tenant ID in Data: Show that data in the ledger now carries a tenant identifier[242][246]. For example, a snippet of a ledger database entry or a telemetry record that includes tenant_id = 'AlphaCorp'. This proves multi-tenant segregation is built-in.
·         Access control test: Provide a test log or description of an attempted forbidden access and its result: e.g. “User from Org Beta attempted to retrieve job from Org Alpha, received 403 Forbidden – PASS.” Also show allowed access: Beta user accesses Beta job – success[247]. Possibly include the automated test cases outputs or a small table of test scenarios (Access Matrix: Beta user -> Beta data = OK; Beta -> Alpha data = DENY; Admin -> Beta data = OK, etc.).
·         Documentation update: The API documentation should note that endpoints are now tenant-scoped. For example: “GET /v1/status returns jobs for the authenticated user’s organization.” Indicate we have updated user docs to reflect multi-tenant behavior and how to onboard a new tenant (issue them certs/keys, etc.).
·         Performance impact: Note that the extra filtering has negligible overhead (just an index on tenant_id). If we did a quick benchmark, mention that queries with tenant filter are as fast as before, confirming no regression.
●	Achieving this is a prerequisite for offering a single instance to multiple customers or teams. We may also use it when deploying Pilot #2 if we host them on our cloud (we might host multiple pilots in one cluster separated by tenant IDs).
 Notes/Risks: Multi-tenancy touches security deeply, so careful review is needed to avoid any leak (we likely do another mini security audit focusing on these changes)[248]. Also need to consider rate limiting or quotas per tenant (maybe note as future work). For now, isolation is mainly at the data access level. With this in place, we can, for example, run a small SaaS for two design partners on one cluster or allow one partner’s multiple teams to share without seeing each other’s work[248]. We will plan a further security review in Phase 6 to double-check no edge-case holes.
●	Task ID: P5-T2 – Pilot Deployment #2 (Cloud Provider Partner)
 Plane: Autonomy/Governance (Integration)
 Package(s): Deployment configs (with custom values), possibly services/orchestrator if new backend needed
 Type: PRE-PROD (expanding usage; real deployments but still design phase)
 Owner: BizDev Engineer / Cloud Partner Lead
 Timebox: 6 weeks (Month 17–18)
 Dependencies: P4-T4 multi-cloud PoC done (so adding another provider is understood); P5-T1 multi-tenant ready (if we host multi pilots together)
 Steps:
·         Engage with a cloud vendor or QPU provider (if our first pilot was not one) as our second pilot. For example, if Pilot 1 was a research lab, Pilot 2 could be a cloud provider who wants to integrate our governance/orchestration into their offering. Collaborate to define the scope: maybe they want to show their hardware working under A4Q’s trust fabric to attract enterprise customers. Ensure mutual incentives are clear (they get compliance and multi-cloud story, we get a high-profile reference).
·         Deploy A4Q in context of the partner’s environment. This could differ from Pilot 1: perhaps the cloud provider wants it in their cloud, or integrated with their existing platform. Deployment might involve linking our orchestrator with their queue (similar to what we did with IBM). Possibly run our services in their Kubernetes cluster or ours but with access to their hardware via API. Sort out networking, credentials (Vault might need to store their API keys), etc.
·         Focus on integration specifics not exercised before: e.g., ensure our orchestrator can interact with their specific API – this might require finishing a second backend plugin (if PoC was incomplete). Reuse multi-cloud architecture: maybe spin up a dedicated orchestrator instance for this provider if that’s how we decided. Implement any missing adapter logic and test it thoroughly with their test endpoints.
·         Once up, demonstrate a workflow highlighting that provider’s strength. For instance, if this partner’s QPU has special capabilities (like high qubit count), run a slightly larger circuit through A4Q to show we handle it. Or if they care about cost optimization, maybe test that our orchestrator can choose cheapest region which is theirs. Essentially adapt to a use-case that resonates with them[249][250].
·         Optionally, use our multi-tenant feature if we run both pilot 1 and 2 on our hosted environment: e.g. have both on one cluster separated by tenant IDs (or separate clusters if preferred). Document whichever route we take.
·         Collect new feature requests or differences: e.g. this partner might want a certain metric exposed, or have their own monitoring that we should integrate (maybe push our metrics to their system). Implement small tweaks as needed to make sure A4Q fits their production environment (these might be minor and not fundamental changes).
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Cloud partner confirmation: A statement or email from the partner like “A4Q successfully connected to [Vendor] Quantum Service – jobs ran through Invarian’s orchestrator on our QPUs”[251]. This shows we did indeed integrate. We should include details: e.g. which QPU or service, any specific figures (like “ran on [Vendor] 20-qubit device, circuit executed successfully with provenance logged”).
·         Example job run: Provide a log or case study of a job that ran on this new partner’s hardware via A4Q[252]. E.g., “Job 77: executed on [Vendor] IonQ system via A4Q, took 30s, results recorded. Ledger entry: ...” or even better, perhaps they allowed us to do a cross-cloud scenario (one job on IBM, one on IonQ) to demonstrate multi-cloud – but that might be later. At least show one significant execution.
·         Integrations and differences resolved: List any modifications we had to make for this pilot (if any) – e.g. “Added support for [Vendor]’s specific authentication method, integrated their cost tracking API for showing $/job”[253]. Show that invariants held: e.g. even on their device with possibly slower queue, our system handled it (maybe job success metric slightly different due to external factors but within expectation).
·         With two reference deployments in two distinct contexts, we can claim versatility. Possibly gather a second reference letter or at least internal statement from them praising how it helped them add governance or multi-tenant capability[254]. That’s more narrative, but we could get something like “It took only 2 weeks to integrate Invarian’s stack with our quantum cloud – their trust fabric added a layer of security our enterprise clients look for.” This could be redacted or paraphrased for evidence.
●	Multi-tenant usage (if applicable): If we hosted multiple pilot orgs in one cluster, show a snippet that indeed one org’s jobs didn’t interfere with another’s and we can monitor both. If we didn’t, that’s fine – we still have the capability ready.
 Notes/Risks: A cloud provider pilot involves ensuring we aren’t seen as competitive but complementary[255]. We emphasize how A4Q can drive more usage of their hardware by making it accessible to enterprises (we basically become an overlay). There’s a risk they have their own orchestrator; we position ours as value-add (policy, multi-cloud). Politically, success here might lead to deeper partnership or even acquisition interest – we handle carefully (focus on tech validation first)[256]. Success is twofold: another environment validated, and relationship built with a major ecosystem player.
●	Task ID: P5-T3 – Pilot Deployment #3 (Enterprise R&D Use Case)
 Plane: Discovery/Governance (Use-case validation)
 Package(s): Possibly integration with their dataset or HPC infra, plus standard deployment
 Type: PRE-PROD
 Owner: Solutions Engineer / Product Manager
 Timebox: 6 weeks (Month 18–19)
 Dependencies: v1.0 stable; perhaps QAGF prototype available to leverage
 Steps:
·         Identify an enterprise or lab with a specific quantum use-case for Pilot #3. For example, a pharma company exploring quantum chemistry, or a bank doing quantum optimization. The goal here is to test how our platform supports a real application workflow, not just generic circuits[257]. They might have data sets or domain-specific circuits, and possibly interest in our Discovery plane features (like QAGF generating or optimizing circuits for their problem).
·         Work closely to understand their workflow. Help encode their problem for our platform: e.g. if they have a molecule they optimize, ensure we can run a VQE (variational quantum eigensolver) circuit on our orchestrator, and track everything. If our QAGF is capable by now, perhaps use it to generate some ansatz circuits for them to compare vs their hand-crafted one[258]. If not, at least manage their runs and log results in ledger.
·         Deploy A4Q in whatever environment suits the collaboration: could be on their cloud subscription, or on our cluster with secure access to their team (multi-tenant can help here too: their org gets an isolated tenant in our system, possibly on a separate namespace). Address data security: if their data is sensitive, ensure encryption in transit (we already have mTLS/PQC) and maybe at rest (we might enable ledger encryption or secure archive as needed).
·         Over a few weeks, execute an experiment cycle with them: e.g. generate 10 candidate circuits (maybe with QAGF or just variations they suggest), run them through A4Q on available QPUs (maybe simulation if needed, or small hardware if accessible), collect results (energy measurements or whatever metric). Use trust-fabric to ensure everything is recorded (great for reproducibility)[259]. See if any quantum approach outperforms their classical baseline even marginally.
·         Gather their feedback on platform utility: Did the provenance logging help them track what was tried? Did watermarking assuage any IP concerns collaborating (maybe less relevant in internal project, but if multiple parties are involved, watermarking shows who contributed what)? Are there features they wish existed (like more analytics on results, or easier way to submit batch experiments)? Note those.
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Case study draft: An internal case study doc describing the use case and outcome[260][261]. E.g.: “Using A4Q Discovery, ACME Pharma tested 50 AI-generated circuits for molecule X’s ground state. Outcome: 1 circuit found via A4Q was 5% more accurate than their manually designed one.” Or if no quantum advantage was found, then state “No improvement over classical, but A4Q streamlined the experimentation and audit – all runs traced and reproducible.” The case study should highlight what was done and any improvements or at least how our platform managed the process.
·         Domain expert feedback: If possible, a quote or paraphrase from their team like “A4Q’s ledger and workflow management saved us time in tracking dozens of quantum experiments. We could focus on analysis rather than plumbing.” And/or any critique to guide improvements (maybe they want a more GUI-based interface for experiments – an input for future).
·         Technical evidence: Provide logs or ledger excerpts showing multiple runs for that use-case. For example: “Ledger entries show 10 runs of QAOA with varying parameters, each with unique watermark linking to ACME’s project ID.” Or we include a snippet of the trust-fabric export showing which circuits (maybe hashed) were used and outcomes. If QAGF was used, provide evidence it generated circuits and those were run (like attach an example circuit it generated)[262].
·         Summarize any new requirements discovered: e.g. they loved audit logs but want a dashboard to compare results – note it. Or they felt integrating their classical simulation with A4Q was tricky – maybe we need better APIs. These become backlog items.
●	With three pilots now (cloud, lab, enterprise), we can identify patterns. Acceptance can include a brief note on common asks (e.g. “both enterprise and lab pilots asked for a better UI -> plan for Phase 6” or “multi-user management was important, good we did multi-tenancy”). This shows we’re learning and adapting.
 Notes/Risks: This pilot is about validating value: does our platform actually accelerate quantum solution development? The risk is quantum advantage may not appear yet, but that’s okay – we focus on improved process and readiness for when hardware improves[263]. Must manage expectations that we’re providing infrastructure, not magical algorithmic breakthroughs (though our QAGF tries to contribute). A success story here is gold for investors and marketing: it shows a real-world application attempted with our platform. Even if results are modest, being able to say “X company used our platform to run their quantum pilot and now trust it for future projects” is key evidence of product-market fit in the making[263].
●	Task ID: P5-T4 – High-Availability & SRE Processes
 Plane: Autonomy/Governance (Operations)
 Package(s): ops/ (runbooks, on-call docs), monitoring/alerting configs
 Type: PROD-REQUIRED (for scaling to prod)
 Owner: SRE Lead
 Timebox: 4 weeks (Month 19–20)
 Dependencies: At least one pilot running continuously (to observe real ops); Phase 4 performance improvements done (so system is stable at scale)
 Steps:
·         Establish formal SLOs/SLAs for the service based on what we’ve achieved and what we want to promise. E.g. commit to 99.9% uptime (which is ~9h downtime/year) for core services, maybe 99% for non-critical ML features, etc.[264]. Define these SLOs and set up monitoring to track them (monthly uptime reports, etc.).
·         Create detailed runbooks for common operational tasks and incident response. E.g., “How to replace a failed node”, “How to restore the ledger from backup”, “Procedure to rotate Vault keys”, “Steps to upgrade the system with zero downtime”[265][266]. These documents should be sufficiently clear that an on-call engineer at 2am can follow them under stress. Put them in ops/runbooks/ and maybe test at least one by having someone else perform it step by step.
·         Set up an on-call rotation within the team. Define who gets paged on what alerts, ensure we have escalation policies. Possibly integrate with a tool like PagerDuty or at least Slack alerts. Write an incident response plan: e.g. severity levels, how we communicate internally and to partners, where to log incident reports.
·         Conduct a disaster recovery drill. For example, simulate a scenario where our primary region goes completely offline (or our cluster is wiped). Follow our DR plan: maybe restore the ledger from the last backup to a new instance, re-point orchestrators, etc.[267][268]. Measure how long it takes to recover and compare to our target RTO (recovery time objective). If it took longer than acceptable (e.g. 5 minutes vs we wanted <1 minute for region failover, or hours vs we want <1h for full restore), identify improvements (maybe we need more automation or a warm standby environment).
·         Implement automated canary tests in staging or production. E.g., have a small job submitted every 5 minutes to ensure the system is working (a heartbeat job), and if it fails, alert. Also perhaps before deploying any update, run a quick ORR subset (like compile a sample, run a dummy job) and require 15 minutes of error-free run before considering the update successful (this was an “Ops Gold” practice we aspired to)[269]. Set this up in CI/CD or as a pre-deployment check.
 Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Runbook repository: A listing of runbook documents created (e.g. runbooks/failover.md, runbooks/backup_restore.md, runbooks/oncall_handover.md). Perhaps an excerpt from one to show the format (sections: Purpose, Preconditions, Procedure, Verification, etc., which echo the structure mentioned in the background)[265][266]. The existence of these and their thoroughness is evidence we’re ops-ready.
·         DR drill report: _artifacts/DR_drill_report.txt describing the simulated disaster and outcomes[270]. E.g. “Simulated loss of primary DB at 10:00, followed runbook ‘Restore DB’. New DB up by 10:05, system read-only until 10:06, fully operational by 10:07. Total downtime ~7 minutes, data loss = 0 (all events replicated).” If we overshot targets, note that and how we’ll improve (like implement cross-region DB clustering by Era 2). If below targets, great – highlight that.
·         On-call schedule & alert screenshot: Show that we have a rotation defined (maybe a screenshot of PagerDuty schedule with team members, or an internal calendar). Also a screenshot or description of an alert config: e.g. “Alert: LedgerLatencyHigh if p95 > 120ms for 5 min – currently 0 alerts firing.” Possibly simulate triggering an alert to show it goes to the right people (non-crit hours).
·         SLO dashboard: Possibly produce a monthly ops review slide or doc showing we met/exceeded our SLOs for e.g. February: uptime 99.95%, no P1 incidents, average carbon/job below target, etc. Even if just internal now, it sets baseline for external SLAs.
·         Summarize how this prep will enable us to confidently manage multiple pilots or early customers. E.g., “We now have 24/7 monitoring and documented procedures – by end of Phase 5, our team operates A4Q as a mature service, not a research project.” This maturity is part of acceptance – we can hand on heart say we can run this reliably for paying users.
 Notes/Risks: Unknown unknowns in ops are always a risk; practicing failure modes helps flush them out[271]. Team burnout is a risk as on-call starts; we ensure rotation is sane and maybe plan to hire a dedicated SRE around now if many deployments (mention if needed).
