Today, that means quick wins like audit trails and better utilization; in 2–3 years, it means participating in a quantum network of resources and offering advanced features your competitors can’t; in 5+ years, it means leading an autonomous, standard quantum infrastructure that underpins widespread quantum computing – with you as a core provider of it. We plug in via open APIs and standard containers, we co-innovate (your hardware + our software = optimized solutions), and we share incentives (more usage on our platform means more usage of your hardware/cloud). By betting on our ecosystem, you’re effectively betting on accelerating quantum adoption – which translates to faster growth and leadership for your platform in this new era.
________________________________________
4. Investor-Facing Narrative (Execution-Backed Story)
Problem & Opportunity: Quantum computing is transitioning from laboratory curiosity to a strategic technology, but its progress is hampered by a lack of reliability, governance, and scalability. Early quantum efforts face painful uncertainties: Will my quantum job produce correct results consistently? How do I protect proprietary algorithms when using external quantum clouds? Can quantum hardware be run at scale like classical data centers, or will errors and downtime choke progress? These issues create a barrier for broader adoption – enterprises and governments are intrigued by quantum’s potential, but without solutions to trust and manage quantum operations, the Total Addressable Market (TAM) remains just potential. This is the pain: quantum computing today is where classical computing was in the 1960s – powerful but brittle, single-tenant, and lacking the “operating system” and governance to be mission-critical. The opportunity is huge: As quantum hardware improves, the ecosystem will demand an equivalent of an OS, cloud orchestration, and security layer – a multi-billion dollar infrastructure market riding on top of the quantum hardware wave[486][12]. Invarian is seizing this opportunity by providing the missing Quantum Autopilot layer that turns disparate quantum devices into a robust, enterprise-ready compute cloud. We solve not just a single problem but a cluster: performance variability, IP risk, and operational overhead – essentially, we make quantum computing fast, safe, and accountable. With the quantum market expected to reach ~$50B by 2030, and companies willing to invest in enabling tech, our platform targets a significant slice of that TAM: the software and services spend needed for quantum to actually deliver value in real-world settings.
Product & Architecture: Our product – think of it as the “Quantum Data & Autonomy Network” (AQuDAN) – is a unified stack that ensures quantum computing works reliably and securely at scale. Architecturally, we divide it into three planes (Discovery, Governance, Autonomy) that mirror the needs of a full quantum ecosystem:
 - Discovery Plane (Quantum DNA/Design Studio): This is our “brain” layer where we use AI and domain algorithms to generate and optimize quantum solutions[397][403]. It’s akin to a quantum design studio – feeding in problem statements and outputting improved circuits or calibration strategies. For investors, this means we aren’t limited to reactive infrastructure; we also drive innovation – our platform can automatically find better ways to run quantum tasks (we’ve already demonstrated ~5–10% circuit improvements in pilots via our generative algorithms). This not only differentiates our product (providing unique performance boosts), but also could open new IP avenues (some of these AI-discovered techniques are patentable, contributing to our moat).
 - Governance Plane (Quantum IP Ledger / Legal System): This is the “heart” that enforces trust – essentially providing the legal and compliance framework for quantum computing[4][30]. We implemented a tamper-proof ledger and watermarking scheme so that every quantum operation is recorded and attributable[9][487]. Think of it as the quantum computing equivalent of secure boot or blockchain for transactions. It ensures no unauthorized use of algorithms, compliance with regulations (like data can be restricted to certain geographies, usage can be audited) – features critical for Fortune 500 adoption. Architecturally, it’s built on robust distributed systems (active-active with failover, see our meeting of 99.5% uptime invariant[488][12]) and leverages modern crypto (post-quantum TLS, etc.) so it’s future-proof. This plane gives our platform a unique stickiness: once a customer starts recording their quantum IP in our ledger, switching out becomes costly (we become their source of truth and audit evidence). It’s akin to a quantum trust fabric that competitors will find hard to replicate, especially given our head start and patents (e.g. we patented our invisible watermarking technique for quantum circuits – a key IP protection method).
 - Autonomy Plane (Fault-Tolerant Autonomy Engine / Operating System): This is the “muscle” – our runtime that orchestrates and monitors quantum computations across hardware[346][405]. It’s effectively a quantum cloud operating system – scheduling jobs, managing errors, allocating resources, and providing an abstraction layer (via our Portable QIR-based IR) to run on any QPU backend[5][489]. In classical terms, imagine a combination of Kubernetes and VMware but for quantum: it ensures high utilization, load balancing, and automatic failover of quantum workloads. This is a complex technical moat – we’ve integrated everything from compiler optimizations (47ms compile times achieved vs 250ms budget[11]) to distributed failover logic (<60s recovery tested[490][491]). The result is we currently offer ~10× better performance on key metrics than baseline (for example, our GA compile was ~47ms vs typical ~500ms in raw frameworks[13], watermark overhead ~0.2% vs up to 5% in naive approaches – numbers that show we have a serious engineering lead). Going forward, as fault-tolerant hardware emerges, this plane is what will allow data centers to run quantum computers with minimal downtime and manpower – a critical capability for scaling quantum computing economically[33][34].
In essence, our architecture is not a single software, but a holistic platform addressing quantum’s trifecta of needs: innovation (via AI-driven discovery), trust (via governance ledger), and reliable operations (via autonomy engine). This integrated approach is unique – competitors might do a piece (e.g., some offer quantum SDKs, others security layers), but none we’ve seen unify all three with rigorous SLAs like we do. And we enforce quality by design: our “Design Key” invariants (10 numeric SLOs encoded in our code and CI) ensure every build meets performance/security targets[9][12]. This discipline – essentially treating operational metrics as features – is unheard of in startups, and it has resulted in a robust product (all 10 invariants met at GA, providing an unshakeable foundation[13][492]). For investors, this architecture and approach mean we’re not just promising reliability – we’re demonstrating it with every release (we can show CI records of invariants and ORR results[168][169]). That instills confidence that we can deliver enterprise-grade product, which in deep tech is often a major risk.
Go-To-Market & Revenue Model: Early on, our GTM is focused on design partnerships and cloud alliances – we secured a national lab and a major cloud provider in pilot deployments (the lab provided domain problems, the cloud provider integrated our governance for their quantum service)[493][179]. This serves as both product validation and beachhead customers. Our revenue in the initial 24 months is pilot-based (paid PoCs or NREs) – e.g., Lab X paid a small license fee for our v1.0 to secure their IP, and Cloud Y engaged in a paid pilot to offer quantum ledger service to their clients. These are modest, but crucially they convert to subscription licenses as we reach production readiness (we have in principle agreements that Lab X will move to an annual subscription ~\$Y and Cloud Y will include our platform in their enterprise tier offering, giving us per-use fees or revenue share). Our main model moving forward is SaaS / subscription: either we host a multi-tenant instance for mid-size customers (charging per qubit-hour or per job with a premium for governance), or we deploy dedicated instances for big clients (charging annual license + support). Additionally, design/compute marketplace aspects of our platform open transactional revenue: e.g., if we facilitate a cross-provider execution (job moves from one cloud to another), we could take a broker fee – effectively monetizing quantum resource brokerage once that market matures in Era 2[326][330].
In near-term sales, we target R&D-heavy enterprises and quantum cloud providers (those feeling the pain of managing increasingly complex quantum workflows). We already have LOIs from a pharma and a finance company’s quantum teams contingent on our pilot success. Their willingness to pay stems from our solving compliance and uptime issues that currently make their quantum experiments labor-intensive. For instance, Pharma Inc. expects that by using our platform, they can run 5× more experiments per quarter because our autonomy and error handling reduce failed runs – that directly accelerates their drug discovery timeline (a quantifiable ROI). Similarly, Cloud Y sees adopting our trust fabric as a way to upsell a “compliance guaranteed” quantum service tier to finance/government clients at perhaps 1.5× the price – revenue we’d share or capture through licensing. Over 2–3 years, as we become the standard, network effects kick in: more providers on our trust network attract more enterprise users (who want interoperability and auditability), and vice versa. We might later introduce usage-based pricing akin to cloud (charging by the number of quantum jobs managed or qubits under management), especially as customers go into production – giving us a scaling revenue aligned with their quantum usage growth (which by 2028+ could be substantial as hardware improves).
Our design partner approach also serves as our go-to-market strategy to scale: it builds champions in key verticals who prove out the tech, after which we convert those successes into case studies and target fast followers in those verticals. For example, Lab X’s success in national security domain opens doors to other government labs; Cloud Y’s endorsement helps us land another major cloud or regional provider. We anticipate doing joint marketing with those early partners (as noted, showing metrics like 99% reliability achieved[11][12], or X% cost saving via better scheduling) to drive inbound interest from organizations that realize they need these capabilities.
Execution & Moat: Investors often worry about execution risk in deep-tech – can this team deliver on a multi-year roadmap? We mitigate that by our track record and methodology: in the first 18 months, we met every major milestone in our execution plan (v1 GA released with all invariants green[13][492], first external pilot live, etc.), and we did so by following a rigorous SSOT (Single Source of Truth) plan with evidence-based gates. We can literally show you our “execution bible” with checklist completion from Phase 1[168][177] – an uncommon level of operational maturity for a startup. This not only lowers execution risk going forward (we will continue to use this plan to drive Phases 2 and 3), but also is a due-diligence goldmine (when a big customer or future investor asks “how do we know it works?”, we hand them ORR reports, metrics logs, security audit letters – few startups can do that). Essentially, our process is part of our moat – it’s how a small team out-executes far bigger players or new entrants, by keeping quality high and learning cycles tight.
In terms of defensibility: our moat comes from a combination of deep integration, data network effects, and domain know-how. By being the platform of record through which quantum programs run, we accumulate valuable data (performance profiles, error stats, usage patterns). With privacy preserved, that data allows us to train our discovery AI better and optimize scheduling in ways a competitor without such data couldn’t – a classic data network effect (akin to how Google’s data on searches made it impossible to catch up in search quality). On top of that, we have key intellectual property: patents around watermarking quantum circuits, novel error recovery techniques, etc., and more importantly, the integrated know-how of balancing all these facets – our head start means even if a cloud giant dedicates a team, they’ll have to replicate years of failure modes and edge cases we’ve already handled (we’ve built in fail-safes for things like a region outage – scenario testing that’s not obvious until you operate the system[77][78]). Also, by establishing our platform as a de facto standard via partnerships and alliances, we create ecosystem lock-in – not in a negative way, but in the sense of positive externalities. If by year 5, say, a dozen major organizations are using our ledger to share quantum IP, any newcomer trying to sell them something different faces a huge coordination barrier and trust gap. We’d have become embedded as industry “plumbing.”
Financially, this translates to a high-margin, SaaS-like business with network effects. Our initial deployment might be more high-touch (with integration services), but as the standard solidifies, deploying to a new customer gets easier (maybe even self-service by year 5 for smaller orgs). Gross margins on software are high; plus, if we facilitate cross-provider usage (taking a fee), those are pure transaction revenues on top. And importantly, because we focus on software and standards, we remain hardware-agnostic – we ride the rising tide of quantum hardware improvements without betting on one technology. This hedges the risk: whichever QPU tech wins (superconducting, trapped ion, etc.), they will all need the reliability and governance layer we provide.
In summary, to investors: we have a clear path to market leadership in the “quantum DevOps” segment, validated by early partners, protected by technical and network moats, and driven by a team that has executed flawlessly against a complex plan (turning slideware into a deployed system in <2 years, an eternity in quantum time). We’ve de-risked the core tech (met our invariants, passed pilots), leaving the main risk as scaling go-to-market – which we are mitigating via partnerships and an ecosystem strategy (making friends not enemies among key players). If quantum computing is to fulfill its multi-billion-dollar promise, it will absolutely require the kind of platform we’ve built – we aim to be the “Cisco + VMware” of the quantum era (the network and OS enabling a new industry), and our execution to date provides a solid foundation to achieve that lofty vision.

5. Follow-Up Prompts for Deeper Passes
To further refine or zoom into specific slices of this execution program, here are some follow-up prompts that can be used:
1.	“Drill-Down on Trust Fabric Test Harnesses” – Using tasks P3-T1 and P5-T1 as context, outline a detailed test plan for verifying trust-fabric DK invariants (ledger latency, audit coverage) in a multi-tenant scenario. (This prompt asks for specifics on testing governance-plane performance when multiple orgs are writing to the ledger simultaneously, ensuring invariants like p95 ≤120ms hold for each tenant.)
2.	“Helm & Infra Hardening Checklist for Production Deployments” – Based on Phase 1’s P1-T3 and Phase 5’s P5-T2, produce a checklist focusing exclusively on infrastructure readiness: covering Helm chart best practices, Kubernetes settings for HA, backup configurations, etc., needed before declaring v1.0 production-ready. (This would yield a table or list of config items and verifications – essentially an Ops readiness subset.)
3.	“Ops Gold-style v2.0 ORR for Discovery Plane” – Assume we are preparing an Ops Gold ORR specifically for the Discovery/QAGF features in v2.0. Using tasks P4-T1, P4-T2, and E2-I3, generate the checklist (Preconditions, Procedure, Verify) for an ORR section on QAGF (e.g., model response time tests, suggestion quality benchmarks, fail-safe checks). (This prompt narrows the focus to testing the new AI/Discovery components thoroughly before GA.)
4.	“Multi-Cloud Integration Playbook (Draft)” – Leveraging P4-T4 (multi-cloud PoC) and E2-I1 deliverables, outline a playbook for integrating a new QPU cloud provider into the A4Q network. Include prerequisites (what APIs/credentials needed), steps to adapt our orchestrator (similar to P2-T1, P2-T3 tasks), and acceptance tests for the new integration. (This helps formalize how we’d onboard additional hardware providers.)
5.	“Security & Vault Integration Deep Dive” – Referring to P2-T2 and P6-T1, provide a deep dive on our post-quantum security approach: detail how Vault/HSM integration works, how keys are rotated (with reference to those tasks), and outline future enhancements (if any) needed by Era 3 for FIPS/SOC2 compliance. (This prompt would yield a focused explanation and roadmap of our security design, useful for investor due diligence or partner security teams.)
6.	“Adaptive Scheduling Feature Specification” – Focus on tasks P2-T6 (carbon-aware scheduling) and P4-T3 (performance tuning) to generate a mini-spec for an adaptive scheduling feature in v2.x: it should describe how the scheduler will weigh factors (carbon, cost, latency) and what metrics control decisions (with citations from those tasks and Era 2 goals). (This prompt helps flesh out the next-gen scheduler design that came up in narrative and era plans.)
7.	“Quantum Standards Alignment Report” – Using info from P3-T3 and Era 3 Item I2, simulate a report or brief that maps our platform’s features to emerging standards (QIR Alliance, IEEE QIP, etc.). Include which tasks delivered standards compliance (e.g., watermark schema in P3-T3, OPA policy format from P2-T4) and what remains to align by Era 3. (This prompt would create an explicit mapping for investors/partners to see how we fit into standards – essentially summarizing our influence and compliance trajectory.)
8.	“Post-Mortem: Phase 1 Execution Lessons” – Using Section 1 tasks and the Orientation snapshot, produce a concise retrospective summary (for internal use) highlighting 2-3 execution strengths and 2-3 areas to improve in Phase 2. Reference specific task outcomes (e.g., P1-T4 chaos test success[494], P1-T5 security fix notes[85]) to substantiate points. (This prompt encourages reflective analysis to ensure continuous improvement, as hinted in P6-T4 retrospective.)
9.	“Customer Onboarding Guide Excerpt” – Drawing on P5-T3 (pilot #3 enterprise use-case) and P5-T4 (SRE processes), draft an excerpt for a hypothetical “A4Q User Guide” that explains to a new customer’s ops team how to onboard onto our platform: from provisioning (Helm install) to setting up orgs (tie to P5-T1 multi-tenant steps) to verifying invariants via our provided Grafana dashboards. (This prompt shifts perspective to customer documentation, integrating various execution elements into a consumable format.)
10.	“ROI Calculation for Potential Partner X” – Imagine a cloud partner scenario (like Pilot #2). Using data from our execution (metrics from ORR, improvements in watermark overhead etc.), formulate a simple ROI model for them: e.g., how A4Q could save Y hours of dev time (by automating X per P2 tasks) and reduce downtime by Z% (per P1/P3 chaos tests), translating to $ value. Reference relevant tasks (chaos test P1-T4[77], ORR results from P3-T1[170], etc.) to ground assumptions. (This prompt helps articulate business value using execution evidence, something investors or sales teams might ask for.)
These prompts can be copied into new sessions to generate focused elaborations, using the structured content above as context, ensuring continuity and depth on those specific aspects.

Invarian A4Q Ecosystem – 10-Year Execution Program
0. Orientation Snapshot (Very Short)
●	Three-Plane Product Pillars: Discovery, Governance, and Autonomy are the three core planes of the A4Q platform. Discovery (QAGF – Quantum Asset Generative Foundry) drives AI-based generation/optimization of quantum circuits and error-correcting codes. Governance (QIPL – Quantum IP Provenance Ledger) provides a secure trust fabric (policies, ledger, watermarking) to enforce IP rights and compliance across multi-cloud quantum workflows. Autonomy (FTAE – Fault-Tolerant Autonomy Engine) delivers real-time orchestration of quantum resources (job scheduling, failover, carbon-aware routing)[1][2]. These three planes align with the 10-year strategy’s pillars: Discovery/QAGF for quantum content innovation, Governance/QIPL for trust/compliance, and Autonomy/FTAE for operational runtime.
●	Unified Monorepo & Core Components: The entire platform resides in a single monorepo with modular directories corresponding to each plane[3]. Key modules include /services (Go microservices like trust-fabric and orchestrator), /platform (common libraries and compiler components, e.g. qir-adapters for QIR/LLVM integration and a Rust watermark-pass for circuit tagging), /ml (machine-learning tools such as autocal-rl for automated calibration and qec-decoder for error correction R&D), plus support packages like /infra (Helm charts, Docker Compose), /ops (observability scripts and deployment ops), and /sdk (TypeScript CLI and client API)[4][4]. This structure (established by the GA V8 release) cleanly maps features to teams and KPIs – for example, the compiler team owns the “compile latency” invariant through the platform code[5].
●	Trust-Fabric Service (Governance Plane): A Go-based microservice implementing the “Trust Contract” engine and the provenance ledger[6]. It exposes secure APIs like /v1/seal (seal a job with a policy and invisible watermark), /v1/append (append an entry to the immutable ledger), and /v1/verify (check watermark signatures). Under the hood it enforces region privacy and compliance policies via an embedded policy engine (OPA/Rego) and uses post-quantum cryptography for all signatures and encryption. The ledger index is backed by PostgreSQL, running in active-active HA across regions (demonstrated in GA with sub-second failover per zone and <60 s for regional failover)[7][8]. This ensures tamper-evident, globally consistent tracking of all quantum jobs and their IP metadata.
●	Orchestrator Service (Autonomy Plane): A Go-based scheduler and execution manager that handles the entire job lifecycle[8]. Clients submit quantum jobs via /v1/submit and query /v1/status for results; the orchestrator routes jobs to available quantum backends based on configurable policies (cost, queue wait time) and live telemetry like regional carbon intensity[9]. By design it’s highly available: in the GA baseline it ran active/active across multiple regions, achieving ~1 s failover for zone outages and <60 s for full region outages[10]. The orchestrator tracks SLO metrics such as uptime and recovery time, and it coordinates closely with the trust-fabric (e.g. respecting region constraints from trust contracts when dispatching jobs).
●	Portable Quantum Compiler & Watermarking Pipeline: A Rust-based compiler subsystem (under platform/) that ingests high-level circuits (OpenQASM 3, QIR) and lowers them to a hardware-agnostic Portable IR with embedded watermark metadata[11]. A custom watermark-pass introduces subtle, invisible modifications (e.g. slight angle biases in certain gates) to tag intellectual property without breaking the circuit[11][12]. The compiler automatically computes the added circuit depth overhead (Δdepth) and ensures it stays within a strict budget (≤0.5% extra depth) so performance impact is negligible[13]. These watermarks have a true-positive detection rate ≥99% in tests, enabling robust IP provenance tracking across different QPU backends. Adapters in platform/qir-adapters ensure compatibility with standard toolchains (aligning A4Q’s IR and watermark tags with evolving industry standards for quantum IR).
●	ML Toolkit (Discovery & Autonomy): Under /ml, A4Q includes forward-looking AI components that currently operate in off-line or advisory roles. For example, AutoCal-RL applies reinforcement learning to calibrate qubit control parameters, running alongside vendor calibration routines to suggest improvements, and a QEC Decoder module analyzes error syndromes from quantum error-correcting codes to propose decoding strategies[14][15]. In the GA (V8) baseline, these were pilot features used in simulated environments (“digital twin” labs) only, not in the critical production path[16]. However, they lay the groundwork for the Autonomy plane’s future: over time, these ML services will be integrated to enable automatic tuning and adaptive error mitigation in live quantum operations.
●	Design Key Invariants (DK) – Non-Negotiable SLOs: From day 1, A4Q established 10 immutable invariants that define minimum performance, accuracy, and reliability targets for the system[17]. Examples include: p95 compile latency ≤250 ms, p95 ledger append ≤120 ms, PQC encryption overhead ≤20 ms; watermark true-positive rate ≥99%; ≤0.5% added circuit depth from watermarking; overall system uptime ≥99.5% with failover recovery <60 s; audit log coverage ≥99% of events; carbon efficiency ≤55 gCO₂ per job; end-to-end job success rate ≥92%[17][18]. These DK metrics are built into the codebase (as constants gating CI tests) and every release must produce evidence that they are met or exceeded[19][20]. Notably, the GA baseline (v1.1/V8) met or surpassed all 10 invariants – e.g. it achieved ~47 ms compile p95 and 100% watermark detection in test suites[20]. This invariant-driven discipline is the backbone of the execution program: no feature is added without preserving these guarantees (and stretch “Ops Gold” targets tighten them by ~10% for safety[21]).
●	10-Year Roadmap & Eras: The high-level V10 Plan lays out five sequential eras (~2 years each) from 2025 to 2035[22]. Each era corresponds to major version milestones (v2.x through v6.0) and focuses on advancing each plane in stages. For example, Era 0–2 (late 2025–27) targets post-GA hardening and initial pilot deployments; Era 2–4 (2027–29) emphasizes scaling to multi-tenant, multi-partner ecosystems; Era 4–6 (2029–31) centers on fault-tolerance readiness and broader adoption; Era 6–8 (2031–33) focuses on optimization at scale; and Era 8–10 (2033–35) achieves full autonomy of operations and industry-wide standardization[22][23]. Each era has defined goals, “go/kill” checkpoints, and SLO improvements, ensuring progress is measured and adjustments are made before proceeding to the next phase.
1. 0–24 Month Execution Program (Phase-by-Phase Checklists)
We divide the first 24 months post-GA into six 4-month phases, each with explicit goals and concrete task blocks. Bolded tasks are mission-critical for achieving a credible “v1.0.0 production-ready” system (i.e. required for a trustworthy external pilot). All tasks follow a structured checklist format (Owner, Timebox, Steps, Acceptance criteria, etc.) to ensure evidence-driven completion. The emphasis is on eliminating any remaining “simulated” or stub components from the GA codebase, hardening core workflows (/v1/seal, /v1/submit, /v1/status, telemetry, etc.), integrating real-world dependencies (post-quantum cryptography keys, real QPU backends, live carbon data feeds), and establishing a repeatable deployment and monitoring pipeline. By the end of Phase 6, the platform should support a credible external pilot deployment with full observability on all DK invariants in a production-like environment[24].
Phase 1 (Months 0–4) – Post-GA Hardening & DevOps Foundation
Phase Goals (0–4 mo): Focus on shoring up the GA prototype for real-world conditions and establishing a solid DevOps foundation. Key objectives include:
·         Real-Mode Security: Wire in real post-quantum key management (KMS/HSM) and eliminate any dev/test-only crypto shortcuts or simulation flags[25]. The system should default to full security mode (no plaintext keys or “fake crypto”).
·         DK Metrics & Telemetry: Instrument every service to measure all DK invariants at runtime and export them via /metrics for monitoring[26]. No performance target should go unmeasured in ops – e.g. compile latency, ledger latency, watermark TPR, etc., all visible in Prometheus.
·         Deployment Pipeline: Achieve a one-click local deployment (Docker Compose) and a reproducible cluster deployment (Helm/K8s) with basic monitoring built-in[27]. This provides developers and partners an easy way to run the full stack and ensures our config works in real environments.
·         Stability Testing: Perform initial chaos engineering and load tests to validate HA and failover (meet uptime ≥99.5% and recovery <60 s targets)[27]. Shake out any assumptions by killing services, simulating network issues, and verifying the system self-recovers within design tolerances.
Task ID: P1-T1
 Name: Freeze DK Constants & Enforce “Real Mode” Defaults
 Plane: Cross-Cutting (Applies to all services)
 Package(s): services/common/constants.go, global env/config
 Type: PROD-REQUIRED
 Owner: PMO Lead (with SRE support)
 Timebox: 2 weeks (Month 0–1)
 Dependencies: GA codebase (V8) in place; CI pipeline accessible
Steps:
·         Define all Design Key (DK) invariant thresholds as constants in a common module (if not already). For example, set CompileP95Millis = 250, LedgerAppendP95 = 120, etc., reflecting the official SLO budget values[28]. Also include the stretch targets (e.g. a “gold” constant for compile p95 = 230 ms). This makes performance budgets explicit in code and versioned.
·         Audit configuration files and environment flags for any simulation or dev mode toggles (e.g. an A4Q_SIM flag). Change defaults so that “real mode” is always on by default. Remove or hard-code off any backdoor dev settings. Essentially require A4Q_SIM = 0 unless explicitly overridden for tests, and ensure no test-only crypto is used in default config[29].
·         Update service startup logs to print out the loaded DK constants at boot (a banner listing all invariant budgets in effect). This provides quick visibility in logs about the performance and reliability targets the system expects, reinforcing the invariant culture[30].
·         Implement a CI static check or linter that scans the codebase for forbidden dev patterns. For instance, flag any usage of a stubbed backend, or any occurrence of a "SIMULATION_MODE" token or insecure crypto usage. The CI should fail (fail closed) if such patterns are present outside of allowed test directories[31].
Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Code Constants: The common constants file (e.g. services/common/constants.go) contains definitions for all 10 DK invariants with correct values, and these are consumed by the respective modules (e.g. orchestrator uses the RecoveryTargetSeconds constant)[32]. Unit tests or a simple script should prove that these constants are being loaded at runtime (e.g. an endpoint that dumps current thresholds).
·         Simulation Disabled: Running the full stack with default settings results in no simulation paths. For example, if one starts the docker-compose setup without any special flags, the logs should show A4Q_SIM=0 and any attempt to use a sim-only endpoint or feature is either ignored or actively refused (fail closed)[33]. We store an artifact (like _artifacts/real_mode_startup.log) from the first successful run with real-mode enforced, and that log should show all services initializing in secure mode.
·         CI Gate: The CI pipeline includes a step (e.g. a script or lint rule) that fails the build if disallowed dev patterns exist. Evidence could be a deliberately introduced dummy “sim mode” call causing CI to catch it. We capture a CI log snippet as proof (e.g. _artifacts/ci_sim_mode_check.txt) showing the static check blocking a forbidden pattern.
·         Artifact: Save a JSON summary from a real-mode trial run, e.g. _artifacts/run_header.json, containing the version info and DK budgets loaded (with a SHA-256 checksum of this artifact committed to our repo as SSOT evidence)[34]. This demonstrates that the system not only runs in real mode but we have traceable proof of the exact invariant settings in effect.
Notes/Risks: This task is foundational; by forcing real-world defaults, we risk breaking developer workflows that relied on easier sim settings. We mitigate this by providing alternative dev configurations (e.g. a separate docker-compose override for using a local simulator QPU behind the scenes, rather than altering core logic). The outcome sets a zero-trust stance in our code: everything is production-real unless explicitly sandboxed. It’s critical to get team buy-in on this philosophy early.
Task ID: P1-T2
 Name: Instrumentation of DK Metrics & Observability Baseline
 Plane: Cross-Cutting (All services)
 Package(s): services/common/otel.go, ops/observability, infra/compose
 Type: PROD-REQUIRED
 Owner: SRE/Observability Lead
 Timebox: 4 weeks (Month 1–2)
 Dependencies: P1-T1 (DK constants) complete; basic services running locally
Steps:
·         Add Prometheus metric collectors in each service covering every DK invariant. For example, in trust-fabric service, record a histogram metric a4q_ledger_append_ms for ledger append latency (to track the 120 ms p95 goal); in orchestrator, record a4q_recovery_time_seconds for failover recovery times; in the compiler, track a4q_compile_ms for compilation latency, etc.[25][35]. Use a consistent naming scheme (a4q_*) and attach units and labels (like region, outcome) as appropriate.
·         Verify metrics exposure end-to-end: Launch the stack via Docker Compose (including Prometheus in the compose file). Hit the /metrics endpoint of each service (e.g. trust-fabric:8081/metrics) and confirm the new metrics appear with sensible values (zero or near-zero when idle). We should see time-series for each DK metric family, even if initially they’re just default.
·         Implement a simple load generator (script or using a4qctl) to produce sample metric data. For instance, have a script submit 100 dummy jobs to the orchestrator (possibly using a built-in simulator backend) to generate non-zero data for compile latency, job success rate, etc. Use this to ensure our metrics correctly accumulate (e.g. a4q_job_success_fraction goes to 1.0 if all jobs succeed).
·         Configure a local Prometheus + Grafana monitoring stack. In Docker Compose, add Prometheus scraping all services’ /metrics and import a baseline Grafana dashboard JSON that visualizes the DK invariants (p95 lines, error rates, etc.). This is mostly for our internal use initially, but it proves that anyone deploying A4Q gets a ready-made observability stack.
·         Set up basic alerting rules for any DK metric breach. For now, just log or console alerts if thresholds are exceeded for some duration. E.g. an alert if a4q_ledger_append_ms_p95 > 120 ms for 5 minutes, or if a4q_wm_tpr (watermark true-positive rate) drops below 0.99. This ensures we have early warning of invariant regressions.
Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Metrics in Endpoint: Screenshots or text dumps of the /metrics endpoint from each service showing the new metrics. For example, a4q_ledger_append_ms_bucket{le="100"} 0 ... a4q_ledger_append_ms_p95 0 should be visible on trust-fabric’s metrics, etc., confirming that all invariants are accounted for in telemetry[35].
·         Grafana Dashboard: An exported Grafana dashboard (JSON or screenshot) named e.g. “A4Q DK Metrics” that displays real-time values for all 10 invariants (latencies, rates, etc.). This dashboard should be included as an artifact (_artifacts/grafana_dashboard_v1.json).
·         Test Load Results: A log from the load generator (e.g. _artifacts/metrics_load_test.log) demonstrating that after submitting test jobs, key metrics reflect expected behavior (e.g. a4q_job_success_fraction ~1.0 if all dummy jobs succeeded, non-zero histogram counts for compile latency).
·         Alert Simulation: Proof that an alert would trigger on invariant breach. We might manually lower a threshold or inject a slow operation to simulate a breach. For instance, configure ledger threshold to 50 ms and observe an alert firing in logs or an alert manager. Capture this event in _artifacts/alert_test.txt.
·         The presence of these metrics and alerts in our Phase 1 stack means moving into Phase 2+ we have full visibility. By Phase 3, we expect to integrate this into a more permanent ops setup, but the baseline is demonstrated here.
Notes/Risks: This task significantly improves observability. The main risk is performance overhead of telemetry – we must ensure that adding metrics (especially histograms on hot paths) doesn’t itself cause slowdowns. We mitigate by using coarse buckets and sampling if needed. Another risk is information overload: too many metrics could distract. We stick to the DK essentials first. This groundwork lets us catch regressions early and builds confidence for external users who will eventually see our system reliably meeting its SLOs.
Task ID: P1-T3
 Name: Deployable Stack – Docker Compose & Helm Charts v1
 Plane: Cross-Cutting (Infra/Ops)
 Package(s): infra/compose, infra/helm, CI/CD pipeline
 Type: PROD-REQUIRED
 Owner: DevOps Engineer
 Timebox: 4 weeks (Month 2–3)
 Dependencies: P1-T2 (observability metrics) ideally done (so charts include monitoring); Postgres instance available
Steps:
·         Finalize a Docker Compose configuration for local development. Define services for trust-fabric, orchestrator, a Postgres DB instance, and optionally our monitoring stack (Prometheus & Grafana) as additional containers[36]. Test that a developer (or tester) can bring up the entire system with a single docker-compose up. After startup, run a simple end-to-end smoke test: use the CLI to submit a sample circuit through the orchestrator and verify it completes (even if using a dummy quantum backend)[37].
·         Develop initial Helm charts (or Kustomize manifests) to deploy A4Q onto a Kubernetes cluster[38]. The chart should include all core components: Deployments for trust-fabric and orchestrator (with replicas for HA), a StatefulSet for Postgres, ConfigMaps for configuration (including the DK constants and any feature flags), and Kubernetes Services for the APIs. Integrate monitoring: e.g. add Prometheus annotations on the pods to scrape metrics[38]. Ensure the chart can be customized (values.yaml) to toggle optional components (like enabling the ML services or not).
·         Include HA settings in the deployment manifest to mimic production. For instance, configure two replicas of each service (placed in different zones or with anti-affinity to simulate multi-zone HA), and for Postgres use a primary-secondary setup (if not active/active yet, at least have failover capability). The goal is a minimal but production-like topology by default[39].
·         Test the Helm chart on a local K8s (KinD or Minikube) and on a real cloud dev cluster if possible. Iterate until helm install a4q-chart brings up all pods healthy. Then run the same smoke test job on the cluster: use the CLI or API to submit a job and ensure it goes through on Kubernetes (networking, service endpoints, etc., all okay)[40].
·         Automate the build & deploy pipeline: e.g. extend CI to build Docker images for services on each commit and push them to a registry (could be a local registry for test). Then have CI run helm template or a dry-run to validate that the chart is syntactically correct and references the latest image tags[41]. This ensures that as code changes, our deployment config stays up-to-date and doesn’t break.
Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Docker-Compose Smoke Test: Provide a log or recording of docker-compose up bringing up the stack and successfully running a test job. As evidence, attach a snippet showing both trust-fabric and orchestrator services running (health checks passing) and a test circuit going from submission to result[42]. For example, an artifact _artifacts/compose_smoketest.log where the last lines confirm a job completed.
·         Helm Deployment Proof: A screenshot or log of kubectl get pods after deploying the Helm chart, showing all components (services and DB) in Running state[43]. Include an artifact _artifacts/helm_deploy_test.txt that captures the output of a test job submission on the cluster (for instance, the CLI output or orchestrator log confirming a job ran to completion on K8s).
·         CI Integration: An excerpt from the CI pipeline (or a status badge) demonstrating that on a new commit, Docker images are built and the Helm chart passes lint/validation[41]. We can store _artifacts/ci_deploy_pipeline.txt showing, for example, images built with tag v1.0.0-dev and Helm chart lint result “PASSED”.
·         Versioned Deployment Assets: The initial Helm chart is versioned (e.g. a4q-0.1.0.tgz packaged). This artifact is attached to the repo or internal registry, indicating we have a deliverable package. The acceptance could be listing the file with its SHA-256 checksum.
Notes/Risks: Getting a deployable stack early is crucial for both internal testing and eventual partner trials. A risk is maintaining parity between the compose (dev) and Helm (prod) environments – we must keep configurations in sync to avoid “it works on compose but not on K8s”. To manage that, we’ll use the same base env constants and ensure dev uses as few special-cases as possible (perhaps the dummy QPU backend in dev is the main divergence). Also, running stateful components (like Postgres) in CI or local can be tricky (volume handling, etc.), but we can use in-memory or ephemeral volumes for tests. This task unlocks Phase 3’s external pilot work by providing the means to deploy A4Q reproducibly on real infrastructure.
Task ID: P1-T4
 Name: Chaos & Failover Drill (HA Test)
 Plane: Autonomy (Resilience)
 Package(s): services/orchestrator, ops/observability
 Type: PROD-REQUIRED
 Owner: SRE Lead
 Timebox: 2 weeks (Month 3–4)
 Dependencies: P1-T3 (deployable stack) running on a cluster; baseline metrics (P1-T2) in place
Steps:
·         Define a chaos test plan targeting our high-availability invariants. For example: Test 1: “If one orchestrator instance dies, another instance takes over within <1 s (zone-level failover)”; Test 2: “If an entire region’s orchestrator and DB go down, the secondary region recovers all tasks within <60 s (regional failover)”[44][45]. Also plan smaller chaos events (e.g. network latency spikes) to observe system behavior.
·         Deploy the platform in an HA configuration on a cluster (could be the one from P1-T3). For simulation, we might treat two Kubernetes zones or namespaces as separate “regions.” Ensure at least two orchestrator pods (on different nodes/zones) and a replicated Postgres (primary/standby).
·         Start a steady load of jobs using a script – e.g. continuously submit dummy jobs (that maybe just wait/sleep) at a constant rate. Monitor key metrics: job throughput, success rate, and the custom metrics for uptime and recovery we created (like a4q_uptime gauge, etc.). Basically, create a stable baseline of operations.
·         Induce chaos: e.g., kill the primary orchestrator pod abruptly (kubectl delete pod) and observe if the standby orchestrator seamlessly picks up the queue. Then simulate a “region” outage: kill the Postgres primary or partition the network so that orchestrator can’t reach it, forcing failover to the secondary DB. Observe the orchestrator’s behavior – it should reconnect to the standby and continue processing jobs with minimal interruption[46][47].
·         Record the timing and outcomes of each failure scenario. For the orchestrator crash, measure how long before the secondary begins handling jobs (should be ~1 s ideally)[44]. For the DB failover, measure the interval from fault to recovery (target <60 s)[48]. Verify that no jobs are lost: each submitted job should either complete or be marked failed in the ledger, even through the chaos.
·         Adjust configurations if needed (e.g., if failover takes too long, perhaps reduce the heartbeat timeout or tune DB replication settings). Repeat chaos events until we consistently meet the uptime/recovery targets (≥99.5% uptime implies <=~3.65 h annual downtime, which our tests approximate in shorter windows)[49][50].
Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Failover Report: A document or table summarizing at least two chaos test scenarios and their results (e.g. _artifacts/ha_chaos_test_results.md). For example: Scenario A: “Orchestrator instance killed – failover time observed: 0.8 s (PASS, met <1 s goal)”; Scenario B: “Primary DB node failure – failover time: 45 s (PASS, met <60 s)”[51]. Each scenario entry should include evidence, such as timestamps from logs or Grafana screenshots marking the event.
·         Metrics during Chaos: Captured telemetry showing that during the fault, our metrics stayed within acceptable bounds. For instance, a graph of a4q_recovery_time_seconds spiking but not exceeding the limit, or a4q_uptime gauge staying above 99.5%. We can include an artifact like _artifacts/failover_metrics.json summarizing uptime before/during/after failures[52].
·         Ledger Audit Check: Verify that for all jobs submitted during the chaos tests, there is a corresponding ledger entry (either completion or explicit failure record). We can run a script after tests that queries the ledger for job IDs and ensure none are missing. Evidence could be a log _artifacts/ledger_audit_after_chaos.log confirming no gaps. This ensures our audit coverage remained ~100% even under failure[53].
·         The overall outcome should be that the system self-recovers within the invariant limits for all tested failure modes. If any test initially failed, we note how it was fixed. Achieving this in Phase 1 proves our HA design (active-active orchestrator, active-passive DB) works as intended outside of theory.
Notes/Risks: Simulating failures in a controlled environment is different from real-world outages (where things can be slower or less predictable). We built some buffer into the targets (60 s for region failover was met with ~45 s in test). Document any assumptions (e.g. “network partition healed within X seconds”) for future reference. Also, while we test orchestrator and DB failovers, other components (metrics, CLI) might not be HA – we acknowledge those and perhaps accept some non-critical downtime (e.g. Grafana might restart). The key risk is over-tuning to our test environment (e.g. Minikube) – we should repeat these tests in a staging cloud environment early in Phase 2 to validate under realistic conditions.
Task ID: P1-T5
 Name: Initial Security Audit & Secrets Management
 Plane: Governance (Security)
 Package(s): services/trust-fabric, infra/secrets (Vault integration)
 Type: PRE-PROD
 Owner: Security Engineer (Governance)
 Timebox: 3 weeks (Month 3–4)
 Dependencies: Basic system up (Phase 1 deploy); real-mode enforcement (P1-T1) done
Steps:
·         Conduct a thorough code-level security review of the core services (trust-fabric and orchestrator primarily). Look for any hard-coded secrets (e.g. default keys, passwords in code), any crypto that is not truly PQC (e.g. if we still use RSA for convenience anywhere, replace it with PQC algorithms), and ensure all APIs have proper authentication and authorization in place. Cross-check our implementation against the intended security architecture from design docs and GA compliance checklists – e.g. confirm mutual TLS is required on all gRPC/HTTP calls, that the PQC envelope is actually wrapping job data, etc.
·         Introduce an external KMS/HSM for managing secrets. In GA, we may have used a simple in-memory key provider for the PQC keys; now replace that with a real KMS integration (e.g. HashiCorp Vault or a cloud KMS service). Set up Vault (in dev mode for now) and store the master encryption keys used by trust-fabric inside it. Modify trust-fabric to fetch keys from Vault instead of config files. This ensures no plaintext keys in code or config[54][55].
·         Implement a basic key rotation procedure: e.g. generate a new PQC key pair (or symmetric key for ledger signing) in the HSM, update the trust-fabric to start using the new key for new jobs while still accepting old signatures for existing records. Perhaps script this rotation and test it. This may involve versioning keys (key ID increments) and ensuring both old and new keys are accessible during transition.
·         Harden the authentication scheme: ensure that the CLI/user interactions use strong identities. If not already, issue distinct client certificates or tokens per user, and verify trust-fabric is checking them. If any “dev shortcuts” were in place (like a universal admin token), remove them. Possibly integrate an OIDC identity provider stub for future extensibility.
·         Run a lightweight penetration test (internal) in our dev environment: e.g. attempt to call the trust-fabric APIs without auth, attempt SQL injection via the ledger API, try to break watermark by modifying circuits, etc. Use this to identify any obvious weaknesses early. Document any findings and create issues for anything not fixed immediately.
Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Security Review Log: A report (_artifacts/sec_review_phase1.md) summarizing the code audit findings and actions taken. For example: “Found hard-coded test private key in config.go – removed and replaced with Vault fetch (commit XYZ)”, “Verified all external endpoints check auth token/cert – PASS”, “Upgraded PQC library to latest liboqs version for NIST round3 algorithms,” etc. This report should show we addressed or ticketed each issue discovered.
·         Vault Integration Proof: System running with Vault in the loop. Provide evidence such as trust-fabric logs showing it successfully retrieving a key from Vault (e.g. “Vault: fetched key tfledgerkey-v1 for signing”). Additionally, demonstrate that the previous in-memory key is no longer accepted (e.g. if we try to use an old test key, it fails). We can include an artifact _artifacts/vault_integration_test.log.
·         Key Rotation Test: An artifact, e.g. _artifacts/key_rotation_test.txt, describing a scenario where we rotated a key. It should show that jobs sealed under old key still verify, and new seals use the new key. If using Vault’s versioned keys, show before/after key versions and trust-fabric handling both. Also note the measured PQC overhead before vs after – ensure the HSM call is still within 20 ms p95 budget (if not, document mitigation like caching)[56][54].
·         Pen-test Results: A short summary of our internal pentest attempts and results. E.g. “Attempted SQL injection on /append – input is sanitized (PASS)”, “Tried calling /v1/seal without valid cert – request rejected (PASS)”, “Tried replaying a watermark on a different payload – caught by verification (PASS)”. If any attempt was not caught, that is a bug to fix – but by acceptance, those should be fixed or at least flagged as known issues with owner.
Notes/Risks: This task is labeled PRE-PROD because while not all aspects need to be fully production-hardened now (e.g. we might not do a formal external audit yet), we want to surface major security gaps early. One risk is spending too long polishing security in Phase 1 – we should fix critical exposures now (no plaintext secrets, no unauthenticated access), but can schedule less urgent hardening for later phases. Another risk is that integrating Vault or KMS might introduce slight latencies or complexity; however, doing it now in dev ensures we uncover performance issues (like Vault API slowness) and can design around them (caching, etc.). By the end of Phase 1, we aim to have no “obvious” security holes – so that if we deployed to a partner in Phase 3, we wouldn’t embarrassingly leak keys or allow unauthorized use.
Phase 2 (Months 4–8) – External Integration & Policy Layer
Phase Goals (4–8 mo): Begin integrating with real external systems and fleshing out governance policies, moving beyond the self-contained prototype. Key objectives:
·         Real QPU Backend Integration: Replace any remaining simulated quantum backends with at least one real cloud QPU API (e.g. IBM Quantum, IonQ). We need to prove the orchestrator can submit jobs to a real quantum service and get results back[57]. This will flush out API mismatches and network issues, and demonstrate end-to-end operation on actual quantum hardware.
·         Enterprise Key & Ledger Hardening: Deploy the trust-fabric ledger in a multi-region configuration and trial an immutable blockchain-based ledger in parallel as a pilot[58]. The idea is to enhance our provenance store: run the SQL ledger in active-active mode across regions for reliability, and optionally feed a subset of transactions into a permissioned blockchain for extra audit immutability. By end of Phase 2, decide if the blockchain approach is viable for production or not[59][60].
·         Advanced Policy Enforcement: Extend the Trust Fabric’s policy engine to handle fine-grained compliance controls. For example, implement job-level data residency restrictions (only run in EU, etc.), export control rules (flag or prevent certain algorithm usage), and PII/privacy tagging for sensitive workloads[61]. Essentially, go from basic “trust contracts” to enterprise-grade policies integrated into /v1/seal.
·         Watermark & Carbon Analytics: Build tooling to extract and verify watermarks from output data (so partners can prove IP provenance on results) and integrate live carbon-intensity data into the scheduler for ESG-aware job routing[62]. This means the orchestrator will start to route or delay jobs based on real-time carbon emissions of data centers, and we’ll track per-job carbon in metrics.
By the end of Phase 2, the platform should be much closer to “enterprise-ready”: it will be secured and compliant, connected to real hardware, and capable of multi-region, multi-policy operation[63]. We expect to demonstrate the system in at least one realistic environment (even if small scale) under these conditions.
Task ID: P2-T1
 Name: Integrate First Quantum Hardware Backend (IBM Quantum)
 Plane: Autonomy (Execution Backend)
 Package(s): services/orchestrator, platform/qir-adapters
 Type: PROD-REQUIRED
 Owner: Autonomy Lead (Orchestrator Engineer)
 Timebox: 6 weeks (Month 4–5)
 Dependencies: Phase 1 tasks done (especially P1-T3 deploy pipeline and P1-T4 stability); IBM Quantum API credentials available (or analogous provider access)
Steps:
·         Abstract the orchestrator’s backend interface to support plug-in modules for different quantum providers. In the orchestrator code, define an interface (or Go interface) for a quantum backend with methods like SubmitJob(qirPayload) -> jobID and PollJobStatus(jobID) -> status/result. Refactor the current submission logic (which may have been calling a local simulator or stub) to call through this interface[64]. Initially implement a “LocalSimulator” plugin that wraps whatever stub we had, to ensure nothing breaks.
·         Implement a plugin for IBM Quantum: using IBM’s public SDK or REST API. Likely, this involves either invoking their Python client (which might require a sidecar or gRPC call from Go) or directly calling their REST endpoints for job submission and result fetching. Develop a small Go module (or use Cgo/Python bridging if necessary) that handles: auth with IBM cloud (using an API token), formatting our circuit (QIR or OpenQASM) into IBM’s input format, submitting the job, and periodically polling for completion[65]. Ensure to handle IBM specifics, e.g. queueing behavior and asynchronous job IDs.
·         Update the compiler QIR adapter if needed to ensure compatibility with the IBM backend. IBM might expect OpenQASM 2 or a particular gate set; if so, either use our QIR->OpenQASM translator or implement conversion logic so that our Portable IR can map onto IBM’s requirements[66]. Test this on a simple circuit (like a Bell state) offline to verify format correctness.
·         Add configuration to orchestrator (e.g. via Helm values or an env var) to select the backend plugin (e.g. backend.type = IBM vs LOCAL). Also ensure the IBM credentials (API key, hub/project identifiers) can be provided securely (through a Kubernetes Secret or Vault).
·         Conduct an end-to-end test with IBM: Deploy the updated orchestrator (likely in our dev cluster or even locally if possible) and point it to IBM’s test quantum backend (could be one of their small simulators or a real device with a short queue). Use the A4Q CLI to submit a simple job (e.g. a 2-qubit circuit). Verify that the orchestrator sends it to IBM (we should see a job ID from IBM), and eventually receives a result which flows back through our trust-fabric and CLI status[67][68]. This might take some waiting if using a real device, but even one successful round-trip is enough for now.
·         Implement basic error handling for this external integration: e.g. if the IBM API is unreachable or returns an error, ensure our orchestrator marks the job as failed and logs appropriately (maybe with a retry policy for transient network issues). Also handle asynchronous completion: likely IBM jobs are not instant, so our /v1/status should show “Running (on IBM backend)” and update when done[69].
·         Document any limitations or observations: e.g. “IBM job submission adds ~2 seconds overhead” or “IBM QIR had to be limited to X gates due to their compiler”. This will inform whether we keep this integration always on or feature-flagged until improved.
Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Backend Abstraction in Code: Show that the orchestrator code now contains an interface and at least two implementations (Local and IBM). For evidence, we can reference a code snippet or design doc highlighting backend.go interface definition and mentioning the IBM plugin module (no need to show code here, just note that it exists and passes unit tests)[64][65].
·         Demo Run Output: Provide CLI output or orchestrator logs from a successful end-to-end test: e.g. an artifact _artifacts/ibm_backend_demo.log that includes lines like “Job submitted to IBM Q, IBM JobID=abc123” and later “Job abc123 completed, result received”[70]. The artifact should also show that our system processed the result (perhaps a log in trust-fabric showing it appended the result to ledger, etc.). If IBM’s API returned the actual quantum result (bitstring probabilities, etc.), confirm we handled it (even if just stored or discarded).
·         Invariant Check: Confirm that introducing the IBM calls didn’t break our invariants drastically. For instance, note if compile latency or append latency changes. If one IBM job takes a long wall-clock time, that’s expected (quantum execution is slow), but it should not crash anything. Our orchestrator should treat it as a long-running job without skewing metrics like “success rate” (perhaps we exclude pending jobs from success calc). The acceptance here is qualitative: no invariant alert should fire solely due to using IBM. If needed, we adjust metrics (like mark external wait separately).
·         Go/Kill Decision Criteria: Document (in the task notes or separate pilot log) any issues encountered and whether we consider the IBM integration “stable enough” to enable by default. For example, if the latency is too high or their API flaked often, we might decide to keep it behind a feature flag for now. Conversely, if it worked fine for a few runs, we may decide to ship with IBM backend support on. The evidence could be a short note: “Tested 3 jobs on IBM 5-qubit backend: all succeeded (latency ~30s each). Integration will remain optional until more testing, but no blockers.”
Notes/Risks: Working with an external cloud API introduces flakiness and latency we don’t control. We sandbox this integration under a flag (like --backend=ibm) so that by default, our system can still use a local simulator for fast tests[71]. The risk is that IBM’s service might be slow or change APIs – we should engage with their developer program if possible to stay updated. Another risk is security: we must ensure we don’t log sensitive info (like API keys or maybe the circuit content if it’s IP-sensitive – though we watermark it anyway). Achieving this milestone is huge: it’s our first real quantum hardware run, proving the concept in reality. Even if it’s just one provider, it paves the way to integrate others (Phase 5 plans add more). We will use the learnings (API handling, job tracking, etc.) to streamline adding future backends.
Task ID: P2-T2
 Name: Enterprise Key Management (Vault Integration Full)
 Plane: Governance (Security/Compliance)
 Package(s): services/trust-fabric, services/common/pqc
 Type: PROD-REQUIRED
 Owner: Governance Lead (Security Engineer)
 Timebox: 4 weeks (Month 4–5)
 Dependencies: P1-T5 (basic KMS usage) completed; Vault server accessible (dev or staging mode)
Steps:
·         Evolve the trust-fabric’s PQC key handling from the partial integration done in Phase 1 to a fully managed solution via Vault (or equivalent HSM). Specifically, ensure that all cryptographic operations that involve long-term keys happen inside Vault/HSM. For example, when the trust-fabric service needs to seal a job payload with a post-quantum public key, it should request Vault to perform the encryption or to provide the key – such that private keys never reside in our service memory[56]. We likely will use Vault’s Transit Engine API for this (which can perform cryptographic actions with stored keys).
·         Similarly, manage the ledger signing key (if we sign ledger entries or watermarks) via Vault. If not already, generate a keypair in Vault for signing watermarks or ledger records. Refactor the code so that whenever a watermark signature is needed, it calls Vault to sign the data. This ensures even if our service is compromised, the attacker cannot forge past records without the HSM.
·         Implement a routine or admin command for key rotation using Vault’s versioning: e.g. promote the active encryption key to a new version after X time. Test that trust-fabric seamlessly handles such rotation: new submissions use the new key version, but it can still decrypt old payloads with previous key versions. Vault can handle versioned keys so we must ensure our logic passes the correct version info.
·         Performance test the Vault-based operations to ensure they meet our 20 ms PQC overhead invariant. If direct calls to Vault for each job are too slow or cause high latency, consider strategies like caching a session key: for instance, use Vault to securely generate a symmetric session key, use it for batch of operations, then rotate. Ensure we always remain within the 20 ms p95 overhead for encryption operations[72]. Add extra telemetry if needed (like measure how long Vault calls take and log if > threshold).
·         Update our Helm chart (from P1-T3) to include configuration for Vault: e.g. Vault address, tokens/roles, etc., so that in a real deployment it’s clear how to plug in a Vault instance. Possibly set up a Vault sidecar agent with Kubernetes auth for production use (this might be future, but make a note). Also, create a runbook for key management (how to add a new key, how to rotate, how to revoke, etc.) since enterprise clients will need this process well-documented.
Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Latency Test Results: A report or CSV (_artifacts/pqc_vault_latency.csv) showing distribution of Vault call latencies for sealing operations[72]. It should confirm that p95 overhead ≤20 ms is achieved (e.g. median ~5 ms, p95 maybe 18 ms). If latencies are slightly above target in dev (say 25 ms), document what mitigation or config tuning is planned (e.g. enabling Vault caching or increasing client throughput)[54]. The key point: we have measured it and we know if we’re within budget or need to adjust.
·         Functional Evidence in Logs: Provide sanitized trust-fabric logs demonstrating Vault in action. For example, a log line “Vault KMS: Encrypted job payload with key=tenantKey_v2 (took 12 ms)” or “Vault: signature generated for ledger entry 0xabc...”. This shows that at runtime, Vault is actually performing crypto operations. An artifact _artifacts/vault_ops.log can be included with debug-mode logs around key usage[73][74].
·         Rotation Simulation: Document a test where we rotated a key and ensured continuity. For instance, generate a new key version in Vault for the watermark signing key, then submit a job. Confirm new jobs are signed with the new key (maybe by verifying signature keys in ledger entries), while older entries still verify with the old key. Evidence could be a snippet from trust-fabric showing it recognizing multiple key versions, or an artifact _artifacts/key_rotation_demo.md describing the scenario. This proves we can handle key rollover without downtime.
·         Helm/Config Update: Show that our deployment configuration now accommodates external KMS. For example, include an excerpt from values.yaml like:


     vault:
   enabled: true
   address: https://vault.example.com
   authTokenRef: my-vault-token-secret
            and that our chart configures the trust-fabric with this info. We don’t need to run Vault in CI, but at least the templates should not break when vault is enabled. Perhaps provide the rendered configmap snippet _artifacts/helm_vault_render.txt to confirm.
·         The acceptance is that by end of Phase 2, we can confidently say: “No long-lived secrets are stored in plaintext by A4Q services; all critical keys are in an HSM (Vault) and we can rotate them.” This will be a strong point for enterprise trust.
Notes/Risks: Vault integration complexity is high – network calls, token management – and could become a single point of failure if not managed (if Vault is down, jobs might fail). We must set sensible fallbacks: e.g. if Vault is temporarily unreachable, do we queue jobs or fail them? Likely fail closed for security. We’ll communicate to partners that an HA Vault setup is required in production. Another risk is developer friction (needing Vault running to test). We might maintain a dev mode that uses an ephemeral in-memory key to not block every local run (with loud warnings). But for staging/prod, Vault is mandatory. This task greatly reduces the risk of key exposure and moves us closer to a real-world security posture (auditors will require this).
Task ID: P2-T3
 Name: Distributed Ledger Deployment (A/B Regions and Blockchain Trial)
 Plane: Governance (Ledger)
 Package(s): services/trust-fabric/ledger, infra/chains
 Type: PRE-PROD (pilot experiment)
 Owner: DevOps or Backend Engineer (Governance)
 Timebox: 6 weeks (Month 5–6)
 Dependencies: Phase 1 HA test (P1-T4) done; multi-region Postgres ready; consider an open-source blockchain (Hyperledger or similar) for trial
Steps:
·         Deploy the SQL ledger in active/active multi-region mode. Use two Postgres instances (Region A and Region B). Configure logical replication or a distributed SQL layer so both can accept writes and remain in sync, or at least set up a primary in A and standby in B that can quickly take over[75]. Tune the settings for low commit latency (we still need append p95 ≤120 ms). Test by writing ledger entries in Region A and reading immediately in Region B and vice-versa, verifying they propagate correctly. Also simulate a region outage: bring Region A’s DB down and ensure Region B continues processing ledger writes without data loss.
·         Introduce a blockchain-based shadow ledger as a parallel audit log. Stand up a minimal blockchain network (e.g. Hyperledger Fabric or Quorum with 2–3 nodes, could even be on the same machines for now)[76]. In the trust-fabric service, implement an optional hook that on each ledger append, also submits a transaction to the blockchain network (with essential data like job ID, timestamp, perhaps a hash of the job or result for tamper-evidence). This can be a simplistic smart contract or just a key-value store where key=LedgerEntryID, value=hash. The goal is to have an immutable log of job submissions at the consortium level.
·         Keep the blockchain writes asynchronous so as not to impact the main flow. For example, put the blockchain transaction in a background goroutine or a queue that the trust-fabric processes after responding to the client. This way, even if the chain is slow, the user-facing latency isn’t directly hit (aside from maybe very slight enqueue cost)[77]. Monitor how much delay the chain introduces and ensure our 120 ms ledger p95 is still met for the SQL path[78][79].
·         Measure the performance and overhead of maintaining the blockchain log. Specifically, measure average and p95 transaction times on the chain, throughput, and if any backlog occurs under load. If writing to the chain significantly slows down operations (e.g. >250 ms per entry as a ballpark), note that as a problem[80].
·         Evaluate the benefits of this blockchain approach in Phase 2: Does it provide substantially more trust (e.g. an external auditor or partner node can verify all entries independently)? Is the overhead acceptable? By Phase 2’s end, make a decision: Go (plan to integrate blockchain ledger fully in next era) if overhead is negligible and at least one partner values the feature; or No-Go (drop or postpone the blockchain idea) if it’s impractical or no one needs it[59][81]. Document clear criteria for this decision (e.g. “if chain overhead <5% and a design partner plans to run a node, then continue; otherwise, stick to optimized SQL + periodic hashing”).
Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Multi-Region Ledger Demonstration: A short report (_artifacts/ledger_HA_test.md) describing a test where we performed ledger operations in a multi-region setup[82]. For example: “Wrote 100 entries in Region A, all 100 visible in Region B within 2 seconds; failover test: Region A down at entry 50, Region B continued from 51–100 with no data loss.” This report should include the measured failover time and confirm no entries were lost or duplicated.
·         Performance Metrics: Prometheus metrics or logs showing that ledger_append p95 remains at or below 120 ms with multi-region replication on[83][84]. If we have a metric a4q_ledger_append_ms from P1-T2, show a before/after comparison enabling replication. It should ideally be the same or only slightly higher. If it regressed, note by how much (and perhaps that’s acceptable within error margin or plan to optimize using tuning or better hardware).
·         Blockchain Pilot Evidence: Provide proof that the trust-fabric successfully wrote some entries to the blockchain. For example, an artifact _artifacts/blockchain_ledger_output.txt containing a snippet from a blockchain explorer or query that shows stored records (like a transaction list with our job IDs)[85]. Additionally, trust-fabric logs could show “Blockchain TX submitted for Job XYZ, txHash=...”.
·         Chain Overhead Notes: Document how much overhead the blockchain logging added. E.g. “Blockchain commit avg 200 ms, done async, so user latency unaffected but backlog of 5–10 tx under load.” If we encountered any issues (like chain transactions failing or taking seconds), note them. The acceptance is not that chain is production-ready, but that we measured it and have a conclusion.
·         Go/Kill Decision Recorded: A decision point documented (perhaps in the same report or a separate design note) stating whether we’ll pursue blockchain integration. For example: “Criteria: chain overhead >5% and no partner demand => pivot away. Result: Overhead ~10%, and none of current pilots require on-chain audit, so we will not integrate blockchain in Era 1–2 beyond this experiment.[81]” Or the opposite if it went well. This sets clear direction heading into Phase 3.
Notes/Risks: Operating a blockchain network is non-trivial (consensus issues, node management). We kept it small and local for the trial; the risk is this doesn’t fully represent a true consortium scenario’s complexity. But it gives a flavor. Also, by making it async, we risk inconsistency if a blockchain write fails while SQL succeeded (we should log and possibly reconcile later – acceptable in a pilot context). We should ensure that turning off the blockchain integration is easy (feature flag) so that if we decide “no-go”, we can cleanly disable it and not carry unnecessary overhead into Phase 3. The upside of this experiment is learning – even if we decide against blockchain now, we can confidently justify that choice to investors/partners with data, rather than speculation.
Task ID: P2-T4
 Name: Trust Contracts – Policy Engine Extensions
 Plane: Governance (Policy/Compliance)
 Package(s): services/trust-fabric/policy (OPA modules), services/trust-fabric/routes.go
 Type: PROD-REQUIRED
 Owner: Governance Lead (Policy/Compliance Engineer)
 Timebox: 5 weeks (Month 6–7)
 Dependencies: Base OPA engine from GA exists; possibly need input from legal/compliance advisors on policy specifics
Steps:
·         Extend the Trust Contract DSL (our job submission policy language) to support new clauses required by enterprise governance. Specifically, implement:
·         Data Residency controls: e.g. a contract can specify “region: EU” meaning the job must only execute on backends in the EU.
·         Export Control flags: e.g. mark certain algorithms or data as export-restricted so if a user from a non-approved region tries to run them, the system blocks it.
·         Privacy/Retention tags: e.g. a contract can declare a job contains personal data, which should trigger special handling like not storing full data or deleting telemetry after X days[86][87].
·         Implement these policies either using our OPA (Open Policy Agent) policy module or in Go (or a mix). Likely, for flexibility, encode them in Rego policies. For example, write Rego rules that enforce: if contract.region is set, then check orchestrator’s chosen region matches it (or else deny on /seal). Another rule for export control: maintain a list of disallowed countries or user groups for certain job labels, etc. The privacy retention might involve marking records for deletion later (which could be done via metadata in ledger or a background job).
·         Write unit tests for each new policy. Use OPA’s testing framework or Go tests to simulate various scenarios: e.g. submit a job with contract {"region": "EU"} while the only available backend is US – the expectation is the /seal API call is rejected with an error like “Policy violation: region constraint”. Another test: tag a job as {"exportControlled": true} and simulate a user not allowed under that – ensure it’s blocked or flagged appropriately[88][89].
·         Integrate policy decisions into audit logs. Every time a job is denied due to policy, log an audit event: e.g. “Job 123 denied by policy: reason = data residency (EU required)”. If a job is allowed but has special handling (like retention), log that as well (“Job 456 marked for deletion after 30 days per privacy tag”). This ensures traceability of policy enforcement[90].
·         Update the CLI/SDK to allow users to specify these new contract fields easily. For instance, add flags like --region EU or --export-control-tag Finance on a4qctl submit, which the CLI then includes in the contract JSON. Also update any documentation or examples to illustrate how to use these features.
·         By end of implementation, run an integration test: set up trust-fabric with a policy that, say, forbids cross-border execution, and then attempt such a submission. Observe it being blocked. Conversely, do a valid submission and see it pass. This doubles as a mini dry-run for how we’ll demonstrate compliance features to partners.
Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Example Contract & Outcome: Provide one or two concrete examples demonstrating the new policies. For instance, an artifact _artifacts/policy_tests.md that shows: Input: contract JSON {"region": "EU"}, backend chosen in US – Result: trust-fabric response “Rejected: region policy violation” (with appropriate HTTP 4xx). Another example: contract {"retainDays": 30} – Result: job accepted, and logs show “retention = 30 days set”[91][92]. These examples prove the policies actually trigger.
·         OPA Policy Module Artifact: If using OPA, include the new Rego module or its diff as evidence. For example, _artifacts/trust_policy_v2.rego with rules like deny[msg] { input.contract.region != input.backend.region; msg = "region mismatch" }. And tests for them (could even include OPA test results if available). This demonstrates the logic implemented.
·         Test Suite Results: Output from our automated tests (could be in CI logs or a summary file) confirming that all new policy test cases pass. E.g. “TestPolicyResidency: PASS”, “TestPolicyExportControl: PASS”, etc. If we have them, a snippet _artifacts/policy_unit_tests.txt can be included.
·         No DK Invariant Regression: Ensure that adding these checks doesn’t noticeably degrade performance. Sealing a job with extra checks should still be fast (likely OPA evaluation is ms-level). If concerned, measure /v1/seal latency before vs after under load; it should remain within tens of milliseconds. We can note “Seal API with policy check took 5 ms avg, well within budgets”[93].
·         The acceptance is qualitative beyond tests: by Month 8, we can tell a stakeholder “You can specify complex trust contracts (region locking, etc.) and our system will enforce them – we have evidence via tests and logs.” This sets us up to claim compliance-friendly features in any pilot demos.
Notes/Risks: Policy rules can get arbitrarily complex; we must avoid scope creep. We implemented key ones we know are needed (region, export, retention). If new requirements come (e.g. “only run on carbon <50g jobs”), we might add later. Another risk is mis-enforcement – we must be careful that policies don’t block legitimate jobs incorrectly (false positives). Thorough testing mitigates this. We should also consider the user experience: if a job is denied, the error message should be clear (“Denied by policy: reason X”) so they can correct it. Since this touches compliance, we may later need a review by legal, but for now we use common sense and known standards (like GDPR data locality, etc.). This task strengthens our Governance plane and will be a highlight for enterprise discussions (e.g. “you can enforce your org’s quantum policies on our platform”).
Task ID: P2-T5
 Name: Watermark Extraction & Verification Toolkit
 Plane: Discovery → Governance (IP Provenance)
 Package(s): platform/watermark-pass, services/trust-fabric, tools/wm-inspector
 Type: PRE-PROD (tooling, not critical path)
 Owner: Compiler/Platform Engineer
 Timebox: 4 weeks (Month 7–8)
 Dependencies: Basic watermark embedder in compiler exists (from GA); need sample watermarked circuits
Steps:
·         Develop a watermark inspection tool – likely a small CLI utility (under a new tools/wm-inspector or integrated into a4qctl).
