This tool takes as input a quantum circuit (or possibly the results of a computation) and attempts to detect the A4Q watermark signature within it[94]. Start with the known method from GA (e.g. slight rotation angles on specific gates): implement a detection algorithm that compares gate parameters to expected “watermark patterns”. If needed, enhance it (e.g. use statistical analysis to distinguish watermark bias from noise)[94][95].
·         Integrate this capability into the trust-fabric service via a new API endpoint, e.g. /v1/verifyWatermark. This API might accept a circuit or job result and return a boolean or confidence score indicating if the watermark is present[96]. Under the hood, it can call the same library that the CLI uses. Ensure this endpoint requires appropriate auth (only rightful owners or auditors can call it for a given job).
·         Test the detection accuracy against known data. Take 100 circuits that we know have watermarks (maybe from unit tests or by running the compiler on sample circuits) and 100 clean circuits. Run the detection on all. Our target: True Positive Rate ≥99% on watermarked circuits, and near 0% false positives on clean circuits[97]. If results are subpar, tweak the watermark scheme or detection. For example, if detection is only 95%, consider increasing the watermark signal (slightly larger angle bias) until we hit ~99%. The watermark overhead budget is 0.5% depth – ensure any adjustments stay within that (maybe increase bias but apply to fewer gates if needed)[97][98].
·         Measure the Δdepth overhead on circuits after watermarking to confirm it’s within invariant (0.5%). This was already tracked, but now explicitly verify with a variety of circuits and produce stats. If any circuit type tends to exceed budget, adjust the pass (e.g. skip watermarking on very depth-sensitive subcircuits).
·         Package the results and detection logic neatly. The CLI could output a JSON like {"watermarkDetected": true, "confidence": 0.997}. The trust-fabric could store a verification proof (maybe in the ledger, attach a field saying “watermark verified=true” for a job result that was checked).
·         Run an end-to-end simulation: compile a circuit with watermark, execute it (even on a simulator), then use the new toolkit to extract the watermark and confirm it matches what was embedded. This validates the full watermark lifecycle: embed -> execute -> extract matches. Log this process as a final demo.
Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Detection Efficacy Report: A report or table (_artifacts/watermark_detection_results.md) summarizing the true-positive/false-positive results of our test batch[97][99]. It should state something like: “Out of 100 watermarked circuits, 99 were correctly identified (99% TPR); out of 100 unmarked circuits, 0 false identifications (0% FPR).” If any misses, note if adjustments were made. This demonstrates we hit the ≥99% watermark TPR invariant in practice.
·         Overhead Confirmation: A small data table showing a few example circuits with and without watermark and their depths/gate counts. All should show ≤0.5% overhead. For example: “Circuit A: depth 200, watermarked depth 201 (0.5% increase) – OK. Circuit B: depth 50, watermarked 50 (no change other than angle tweaks) – OK.” This can be integrated into the report. This reconfirms we respect the Δdepth budget[100][100].
·         Tool Output Example: Provide an example usage of the verification tool. E.g.:


     a4qctl verify-watermark --job 1234 --circuit output.qir 
            Output: Watermark Present: Yes (confidence 99.5%)
 and correspondingly, a trust-fabric API call example: POST /v1/verifyWatermark { jobId: 1234 } -> { verified: true }. We can document this in _artifacts/wm_tool_demo.txt.
·         End-to-End Evidence: A log or narrative of a full test: “Compiled circuit with watermark (ID 0xabc), executed on simulator, got results; ran verify tool on result – watermark confirmed with 99% confidence, matching the ledger record for job 0xabc.” Possibly include logs from trust-fabric showing “verified watermark for job 0xabc – matches contract owner X”[101][102].
·         The acceptance is that by having this toolkit, we can prove to any third-party (courts, partners) that a given result or circuit originated from our system (or conversely, show if something doesn’t have our watermark, indicating it might be unlicensed). This significantly strengthens our IP protection story.
Notes/Risks: One risk is if our watermark technique is too naive and could be stripped by a clever adversary. The current passive watermark (slight gate biases) is robust to random noise but not if someone knows our scheme. We note that a more sophisticated watermark (e.g. multi-qubit correlations or using obscure error patterns) might be needed in future eras. For now, we ensure our existing scheme is rock-solid detectable and document its limitations. We might plan a Phase 4 or Era 2 task to upgrade the watermark if needed. The toolkit created here is also useful for internal QC: it ensures our watermark pass actually does embed what we think it does. By Phase 2 end, we have both ends of the IP pipeline (embedding and verifying) implemented, even if the verifying is an offline tool initially. This is an important deliverable for partners concerned about IP theft – we can hand them a tool to check any circuit for our watermark.
Task ID: P2-T6
 Name: Carbon-Aware Scheduling (Phase 1 – Data Feed & Tagging)
 Plane: Autonomy (ESG/FinOps)
 Package(s): services/orchestrator, services/common/carbon.go
 Type: PRE-PROD (feature-flagged)
 Owner: Autonomy Engineer (Scheduler)
 Timebox: 4 weeks (Month 7–8)
 Dependencies: Basic orchestrator running; access to a carbon intensity API (e.g. ElectricityMap or WattTime)
Steps:
·         Implement a module to fetch real-time carbon intensity data for given regions. Use a public API like ElectricityMap: for a given location, get current grams of CO₂ per kWh. Build a simple client in services/common/carbon.go to query this periodically (e.g. update every 5 minutes for each region we care about)[103]. Include caching so we don’t overload the API (maybe store the last value and timestamp). If API keys or accounts are needed, integrate those via config.
·         Enhance the orchestrator’s scheduling logic to utilize this data when a feature flag is on. For example, if two backends (say one in region with clean energy, one in region with dirty energy) are available and roughly equal in queue/wait, prefer the one with lower carbon intensity[104][105]. Additionally, if a job is not urgent (perhaps the user didn’t set high priority), and the current region’s carbon is above a threshold, we might delay the job slightly to wait for greener conditions[106]. Implement these rules in a careful way: maybe add a new scheduling policy component that modifies the scoring of backends or decides to postpone dispatch by X seconds if conditions are poor.
·         Add a new field in job status or metrics to record carbon usage. For instance, when a job completes, calculate an approximate gCO₂ emitted for it. This can be done by multiplying execution time by an estimated power of the QPU and using the regional carbon intensity. It doesn’t need to be super precise now; even a coarse number is valuable. Store this in the ledger or at least emit as a metric a4q_job_gco2e[107][108].
·         Unit test the scheduling logic with dummy data: simulate two regions with known carbon values and verify that the orchestrator chooses the lower-carbon one when the flag is enabled. Also test that when the flag is off, it behaves exactly as before (to ensure no regression).
·         Keep this feature disabled by default in production config since it’s experimental. But have a way to enable it (e.g. carbonAware: true in config). Possibly run it silently to collect data: even if not actively rerouting, we can have it calculate what it would do and log that, to analyze later.
·         As a final test, run two scenarios on our dev setup: one with carbon feature off, one on. Configure different carbon intensities for two dummy regions (if we can’t use real data easily, we can stub the API to return e.g. 100 vs 300 gCO₂). Submit a batch of jobs in both scenarios. Observe that in the “on” case, more jobs went to the greener region (or some were delayed)[109]. Measure the impact on average wait time and the average gCO₂/job.
Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Feature Demonstration: An artifact _artifacts/carbon_sched_comparison.txt describing a test where the carbon-aware scheduling was toggled. For example: “Test scenario: Region X carbon 150, Region Y carbon 400. With feature OFF: jobs distributed ~50/50. With feature ON: 80% of jobs went to Region X (low carbon), 20% to Y (when X queue filled), reducing average carbon per job from 100 g to 85 g (15% improvement)[109][110].” This shows the feature’s intended effect.
·         New Metric evidence: Show that metric a4q_job_gco2e is being populated[111]. For example, after running some jobs, an excerpt of /metrics might contain a4q_job_gco2e_sum and _count, etc. Or provide a snippet from the ledger or job status that includes an estimated carbon value. The acceptance criteria is simply that we are capturing this data point.
·         No SLO Regression: Confirm that enabling carbon-aware mode doesn’t break other invariants. E.g., if a job was delayed for carbon reasons, ensure it wasn’t delayed so long as to count as a failure or time-out incorrectly. We should assert that the job success rate metric doesn’t treat “delayed but eventually ran” as failure. Possibly present a note: “All jobs eventually ran, orchestrator throughput was not significantly impacted (just minor scheduling delays by design).” If we had a target like not delaying beyond a certain time, confirm we adhered to it (maybe all delays <5 min or such).
·         Decision Criteria: This feature is experimental; we plan to observe if carbon optimization yields substantial benefits (targeting ≥10% emission reduction without major performance hit)[111][112]. We can note: “After Phase 2, we will evaluate data from any pilot that opts in. If benefit is negligible or causes queue issues, we might drop or rethink this in Era 2. If benefit is clear (like that 15% improvement in test) and doesn’t anger users, we’ll plan to make it default in Era 2.” This sets a stage for later.
Notes/Risks: The actual benefit of carbon-aware scheduling depends on workload flexibility and regional differences. We must be careful not to introduce unpredictable delays for users; doing this “transparently” could conflict with SLAs. For now, it’s opt-in and our design partners will likely be interested in the data. We also need to be wary of accuracy – our carbon estimates are rough, but as long as they’re consistently calculated, they’re fine for relative improvement. Real hardware power usage might not be known; we might assume a constant or use published estimates. It’s a start to differentiate our platform as eco-conscious. Over time, if this works, it could even be a selling point (“our scheduler cuts carbon footprint by XX%”). If it doesn’t, we’ll have data to justify deprioritizing it. This task introduces an innovative angle but in a controlled, low-risk manner (feature-flagged).
Phase 3 (Months 8–12) – Pilot Deployment & v1.0 “GA” Readiness
Phase Goals (8–12 mo): Achieve a level of maturity sufficient to support an external pilot deployment with a design partner by the end of Year 1. The focus is on end-to-end validation, documentation, and incorporating feedback in preparation for a “GA” label on the product. Key goals:
·         End-to-End Validation: Perform a full Operational Readiness Review (ORR) on the integrated platform – essentially a dress rehearsal of everything working together[113][114]. This involves running extensive test suites covering all invariants (performance under load, failovers, security tests, data integrity checks) and gathering evidence (logs, metrics, reports) to prove the system meets its SLOs.
·         Documentation & Support Prep: Develop comprehensive admin and user documentation and establish basic support procedures[115]. This includes deployment guides, user manuals for the CLI/SDK, runbooks for ops (incident response, backup/restore procedures), and an internal knowledge base so that a partner’s ops team can run or trial the system with guidance. Essentially, productize the experience beyond just the code.
·         Pilot Deployment with Real Workload: Deploy A4Q in a controlled environment at a partner site (or a cloud tenancy) and run a meaningful proof-of-concept workflow[116]. Rather than a toy example, use a quantum workload relevant to the partner (e.g. a small quantum chemistry calculation or an error correction routine) to shake out integration issues. This will validate our ability to install and interoperate in someone else’s environment and handle real use-cases end-to-end.
·         Standards Engagement & Feedback Loop: Start engaging external bodies and users with what we’ve built. For example, present our watermarking and provenance approach to relevant industry groups (QIR Alliance, etc.) to get early buy-in and feedback[117]. Also incorporate initial feedback from pilot users about features, UI, etc., and fix any critical gaps. By doing so, we ensure by 12 months that we’re not building in a vacuum and that our design aligns with emerging standards and user expectations.
By the end of Phase 3, we target having an internal version v1.0.0 (or v1.1 per earlier versioning) that we consider a production release candidate[118]. All mission-critical tasks for a credible production system must be done by now (any straggler features can be flagged as beta). In short, Phase 3 culminates in achieving an initial GA that’s been tested in the real world and is accompanied by the evidence and artifacts to prove it.
Task ID: P3-T1
 Name: Full-Stack ORR (Operational Readiness Review) Simulation
 Plane: Cross-Cutting (Quality Gates)
 Package(s): All (integration testing; scripts in ops/orr_tests)
 Type: PROD-REQUIRED
 Owner: QA Lead / PMO
 Timebox: 4 weeks (Month 8–9)
 Dependencies: All Phase 1–2 prod-required tasks complete; stable deployment environment available
Steps:
·         Define a comprehensive ORR checklist mirroring those used in prior execution plans and GA trials[113][114]. Each item on the checklist corresponds to a DK invariant or major capability that must be validated. For example: “Performance: Compile latency p95 ≤250 ms under nominal load – PASS/FAIL”, “Reliability: Orchestrator failover <60 s during chaos test – PASS/FAIL”, “Security: All critical vulnerabilities resolved – PASS/FAIL”, etc. Include approximately 10–15 such items covering performance, security, failover, data integrity, compliance (e.g. audit trail coverage), and anything else needed for go-live.
·         Develop or refine automated test harnesses for each checklist item. Many we have from earlier tasks: e.g. a script to submit N jobs and measure compile latency distribution (from P1-T2/P1-T4), a chaos test script (from P1-T4) to measure failover times, a security scan (from P1-T5) for vulnerabilities, etc. Where needed, create new ones – for instance, a script to simulate 100 concurrent job submissions to test throughput and success rate. Also include a ledger integrity check: e.g. simulate a recover-and-replay scenario to ensure ledger can restore all jobs. These tests can be orchestrated via a master script in ops/orr_tests that runs them sequentially or in parallel as appropriate[119].
·         Execute the full battery of ORR tests on a fresh deployment. Ideally spin up a clean environment (maybe a dedicated staging cluster) using our Helm charts, to mimic a production setup. Run all tests, which might take multiple hours (e.g. soak tests, etc.). Ensure to capture all outputs: metrics data, logs, and any pass/fail outcomes. This execution should be done in a controlled manner (maybe overnight to gather reliability data)[120].
·         Collect and aggregate the results into a structured ORR report. For each checklist item, mark PASS or FAIL and attach evidence: e.g. for latency, include the measured p95 from Prometheus (say 180 ms – PASS); for failover, show timestamps from logs (region outage at T0, resumed at T0+45 s – PASS); for audit coverage, show that 100% of jobs had ledger entries – PASS[121][122]. If any item is borderline or failing, either address it immediately (tune code or config) and re-run that test, or document a mitigation/acceptance rationale (e.g. “ledger latency was 130 ms, above 120 ms target, but we plan to optimize indexing by v1.1”).
·         As part of the ORR, also test stretch goals (Ops Gold) if applicable. For instance, see if compile p95 is actually much lower (maybe 50 ms) – record that as extra buffer achieved[123][123]. Also test operational processes: e.g. do a rolling upgrade simulation (deploy new version on cluster, ensure no downtime beyond X sec), and include that in report if relevant.
·         Review the ORR results internally (with core team) as if it’s a Go/No-Go meeting. Identify any absolute no-go findings – those must be fixed before labeling GA. For example, if job success rate was 85% (target 92%), that’s a blocker – find cause and fix (maybe a bug causing sporadic failures). If everything is PASS or minor issues, we are ready. Document this internal sign-off.
Acceptance / Evidence:
·         ORR Checklist Document: The primary artifact is a formal ORR report, e.g. A4Q_v1_ORR_Checklist.md stored in our repo or share[121][122]. This document should list each check (with short description), its status (PASS/FAIL), and reference evidence (like “see Grafana snapshot in Appendix A” or link to log file). It should cover all invariants: e.g. compile latency, append latency, PQC overhead, watermark TPR, uptime, recovery, audit%, carbon/job, success%. For each, show the measured value vs target.
·         Artifacts with Checksums: All raw evidence from the ORR should be saved with cryptographic hashes to maintain SSOT. For example, _artifacts/orr_compile_latency.json (Prom data) with SHA-256, _artifacts/orr_chaos_log.txt with SHA-256, etc. The ORR doc can include these hashes[124][125]. This proves we didn’t tamper with results and can reproduce them if needed.
·         Overall Result: We expect by Phase 3 that all DK invariants are passing under realistic conditions[126][127]. The ORR should reflect that (or explicitly call out if any are not and what we do about it). For example, “All 10/10 DK invariants met. Notably, compile p95 ~180 ms (within 250 ms), ledger p95 ~110 ms (<=120 ms), PQC overhead ~18 ms (<=20 ms), watermark TPR 100% in test set (>=99%), uptime 100% during 8-hour test (>=99.5%), failover 18 s (<=60 s), audit 100%, carbon ~50 g (<=55), success 99% (>=92%) – PASS.” This summary is essentially our go/no-go.
·         If any invariants were only barely met or slightly missed, the report should note our plan (e.g. “Ledger p95 was 130 ms > 120; we will optimize in Phase 4, but given other buffers we judge this acceptable for pilot with caution.”). Ideally, though, we aim to have them all green.
·         The acceptance of this task is effectively an internal sign-off that “Yes, we have a release candidate that meets our stated SLO promises.” It’s arguably the most critical milestone proving we are GA-ready.
Notes/Risks: This ORR is intensive and might reveal late-breaking issues. That’s its purpose. If we find something big (e.g. a memory leak causing orchestrator crash at 6 hours, or a security hole), we may need to extend Phase 3 to fix it. It’s better to catch now than in a customer pilot. We should schedule the ORR with time for fixes and re-run if needed. Also, engaging the whole team in reviewing ORR results is useful to build confidence. By treating it like a formal review (maybe even invite an advisor or observer), we ensure rigor. Once we have a passing ORR report filed, it becomes a key artifact to share (with investors, or under NDA with potential customers to show we’ve tested things thoroughly). Essentially, completion of P3-T1 means we have evidence-backed confidence in our v1.0.0.
Task ID: P3-T2
 Name: External Pilot Deployment (Design Partner #1)
 Plane: Cross-Cutting (Deployment/Support)
 Package(s): infra/helm, documentation, support scripts
 Type: PROD-REQUIRED
 Owner: Program Manager (with DevOps)
 Timebox: 6 weeks (Month 9–10)
 Dependencies: P3-T1 (ORR pass – we have a stable release); plus earlier integration tasks (e.g. QPU backend, Vault) should be stable; documentation drafted
Steps:
·         Identify and coordinate with the pilot partner (assumed to be pre-arranged in planning – e.g. a research lab or a cloud provider’s quantum team). Gather their infrastructure details and requirements: Will we deploy A4Q in their cloud account? On their on-prem servers? What access or credentials do we need? Determine networking setup (firewalls, VPN, etc.) and security reviews needed (they might do their own scan of our software).
·         Prepare a tailored deployment bundle for that partner. Likely this means using our Helm chart but providing partner-specific values. For example, if they have their own Vault or KMS, configure our chart to use it; if they want to integrate their monitoring, decide whether to use our Prom/Grafana or feed into theirs. Remove any dev settings – ensure A4Q_SIM=0 and all real integrations (PQC, etc.) are on. Write a concise deployment guide for them (even if we will do most of it, document steps).
·         Execute the deployment in the partner’s environment. This could involve on-site or remote assistance. Helm install our chart (or if they prefer manual k8s, apply YAMLs). Troubleshoot any issues on the spot (network connectivity, missing permissions, etc.). Verify all components come up (/healthz checks on orchestrator and trust-fabric). Essentially get to a point where the platform is running on their infrastructure with minimal issues.
·         Once up, run a joint shakeout test: e.g. use the CLI on their side (or our side connecting to their endpoint) to submit a few test circuits. Confirm that jobs go through end-to-end: contract sealed, job executed (maybe on a simulator if they don’t have hardware, or on whichever backend is available), result retrieved, metrics flowing. This ensures the deployment actually works for the intended purpose. Possibly re-run a subset of ORR tests in this environment (not all, but critical ones like failover if multi-node, and performance checks). But since it’s a pilot, we might not stress it fully at first.
·         Set up monitoring and support channels for the duration of the pilot. For example, establish that our team has either read-access to their Grafana or we run a lightweight Prom on the side scraping their endpoints to watch DK metrics. Also set up a Slack/Teams channel or support email for quick communication if something goes wrong. Possibly provide the partner with a list of known issues and workarounds, and instructions on how to collect logs to send to us.
·         Provide a short training session or demo to the partner’s team. Show them how to use the CLI or API to submit jobs, how to attach trust contracts, how to interpret the outputs (e.g. how to verify a watermark on their end), and how to check system status (perhaps a basic Grafana view). This will empower them to actually use the system meaningfully. Gather any immediate feedback or confusion points to address in documentation.
Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Deployment Confirmation: An email or signed document from the partner confirming that “Invarian A4Q v1.0 deployed successfully on [Partner infrastructure]” with basic sanity tests passed[128]. If possible, include a screenshot of their environment’s dashboard showing our services running. This is a qualitative acceptance that the software is running outside our lab.
·         Metrics/Health Evidence: Provide a snippet from the partner deployment’s Prometheus or our remote monitoring, showing that DK metrics are being collected and are within expected range in the real environment. For example, _artifacts/partner_metrics_snapshot.json might show compile and ledger latencies similar to what we saw internally (indicating the deployment config didn’t regress performance). If direct metrics aren’t accessible due to NDAs, at least a statement “monitored metrics show all invariants healthy over X days of pilot” could suffice.
·         Workload Execution Proof: A log or output from a real quantum workload run on the partner deployment. For instance, if the partner is a lab, maybe we ran a small QAOA circuit on their ion trap QPU via our orchestrator. Provide an artifact like _artifacts/pilot_job_output.txt summarizing: job ID, contract used, which backend it ran on, and result (could be just a successful completion status with some output data). This demonstrates the platform’s core value delivered in a real scenario.
·         Post-Pilot Report (Initial): After a short period (say 2–4 weeks of pilot), gather a brief report of how it went. E.g., “Pilot Phase 1: 50 jobs executed, 0 crashes, one minor issue with Vault token renewal (fixed), user feedback: wanted better logging format, etc.” This isn’t final acceptance of pilot (which might run longer), but it gives evidence that the system handled real usage. Perhaps attach _artifacts/pilot1_summary.md with these details.
·         The main acceptance is that by end of Month 10, we have a real user running our system and seeing value. It’s hard evidence of viability. Additionally, any critical issues discovered are fed back as tasks (if, say, the partner’s use case uncovered a bug, we log it and plan a fix in Phase 4).
Notes/Risks: Deploying on someone else’s environment can be unpredictable (differences in k8s versions, restrictive security, etc.). We allocate generous time (6 weeks) including planning and troubleshooting. A risk is if the partner’s priorities change or they delay – we have to adapt (maybe pick a backup pilot or do more internal testing). Another risk is exposing our team to “support mode” too early – we must balance supporting this pilot and continuing development. We should treat it as a learning exercise not just for tech but also for our support approach. Success here is a huge milestone for investor confidence and for refining the product – we will get invaluable feedback. We should be prepared to handle that feedback objectively (some features might need re-prioritization if pilot shows new needs). Essentially, completion of P3-T2 means we’ve left the lab and stepped into a real-world scenario, marking the true transition to a product.
Task ID: P3-T3
 Name: Standards Contribution & IP Alignment
 Plane: Governance (Standards)
 Package(s): platform/watermark-pass (spec docs), docs/standards
 Type: PRE-PROD (External Collaboration)
 Owner: CTO or Chief Scientist
 Timebox: 3 weeks (Month 10–11, overlapping with pilot support as needed)
 Dependencies: A stable description of our watermark/provenance approach; possibly membership in relevant working group (QIR Alliance or similar)
Steps:
·         Compile a technical whitepaper or specification draft detailing A4Q’s watermarking and provenance scheme[129][130]. This should include: how we embed watermarks (method, parameters), how they’re detected, how the trust fabric ledger ties to these marks (ensuring provenance). Essentially, document our approach in a form that others (academics, standard bodies) can understand and evaluate. Use this to propose it as an open standard or at least to solicit comments.
·         Engage with relevant standards bodies or industry consortia. For example, if there’s a QIR Technical Committee or an IEEE Quantum Tech group focusing on IP or metadata, get on their agenda. Submit our whitepaper as a proposal or present it at a meeting[131]. Emphasize interoperability: for instance, suggest that a section of the QIR spec could incorporate an “IP provenance tag” in IR, and that our watermark scheme is one implementation. Also discuss how our ledger approach might integrate with wider quantum workflow standards. The goal is to not be an island – show thought leadership and drive the conversation, which also lends credibility to our platform.
·         Concurrently, review our own IP strategy (patents, trade secrets) to ensure contributing to standards won’t jeopardize protection[132]. We likely have filed patents on watermarking; when engaging standards, often you commit to FRAND (fair licensing) or similar. Coordinate with legal to do this properly – we might offer it under certain terms. If needed, prepare a Letter of Assurance about any essential patents. Essentially balance open collaboration with maintaining our moat.
·         Share other best practices: e.g., our approach to multi-tenant trust fabric or carbon metrics might interest some consortia (like Green Software Foundation, etc.). Without giving away any secret sauce, contribute in discussions or forums about these topics. The aim is to shape emerging standards in a direction favorable to our methods (or at least ensure we’re compatible).
·         Gather feedback from these external engagements and bring it back to the roadmap. For instance, if the QIR group says “if only you tagged gates in JSON instead of binary, more tools could use it,” consider adjusting our implementation in a future update[133]. Or if other vendors express interest in using our watermark format, perhaps plan how to allow that (could lead to an open-source component or a license model by Era 2).
·         By the end of Phase 3, ideally we have at least one or two meaningful interactions: e.g. our watermark concept discussed in a standards meeting, maybe an invite to co-author a proposal. Document any such progress for internal records and investor updates (showing we are influencing the ecosystem).
Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Submitted Proposal: Evidence that we submitted our ideas to a standards body. For example, an artifact A4Q_Watermarking_StandardDraft.pdf that we sent to the QIR Alliance, along with a confirmation email or meeting minutes acknowledging it[134][135]. This shows we took concrete action to standardize our approach.
·         Standards Body Feedback: If possible, some record of response – e.g. “QIR Working Group #5, Jan 2026: Invarian presented watermarking draft; group expressed interest, with questions on overhead. Agreed to form a task force to explore metadata tags.” It might be informal, but even an email from a committee member like “Your proposal looks promising, we’ll discuss next quarter” is useful evidence[136][137].
·         Internal IP Review Outcome: A brief note from our legal/IP counsel indicating we’ve aligned our patent strategy with our standards contribution. For instance, “Patent for watermarking filed Oct 2025; safe to share high-level details now; any licensing if adopted will be on our terms.” Alternatively, if we choose not to patent something and go the open route for goodwill, note that decision. The acceptance is ensuring we didn’t accidentally give away key IP without plan.
·         Repository Update: Possibly a new docs/standards/ directory in our repo containing the proposals or tracking standardization efforts[138]. This shows we’re making this part of our project artifacts. E.g. docs/standards/provenance_proposal.md with the text we sent out, version-controlled.
·         Strategic Alignment: As a result of this task, we should update our roadmap if needed. For example, if the standardization process indicates a new feature (like a new watermark format) might become needed, mark that for Era 2 planning. Evidence of this could be a line in our Phase 4 planning docs like “Incorporate standard X if ratified – scheduled for Era 2.”
·         Overall, acceptance is a bit soft – it’s about engagement. But success looks like: we’re seen as thought leaders (at least starting that journey) and we aren’t developing features that conflict with where the industry is heading. In investor terms, this shows we’re proactive in building moat through ecosystem buy-in, not just proprietary tech.
Notes/Risks: Participating in standards can be time-consuming and can distract, which is why we budgeted only 3 weeks and likely the CTO does it (to avoid pulling too many dev resources). The risk is spending effort and it going nowhere (standards bodies can be slow). But early engagement is usually worthwhile; even if formal adoption is slow, we gain connections and insight. Another risk is revealing too much – we mitigate by focusing on concepts, not every implementation detail (enough to be useful, not enough to let someone copycat easily). In summary, by doing this in Phase 3 we send a signal: we’re confident in our approach and want it to be the foundation others build on (which if achieved, locks in our relevance long-term).
Task ID: P3-T4
 Name: Version 1.0.0 Release Packaging
 Plane: Cross-Cutting (Release Management)
 Package(s): infra/helm, docs/CHANGELOG.md, CI/CD
 Type: PROD-REQUIRED
 Owner: Release Manager / DevOps
 Timebox: 2 weeks (Month 12)
 Dependencies: All critical fixes from ORR and pilot feedback applied; ORR passed (P3-T1); documentation updated (P3-T2, P3-T3)
Steps:
·         Create a release branch and freeze code for v1.0.0 (or v1.1.0 depending on version scheme). Bump version numbers in all modules to 1.0.0. Ensure all dependency versions are locked (no SNAPSHOT builds). This is the official GA candidate. Tag the repository at this commit as v1.0.0-rc1 initially[139].
·         Generate final build artifacts: build Docker images for each service with tag v1.0.0 and push to whatever registry we use (even if internal). Package the Helm chart as a4q-1.0.0.tgz. Compile CLI binaries (if distributing CLI outside the repo) for common platforms (Linux, Mac, Win). Basically produce everything a third-party would need to deploy or use the system.
·         Write Release Notes / CHANGELOG.md for v1.0.0[139][140]. This should highlight key features (perhaps organized by plane: Discovery features, Governance features, etc.), any improvements over the previous “internal GA” (V8), known limitations, and references to the evidence (for credibility). Also include usage notes like required configurations. This serves both as internal record and something we can share under NDA with select partners or investors.
·         Perform a clean-slate installation test using the release artifacts. E.g., in a fresh VM or cluster, follow our own README/Helm instructions exactly to ensure someone outside the core team could get it running. Fix any last-minute packaging issues (like missing config in default chart). This test is essentially simulating an open-source user or new hire setting it up from scratch.
·         After verification, finalize the release: push the tag v1.0.0 (or update rc -> final), record checksums of artifacts (images, binaries) in a manifest file (e.g. A4Q_v1.0.0_SHA256SUMS.txt)[141]. If distributing to a partner or internal repository, do that.
·         Optionally, run one more sanity check on the final artifacts in our staging environment (like deploy the v1.0.0 Helm chart one more time) just to double-confirm everything works as packaged[142].
·         Communicate the release internally: an email or meeting to say “v1.0.0 is out, here’s what’s in it.” Also prepare to communicate to external stakeholders: perhaps draft an announcement for design partners or a summary for investors, highlighting that we hit our Year 1 milestone.
Acceptance / Evidence:
·         Git Tag and Checksums: Confirmation that the repo is tagged at v1.0.0. We can show the tag commit ID and a short diff that version numbers updated[141]. Also present the checksum file listing hashes of, say, the Docker images or tarballs[141][143]. This ensures integrity – anyone can verify they got the exact build we tested.
·         Release Notes: The CHANGELOG.md entry for v1.0.0 (or a separate ReleaseNotes doc) is complete and attached. We expect items like “Integrated PQC KMS (replacing dev keys)[144], Added IBM Quantum backend support (beta)[145], Achieved compile p95 ~50 ms (against 250 ms target)[146], etc.” and “Known issues: e.g. blockchain ledger experimental, off by default.” This artifact shows transparency in what exactly we delivered.
·         Distribution Artifacts: A screenshot or listing of our artifact repository showing a4q-orchestrator:1.0.0 image, a4q-trustfabric:1.0.0 image, a4q-1.0.0.tgz Helm chart, etc. Or if delivered to partner, an email of files delivered. Essentially, evidence that the build artifacts exist outside just our dev environment.
·         Final Test Evidence: A brief log _artifacts/v1.0.0_fresh_install.log or similar, capturing the output of a deployment from scratch using the 1.0.0 release. It should show all services coming up cleanly. If any last fixes were made during this test, they should be captured in the final commit. But ideally, it just confirms smooth install.
·         Completion Checklist: A small internal checklist verifying all PROD-REQUIRED tasks from Phases 1–3 are done or consciously deferred. This could be an updated execution plan doc row marking each P1/P2/P3 task as done. All mission-critical ones should be marked done (we bolded them). If any are not, we have justification. But acceptance means essentially all critical checkboxes are checked.
·         At this point, we consider the platform “GA-ready.” The evidence is we have a formal release that could in principle be given to an external user with high confidence. Phase 4 will likely begin enhancements and scaling, but the baseline is now solidified.
Notes/Risks: Even after release, we must be ready to issue a 1.0.1 patch if an issue arises in pilot. But the packaging exercise ensures we treat the software as a product, not just a project. A risk is we rush this and miss something – but given ORR and pilot happened, it should be straightforward. Another risk is confusion around versioning (we had an internal V8 as GA, now calling this v1.0.0). We might clarify that internally we consider this v1.2 (since v1.1 was GA on sim)? It’s minor but worth documenting to avoid confusion. In any case, by end of Phase 3 we have achieved the primary goal: a credible, test-proven release that we can present to stakeholders as “Version 1.0 is delivered.”
2. 2-Year Eras and Product-Launch Iterations (Years 2–10)
Following the initial 0–2 year sprint, the roadmap shifts to era-level planning for the subsequent eight years, in roughly 2-year increments (aligned with the five eras outlined in the V10 plan[22]). Each era corresponds to major product “release trains” with strategic goals, tightening SLOs, and external milestones. Below, we define each era’s focus and break down a few era-level checklist items – high-level deliverables or gates that must be achieved before moving to the next era.
Era 1 (Years 0–2, 2025–2027) – Post-GA Pilot Validation & Foundation
Strategic Goals (Era 0–2): Establish a production-grade foundation and prove the platform’s value in real environments. This era was about going from a GA prototype to a credible deployed system. Key goals included: (1) Hardening the Core – ensure all DK invariants are met in production settings (latency, failover, security) and eliminate any “simulated” components[113][114]; (2) First Pilot Success – deploy with at least one design partner and demonstrate end-to-end functionality on a real quantum workflow; (3) IP and Trust Provenance – validate that watermarking and ledger systems hold up (e.g. show watermark detection ≥99% in practice, audit logs 100% complete in pilot runs); (4) Team & Process Setup – build out documentation, CI/CD, and operational practices (ORR, support channels) to transition from development to a product organization.
Era 1 Checklist Items:
●	E1-I1: “V1.0 Production-Ready” Baseline Achieved
 Planes: Cross-Cutting (All)
 Major Deliverables: A fully hardened v1.0 system meeting all original design invariants under real conditions. This includes completing all Phase 1–3 tasks (P1–P3) and resolving any critical issues from pilot testing. Deliverables: GA release 1.0.0 packaged[139][140], along with an ORR report demonstrating performance and reliability goals met (e.g. compile p95 ~180 ms, ledger p95 ~110 ms, etc. in pilot)[146][126]. Evidence artifacts (metrics logs, audit trails) are archived with checksums as SSOT proof.
 Prerequisites: All essential implementation tasks from 0–24 mo (list of PROD-REQUIRED P1–P3 tasks) completed and verified. Pilot deployment running successfully for at least N weeks without showstopper issues.
 DK/SLO Targets: Confirm all 10 DK invariants at or better than spec (p95 latencies, TPR, etc.) in a production-like environment[146]. Stretch targets optionally achieved (some latencies ~10% better than spec, zero-downtime deploys, etc.).
 Exit Criteria: Go for broadening adoption. Specifically: internal sign-off that v1.0 is GA (all tests PASS)[121]; ≥1 external design partner is actively using the system with successful outcomes; and an organized body of evidence (ORR report, release notes) exists to share with stakeholders. This item essentially closes Era 1 – the platform is ready to move beyond pilot into more general use.
●	E1-I2: QAGF Prototype & Demonstration
 Planes: Discovery
 Major Deliverables: A working Quantum Asset Generative Foundry (QAGF) prototype (v0.1) delivered and evaluated. It can autonomously generate or optimize small quantum circuits/codes. Deliverables include integration of QAGF with the A4Q stack in a lab setting (e.g. the ability to suggest a circuit which is then watermarked and run) and experimental results demonstrating its potential. For example, one case study where QAGF output beats a human design by >5% on some benchmark[147]. Also, define initial KPIs for QAGF (e.g. percentage of generated circuits that are non-trivial or improve performance[148]).
 Prerequisites: Basic ML models developed; QAGF connected to the pipeline in a non-production mode (perhaps as an offline tool). Task P2-T5 (watermark toolkit) indirectly supported this by ensuring any AI-generated circuits can be watermarked[149]. Era 1 allocation of ML/R&D resources to QAGF.
 DK/SLO Targets: No formal production SLOs (QAGF not in prod use yet), but experimental targets: e.g. At least 30% of auto-generated circuits are novel (not mere rehash of training data)[148]; ≥1 AI-designed solution shows >5–10% improvement in error rate or algorithm metric vs known baseline[147]. Also, QAGF integration must not violate core platform invariants – meaning any circuits it produces still compile within 250 ms etc., implying QAGF is run asynchronously or offline to avoid impacting live performance[150].
 Exit Criteria: By end of Year 2, evidence of QAGF value: e.g., an internal demo or partner demo where the QAGF-designed circuit outperforms a baseline, or a novel error-correcting code is validated in simulation[147]. If achieved, proceed with further investment in Discovery in Era 2. If not (QAGF fails to produce useful results), trigger a pivot decision[151]: either refocus QAGF on a narrower problem or integrate an external generative tool. The “Go” gate is at least one tangible success (and partner enthusiasm) to justify continuing QAGF development[147][151]. A “Kill/Pivot” would mean re-scoping or pausing QAGF to conserve resources for core features. This decision (with supporting data) is explicitly documented before entering Era 2.
(By the end of Era 1, Invarian has delivered on its initial promises: a stable v1 platform with demonstrated trust fabric and orchestration, plus a proof-of-concept of the AI Discovery plane. The company moves from proving feasibility to scaling and broadening the system.)
Era 2 (Years 2–4, 2027–2029) – Scaling Up & Early Ecosystem Integration
Strategic Goals (Era 2): Transition from bespoke single-tenant deployments to a scalable, multi-tenant platform, and begin knitting A4Q into a broader quantum ecosystem. This era focuses on extensibility and external integration. Goals include: (1) Multi-Tenancy & Federation: Evolve the governance plane so that multiple organizations or teams can securely co-exist on one network – e.g. enabling a consortium ledger or cross-org trust fabric[152][153]. (2) Autonomy Enhancements: Advance the orchestrator toward a predictive, self-healing system – introducing anomaly-driven optimizations and preparing for early fault-tolerant control loops (even if hardware isn’t fully ready)[154][155]. Reliability targets tighten (aim for “four nines” uptime ~99.99% in design). (3) Discovery Integration: Grow QAGF from a prototype to a beta feature integrated into A4Q – allow internal or select external users to invoke AI-generated circuit suggestions through the platform[156]. (4) Ecosystem & Standards: Increase adoption across providers – integrate more cloud QPU backends, adopt emerging standards for quantum job definitions, possibly spin off the trust fabric as a more neutral service if it encourages industry uptake[153][157]. Success in Era 2 is measured by broader adoption: multiple organizations actively using A4Q (some in a shared network), more cloud/hardware vendors integrated, and first real-world use of AI-generated quantum solutions.
Era 2 Checklist Items:
●	E2-I1: Federated Trust Fabric & Multi-Org Ledger Network
 Plane(s): Governance
 Major Deliverables: Transition the trust fabric from single-tenant deployments to a federated multi-organization model. Deliverables include launching a permissioned ledger network where multiple organizations run their own trust-fabric nodes that interoperate[152][158]. Develop cross-node trust synchronization: e.g. periodic exchange of ledger summaries or a consensus mechanism so that separate org ledgers can validate shared transactions (like a quantum IP exchange). Possibly introduce an organization identifier in ledger entries and policies to isolate data by org while still allowing selective sharing[159][160]. Also, produce governance procedures for consortium use (e.g. how new members join, how root of trust is shared).
 Prerequisites: Era 1’s robust single-tenant ledger (active/active Postgres) and the blockchain pilot (P2-T3) results to inform design. Also P5-T1 (multi-tenant access control) will lay groundwork at the app layer[161][162]. Need at least two partner organizations willing to test a shared ledger (perhaps a research consortium or cloud provider + customer scenario).
 DK/SLO Targets: Ledger consistency and availability become paramount. In Era 2, tighten ledger reliability: e.g. ensure no more than 0.001% discrepancy between org ledgers (perhaps via periodic hash comparisons) – essentially eventual consistency within seconds. Uptime target for ledger network maybe 99.99% (with distributed nodes). Ledger append latency might remain ~120 ms per org, but cross-org verification might have a target (e.g. cross-check within 5 s). Also, security invariants: no org should be able to tamper with another’s entries (test by attempting unauthorized cross-org actions – should be impossible by policy).
 Exit Criteria: By mid-2029, operate a functional multi-org A4Q network with at least ~3 distinct organizations connected[153][157]. For example, a cloud provider, a national lab, and an enterprise all sharing a ledger or at least recognizing each other’s watermark/ledger entries. Concrete exit metrics: e.g. 5+ cross-organization workflows executed (jobs where one org generates IP and another executes, with trust maintained), Y organizations running their nodes continuously for Z months. Additionally, an external audit or evaluation of the trust fabric in this federated mode is completed (proving we can scale trust beyond one silo). A “Go” means consortium governance is viable and will be expanded; a “No-Go” would mean fall back to single-tenant per org and maybe integrate via periodic exchanges (less desirable).
●	E2-I2: Advanced Autonomy & Predictive Self-Healing
 Plane(s): Autonomy (with Cross-cutting reliability)
 Major Deliverables: Evolve the orchestrator from reactive scheduling to a predictive, self-healing orchestrator. Deliver new features like: anomaly detection alerts actively triggering responses (e.g. automatically pausing a backend that shows high error rates and rerouting jobs)[163][164]; initial fault-tolerance control loops (simulate or manage “magic-state” resources or error correction processes in anticipation of FTQC hardware)[165][166]. Also integrate reinforcement learning or control systems (maybe promoting the AutoCal-RL from shadow mode to active mode on real hardware calibration)[167][168]. Essentially, Autonomy plane begins moving towards its “autopilot” role. Deliverable examples: a Magic State Factory simulator running in orchestrator to test scheduling of quantum error correction routines, an Anomaly Dashboard that ops can see showing automated interventions taken[169][170]. Possibly a public demonstration where the platform handles a minor qubit failure event automatically (in a partner’s quantum system).
 Prerequisites: Era 1’s anomaly detection in passive mode (from P4-T1)[163], calibration integration in shadow (P4-T2)[171]. Need accumulating operational data from Year 1–2 to train predictive models (error rate baselines, etc.). Coordination with hardware partners to get telemetry on actual issues to respond to.
 DK/SLO Targets: Increase reliability SLOs: target 99.99% uptime (approx 1 hour/year downtime) across orchestrator clusters[154][155]. This implies automated failovers must be nearly seamless. Define a new invariant: Anomaly Response Time – e.g. if a backend’s error rate spikes beyond threshold, the system mitigates (reroute or alert) within <30 s. Also, for early fault-tolerance, perhaps define a metric like quantum resource utilization – ensure that orchestrator can manage long-running FTQC simulations without dropping below e.g. 98% resource availability (a way to measure it can handle heavy continuous load). We also likely tighten recovery time (maybe target <30 s from 60 s). Essentially, this era’s autonomy improvements aim at “four nines” reliability and demonstrable self-correction capabilities.
 Exit Criteria: By end of Era 2, the orchestrator should have autonomic features active in production for at least one design partner[154][155]. Exit success looks like: e.g. “During a partner’s 48-hour test, the system automatically detected and corrected 3 minor backend failures with zero human intervention, maintaining overall uptime >99.99%.” Or “An AutoCal-RL routine was deployed live on a test device and improved its error rate by 10% over two weeks, automatically applied by orchestrator.” In quantitative terms: maintain 99.99% uptime in a multi-region setup (maybe no more than ~5 min downtime per year, measured over pilot period) and have anomaly mitigation at least in a pilot stage (maybe not fully closed-loop on all issues, but at least one significant automated recovery event in the wild). If these are met, proceed to push more autonomy. If not (e.g. auto-systems caused instability), we might decide to keep humans in the loop longer and refine in next era (so the go/kill here might be about enabling full auto-healing by Era 3 or holding off).
●	E2-I3: QAGF Integration & Beta Release
 Plane(s): Discovery (with ties to Autonomy for workflow integration)
 Major Deliverables: Evolve the Quantum Asset Generative Foundry from a standalone prototype into a platform-integrated beta feature available to users[172][173]. Deliverables: AQuDAN “Discovery Studio” interface (maybe an extension of CLI or SDK) where a user can request AI-generated circuit suggestions for a given problem. A knowledge base/repository is set up to store AI-discovered circuits along with metadata (performance metrics, tags)[174][175]. Integration means QAGF suggestions can be fed directly into the A4Q workflow – e.g. a user runs a command to get a circuit, which is then watermarked, executed, and results compared. Also possibly expose an API for partners to plug in their data or models into QAGF (extensibility)[176]. Internally, the QAGF models would be improved using the accumulating data from platform runs (transfer learning from all circuits run so far). By labeling this “beta”, we might release it to select power users or as an opt-in feature on the platform.
 Prerequisites: Era 1 QAGF success (E1-I2 go decision) – we assume there was at least a promising result. Additional hires or ML research in Year 2 to refine models. Some integration tasks likely in Phase 4 or 5 (e.g. building out data pipelines for QAGF)[177]. Also need platform support: e.g. maybe a dedicated service for QAGF or reuse orchestrator to call QAGF modules.
 DK/SLO Targets: Introduce Discovery-plane KPIs: e.g. Recommendation Precision – at least X% of QAGF-generated suggestions yield a measurable improvement (say ≥20% of suggestions provide >5% performance gain on their test problem)[173][178]. Also track usage: number of QAGF-generated solutions adopted per quarter – aiming for an upward trend[179][180]. Crucially, ensure QAGF doesn’t degrade core SLOs: it likely runs asynchronously or in parallel so it doesn’t slow down compile times or such (perhaps run it on separate compute resources). If QAGF starts to be invoked in workflows, we might set a bound like “QAGF suggestion generation ≤ X hours” (non-realtime, but some expectation so users aren’t left waiting indefinitely). Also maintain security – any QAGF model outputs are still watermarked and governed, and if using any external training data, track its provenance.
 Exit Criteria: By end of Era 2, QAGF should be a credible value-add to the platform[181][182]. Concretely: at least 1–2 case studies from pilot users where QAGF contributed something valuable (e.g. “A4Q’s Discovery feature suggested an error mitigation strategy that improved our algorithm’s fidelity by 15%”)[181][183]. The feature is made available (in beta) to a limited user base, with documentation and perhaps a feedback mechanism. If those users find it useful (even if still rough), that’s a “Go” to continue heavy investment (with plan to monetize it or include in premium offering). If underperforms (no clear wins, or no one uses it due to complexity), we consider pivoting – perhaps focusing QAGF on a narrower niche or partnering with an external AI. Essentially, success means by v3.0 (end of Era 2) we can announce “AI-assisted quantum design now part of A4Q (beta)” and have evidence to back it.
(Era 2 ends around mid-2029 with A4Q evolving from a single-tenant product to a multi-tenant, semi-autonomous platform integrated in a small network of quantum players. We’ve also turned our moonshot AI idea into a real (if experimental) feature. Next, we prepare for the quantum industry’s big leap: fault-tolerance and scale.)
Era 3 (Years 4–6, 2029–2031) – Fault-Tolerance Readiness & Broad Adoption
Strategic Goals (Era 4–6): Prepare the platform for the coming of fault-tolerant quantum computing (FTQC) and push towards broader industry adoption before that wave hits. This era likely coincides with early FTQC prototypes emerging (~2030)[184][185]. Key goals: (1) FTQC Integration & Autonomy – Ensure A4Q can manage fault-tolerant hardware operations, including long-running jobs, error correction cycles, and new resource types (e.g. magic state factories)[154][155]. Essentially deliver the “FTAE” (Fault-Tolerant Autonomy Engine) full capabilities. (2) Production Multi-Party Governance – solidify the multi-org trust fabric into a production network, potentially establish A4Q as a backbone for an industry-wide quantum IP exchange or consortium. By now, multiple external audits/certifications should endorse our security and compliance (maybe even required for enterprise deals). (3) Discovery at Scale – turn QAGF from beta to a fully productized tool embedded in user workflows, possibly even enabling a marketplace of quantum assets (users sharing or selling circuits via our platform). (4) Standards & Compliance Leadership – by era’s end, ensure A4Q is aligned with (or leading) any de facto standards (e.g. quantum job formats, PQC standards, cloud integration protocols) so that big enterprise customers view it as a safe choice. Technically, reliability and efficiency targets tighten further (aim for “five nines” in core services if possible, and any performance overheads minimized). In this era, we expect wider adoption: not just design partners, but perhaps general availability to many paying customers, and perhaps partnerships with major quantum hardware vendors to bundle A4Q as their operations layer.
Era 3 Checklist Items:
●	E3-I1: Fault-Tolerant Orchestration Demonstration
 Plane(s): Autonomy
 Major Deliverables: Achieve a convincing FTQC orchestration demo under A4Q’s control. This could be done on early fault-tolerant hardware (if available by ~2030) or high-fidelity simulations if physical devices lag. Deliverables: orchestrator enhancements that handle new FTQC constructs – e.g. tracking logical qubits vs physical qubits, managing magic-state factories (special resources needed for FTQC)[155][152], scheduling long sequences of error-correction cycles, etc. Possibly integration with a partner’s experimental FTQC system (like a national lab’s small logical qubit setup). We should demonstrate that our platform can run a long-lived quantum workload (hours or days) maintaining target uptime and performance. Another deliverable: an automated recovery from a fault at the logical qubit level – e.g. our anomaly detection triggers a recalibration or re-synthesis of a circuit mid-run without aborting the whole job (a stretch goal). Essentially prove that our “autopilot” can handle the complexity of FT quantum operations.
 Prerequisites: Era 2’s autonomy upgrades (E2-I2) in place, plus likely early access to an FTQC testbed or advanced simulator. Collaboration with hardware teams to get telemetry hooks from their error correction processes. Possibly need to incorporate new telemetry types (syndromes from QEC decoders, etc.) into our trust fabric or orchestrator. By Phase 5 or 6 in our execution, tasks would include building this integration.
 DK/SLO Targets: Redefine reliability at a new level: System Uptime ≥99.999% (five nines) – meaning virtually zero downtime year-over-year in a controlled data center[154][155]. For FTQC runs, define a runtime continuity metric – e.g. platform can sustain an uninterrupted quantum experiment for ≥X hours/days. If a component fails, it recovers without losing the experiment (within allowable error thresholds). Perhaps test an end-to-end 24-hour run with no human intervention as a benchmark. Also efficiency: even with heavy QEC overhead, the orchestrator should keep utilization high (like >90% of quantum cores busy on average, showing it handles scheduling efficiently). Possibly a new invariant like “No single job failure propagates” – i.e. one hardware fault doesn’t cause cascade failure of orchestrator or other jobs.
 Exit Criteria: At least one public or high-profile demonstration of A4Q controlling a fault-tolerant quantum process by ~year 6[186][185]. For example, an experiment at a partner lab where our platform ran a small logical qubit computation (with thousands of physical qubits simulated or actual) – essentially showcasing “A4Q is ready for the FTQC era.” Quantitatively: manage a continuous quantum operation for say >8 hours with full auto-recovery and within performance bounds (if any part fails, it didn’t stop the experiment, just logged and corrected). Also likely gather a milestone like a year-6 ORR focusing on FTQC readiness (ensuring any new invariants for FT are met). If this is achieved, the platform is cleared as “FTQC-ready” and we press full throttle for broad deployment as soon as hardware appears. If not (e.g. our system struggles with the complexity or hardware delays mean we can’t test fully), we might extend this era’s timeline or adjust scope (like focus on more simulation until hardware catches up). But given predictions, year 6 is targeted to coincide roughly with this capability emerging[187][188] – being ready will be a major competitive advantage.
●	E3-I2: Production Multi-Party Governance & Compliance Mastery
 Plane(s): Governance
 Major Deliverables: By this era, the multi-organization trust fabric from Era 2 becomes a production service/network with real value – possibly branded as a “Quantum IP Exchange” or similar consortium. Deliverables: multiple organizations (say ≥5) routinely using a shared ledger or cross-verified ledger system for quantum workflows, with clear legal/compliance frameworks in place (perhaps even turned into a service offering). Possibly spin out the trust fabric as a more neutral entity or foundation (if that helps adoption)[153][157]. Technically, implement any remaining features needed for enterprise compliance: e.g. fine-grained data sovereignty controls (maybe deploying regional ledger nodes to satisfy different jurisdiction requirements), audit tooling that can produce compliance reports (e.g. feed of all quantum jobs and their IP provenance for an auditor) and external certifications (like ISO 27001, SOC2, etc. for our processes). Another deliverable: integration with identity/access management standards – e.g. single sign-on integration for multiple orgs, possibly integration with existing cloud IAM so that adopting A4Q in a big enterprise is seamless from an auth perspective. Possibly also adapt to any emerging standard watermark formats if industry has them (ensuring our watermark can be recognized or we can recognize others, if by now others adopt similar tech).
 Prerequisites: Era 2’s federated trust fabric functioning (E2-I1). At least one successful pilot of multi-org ledger. We’d likely need involvement of legal to set up an actual consortium if going that route (maybe establishing a separate network governance model). We also would have completed at least one external security audit by Phase 6 (P6-T1 in execution)[189][190] – addressing any findings to ensure enterprise readiness.
 DK/SLO Targets: Tighten governance-related invariants: Audit coverage remains ~100%, but now across org boundaries – possibly define consistency SLO for cross-org ledger (e.g. ≤0.001% discrepancy or cryptographic assurance of consistency every T hours). Security/availability: even with multiple parties, maintain ≥99.99% ledger availability and recovery <30 s (improving from 60 s) – likely achieved via distributed consensus improvements. Introduce External audit SLOs: e.g. zero critical vulnerabilities in annual external pen-test; compliance controls meet 100% of target standards (if we aim for SOC2, etc., track that all required controls are implemented and tested). Possibly by year 6, our governance framework might achieve an official certification or government accreditation (especially if targeting gov clients), so one exit criterion might be obtaining such a certification.
 Exit Criteria: Widespread adoption of A4Q governance plane. By end of Era 3, we want to see multiple independent entities using A4Q to manage quantum IP and compliance, not just in pilot but in actual production-like scenarios[186][185]. For instance: a quantum cloud provider and a bank are transacting – the bank sends circuits, the provider runs them, and all of it is logged and governed by A4Q’s ledger that both trust. Or a scenario where a research consortium shares quantum algorithms via our platform (protected by watermarks and contracts). Quantitatively: aim for at least 10 organizations on the trust network, X hundred quantum jobs per month flowing through it, and perhaps Y IP disputes resolved by referring to our ledger (a sign it’s seen as an authority). Also exit with at least one major compliance endorsement: e.g. an auditor signs off that using A4Q meets certain regulatory requirements (which we can tout to customers). If achieved, A4Q becomes the de facto trust layer for quantum collaborations. If not fully (e.g. only a couple orgs use it, or big players make their own competing ledger), we must adjust our go-to-market – either by open-sourcing the trust fabric to drive adoption or focusing on singular enterprise deployments. But Era 3 goal is to ensure by the time FTQC arrives, the legal and trust infrastructure for multi-party quantum computing is basically solved by us, giving us a deep moat.
●	E3-I3: AI Discovery Engine GA & Ecosystem Integration
 Plane(s): Discovery (and Autonomy for workflow embedding)
 Major Deliverables: Graduate the QAGF (Discovery plane) from a beta feature to a fully GA service integrated into the platform and potentially spawning its own ecosystem. Deliverables: a polished “Quantum Discovery Studio” that’s part of A4Q’s standard offering – users (developers, researchers) can invoke AI-driven circuit/coding assistance easily via API/GUI, with robust documentation and support. The QAGF models are improved through Years 2–4 data and perhaps incorporate external knowledge (maybe trained on an industry-wide dataset of circuits if available). We might also open up an extension interface for third parties: e.g. allow plugin models or user-submitted training data, making A4Q something of a marketplace for quantum algorithms (where our trust fabric ensures IP attribution for models too)[176][191]. Possibly an app store or library within A4Q where curated AI-discovered circuits are shared (with our watermark ensuring attribution). We deliver integration such that an AI-suggested circuit can seamlessly loop into execution and back for iterative refinement (closing the human-AI loop). Also by now, we incorporate any emerging competitive tech – e.g. if open-source QAGF alternatives arose, we either outperformed or integrated them.
 Prerequisites: Era 2’s QAGF beta had positive reception (E2-I3 success). Additional investment in ML (maybe specialized models per domain by year 4). Partnerships perhaps with academic AI researchers to stay state-of-art. Also need the data from lots of runs (by year 4 we presumably have thousands of circuits and results logged, to feed into QAGF’s learning). The trust fabric and multi-tenant enhancements allow sharing results across orgs (with permission), giving more training data. Possibly tasks in Phase 5/6 to build a repository and community features.
 DK/SLO Targets: Formalize SLOs for the Discovery service as it goes GA. For example: AI Suggestion Relevance Score – maintain a certain acceptance rate or success metric (like ≥30% of suggestions are used by the user or provide improvement)[173][192]. Turnaround time – if a user requests a suggestion, maybe we guarantee a response within X minutes/hours (depending on compute; by GA maybe aim for interactive or same-day suggestions, using optimized models or caching). Also ensure Integration SLOs: e.g. using the Discovery service does not add more than, say, 5% overhead to the overall workflow time (i.e., it’s efficiently integrated, often running in parallel to execution). And of course, any circuit it generates must comply with invariants (we probably incorporate that into its objective function by now – e.g. penalize suggestions that would break depth limits or cause long compile times). Possibly track User engagement: measure usage and target an increasing trend – e.g. by GA, aim for X% of projects on A4Q have tried at least one AI-generated solution.
 Exit Criteria: By ~2031, A4Q’s Discovery plane is a distinguishing, monetizable feature of the platform. Concrete indicators: external users or customers can cite a success story where A4Q’s AI helped achieve something not previously possible (e.g. discovered a more efficient circuit for a finance algorithm that saved $M in runtime). We likely move it from free beta to a paid tier or integrated value that attracts customers. Perhaps even license certain high-value AI-discovered solutions via the platform (creating a mini economy). Another exit metric: N publications or patents resulting from circuits designed by QAGF (demonstrating innovation). If by era’s end we can say, for example, “5 of the top 10 quantum algorithm breakthroughs this year involved A4Q’s generative suggestions,” that’s massive success. If on the other hand uptake is low or results mediocre, we pivot strategy: maybe integrate with another AI platform or reduce investment. But assuming earlier gates were met, by Era 3 we aim for Discovery to be fully productized, adding to our moat (the more data and user adoption we have, the harder for a competitor to catch up in AI learned knowledge).
●	E3-I4: Industry Standards & Platform Ubiquity Checkpoint
 Plane(s): Cross-Cutting/Ecosystem
 Major Deliverables: Ensure by Year 6 that A4Q is aligned with and ideally helping set the industry standards and that our platform isn’t missing any critical integration point. Deliverables include complying with whatever might have become standard for quantum job representation (if QIR or OpenQASM improvements, or some new cloud API standard, ensure our QIR adapters and SDK support it)[193][194]. Also if any new regulations or best practices emerged (e.g. certain encryption standards, or sustainability reporting standards), incorporate those. Possibly finalize any open standard contributions we started – e.g. by now maybe the watermarking scheme is part of an IEEE/ISO standard and we implement the final version (if different from our original). We should also check platform portability: e.g. ensure A4Q can run on all major cloud infrastructures and even on-prem clusters of various sizes, with automated deployment (by year 6, many more enterprises might want to self-host or hybrid-host as they scale quantum teams). Another deliverable: a formal Operational Readiness Review v4.0 around year 6 that double-checks SLOs at scale and standard compliance before we scale out widely (like an ORR focusing on the new capabilities, analogous to GA ORR but for FTQC readiness and multi-tenant at scale)[195][196].
 Prerequisites: Previous standardization efforts (P3-T3 in Phase 3) have progressed. Possibly involvement in consortia continued through Era 2. Our presence in those groups means we have heads-up on upcoming changes. We also likely expanded our infra tooling to handle large scale (maybe using IaC to deploy easily on different environments).
 DK/SLO Targets: This item is less about specific DK numbers and more qualitative completeness. One could interpret it as an exit Gate: e.g., ensure by this checkpoint that “A4Q supports all major quantum hardware via standard interfaces” – which could be measured by number of backends integrated (aim for all top N QPU vendors). Or “A4Q meets all required standards for targeted industries” – measured by checklists like in P6-T2 (roadmap alignment) and P6-T1 (external audit) being green[197][198]. Also might include tightening any remaining invariants if new demands: e.g. if a new PQC algorithm became recommended, we’ve integrated it by now (so PQC overhead invariant still holds but with updated crypto). If a new metric like “carbon per quantum operation” becomes industry focus, we might add an invariant or tracking for it. Essentially, ensure no technical debt or misalignment lingers before we scale fully.
 Exit Criteria: A formal Go/No-Go at Year 6 (likely coinciding with a v4.0 release) where leadership reviews if the platform is indeed ready for broad marketing as “the OS for the quantum age”[184][195]. Go if: All critical pieces (multi-tenant, autonomy, discovery) are production-grade, SLOs are met at scale, we’ve kept up with standards, and maybe we have at least a handful of reference customers beyond design partners (some paying). Perhaps a key outcome: signing of a major commercial contract (like a big enterprise or government choosing A4Q for their quantum operations) – that often happens around this time if all assurances (security, standards) are in place. No-Go triggers: If, say, fault-tolerance is still not reality and our features stagnated, or if big players are hesitant due to something missing (like they want on-prem support and we don’t have it, etc.), we address those before scaling further. Essentially, by crossing Era 3, we want to be able to claim with credibility: “Invarian A4Q is the proven, standard-compliant backbone for quantum computing – ready for prime time as the industry enters the fault-tolerance era.” Achieving that positions us to dominate as the number of quantum computers and users explodes thereafter.
(By the end of Era 3, roughly 2031, Invarian’s platform should be entrenched: we have navigated the risky transition into fault-tolerant quantum, expanded the user base, and aligned with ecosystem norms. The next eras will be about optimizing and scaling business-wise, leveraging the moat established.)
Era 4 (Years 6–8, 2031–2033) – Optimization at Scale & Network Effects
Strategic Goals (Era 6–8): With fault-tolerant quantum computing likely operational or imminent, Era 4 emphasizes scaling up deployments, optimizing performance/cost at scale, and solidifying network effects. The platform by now could be in use by many customers; the focus shifts to efficiency, automation, and ecosystem lock-in. Key goals: (1) Hyper-Optimization: Optimize everything from algorithm pipelines to hardware utilization – reduce overheads (maybe aim for negligible compile latency even for complex circuits via advanced compilers, minimize carbon footprint via global scheduling, etc.). (2) Autonomous Operations at Scale: One A4Q instance (or network) might be orchestrating many quantum data centers – ensure the platform self-optimizes: automated load balancing across global QPU fleets, maintenance scheduling, energy optimization, etc. (3) Wider Adoption & Standards Consolidation: Turn our de facto lead into an official one – if not done, possibly open source parts of the platform (like the trust fabric or IR) to cement it as industry standard while monetizing value-added services[199][200]. By Era 4, any remaining competitors should either integrate with us or be niche. We likely also focus on business aspects: e.g. packaging the platform into a “Quantum Data Center OS” product that large operators adopt as default[201][202]. (4) Monetization & Efficiency: Drive unit economics – if earlier we charged per pilot or SaaS fees, now quantum workloads might be large enough to generate substantial usage-based revenue; optimize cost (the “FinOps” aspect of quantum jobs) so that running through our platform is cheaper for customers than alternatives (maybe via intelligent scheduling that saves them money). Essentially, Era 4 is about scaling out while maximizing performance and reinforcing our moat (data, standards, network of users).
(Era 4 items will be more about continuous improvement and expansion, and may be fewer specific gates as the platform matures. We provide a couple representative items.)
●	E4-I1: Full Autonomy & Self-Healing Quantum Data Centers
 Plane(s): Autonomy
 Major Deliverables: By this era, any quantum data center (or multi-tenant cluster) running A4Q should operate with minimal human intervention. Deliverables: further enhancements to the orchestration AI – perhaps implementing closed-loop control for calibrations (AutoCal-RL fully production: system detects drift and auto-tunes hardware in real-time)[167][203], and automated fault recovery for complex subsystems (like replacing a failing qubit with a spare if hardware supports it, orchestrated by software). Possibly integration with facility-level systems: e.g. hooking into power management (if a cryo cooler goes down, orchestrator gracefully pauses jobs, etc.). Essentially deliver a “lights out” quantum data center concept.
 Prerequisites: Era 3’s FTQC control successes (E3-I1). More data from running actual hardware (by now we likely manage multiple FTQC devices). Might involve partnership with hardware vendors to have APIs for deeper control.
 DK/SLO Targets: Aim for 99.999%+ uptime (five nines or even six nines if feasible on some subsystems). Possibly guarantee zero job failures due to system faults (meaning any internal failure is handled transparently, jobs only fail due to user error or fundamental hardware error beyond our control). Also target cost efficiency: maybe an invariant like “system overhead uses <5% of quantum compute time” (ensuring almost all QPU cycles are doing user work, not waiting or housekeeping). Carbon and power optimization might come in: e.g. schedule high-power jobs when renewable energy is peaking (achieving some ESG targets automatically). If in earlier eras we flagged jobs for carbon, now we might actively shift loads across global sites to minimize carbon usage.
 Exit Criteria: Achieve a demonstration of a fully autonomous quantum operation for an extended period (e.g. a quantum data center running for a month with negligible manual ops). Possibly an external audit or case study: e.g. a major cloud provider publicly announces that their quantum service uses Invarian’s A4Q to run with an SRE team of near-zero – demonstrating trust in our autonomy. If we see that customers can operate large quantum facilities without needing to build their own quantum ops expertise (because A4Q handles it), that significantly increases our moat and value. For a go, we want customers comfortable scaling up usage knowing the platform will handle reliability and cost – at this point we’re essentially printing money per quantum computation run on our system.
●	E4-I2: Platform Efficiency & “Quantum DevOps” Standardization
 Plane(s): Cross-Cutting (Performance, DevEx)
 Major Deliverables: Push the envelope in performance and developer experience to ensure at scale, A4Q is not the bottleneck anywhere. Deliverables: optimize compile pipeline (maybe new compiler versions that use advanced classical pre-computation or distributed compilation to keep latency low even for huge circuits – aim to compile million-qubit circuits if that’s a thing – basically be ready for bigger problems); optimize ledger throughput (maybe move to high-performance ledger storage or sharding to handle millions of transactions if usage skyrockets); ensure our SDKs and tooling support complex team workflows (like version control for quantum circuits, CI/CD pipelines for quantum software integrated with our platform – essentially enabling a mature DevOps culture for quantum). Possibly deliver a suite of DevOps tools (or integrate with existing ones) such as monitoring dashboards specifically for quantum application performance, automated testing harnesses using digital twins of QPUs, etc., all tied into A4Q environment. This makes our platform sticky for development teams as well, not just runtime.
 Prerequisites: A lot of the basics are done in prior eras; this is about iteration and improvement. We would use insights from possibly thousands of jobs to identify bottlenecks and tackle them. Also by now competitor products might exist; we must stay ahead in efficiency.
 DK/SLO Targets: Possibly introduce stretch goals beyond original invariants since hardware improved.
